Speed Increase] C2 --> D2[Near Zero Net
Time Difference
Small delta_tau] B1 --> C1[Uncompensated
Time Smearing
Large delta_tau] style A1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style A2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a6b3a,color:#000 style C1 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style D2 fill:#99ffcc,stroke:#006633,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Dispersion Compensation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Transmitter
Bit Stream at B Gbps] --> P2[Optical Source
Multimode LED] P2 --> P3[Multimode Fiber Link
Length L, Index Profile alpha] P3 --> P4[Intermodal Dispersion
delta_tau equals L NA squared by 2 n1 c] P4 --> P5{Is delta_tau less than T by 2?} P5 -- Yes --> P6[Successful Detection
No ISI] P5 -- No --> P7[ISI Errors Occur
Bit-Rate Limited] P7 --> P8[Solution: Use Graded-Index Fiber
OR Reduce NA OR Use SM Fiber] style P4 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style P6 fill:#99ffcc,stroke:#006633,color:#000 style P7 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style P8 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip (for KTU Board Exams):** When asked to "explain with a suitable diagram," always include (a) input pulse, (b) ray/mode paths of *different lengths* inside the fiber, and (c) broadened output pulse. Sketches score full marks; verbal-only explanations lose 2–3 marks. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** Define **intermodal dispersion**. Why is it absent in single-mode fibers? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Intermodal dispersion is the temporal broadening of an optical pulse caused by the *time-of-flight difference* between different guided modes propagating through a multimode fiber. Different modes follow different geometric path lengths and travel at different group velocities, so the energy of a single pulse arrives over a finite time interval $\Delta\tau = t_{max} - t_{min}$ (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for cause, 1 for absence in SMF). *Single-mode fiber carries only the fundamental $LP_{01}$ mode, so there is no intermodal time-of-flight difference. The pulse broadening is then governed only by intramodal (chromatic) dispersion.* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** Compare **intermodal dispersion** and **intramodal dispersion** with two points each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Intermodal Dispersion | Intramodal Dispersion | |---|---|---| | Cause | Time difference between modes (3 marks: 1 for each paired point) | Wavelength dependence of group velocity within a single mode | | Occurs in | Multimode fibers only | Both multimode and single-mode fibers | | Mitigation | Graded-index profiling, single-mode fiber | Narrow-linewidth lasers, dispersion-shifted fiber, DCF | --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Internal Choice Format (KTU 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Step-Index Derivation + Numerical** **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]** *(a)* Derive the expression for the maximum **intermodal dispersion** in a step-index multimode fiber in terms of the numerical aperture $NA$, core index $n_1$, fiber length $L$, and velocity of light $c$. State the assumptions clearly. (7 marks) *(b)* A step-index multimode fiber has core refractive index $n_1 = 1.48$, cladding index $n_2 = 1.46$, and length $L = 2$ km. Determine: (i) the numerical aperture $NA$, (ii) the relative index difference $\Delta$, (iii) the maximum intermodal pulse broadening $\Delta\tau$, (iv) the optical bandwidth–length product $BL$. (7 marks) --- ### **Model Solution to Question A** #### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)** **Assumptions (1 mark):** - Step-index, weakly-guiding fiber ($n_1 \approx n_2$). - Meridional rays (skew rays contribute similarly in a statistical sense). - Source is much narrower than the pulse broadening. **Derivation (6 marks):** A meridional ray entering the fiber at angle $\theta_1$ to the axis traverses a geometric path: $$L_{path} = \frac{L}{\cos\theta_1}$$ Its transit time through the medium of index $n_1$ is: $$t(\theta_1) = \frac{n_1 L}{c \cos\theta_1}$$ *Minimum transit time* (axial ray, $\theta_1 = 0$): $$t_{min} = \frac{n_1 L}{c}$$ *Maximum transit time* (ray at the critical angle): Snell's law gives $\sin\theta_{1,max} = NA/n_1$, so: $$\cos\theta_{1,max} = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - NA^2}}{n_1}$$ Therefore: $$t_{max} = \frac{n_1^2 L}{c \cdot n_2} = \frac{n_1^2 L}{c \cdot NA}$$ *Pulse broadening*: $$\Delta\tau = t_{max} - t_{min} = \frac{n_1^2 L}{c \cdot n_2} - \frac{n_1 L}{c} = \frac{n_1 L}{c}\left(\frac{n_1}{n_2} - 1\right)$$ Using $n_1/n_2 = 1/(1-\Delta)$, the bracket simplifies to $\Delta/(1-\Delta) \approx \Delta$ for small $\Delta$: $$\Delta\tau_{SI} \approx \frac{n_1 L \Delta}{c}$$ Substituting $\Delta \approx NA^2/(2n_1^2)$: $$\boxed{\Delta\tau_{SI} = \frac{L \cdot NA^2}{2 n_1 c}} \qquad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$ #### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)** **(i) Numerical Aperture (2 marks):** $$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} = 0.2425$$ $$\boxed{NA \approx 0.2425}$$ **(ii) Relative Index Difference (1 mark):** $$\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.48 - 1.46}{1.48} = \frac{0.02}{1.48} = 0.0135$$ $$\boxed{\Delta \approx 0.0135}$$ **(iii) Intermodal Pulse Broadening (2 marks):** $$\Delta\tau = \frac{L \cdot NA^2}{2 n_1 c} = \frac{(2 \times 10^3 \text{ m}) \cdot (0.2425)^2}{2 \times 1.48 \times 3 \times 10^8}$$ $$\Delta\tau = \frac{2000 \times 0.05881}{8.88 \times 10^8} = \frac{117.62}{8.88 \times 10^8} = 1.325 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta\tau \approx 132.5 \text{ ns (for 2 km), i.e., 66.25 ns/km}}$$ **(iv) Bandwidth–Length Product (2 marks):** $$BL = \frac{0.44}{\Delta\tau_{\text{per km}}} = \frac{0.44}{66.25 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.64 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz·km}$$ $$\boxed{BL \approx 6.64 \text{ MHz·km}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Graded-Index Comparison + Numerical** **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]** *(a)* Explain the principle of **graded-index fiber** in reducing intermodal dispersion. Derive the expression for pulse broadening in an optimally-indexed ($\alpha = 2$) parabolic graded-index fiber. (7 marks) *(b)* A graded-index multimode fiber has core index $n_1 = 1.50$, cladding index $n_2 = 1.47$, and a parabolic refractive index profile ($\alpha = 2$). For a 1 km link, calculate: (i) the relative index difference $\Delta$, (ii) the intermodal pulse broadening $\Delta\tau_{GI}$, (iii) the BL product, and (iv) compare your result with a step-index fiber of the same specifications. (7 marks) --- ### **Model Solution to Question B** #### **Part (a) — Graded-Index Concept (7 marks)** **Principle (3 marks):** In a graded-index (GI) fiber, the refractive index is maximum ($n_1$) at the center and decreases parabolically toward the cladding. Rays propagating at larger angles (longer paths) travel through regions of *lower* refractive index where the local speed $v(r) = c/n(r)$ is *higher*. With the **optimal profile parameter $\alpha = 2$**, the increase in path length is *exactly compensated* by the increase in speed, making the transit time nearly equal for all modes. **Derivation (4 marks):** The index profile is: $$n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta\left(\frac{r}{a}\right)^{\alpha}}, \quad r \le a$$ For $\alpha = 2$ (parabolic), after ray-trace analysis, the transit-time difference between the axial ray and the highest-order ray evaluates to: $$\Delta\tau_{GI} = \frac{n_1 L \Delta^2}{8 c}$$ This is smaller than the step-index result $\Delta\tau_{SI} = n_1 L \Delta / c$ by a factor of $\Delta/8$. #### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)** **(i) Relative Index Difference (1 mark):** $$\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.50 - 1.47}{1.50} = 0.02$$ **(ii) Graded-Index Pulse Broadening (2 marks):** $$\Delta\tau_{GI} = \frac{n_1 L \Delta^2}{8 c} = \frac{1.50 \times 10^3 \times (0.02)^2}{8 \times 3 \times 10^8}$$ $$= \frac{1500 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{2.4 \times 10^9} = \frac{0.6}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 2.5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ s}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta\tau_{GI} = 0.25 \text{ ns/km}}$$ **(iii) Bandwidth–Length Product (2 marks):** $$BL = \frac{0.44}{0.25 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.76 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz·km} = \boxed{1760 \text{ MHz·km}}$$ **(iv) Comparison with Step-Index (2 marks):** $$\Delta\tau_{SI} = \frac{n_1 L \Delta}{c} = \frac{1.50 \times 10^3 \times 0.02}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 100 \text{ ns/km}$$ $$\text{Improvement factor} = \frac{100 \text{ ns/km}}{0.25 \text{ ns/km}} = 400$$ $$\boxed{\text{Graded-index fiber offers 400x lower pulse broadening}}$$ Theoretical check: $1/(\Delta/8) = 8/0.02 = 400$ ✓ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Pitfalls — Where Marks Are Lost:** > > 1. **Unit Blunder (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert $L$ from km to m when using $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Always write units explicitly: $L(\text{km}) \times 10^3$. > 2. **Sign of $n_1/n_2 - 1$ (–1 mark):** Some students compute the bracket as $1 - n_1/n_2$ (negative). Verify that $n_1 > n_2$ always, so $n_1/n_2 > 1$ and the bracket is positive. > 3. **$NA$ Confusion (–2 marks):** Confusing $NA$ (numerical aperture, dimensionless) with $n_1$ or $n_2$. Always quote $NA \approx 0.2$–$0.4$ for typical SI fibers. > 4. **Missing $\Delta^2$ in GI Formula (–1 mark):** Writing $\Delta\tau_{GI} = n_1 L \Delta / 8c$ (forgetting the square on $\Delta$). The square is critical — it is the *signature* of the GI optimum profile. > 5. **Skipping Assumptions (–1 mark):** KTU valuators explicitly allocate 1 mark to "state the assumptions" in derivation questions. Do not skip. > 6. **Confusing $\Delta\tau_{per\ link}$ vs. $\Delta\tau_{per\ km}$ (–1 mark):** The BL product uses **per-km** broadening. Be explicit in units (ns/km). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print & Memorize Before Exam** - ☐ **Intermodal dispersion** = pulse spreading due to different mode transit times. Occurs **only in multimode fibers**. - ☐ **Step-index formula (canonical):** $\Delta\tau_{SI} = \dfrac{L \cdot NA^2}{2 n_1 c}$ ; alt form: $\Delta\tau_{SI} \approx \dfrac{n_1 L \Delta}{c}$. - ☐ **Graded-index formula (optimum $\alpha = 2$):** $\Delta\tau_{GI} = \dfrac{n_1 L \Delta^2}{8 c}$ — better by a factor of $\Delta/8$. - ☐ **NA** (Numerical Aperture): $NA = n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$. - ☐ **$\Delta$** (Relative index difference): $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$ — typical MM fiber: $\Delta \approx 0.01$ to $0.03$. - ☐ **Bandwidth–Length product:** $BL = 0.44 / \Delta\tau$ in MHz·km (Gaussian pulse convention). - ☐ **Bit-rate limit (Nyquist):** $B_{max} \approx 1/(2\Delta\tau)$ for NRZ digital signaling. - ☐ **Single-mode fiber:** zero intermodal dispersion (only one mode propagates), but suffers from **intramodal (chromatic)** dispersion — covered in the next module. - ☐ **Engineering rule of thumb:** For LANs ≤ 1 Gbps over ≤ 500 m → use MM graded-index; for ≥ 10 Gbps or distances > 2 km → use single-mode fiber. - ☐ **Critical-angle relationship:** $\sin\theta_{1,max} = NA/n_1$ and $\cos\theta_{1,max} = n_2/n_1$ — useful for re-deriving if memory fails. - ☐ **Validity:** Both the ray-optics and mode-theories (Bessel-function solutions of the wave equation) yield the *same* $\Delta\tau$ for step-index multimode fibers. - ☐ **Role of skew rays:** Skew rays follow helical paths, but in weakly-guiding fibers their contribution to intermodal broadening is statistically equivalent to meridional rays. - ☐ **Memorize the canonical numerical pattern:** $n_1 = 1.5$, $n_2 = 1.45$ or $1.48 \rightarrow$ $NA \approx 0.24$–$0.39$, $\Delta \approx 0.013$–$0.033$, $\Delta\tau_{SI} \approx 30$–$170$ ns/km, $BL_{SI} \approx 2$–$15$ MHz·km.

topics.title: Chromatic Dispersion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chromatic Dispersion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CHROMATIC DISPERSION** is the phenomenon in an optical fiber wherein different spectral components (wavelengths) of an optical pulse propagate with different **group velocities**, causing the pulse to broaden temporally as it travels along the fiber. It is the primary bandwidth-limiting impairment in Single-Mode Fibers (SMF) operating in the **1310 nm** and **1550 nm** telecommunication windows.

Mathematically, it is defined as the derivative of the **group delay** ($\tau_g$) per unit length with respect to wavelength:

$$
D(\lambda) \;=\; \frac{1}{L}\,\frac{d\tau_g}{d\lambda} \quad \left[\frac{\text{ps}}{\text{nm}\cdot\text{km}}\right]
$$

Chromatic dispersion has **two independent physical origins**, which combine linearly in Single-Mode Fibers:

1. **Material Dispersion ($D_M$)** — caused by the wavelength-dependent refractive index $n(\lambda)$ of silica glass.
2. **Waveguide (Modal) Dispersion ($D_W$)** — caused by the wavelength-dependent power distribution between the core and cladding, which alters the effective index $n_{\text{eff}}(\lambda)$.

$$
\boxed{\,D_T(\lambda) \;=\; D_M(\lambda) \;+\; D_W(\lambda)\,}
$$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rainbow Relay Race"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine a 100-metre relay race where each lane represents a different color (wavelength) of light. The red runner is naturally slower than the blue runner because of the way the track (fiber material) is built. As the race progresses, runners who started shoulder-to-shoulder finish spread out — the same thing happens to a light pulse. A short, sharp pulse enters the fiber, but because its red and blue components travel at different speeds, the pulse **stretches out (broadens)** by the time it reaches the receiver. This stretching is *Chromatic Dispersion*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | **$2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** |
| $n_1, n_2$ | Core / Cladding refractive index | $\sim 1.48 \;/\; \sim 1.46$ |
| $\lambda_0$ | Zero-dispersion wavelength (SMF-28) | **$\approx 1310$ nm** |
| $D$ | Dispersion coefficient | **ps/(nm·km)** |
| $B \cdot L$ | Bandwidth–Distance product | **GHz·km** |
| $\Delta \lambda$ | Source spectral width | nm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Chromatic dispersion is a **frequency-domain impairment** that sets the ultimate limit on the *bit-rate × distance* product of an optical link. Mastering $D(\lambda)$, $D_M$, $D_W$, and the **zero-dispersion wavelength** is mandatory for KTU ESE questions worth 7 to 14 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

**Step 1 — Phase Velocity vs. Group Velocity**

A monochromatic wave travels at the **phase velocity** $v_p = c/n(\lambda)$. However, a real optical pulse is a *wave packet* containing a small band of wavelengths. The envelope of this packet travels at the **group velocity** $v_g$.

**Why does this matter?** Because the group velocity depends on wavelength (via the slope of $n(\lambda)$), each wavelength slice of the pulse arrives at the receiver at a slightly different time.

**Step 2 — Group Index and Group Delay**

The **group index** $N_g$ governs the speed of the pulse envelope:

$$
N_g(\lambda) \;=\; n(\lambda) \;-\; \lambda\,\frac{dn}{d\lambda}
$$

The **group delay** for a fiber of length $L$:

$$
\tau_g \;=\; \frac{L}{c}\,N_g(\lambda) \;=\; \frac{L}{v_g}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiating Group Delay Yields Dispersion Coefficient**

Taking the derivative of $\tau_g$ with respect to wavelength and dividing by length gives the dispersion coefficient in **ps/(nm·km)**:

$$
D(\lambda) \;=\; \frac{1}{c}\,\frac{dN_g}{d\lambda} \;=\; \frac{-\lambda}{c}\,\frac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^{2}}
$$

This single line is the **heart of chromatic dispersion physics**.

**Step 4 — Two Contributions Add Up**

* **Material dispersion** arises from $dn/d\lambda$ of bulk silica.
* **Waveguide dispersion** arises because the mode confinement changes with $\lambda$; longer wavelengths leak more into the cladding, lowering $n_{\text{eff}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** $D(\lambda)$ is the figure of merit quoted on every commercial SMF datasheet (e.g., SMF-28: $D \le 18$ ps/(nm·km) at 1550 nm). It directly tells the network designer how much pulse broadening to expect for a given source linewidth and link length.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE** — it covers ~90% of KTU 2024 chromatic-dispersion numerical questions.

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $v_g = c / N_g$ | Group velocity of pulse envelope | m/s |
| 2 | $N_g = n(\lambda) - \lambda\,\dfrac{dn}{d\lambda}$ | Group index (wavelength-dependent) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\tau_g = L \cdot N_g / c$ | Group delay of length-$L$ fiber | s |
| 4 | $D(\lambda) = \dfrac{-\lambda}{c}\,\dfrac{d^{2} n}{d\lambda^{2}}$ | Material dispersion coefficient (bulk) | s/m² → ps/(nm·km) |
| 5 | $D_T = D_M + D_W$ | Total chromatic dispersion | ps/(nm·km) |
| 6 | $\Delta\tau_{\text{ch}} = D \cdot L \cdot \Delta\lambda$ | Pulse broadening due to CD | ps |
| 7 | $B_T \cdot L = \dfrac{0.187}{\Delta\tau_{\text{ch}}} \quad$ (for Gaussian pulses) | Bandwidth–length product limit | GHz·km |
| 8 | $B_T \le \dfrac{0.44}{\Delta\tau_{\text{ch}}}$ | NRZ bit-rate limit (Gaussian approx.) | Gbps |
| 9 | $n^{2}(\lambda) - 1 = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{3} \dfrac{B_i \lambda^{2}}{\lambda^{2} - C_i}$ | **Sellmeier equation** for pure silica | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\lambda_{0,D_T}$ : solution of $D_M(\lambda) + D_W(\lambda) = 0$ | Zero-dispersion wavelength | nm |

**Conversion note:** $1 \; \text{ps/(nm·km)} = 10^{-6} \; \text{s/m}^{2}$. Always keep SI when chaining equations.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Long-haul DWDM links** at 1550 nm rely on **Dispersion-Shifted Fiber (DSF)** or **Non-Zero Dispersion-Shifted Fiber (NZ-DSF)** to keep $D$ small but non-zero (suppresses four-wave mixing).
* **Submarine cables** use **Dispersion-Compensating Fiber (DCF)** with negative $D$ (~ $-100$ ps/(nm·km)) cascaded with SMF to flatten the link dispersion.
* **Coherent transponders** use digital signal processing (DSP) to electronically compensate chromatic dispersion, eliminating the need for DCF in modern 100G+ systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation I — Group Velocity from Phase Velocity

A quasi-monochromatic wave packet is built from components near a central angular frequency $\omega_0$:

$$
E(z,t) \;=\; \int_{\omega_0-\Delta\omega}^{\omega_0+\Delta\omega} E(\omega)\,e^{j(\omega t - \beta(\omega)\,z)}\,d\omega
$$

For a narrow spectrum, expand the propagation constant $\beta(\omega)$ in a Taylor series around $\omega_0$:

$$
\beta(\omega) \;\approx\; \beta_0 \;+\; (\omega - \omega_0)\,\beta_1 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}(\omega - \omega_0)^{2}\,\beta_2
$$

The dominant phase term becomes:

$$
\Phi(z,t) \;\approx\; \omega_0 t - \beta_0 z \;-\; (\omega - \omega_0)\bigl(\beta_1 z - t\bigr)
$$

The factor $(\beta_1 z - t)$ reveals that the envelope moves at speed $1/\beta_1$. Hence the **group velocity**:

$$
\boxed{\,v_g \;=\; \frac{1}{\beta_1} \;=\; \frac{d\omega}{d\beta}\,}
$$

Using $\beta = n(\lambda)\,\omega/c$ and converting to wavelength domain:

$$
v_g \;=\; \frac{c}{N_g}, \qquad N_g \;=\; n(\lambda) - \lambda\,\frac{dn}{d\lambda}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Material Dispersion Coefficient $D_M$

Start from the group delay of a unit length:

$$
\frac{\tau_g}{L} \;=\; \frac{1}{v_g} \;=\; \frac{N_g}{c} \;=\; \frac{1}{c}\left[n(\lambda) - \lambda\,\frac{dn}{d\lambda}\right]
$$

Differentiate with respect to wavelength:

$$
\frac{1}{L}\,\frac{d\tau_g}{d\lambda} \;=\; \frac{1}{c}\,\frac{dN_g}{d\lambda} \;=\; \frac{1}{c}\,\frac{d}{d\lambda}\!\left[n(\lambda) - \lambda\,\frac{dn}{d\lambda}\right]
$$

Apply the product rule:

$$
\frac{d}{d\lambda}\!\left[\lambda\,\frac{dn}{d\lambda}\right] \;=\; \frac{dn}{d\lambda} \;+\; \lambda\,\frac{d^{2} n}{d\lambda^{2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{1}{L}\,\frac{d\tau_g}{d\lambda} \;=\; \frac{1}{c}\!\left[\frac{dn}{d\lambda} - \frac{dn}{d\lambda} - \lambda\,\frac{d^{2} n}{d\lambda^{2}}\right] \;=\; -\frac{\lambda}{c}\,\frac{d^{2} n}{d\lambda^{2}}
$$

The first-order $dn/d\lambda$ terms cancel, leaving the famous **material dispersion formula**:

$$
\boxed{\,D_M(\lambda) \;=\; \frac{-\lambda}{c}\,\frac{d^{2} n}{d\lambda^{2}}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Pulse Broadening and Bit-Rate Limit

Consider an optical source with RMS spectral width $\sigma_\lambda$ (in nm) and a fiber of length $L$ km having dispersion coefficient $D$ ps/(nm·km). The RMS pulse broadening is:

$$
\sigma_\tau \;=\; D \cdot L \cdot \sigma_\lambda
$$

For an **unchirped Gaussian pulse**, the transmitted and broadened pulses convolve. Assuming source width is negligible compared to broadened width:

$$
\Delta\tau_{\text{ch}} \;=\; D \cdot L \cdot \Delta\lambda \quad \text{(FWHM or RMS depending on convention)}
$$

The **maximum bit rate** before pulses overlap (eye-diagram closure criterion: pulse broadening $\le 0.5$ bit period for NRZ):

$$
B_{\max} \;=\; \frac{0.44}{\Delta\tau_{\text{ch}}} \;=\; \frac{0.44}{D \cdot L \cdot \Delta\lambda}
$$

The bandwidth–length product becomes:

$$
B_{\max} \cdot L \;=\; \frac{0.44}{D \cdot \Delta\lambda}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** An LED source has spectral width $\Delta\lambda = 30$ nm and is used with a 25 km fiber link having $D = 18$ ps/(nm·km). Calculate (a) pulse broadening, (b) maximum bit rate.

**(a) Pulse Broadening:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\tau_{\text{ch}} \;&= \; D \cdot L \cdot \Delta\lambda \\
&= \; 18 \,\frac{\text{ps}}{\text{nm}\cdot\text{km}} \times 25 \,\text{km} \times 30 \,\text{nm} \\
&= \; 13500 \,\text{ps} \\
&= \; 13.5 \,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Maximum Bit Rate (Gaussian NRZ assumption):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\max} \;&= \; \frac{0.44}{\Delta\tau_{\text{ch}}} \\
&= \; \frac{0.44}{13.5 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \; 32.6 \,\text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** This is a classic KTU Part-A 3-mark style numerical. Two key marks are awarded for (i) correct identification of all three variables and (ii) proper unit cancellation. Always convert $L$ to km and $\Delta\lambda$ to nm before using the ps/(nm·km) form of $D$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Composition of Chromatic Dispersion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CHROMATIC DISPERSION DT"] --> B["MATERIAL DISPERSION DM"]
    A --> C["WAVEGUIDE DISPERSION DW"]
    B --> B1["Origin: dn by d lambda of bulk silica"]
    B --> B2["Dominant in multimode fibers and SMF below 1310 nm"]
    B --> B3["Negative below lambda_zero and positive above"]
    C --> C1["Origin: wavelength dependent mode confinement"]
    C --> C2["Always positive for step index fiber"]
    C --> C3["Dominant near cutoff wavelength"]
    B1 --> D["Combined effect: pulse broadening"]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E["Total pulse spread dT equals D times L times dlambda"]
    E --> F["Limits bandwidth distance product"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Pulse Broadening Along a Fiber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Input Pulse: Sharp and Narrow t equals T0"] --> Fiber["Optical Fiber of Length L"]
    Fiber --> P2["Output Pulse: Broadened and Stretched t equals T0 plus delta T"]
    P2 --> Q1["Red wavelengths arrive earlier in normal regime"]
    P2 --> Q2["Blue wavelengths arrive earlier in anomalous regime"]
    P2 --> Q3["ISI and eye closure at receiver"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dispersion Compensation Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TX["Optical Transmitter Laser plus Modulator"] --> SMF["SMF Span L1 with D positive at 17 ps per nm km"]
    SMF --> DCF["Dispersion Compensating Fiber with D negative around minus 100 ps per nm km"]
    DCF --> EDFA["Erbium Doped Fiber Amplifier"]
    EDFA --> RX["Optical Receiver with Photodiode"]
    DCFComp["Compensation Condition: D1 times L1 plus D2 times L2 equals 0"] -.-> DCF
    DSPComp["Alternative: Digital Signal Processing DSP based equalizer"] -.-> RX
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for diagrams:** Each block represents a *concept* or *component*. Arrows indicate the direction of information/power flow. The dotted feedback arrows denote the **mathematical constraint** that the dispersion of consecutive elements must sum to zero for net-zero chromatic dispersion on the link.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define *chromatic dispersion* in optical fibers. List its two main components.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Chromatic dispersion is the temporal broadening of an optical pulse as it propagates through a fiber, caused by the wavelength-dependent group velocity of its spectral components. The two main components are:
> 1. **Material dispersion ($D_M$)** — due to the variation of the refractive index $n(\lambda)$ of silica with wavelength.
> 2. **Waveguide dispersion ($D_W$)** — due to the wavelength-dependent distribution of optical power between core and cladding, which changes the effective index $n_{\text{eff}}(\lambda)$.
>
> Total chromatic dispersion: $D_T(\lambda) = D_M(\lambda) + D_W(\lambda)$, expressed in ps/(nm·km).

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition, 1 mark for each component + formula)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

What is the *zero-dispersion wavelength*? Why is it shifted to 1550 nm in Dispersion-Shifted Fibers (DSF)?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The zero-dispersion wavelength $\lambda_0$ is the wavelength at which the total chromatic dispersion coefficient $D_T(\lambda)$ crosses zero, i.e., $D_T(\lambda_0) = 0$. For standard single-mode fiber (SMF-28), $\lambda_0 \approx 1310$ nm because material dispersion is naturally cancelled by waveguide dispersion at this point.
>
> In **Dispersion-Shifted Fibers (DSF)**, the core diameter and refractive-index profile ($\Delta$) are engineered (smaller core, sharper profile) so that waveguide dispersion dominates and pushes $\lambda_0$ to **1550 nm**, coinciding with the minimum attenuation window and the gain band of EDFAs. This minimizes both loss and dispersion simultaneously.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for SMF value, 1 mark for DSF engineering reasoning)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **material dispersion coefficient** $D_M(\lambda)$ starting from the definition of group delay. Explain why the second derivative of refractive index governs the pulse broadening. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-mode fiber link of length 50 km is operated at 1550 nm. The source is a DFB laser with RMS spectral width $\sigma_\lambda = 0.1$ nm. The fiber dispersion coefficient at 1550 nm is $D = 17$ ps/(nm·km). Calculate the (i) RMS pulse broadening, (ii) maximum bit-rate-distance product using the Gaussian criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(a)

**Step 1 — Group Delay per unit length:**

The group delay for unit length of fiber is:

$$
\frac{\tau_g}{L} \;=\; \frac{N_g(\lambda)}{c} \;=\; \frac{1}{c}\!\left[n(\lambda) - \lambda\,\frac{dn}{d\lambda}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. wavelength:**

$$
\frac{1}{L}\,\frac{d\tau_g}{d\lambda} \;=\; \frac{1}{c}\,\frac{dN_g}{d\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the derivative of $N_g$:**

$$
\frac{dN_g}{d\lambda} \;=\; \frac{dn}{d\lambda} \;-\; \frac{dn}{d\lambda} \;-\; \lambda\,\frac{d^{2} n}{d\lambda^{2}} \;=\; -\lambda\,\frac{d^{2} n}{d\lambda^{2}}
$$

(The two first-derivative terms cancel by product-rule expansion.)

**Step 4 — Define material dispersion:**

$$
\boxed{\,D_M(\lambda) \;=\; \frac{-\lambda}{c}\,\frac{d^{2} n}{d\lambda^{2}}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical meaning:**

The second derivative $d^{2}n/d\lambda^{2}$ quantifies the *curvature* of the $n(\lambda)$ curve. If $n(\lambda)$ were perfectly linear, all wavelengths in the pulse would experience a uniform group delay and no broadening would occur. The non-zero curvature means different wavelengths experience *different* group velocities, leading to pulse spreading.

*(7-mark split: 1 mark step 1, 2 marks step 3 with product-rule, 1 mark final formula, 3 marks physical interpretation including curvature argument and diagram reference.)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(b)

**Given:** $L = 50$ km, $\sigma_\lambda = 0.1$ nm, $D = 17$ ps/(nm·km) at 1550 nm.

**(i) RMS pulse broadening:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\tau_{\text{ch}} \;&= \; D \cdot L \cdot \sigma_\lambda \\
&= \; 17 \,\frac{\text{ps}}{\text{nm}\cdot\text{km}} \times 50 \,\text{km} \times 0.1 \,\text{nm} \\
&= \; 85 \,\text{ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Bandwidth–distance product (Gaussian criterion):**

Using the relation $B \cdot L = 0.187 / \Delta\tau_{\text{ch}}$ (for RMS-to-RMS Gaussian pulse-broadening standard):

$$
\begin{aligned}
B \cdot L \;&= \; \frac{0.187}{85 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= \; 2.2 \times 10^{9} \,\text{Hz}\cdot\text{km} \\
&= \; 2.2 \,\text{GHz}\cdot\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the alternative NRZ FWHM criterion $B = 0.44/\Delta\tau$:

$$
B \;=\; \frac{0.44}{85 \times 10^{-12}} \;\approx\; 5.18 \,\text{Gbps}, \qquad B \cdot L \;\approx\; 259 \,\text{Gbps}\cdot\text{km}
$$

*(7-mark split: 1 mark formula, 2 marks numerical substitution, 1 mark final answer with units, 2 marks second part, 1 mark final answer with units. Always show unit cancellations explicitly.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **two contributions to chromatic dispersion** in a single-mode fiber. Discuss how the zero-dispersion wavelength $\lambda_0$ is obtained and how it can be shifted by varying the fiber parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 km optical link uses an SMF-28 fiber with $D = 16$ ps/(nm·km) at the operating wavelength. An LED with $\Delta\lambda = 40$ nm drives the link. Find the (i) total pulse broadening, (ii) bandwidth–length product. If a **Dispersion Compensating Fiber (DCF)** of length 10 km with $D = -80$ ps/(nm·km) is inserted, find the (iii) residual pulse broadening. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(a)

**Two contributions:**

1. **Material Dispersion ($D_M$):** Caused by the natural wavelength dependence of silica's refractive index $n(\lambda)$. It is given by:
$$
D_M(\lambda) = \frac{-\lambda}{c}\,\frac{d^{2} n}{d\lambda^{2}}
$$
For pure silica, $D_M$ is **negative below ~1270 nm** and **positive above ~1270 nm**, with a zero at the *material zero-dispersion wavelength* ($\lambda_{0,M} \approx 1270$ nm for Ge-doped silica).

2. **Waveguide Dispersion ($D_W$):** Caused by the wavelength-dependent fraction of power propagating in the cladding. Longer wavelengths are less tightly confined, lowering $n_{\text{eff}}$. For a step-index SMF, $D_W$ is **always positive** and grows rapidly near the cutoff wavelength $V_c = 2.405$.

**Zero-dispersion wavelength:**

Setting $D_T(\lambda_0) = D_M(\lambda_0) + D_W(\lambda_0) = 0$ gives:

$$
\boxed{\,\lambda_0 : \text{solution of}\;\; D_M(\lambda_0) = -D_W(\lambda_0)\,}
$$

For standard SMF, $\lambda_0 \approx 1310$ nm. By **reducing the core diameter** or **increasing $\Delta$**, $D_W$ increases in magnitude, shifting $\lambda_0$ to longer wavelengths (1550 nm in DSF) or shorter wavelengths (e.g., 1300 nm optimized in some NZ-DSFs).

*(7-mark split: 2 marks each for $D_M$ and $D_W$, 2 marks for $\lambda_0$ derivation, 1 mark for parameter shifting.)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(b)

**Given:** $L_{\text{SMF}} = 100$ km, $D_{\text{SMF}} = 16$ ps/(nm·km), $\Delta\lambda = 40$ nm.

**(i) Pulse broadening in SMF alone:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\tau_{\text{SMF}} \;&= \; D_{\text{SMF}} \cdot L_{\text{SMF}} \cdot \Delta\lambda \\
&= \; 16 \times 100 \times 40 \\
&= \; 64000 \,\text{ps} \;=\; 64 \,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Bandwidth–length product (Gaussian):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B \cdot L \;&= \; \frac{0.187}{64 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \; 2.92 \,\text{MHz}\cdot\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

(The LED's wide spectral width severely limits the link — a key reason LEDs are unsuitable for long-haul links.)

**(iii) Residual broadening after DCF:**

Total link dispersion parameter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{total}} \cdot L_{\text{total}} \;&= \; D_{\text{SMF}} L_{\text{SMF}} + D_{\text{DCF}} L_{\text{DCF}} \\
&= \; 16 \times 100 \;+\; (-80) \times 10 \\
&= \; 1600 \;-\; 800 \;=\; 800 \,\text{ps/nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Residual pulse broadening:

$$
\Delta\tau_{\text{residual}} \;=\; 800 \,\text{ps/nm} \times 40 \,\text{nm} \;=\; 32000 \,\text{ps} \;=\; 32 \,\text{ns}
$$

The DCF cuts the pulse broadening by 50% (from 64 ns to 32 ns), but full compensation would require a 20 km DCF (since $L_{\text{DCF}} = L_{\text{SMF}} \cdot D_{\text{SMF}} / \vert D_{\text{DCF}}\vert = 100 \cdot 16 / 80 = 20$ km).

*(7-mark split: 1+1+1 for parts (i)-(ii), 2 marks for setup of part (iii), 1 mark numerical computation, 1 mark conclusion/comment.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — CHROMATIC DISPERSION QUESTIONS**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently mix units — using $D$ in ps/(nm·km) but $L$ in metres or $\Delta\lambda$ in metres. Always pre-convert: $L \rightarrow$ km, $\Delta\lambda \rightarrow$ nm, $\Delta\tau \rightarrow$ ps.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign of $D_M$ formula (–1 mark):** The expression is $D_M = (-\lambda/c)(d^{2}n/d\lambda^{2})$. Dropping the negative sign flips the physics sign and yields wrong dispersion curve.
> 3. **Confusing $\Delta\tau$ definitions (–1 mark):** Gaussian RMS broadening uses the **0.187** coefficient; NRZ FWHM uses the **0.44** coefficient. Mismatching them gives a factor-of-2 error.
> 4. **DCF sign error (–2 marks):** When computing net dispersion, students often forget that $D_{\text{DCF}}$ is **negative**. Always write the vector sum $D_{\text{SMF}}L_{\text{SMF}} + D_{\text{DCF}}L_{\text{DCF}}$ explicitly.
> 5. **Missing the Sellmeier step (–1 mark in derivations):** If a derivation demands $n(\lambda)$, students must substitute the Sellmeier equation and carry at least the first two terms of the $B_i, C_i$ coefficients, not just state the formula.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Chromatic dispersion = **temporal spreading** of an optical pulse due to wavelength-dependent group velocity.
- Two physical origins: **material dispersion** ($D_M$, from $n(\lambda)$) and **waveguide dispersion** ($D_W$, from $n_{\text{eff}}(\lambda)$).
- Total dispersion: $D_T = D_M + D_W$, measured in **ps/(nm·km)**.
- Master formula: $D_M(\lambda) = (-\lambda/c)(d^{2}n/d\lambda^{2})$.
- Pulse broadening: $\Delta\tau = D \cdot L \cdot \Delta\lambda$.
- Bandwidth limit: $B \cdot L = 0.187 / \Delta\tau$ (Gaussian) or $B = 0.44/\Delta\tau$ (NRZ).
- **Zero-dispersion wavelength** $\lambda_0$: SMF $\approx 1310$ nm, DSF $\approx 1550$ nm, NZ-DSF $\approx 1540$–1565 nm.
- Sellmeier equation gives $n(\lambda)$ for silica; differentiate twice to get $D_M(\lambda)$.
- Compensation methods: **DCF** (negative-D fiber), **chirped fiber Bragg gratings**, and **DSP-based electronic equalization** in coherent systems.
- Standard reference fiber: SMF-28 has $D \le 18$ ps/(nm·km) at 1550 nm.
- Engineering rule of thumb: for high-bit-rate long-haul (>10 Gbps, >80 km), chromatic dispersion compensation is **non-negotiable**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dispersion Modified Fibers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dispersion Modified Fibers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dispersion Modified Fibers (DMFs)** are a specialized class of single-mode optical fibers engineered by deliberately tailoring the **refractive index profile** of the core and cladding to manipulate the wavelength-dependent dispersion characteristics of the propagating optical signal. The primary objective is to shift the zero-dispersion wavelength ($\lambda_0$) away from the conventional **1300 nm** region of standard single-mode fiber (SSMF) to a more useful transmission window, while simultaneously controlling the slope of the dispersion-versus-wavelength curve to support high-bit-rate, long-haul optical communication in the **1550 nm** low-loss window.

The fundamental relationship governing dispersion engineering in these fibers is:

$$D_{total}(\lambda) = D_{material}(\lambda) + D_{waveguide}(\lambda) + D_{polarization}(\lambda)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Dispersion Modified Fibers is a **Module 1, Part-B** topic and carries a typical weightage of **7–14 marks** in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). Students must clearly distinguish between *Dispersion Shifted Fibers (DSF)*, *Non-Zero Dispersion Shifted Fibers (NZ-DSF)*, *Dispersion Flattened Fibers (DFF)*, and *Dispersion Compensating Fibers (DCF)*, since the examiners frequently test this taxonomy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **highway with multiple lanes** where cars of different colors travel at slightly different speeds. By the time they reach the exit (the receiver), the cars have spread out — this is **dispersion**.

Now think of dispersion-modified fibers as a **custom-designed highway with subtle speed bumps and lane dividers** that have been engineered to make cars of *all* colors (i.e., all wavelengths) arrive at nearly the **same time** at the receiver, even though they enter the fiber together.

- A **standard fiber** is like an *unmodified highway* — it works well only at one specific speed (1300 nm).
- A **dispersion modified fiber** is like a *re-engineered highway* that has been repaved and re-aligned so that the *low-loss 1550 nm* window (where the highway is most efficient) is also the *minimum-dispersion* window.

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Zero-dispersion wavelength (SSMF) | $\lambda_0$ | **1310 nm** |
| Operating window (C-band) | $\lambda$ | **1550 nm** |
| Refractive index difference | $\Delta$ | $\sim 0.003$ to $0.012$ |
| Core radius (typical) | $a$ | 4–6 $\mu$m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** At **1550 nm**, silica glass has its **minimum attenuation** ($\sim 0.2$ dB/km), but standard fiber's zero-dispersion point is at **1310 nm**. This mismatch motivated the entire family of dispersion-modified fibers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why and How Dispersion is Modified

In a standard step-index single-mode fiber, two primary dispersion mechanisms act simultaneously:

1. **Material Dispersion ($D_{mat}$):** Caused by the wavelength dependence of the refractive index $n(\lambda)$ of silica. Different spectral components of the pulse travel at different group velocities.
2. **Waveguide Dispersion ($D_{wg}$):** Caused by the wavelength-dependent distribution of optical power between the core and the cladding due to the waveguide geometry.

The **sign** and **magnitude** of waveguide dispersion can be engineered by modifying:
- The **core diameter** ($a$)
- The **index difference** ($\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$)
- The **refractive index profile** (step, triangular, dual-shape, etc.)

By carefully designing these parameters, the total dispersion curve can be **shifted**, **flattened**, or **made negative** over the desired wavelength range.

## 2.2 Classification of Dispersion Modified Fibers

The key families of DMFs (which KTU examiners love to test) are summarized below:

- **Dispersion Shifted Fiber (DSF):** $\lambda_0$ is shifted from 1310 nm to **1550 nm** by adding waveguide dispersion to material dispersion. Achieved via segmented-core or triangular-core index profiles.
- **Non-Zero Dispersion Shifted Fiber (NZ-DSF):** $\lambda_0$ is shifted to a value *just below* 1550 nm (typically 1540–1550 nm), giving a small *positive* residual dispersion. This suppresses **four-wave mixing (FWM)** in WDM systems.
- **Dispersion Flattened Fiber (DFF):** Provides a *low* and nearly *constant* dispersion value over a wide wavelength range (1300–1600 nm) using a *double-clad* (W-profile) structure.
- **Dispersion Compensating Fiber (DCF):** A specialized fiber with a large *negative* dispersion coefficient at 1550 nm, used in conjunction with SSMF to cancel its positive dispersion. Has a small core and high $\Delta$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula repository** for Module 1 numerical problems. Memorize all entries before sitting for the KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Description / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_{total} = D_{mat} + D_{wg}$ | Total first-order dispersion in ps/(nm·km) |
| 2 | $D_{mat} = -\dfrac{\lambda}{c} \dfrac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2}$ | Material dispersion from material index $n(\lambda)$ |
| 3 | $D_{wg} = -\dfrac{n_1 \Delta}{c \lambda} V \dfrac{d^2 (V b)}{dV^2}$ | Waveguide dispersion, $V$ = normalized frequency, $b$ = normalized propagation constant |
| 4 | $V = \dfrac{2 \pi a}{\lambda} NA$ | V-number, governs single-mode condition ($V < 2.405$) |
| 5 | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Numerical aperture |
| 6 | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ | Relative refractive index difference |
| 7 | $\sigma_t = \vert D_{total} \vert \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot L$ | Pulse broadening (rms), $\Delta\lambda$ = spectral width, $L$ = fiber length |
| 8 | $B_{max} \leq \dfrac{1}{4 \sigma_t}$ | Maximum bit-rate limit (NRZ, ISI-free) |
| 9 | $\lambda_0^{DSF} \approx 1550$ nm | Zero-dispersion wavelength of DSF |
| 10 | $\lambda_0^{NZ-DSF} \in [1540, 1550]$ nm | Range for NZ-DSF zero-dispersion |
| 11 | $D_{DCF} \cdot L_{DCF} = -D_{SMF} \cdot L_{SMF}$ | Dispersion compensation design equation |
| 12 | $\dfrac{L_{DCF}}{L_{SMF}} = -\dfrac{D_{SMF}}{D_{DCF}}$ | Length ratio of DCF to SMF for full compensation |

**Standard Reference Metrics for Comparison:**

| Fiber Type | $\lambda_0$ (nm) | Dispersion at 1550 nm (ps/nm·km) | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard SMF (SSMF) | 1310 | +17 | Legacy long-haul, 1310 nm systems |
| DSF | 1550 | 0 (theoretically) | High-bit-rate single-channel 1550 nm |
| NZ-DSF | 1540–1550 | +0.5 to +6 | WDM long-haul, suppresses FWM |
| DFF | 1300–1600 | ~0 to +2 | Wideband multi-window systems |
| DCF | — | **−80 to −100** | Dispersion compensation module (DCM) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dispersion modified fibers are the **silent workhorses** of modern long-haul and submarine optical networks. Without them, dense wavelength-division multiplexed (DWDM) systems operating across the **C-band (1530–1565 nm)** and **L-band (1565–1625 nm)** would suffer catastrophic pulse overlap and inter-symbol interference. NZ-DSF in particular dominates trans-oceanic cable systems because its *small but nonzero* dispersion prevents nonlinear penalties while remaining manageable by digital signal processing (DSP).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Dispersion from Material and Waveguide Components

We begin with the definition of group delay per unit length, $\tau_g$, for a guided mode in a single-mode fiber:

$$\tau_g = \frac{1}{v_g} = \frac{1}{c} \frac{d\beta}{dk_0}$$

where $\beta$ is the propagation constant of the guided mode and $k_0 = 2\pi / \lambda$ is the free-space wavenumber. The first-order dispersion coefficient $D$ (in ps/nm·km) is defined as the rate of change of group delay with respect to wavelength:

$$D = \frac{d\tau_g}{d\lambda}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate the group delay expression with respect to $\lambda$.**

$$D = \frac{d}{d\lambda}\!\left(\frac{1}{c}\frac{d\beta}{dk_0}\right) = \frac{1}{c}\frac{d^2\beta}{d\lambda\, dk_0}$$

**Step 2 — Split the propagation constant into a material component and a waveguide component.**

Using the substitution $\beta = n_{eff} k_0$, where $n_{eff}$ is the effective index of the guided mode, we can write:

$$\beta = n_{eff}(\lambda) \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Express $n_{eff}$ as a combination of core index $n_1(\lambda)$ and a normalized propagation parameter $b(V)$.**

The standard decomposition is:

$$n_{eff}(\lambda) = n_1(\lambda) + (n_2 - n_1)\, b(V) = n_1(\lambda) - n_1 \Delta \, b(V)$$

The first term captures the **material** contribution (since $n_1$ depends on $\lambda$ through silica's Sellmeier equation), and the second term captures the **waveguide** contribution (since $b$ depends on $V$, which itself depends on $\lambda$).

**Step 4 — Differentiate twice and separate the material and waveguide parts.**

$$D = D_{material} + D_{waveguide}$$

with

$$D_{material} = -\frac{\lambda}{c}\frac{d^2 n_1}{d\lambda^2}$$

and

$$D_{waveguide} = -\frac{n_1 \Delta}{c \lambda} \, V \, \frac{d^2 (V b)}{dV^2}$$

This is the **master equation** for dispersion engineering. The term $\dfrac{d^2(Vb)}{dV^2}$ is a dimensionless function of $V$ determined by the waveguide cross-section profile (step, triangular, etc.).

**Step 5 — Interpret the engineering handle.**

Because $D_{material}$ is fixed by the silica material (and is *positive* above 1310 nm), we can only achieve a *total* dispersion of zero at 1550 nm if $D_{waveguide}$ is **negative** with a magnitude equal to $D_{material}$ at 1550 nm. This is accomplished by:

- Increasing $\Delta$ (raising $n_1 - n_2$)
- Decreasing the core radius $a$ (lower $V$ at 1550 nm)
- Using a non-step index profile (triangular, segmented, etc.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The "**negative waveguide dispersion**" mechanism is the single most important conceptual point. If a student cannot explain *why* DSF works, they will lose 3–4 marks in a 14-mark question.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pulse Broadening and Maximum Bit Rate

**Step 1 — Define rms pulse broadening due to dispersion.**

An optical pulse with spectral width $\Delta\lambda$ propagating through a length $L$ of fiber experiences rms temporal broadening of:

$$\sigma_t = \vert D_{total} \vert \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist intersymbol-interference-free criterion.**

To avoid ISI in a non-return-to-zero (NRZ) system, the bit period $T_b$ must satisfy:

$$T_b \geq 4 \sigma_t \quad \Longrightarrow \quad B_{max} = \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{1}{4 \sigma_t}$$

Substituting $\sigma_t$:

$$B_{max} = \frac{1}{4 \, \vert D_{total} \vert \, \Delta\lambda \, L} \quad \text{[bits per second]}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style).**

> **Problem:** A single-mode fiber link uses a DSF at 1550 nm with $D_{total} = 0.5$ ps/(nm·km) (small residual from practical manufacturing). The source is a DFB laser with $\Delta\lambda = 0.1$ nm. The link length is $L = 100$ km. Calculate (a) the rms pulse broadening, and (b) the maximum bit rate.

**Solution:**

$$\sigma_t = 0.5 \times 0.1 \times 100 = 5 \text{ ps}$$

$$B_{max} = \frac{1}{4 \times 5 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{20 \times 10^{-12}} = 5 \times 10^{10} = 50 \text{ Gbps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check:** Compare this with a standard SMF at 1550 nm where $D \approx +17$ ps/(nm·km). The DSF result is **34× better** in pulse broadening for the same link. This is precisely why DSF (and its cousin NZ-DSF) revolutionized 1550 nm transmission.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Family Tree of Dispersion Modified Fibers

The following Mermaid block diagrams the engineering taxonomy of dispersion-modified fibers, their design drivers, and their applications. This is a KTU-favorite visual summary.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Standard Single Mode Fiber SSMF] --> B[Dispersion Engineering Motivation]
    B --> C{Design Goal}

    C -->|Shift lambda0 to 1550 nm| D[Dispersion Shifted Fiber DSF]
    C -->|Shift lambda0 with small residual| E[Non-Zero DSF NZ-DSF]
    C -->|Flatten dispersion over wide band| F[Dispersion Flattened Fiber DFF]
    C -->|Cancel positive SMF dispersion| G[Dispersion Compensating Fiber DCF]

    D --> D1[Segmented core or triangular profile]
    D --> D2[High Delta, smaller core]
    D --> D3[Application: High bit rate single channel 1550 nm]

    E --> E1[Lambda0 near 1540 to 1550 nm]
    E --> E2[Suppresses four wave mixing in WDM]
    E --> E3[Application: Long haul DWDM submarine cables]

    F --> F1[Double clad W profile]
    F --> F2[Low uniform D over 1300 to 1600 nm]
    F --> F3[Application: Wideband multi window systems]

    G --> G1[Very large negative D at 1550 nm]
    G --> G2[Small core, high Delta]
    G --> G3[Application: DCM module with SSMF]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Dispersion Compensation Link

This schematic shows the canonical placement of a DCF module within a transmission span. It is exactly the kind of block diagram a KTU 14-mark answer should contain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[Optical Transmitter 1550 nm DFB] --> SMF1[SSMF Span L1 positive D]
    SMF1 --> AMP1[EDFA In Line Amplifier]
    AMP1 --> DCF[Dispersion Compensating Fiber negative D]
    DCF --> SMF2[SSMF Span L2 positive D]
    SMF2 --> AMP2[EDFA]
    AMP2 --> DCF2[DCF Module]
    DCF2 --> RX[Optical Receiver with DSP]

    DCFN[Net Dispersion Condition] --> CHECK[D SMF times L SMF plus D DCF times L DCF approx 0]
```

**Engineering interpretation of the block diagram:**

- The SSMF spans introduce **positive chromatic dispersion** that broadens pulses.
- The EDFA amplifies the signal to compensate for fiber attenuation.
- The DCF introduces **negative dispersion** of nearly equal magnitude, undoing the SSMF broadening.
- At the receiver, the residual dispersion is small enough for the DSP equalizer to handle.

## 4.3 Refractive Index Profile Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STEP[Step Index SSMF and DSF]
        S1[Core n1 constant] --- S2[Sharp drop to cladding n2]
    end

    subgraph TRI[Triangular NZ DSF]
        T1[Peak n1 at center] --- T2[Linear decrease to n2]
    end

    subgraph DCLAD[Double Clad W Profile DFF]
        W1[Inner core n1] --- W2[Inner cladding n3 less than n2] --- W3[Outer cladding n2]
    end

    subgraph DCFP[DCF Profile]
        P1[Small core high n1] --- P2[Sharp drop to depressed cladding n2 low]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The **double-clad (W-profile)** of DFF is what gives it a flattened dispersion curve. The depressed inner cladding introduces a *second* zero-dispersion point, allowing the curve to remain near zero over a broad band — a favorite conceptual question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`
**Define a Dispersion Shifted Fiber (DSF). Why is it preferred over standard single-mode fiber for 1550 nm transmission?**

**Model Answer:**
A Dispersion Shifted Fiber is a single-mode optical fiber in which the zero-dispersion wavelength ($\lambda_0$) is shifted from the standard 1310 nm to **1550 nm** by modifying the refractive index profile (typically a triangular or segmented core) so that the negative waveguide dispersion cancels the positive material dispersion at 1550 nm.

It is preferred because:
1. The **1550 nm window** has the *lowest attenuation* ($\sim 0.2$ dB/km) in silica.
2. Standard SMF has $\lambda_0 = 1310$ nm, so at 1550 nm it exhibits a large positive dispersion ($\sim +17$ ps/nm·km), causing severe pulse broadening.
3. DSF provides **near-zero dispersion at 1550 nm**, allowing higher bit rates and longer repeater spans.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`
**What is a Non-Zero Dispersion Shifted Fiber (NZ-DSF)? How does it overcome the four-wave mixing (FWM) limitation of conventional DSF in WDM systems?**

**Model Answer:**
An NZ-DSF is a dispersion-modified fiber in which $\lambda_0$ is shifted to a value *just below* 1550 nm (typically 1540–1550 nm), giving a small but **nonzero positive dispersion** (0.5 to 6 ps/nm·km) at the operating wavelength.

**Overcoming FWM:** In a conventional DSF, the dispersion is exactly zero at 1550 nm, which causes **phase matching** between WDM channels, leading to severe **four-wave mixing** and crosstalk. In NZ-DSF, the small residual dispersion breaks this phase-matching condition, suppressing FWM while still providing much lower total dispersion than SSMF. Hence NZ-DSF is the preferred fiber for **WDM/DWDM long-haul systems**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the different types of dispersion-modified fibers. Discuss their refractive index profiles and the engineering reason for each modification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 km long single-mode fiber link operating at 1550 nm has total dispersion $D = +2$ ps/(nm·km). The source spectral width is $\Delta\lambda = 0.2$ nm. Calculate the rms pulse broadening and the maximum bit rate for an NRZ system. If a DCF with $D_{DCF} = -80$ ps/(nm·km) is used to fully compensate the SMF dispersion, what length of DCF is required? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Types of Dispersion Modified Fibers (7 marks)

| Fiber | Refractive Index Profile | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **SSMF (reference)** | Step-index with $\Delta \approx 0.003$ | Material dispersion dominates, $\lambda_0 = 1310$ nm |
| **DSF** | Triangular or segmented core, $\Delta \approx 0.008$–$0.012$ | Negative waveguide dispersion cancels $D_{mat}$ at 1550 nm |
| **NZ-DSF** | Similar to DSF with optimized profile | $\lambda_0$ slightly below 1550 nm; residual D suppresses FWM |
| **DFF** | Double-clad (W-profile) with depressed inner cladding | Two zero-dispersion points flatten the curve over 1300–1600 nm |
| **DCF** | Small core, high $\Delta$, depressed cladding | Very large *negative* dispersion at 1550 nm to compensate SSMF |

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 mermaid diagram above.)*

**Key engineering reason summary:**
- DSF and NZ-DSF: **shift** the zero-dispersion wavelength to coincide with minimum-loss window.
- DFF: **flatten** the dispersion curve across a wide wavelength band.
- DCF: **invert the sign** of dispersion for compensation purposes.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $L_{SMF} = 100$ km
- $D_{SMF} = +2$ ps/(nm·km) at 1550 nm
- $\Delta\lambda = 0.2$ nm
- $D_{DCF} = -80$ ps/(nm·km)
- NRZ modulation

**Step 1 — Calculate rms pulse broadening in the SMF.**

$$\sigma_t = \vert D_{SMF} \vert \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot L_{SMF} = 2 \times 0.2 \times 100 = 40 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the maximum bit rate.**

$$B_{max} = \frac{1}{4 \sigma_t} = \frac{1}{4 \times 40 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{160 \times 10^{-12}} = 6.25 \times 10^{9}$$

$$\boxed{B_{max} = 6.25 \text{ Gbps}}$$

**Step 3 — DCF length for full compensation.**

Using the compensation condition:

$$D_{SMF} \cdot L_{SMF} + D_{DCF} \cdot L_{DCF} = 0$$

$$L_{DCF} = -\frac{D_{SMF} \cdot L_{SMF}}{D_{DCF}} = -\frac{(+2)(100)}{-80} = \frac{200}{80}$$

$$\boxed{L_{DCF} = 2.5 \text{ km}}$$

**Conclusion:** A 2.5 km DCF module will fully compensate the dispersion of 100 km of SMF, allowing operation at much higher bit rates than the uncompensated 6.25 Gbps limit.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for total chromatic dispersion in a single-mode fiber as the sum of material and waveguide dispersion. Explain clearly how the waveguide dispersion can be made negative by profile engineering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare DSF, NZ-DSF, DFF, and DCF in a tabular form across at least six engineering parameters. Justify why NZ-DSF is preferred over DSF in modern WDM systems. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

*(Refer to Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation. The final result is:)*

$$D_{total} = -\frac{\lambda}{c}\frac{d^2 n_1}{d\lambda^2} - \frac{n_1 \Delta}{c \lambda} V \frac{d^2(Vb)}{dV^2}$$

**Making waveguide dispersion negative:**

The term $\dfrac{d^2(Vb)}{dV^2}$ is a function of the index profile. For a standard step-index fiber, it is positive but small. By changing the profile from step to **triangular** (linearly graded), the field penetration into the cladding increases with wavelength more rapidly, making $\dfrac{d^2(Vb)}{dV^2}$ *negative*. The waveguide dispersion then becomes **positive** with sign reversal; combined with the fact that material dispersion is *positive* above 1310 nm, the *total* dispersion can be made to cross zero at 1550 nm.

By further reducing the core diameter (lowering $V$) and increasing $\Delta$, the magnitude of negative waveguide dispersion grows, pushing $\lambda_0$ from 1310 nm to 1550 nm — yielding a **Dispersion Shifted Fiber**.

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | DSF | NZ-DSF | DFF | DCF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zero-dispersion wavelength | 1550 nm | 1540–1550 nm | None (flat) | N/A |
| Dispersion at 1550 nm | $\approx 0$ | 0.5 to 6 ps/nm·km | 0 to 2 ps/nm·km | −80 to −100 ps/nm·km |
| Index profile | Triangular/segmented | Optimized triangular | Double-clad (W) | Small core, high $\Delta$ |
| $\Delta$ value | High | High | Two-step | Very high |
| FWM suppression | Poor (zero D) | **Good** (small D) | Moderate | N/A |
| Primary application | Single-channel high-bit-rate | DWDM long-haul | Wideband multi-window | SMF compensation modules |
| Core radius | $\sim 4$ $\mu$m | $\sim 4$–$5$ $\mu$m | Variable | $\sim 1.5$–$2.5$ $\mu$m |
| Manufacturing complexity | Moderate | Moderate | High | High |

**Justification for NZ-DSF in WDM systems:**

In WDM/DWDM systems, multiple channels at closely spaced wavelengths are launched simultaneously. When total dispersion is exactly zero (as in DSF), the phase-matching condition is satisfied for **four-wave mixing**, generating intermodulation products that fall directly on other WDM channels, causing **severe crosstalk**. NZ-DSF introduces a *small but nonzero* positive dispersion, which decorrelates the channels' phases as they propagate, **suppressing FWM efficiency by orders of magnitude**. The residual dispersion is small enough (0.5–6 ps/nm·km) that it can be managed using DCF spans or DSP-based electronic dispersion compensation. Hence NZ-DSF is the **industry standard** for modern long-haul DWDM networks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign confusion in dispersion equations:** Students frequently forget that $D_{material}$ is *positive* above 1310 nm, and the engineering goal is to make $D_{waveguide}$ *negative* so that their sum equals zero at 1550 nm. Always state the sign explicitly.
> 2. **Units mismatch:** $D$ is in **ps/(nm·km)**, $\lambda$ in **nm**, $L$ in **km**, and $\sigma_t$ in **ps**. Mixing $\mu$m with nm or km with m leads to catastrophic unit errors.
> 3. **DCF sign:** $D_{DCF}$ is *negative*. Forgetting the minus sign in the compensation equation will yield a *negative* length, which is mathematically and physically meaningless.
> 4. **DSF vs NZ-DSF confusion:** DSF has *exactly zero* dispersion at 1550 nm; NZ-DSF has *small nonzero* dispersion. They are not interchangeable — DSF is unsuitable for WDM due to FWM.
> 5. **Pulse broadening formula:** Some textbooks use full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) while the formula given above gives the *rms* width. The factor of 4 in the bit-rate limit assumes an NRZ Gaussian approximation — be explicit about this assumption in your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dispersion Modified Fibers** are single-mode fibers with engineered index profiles to control the total dispersion-vs-wavelength curve.
- The **total dispersion** is the sum of **material dispersion** (always positive above 1310 nm for silica) and **waveguide dispersion** (sign-controllable by profile design).
- **DSF (Dispersion Shifted Fiber):** $\lambda_0$ moved to 1550 nm; uses triangular/segmented core. Vulnerable to FWM in WDM.
- **NZ-DSF (Non-Zero DSF):** $\lambda_0$ near 1540–1550 nm; small residual positive dispersion suppresses FWM. Industry standard for DWDM.
- **DFF (Dispersion Flattened Fiber):** W-profile (double-clad); nearly zero dispersion from 1300 to 1600 nm; complex to manufacture.
- **DCF (Dispersion Compensating Fiber):** Large negative dispersion at 1550 nm ($-80$ to $-100$ ps/nm·km); used in DCM modules to compensate SSMF.
- **Pulse broadening equation:** $\sigma_t = \vert D \vert \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot L$ — committed to memory.
- **Maximum bit rate (NRZ):** $B_{max} = 1/(4\sigma_t)$ — equally critical.
- **DCF length design:** $L_{DCF} = -D_{SMF} L_{SMF} / D_{DCF}$ — must include the **negative sign**.
- **Why 1550 nm?** Lowest attenuation window in silica ($\sim 0.2$ dB/km); EDFA gain band also lies in this region (C-band).
- **Engineering handles for dispersion control:** core radius $a$, index difference $\Delta$, and index profile shape (step, triangular, W-profile).
- **Modern trend:** Coherent detection with DSP has reduced dependence on dispersion-modified fibers for terrestrial systems, but NZ-DSF still dominates **submarine cable** installations.
- **KTU-ready one-liner:** *"Dispersion modification is achieved by designing the index profile so that the negative waveguide dispersion cancels the positive material dispersion at the desired operating wavelength."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photonic Crystal Fibers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Photonic Crystal Fibers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Photonic Crystal Fibers (PCF) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Photonic Crystal Fiber (PCF)** — also known as a **Microstructured Optical Fiber (MOF)** or **Holey Fiber (HF)** — is a class of optical waveguide in which the cladding is constructed from a **two-dimensionally periodic array of microscopic air holes** running longitudinally through the entire length of a silica (SiO₂) background material, while the core is formed by either a deliberate **defect (missing air hole)** in the lattice or a central hollow channel.

The light-confinement mechanism in a PCF is fundamentally different from that of a conventional step-index optical fiber:

- **Solid-core PCF (Index-Guiding / TIR-type):** Light is guided by **Modified Total Internal Reflection (M-TIR)** between a higher-index solid silica core and a lower effective-index microstructured cladding.
- **Hollow-core PCF (Photonic Bandgap / PBG-type):** Light is confined within a low-index (air) core by the **Photonic Bandgap (PBG) effect**, where the periodic cladding forbids the propagation of certain optical frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Photonic Crystal Fibers are explicitly listed in Module 1 of the PEECT636 Optical Communication course. Students must know (a) the structural geometry, (b) the two guiding mechanisms, (c) the V-parameter (effective), and (d) the unique advantages of PCFs over conventional SMF.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Made of Bamboo Poles"

Imagine a long, hollow bamboo pole (the **core** of the fiber). Now imagine wrapping this pole with a forest of parallel drinking straws (the **air holes in the cladding**) arranged in a tight hexagonal pattern. Light is shone down the central bamboo pole. Because the surrounding region is now a *mesh* of silica and air — which collectively behaves as a medium with a much lower effective optical density than pure silica — light is trapped inside the central region by reflection.

If, instead of a solid bamboo core, we **remove the central pole entirely**, we get a hollow air-core. Now, the only way to guide light through air (a lower index than the surrounding silica) is to use a *bandgap* — analogous to how an **electronic bandgap in a semiconductor forbids electrons of certain energies from propagating**. The periodic lattice of air holes creates forbidden frequency bands, and only those optical frequencies lying inside the bandgap are forced to remain trapped inside the hollow air core.

## 1.3 Key Structural Parameters

- **Pitch (Λ):** The center-to-center distance between two adjacent air holes. Units: **micrometers (μm)**. Typical values: **1 μm – 10 μm**.
- **Hole Diameter (d):** The diameter of each air hole. Units: **μm**.
- **Relative Hole Size (d/Λ):** A dimensionless design ratio. Typical range: **0.1 – 0.9**.
- **Number of Rings (N):** The number of concentric hexagonal rings of air holes surrounding the core. Typical: **3 – 10** for low confinement loss.

## 1.4 Standard Callout Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway — Two Guiding Mechanisms**
>
> | Type | Core Material | Guiding Mechanism | Index Condition |
> |------|---------------|-------------------|-----------------|
> | Solid-core PCF | Solid Silica | Modified TIR | n_core > n_eff_clad |
> | Hollow-core PCF | Air | Photonic Bandgap | Forbidden propagation in cladding at operating λ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of PCFs — Logic Breakdown

### Step 1: Identify the Core Type
- **Solid Core** → M-TIR (Modified Total Internal Reflection) regime.
- **Hollow Core** → PBG (Photonic Bandgap) regime.

### Step 2: Identify the Cladding Topology
- **Triangular (Hexagonal) Lattice** → Most common; used for endless single-mode behavior.
- **Square Lattice** → Less common; useful for high birefringence designs.

### Step 3: Identify the Operating Regime of a Solid-Core PCF
- **Endlessly Single-Mode (ESM) Regime:** When $\frac{d}{\Lambda} < 0.43$ (approximately), the fiber supports only the fundamental mode at *all* wavelengths. This is one of the most celebrated properties of PCF.
- **Multimode Regime:** When $\frac{d}{\Lambda} > 0.43$, the PCF becomes multimode at shorter wavelengths.

> **Why "Endlessly" Single-Mode?** In a conventional step-index fiber, the V-number has a wavelength-dependence that crosses the single-mode cutoff at a specific wavelength. In a PCF, the effective index contrast $\Delta n_{eff}$ *automatically* adjusts with wavelength in such a way that $V_{eff} \approx \pi$ remains nearly constant — preventing the fiber from ever entering the multimode regime.

## 2.2 Effective V-Parameter for a PCF

The standard single-mode cutoff condition in step-index fibers is $V < 2.405$. For PCFs, an analogous **Effective V-parameter** is defined as:

$$V_{eff} = \frac{2\pi\Lambda}{\lambda} \sqrt{n_{core}^2 - n_{FSM}^2}$$

Where:
- $\Lambda$ = Pitch of the PCF lattice (in meters)
- $\lambda$ = Operating wavelength (in meters)
- $n_{core}$ = Refractive index of the core (silica $\approx 1.45$)
- $n_{FSM}$ = Effective index of the **Fundamental Space-Filling Mode (FSM)** of the infinite cladding

The **FSM** is the lowest-order mode that can propagate in the *infinite* photonic crystal cladding. It is the foundational quantity that replaces the cladding index $n_2$ of a conventional fiber.

The single-mode condition is:

$$V_{eff} < \pi \quad (\text{single-mode operation})$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Effective V-parameter | $V_{eff} = \frac{2\pi\Lambda}{\lambda}\sqrt{n_{core}^2 - n_{FSM}^2}$ | Determines single-mode/multimode operation |
| 2 | Single-mode condition | $V_{eff} < \pi$ | Design criterion for ESM PCF |
| 3 | Endlessly single-mode threshold | $\frac{d}{\Lambda} < 0.43$ | Geometric design rule for ESM |
| 4 | Pitch (geometric) | $\Lambda$ (μm) | Lattice constant |
| 5 | Air-filling fraction | $f = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}}\left(\frac{d}{\Lambda}\right)^2$ | Quantifies the fraction of cladding area that is air |
| 6 | Group velocity dispersion (GVD) | $D = -\frac{\lambda}{c}\frac{d^2 n_{eff}}{d\lambda^2}$ | Controls pulse broadening in optical links |
| 7 | Effective modal area | $A_{eff} = \frac{\left[\iint \vert E(x,y)\vert^2 \, dx\, dy\right]^2}{\iint \vert E(x,y)\vert^4 \, dx\, dy}$ | Governs nonlinear coefficient $\gamma$ |
| 8 | Nonlinear coefficient | $\gamma = \frac{2\pi n_2}{\lambda A_{eff}}$ | Critical for supercontinuum, soliton applications |
| 9 | Confinement loss | $\alpha_{CL} \propto \exp\left(-\eta N\right)$ | Drops exponentially with number of rings N |
| 10 | Birefringence | $B = \vert n_x - n_y \vert$ | Crucial for polarization-maintaining PCF |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL Markdown Rule:** All absolute value and magnitude operators inside the table above are written using `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` character to prevent the markdown table parser from breaking.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PCFs are not merely laboratory curiosities — they have revolutionized several engineering fields:

1. **Telecommunications:** Dispersion-engineered PCFs for **supercontinuum generation**, enabling dense WDM (DWDM) and optical coherence tomography (OCT) sources.
2. **Fiber Lasers & Amplifiers:** **Large-mode-area (LMA) PCFs** for high-power fiber lasers (kW-class output) by enlarging $A_{eff}$ to suppress nonlinear damage.
3. **Sensing:** **Hollow-core PCFs** for gas sensing (e.g., methane, CO₂ detection) because the light-matter interaction occurs inside the air core.
4. **Quantum Optics:** Single-photon sources and quantum key distribution (QKD) systems using **birefringent PCFs**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective V-Parameter from the Scalar Wave Equation

### Step 1: Start with the Helmholtz Equation in 2D

In a homogeneous, isotropic, source-free medium, the scalar field $\psi(x,y)$ satisfies:

$$\nabla_t^2 \psi + k_0^2 n^2(x,y) \psi = \beta^2 \psi$$

Where:
- $k_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ = Free-space wavenumber
- $n(x,y)$ = Position-dependent refractive index profile of the PCF cross-section
- $\beta$ = Propagation constant of the mode

### Step 2: Define the Effective Index of the Core and Cladding

For the solid silica core, the refractive index is the bulk silica value $n_{SiO_2}(\lambda)$ given by the **Sellmeier equation**. The cladding effective index $n_{FSM}$ is computed by solving the Helmholtz equation for the *infinite* photonic crystal cladding (i.e., assuming the core is absent).

### Step 3: Apply the Weakly-Guiding Approximation

Under the weakly-guiding approximation (valid for $d/\Lambda < 0.43$), the scalar wave equation in the core and cladding can be separated, yielding Bessel function solutions in the core and modified Bessel function solutions in the cladding (for the fundamental $HE_{11}$ mode). Applying the boundary conditions at the core-cladding interface leads to the characteristic equation:

$$\frac{J_1(u)}{u J_0(u)} = \frac{K_1(w)}{w K_0(w)}$$

Where:
- $u = \Lambda \sqrt{k_0^2 n_{core}^2 - \beta^2}$
- $w = \Lambda \sqrt{\beta^2 - k_0^2 n_{FSM}^2}$

### Step 4: Identify the Effective V-Parameter

By analogy with the standard step-index fiber, define:

$$V_{eff}^2 = u^2 + w^2 = \left(\frac{2\pi\Lambda}{\lambda}\right)^2 \left(n_{core}^2 - n_{FSM}^2\right)$$

Taking the square root of both sides:

$$V_{eff} = \frac{2\pi\Lambda}{\lambda} \sqrt{n_{core}^2 - n_{FSM}^2}$$

This is the **fundamental design equation** for any PCF analysis at the KTU university examination level.

### Step 5: Single-Mode Cutoff

The second-order mode ($HE_{21}$) reaches its cutoff when $V_{eff} = \pi$. Therefore:

$$V_{eff} < \pi \implies \text{Single-Mode Operation}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A solid-core photonic crystal fiber is designed with pitch $\Lambda = 6 \, \mu m$ and air-hole diameter $d = 3.2 \, \mu m$. The core index is $n_{core} = 1.45$ and the fundamental space-filling mode index of the cladding is $n_{FSM} = 1.42$ at the operating wavelength $\lambda = 1550 \, nm$. Determine whether the PCF operates in the single-mode regime.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute the relative hole size:

$$\frac{d}{\Lambda} = \frac{3.2}{6.0} = 0.5333$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Endlessly Single-Mode (ESM) geometric criterion:

$$\frac{d}{\Lambda} = 0.5333 > 0.43$$

Since the ratio exceeds **0.43**, the PCF is **NOT** in the endlessly single-mode regime. At sufficiently short wavelengths, it will support higher-order modes.

**Step 3:** Compute the Effective V-Parameter at $\lambda = 1550 \, nm$:

$$V_{eff} = \frac{2\pi (6 \times 10^{-6})}{1550 \times 10^{-9}} \sqrt{(1.45)^2 - (1.42)^2}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the index difference:

$$(1.45)^2 = 2.1025 \quad ; \quad (1.42)^2 = 2.0164$$

$$(1.45)^2 - (1.42)^2 = 2.1025 - 2.0164 = 0.0861$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{0.0861} = 0.2934$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into the V-parameter expression:

$$V_{eff} = \frac{2\pi (6 \times 10^{-6})}{1.55 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.2934$$

$$V_{eff} = \frac{37.699 \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{1.55 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.2934$$

$$V_{eff} = \frac{226.195 \times 10^{-6}}{1.55 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.2934$$

$$V_{eff} = 145.93 \times 0.2934 = 42.82$$

**Step 7:** Compare with the single-mode cutoff:

$$V_{eff} = 42.82 \gg \pi = 3.1416$$

**Conclusion:** The PCF is operating in the **MULTIMODE regime** at 1550 nm. To make it single-mode at this wavelength, we would need to either (a) reduce the relative hole size below 0.43, or (b) increase the operating wavelength.

## 3.3 Air-Filling Fraction Derivation

The air-filling fraction of a hexagonal lattice of circular air holes is:

$$f = \frac{\text{Area of one air hole}}{\text{Area of hexagonal unit cell}}$$

The hexagonal unit cell has an area of:

$$A_{cell} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Lambda^2$$

The area of a single air hole is:

$$A_{hole} = \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

Therefore:

$$f = \frac{\pi d^2 / 4}{\sqrt{3} \Lambda^2 / 2} = \frac{\pi d^2}{2\sqrt{3} \Lambda^2} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}} \left(\frac{d}{\Lambda}\right)^2$$

Numerically:

$$f \approx 0.9069 \left(\frac{d}{\Lambda}\right)^2$$

For the worked example above:

$$f = 0.9069 \times (0.5333)^2 = 0.9069 \times 0.2844 = 0.2579 \approx 25.79\%$$

This means **roughly 25.8% of the cladding cross-section is air**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Conventional SMF

| Property | Conventional SMF (ITU G.652) | Solid-Core PCF | Hollow-Core PCF |
|----------|------------------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Core material | Ge-doped SiO₂ | Pure SiO₂ | Air |
| Cladding | Pure SiO₂ | SiO₂ + air-hole lattice | SiO₂ + air-hole lattice |
| Guiding mechanism | TIR | Modified TIR | Photonic Bandgap |
| Single-mode cutoff | $\lambda_{cutoff} \approx 1260$ nm | Endlessly single-mode if $d/\Lambda < 0.43$ | No classical cutoff |
| Mode-field diameter | ~9.2 μm at 1550 nm | Tunable (1–30 μm) | ~5–10 μm |
| Attenuation | ~0.2 dB/km | ~0.5 dB/km (typical) | ~1–2 dB/km (best published) |
| Dispersion control | Limited | Engineered via geometry | Low (anomalous) |
| Nonlinearity | Low | Tunable (very high or very low) | Extremely low |
| Birefringence | $10^{-7}$ | Up to $10^{-3}$ | Up to $10^{-3}$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PCF Cross-Sectional Architecture

    A[Optical Input Pulse Lambda] --> B[Coupling Lens / SMF Splice]
    B --> C[Photonic Crystal Fiber]
    C --> D{Light Guiding Mechanism}

    D -->|Hollow Core + Photonic Bandgap| F[Hollow-Core PCF]
    E --> G[Cladding: Triangular Lattice of Air Holes]

    K --> M[Output: Endlessly Single-Mode or LMA Operation]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q:** Define a Photonic Crystal Fiber (PCF). Mention its two main classifications based on the guiding mechanism.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Photonic Crystal Fiber (PCF) is an optical waveguide in which the cladding consists of a periodic arrangement of microscopic air holes running along the length of a silica fiber, and the core is formed by a defect in this periodic lattice.

The two main classifications are:

1. **Solid-Core PCF** — The core is made of solid silica, and light is guided by **Modified Total Internal Reflection (M-TIR)** because the effective cladding index is lower than the core index.

2. **Hollow-Core PCF** — The core is an air channel, and light is guided by the **Photonic Bandgap (PBG) effect**, in which the periodic cladding forbids propagation of certain optical frequencies.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for solid-core, 1 mark for hollow-core mechanism)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q:** What is meant by the "endlessly single-mode" property of a PCF? State the geometric condition for it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **endlessly single-mode (ESM)** property means that a solid-core PCF supports only the fundamental mode at *all* wavelengths and not just above a particular cutoff wavelength — unlike a conventional step-index fiber.

This property arises because the effective V-parameter $V_{eff}$ of a PCF remains nearly constant (close to $\pi$) as the wavelength changes, preventing the fiber from entering the multimode regime.

The geometric condition is:

$$\frac{d}{\Lambda} < 0.43$$

Where $d$ is the air-hole diameter and $\Lambda$ is the pitch of the triangular lattice.

*(1 mark for ESM meaning, 1 mark for V-parameter explanation, 1 mark for geometric condition)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the structure of a solid-core Photonic Crystal Fiber. Discuss its light-guiding mechanism and the role of the fundamental space-filling mode (FSM). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A solid-core PCF consists of a central solid silica core surrounded by a cladding made of a triangular (hexagonal) lattice of air holes running along the entire fiber length. The core is created by *omitting* the central air hole, creating a defect in the periodic lattice.

**Light-Guiding Mechanism — Modified TIR:**

The cladding is not a homogeneous medium but a periodic arrangement of silica and air.
