(1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Module Convention:** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]** **(a)** [7 marks] Starting from the Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (NLSE), derive the expression for the **peak power of a fundamental soliton**. Clearly define the soliton order N and state the condition for a fundamental soliton. **(b)** [7 marks] A single-mode optical fiber has the following parameters: $\beta_2 = -20$ ps²/km, $\gamma = 1.5$ W⁻¹·km⁻¹, and the input pulse has $T_0 = 4$ ps. Calculate: - (i) the required peak power for a fundamental soliton - (ii) the dispersion length - (iii) the nonlinear length - (iv) the soliton period. #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Write the NLSE in physical units (1 mark):** $$i\frac{\partial A}{\partial z} - \frac{\beta_2}{2}\frac{\partial^2 A}{\partial T^2} + \gamma \vert A \vert^2 A = 0$$ **Step 2 — Normalize using $\xi = z / L_D$ and $\tau = T / T_0$, with $A = \sqrt{P_0}\, a$ (1 mark):** $$i\frac{\partial a}{\partial \xi} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2 a}{\partial \tau^2} + N^2 \vert a \vert^2 a = 0$$ **Step 3 — Identify the soliton order (1 mark):** $$N^2 = \frac{\gamma P_0 T_0^2}{\vert \beta_2 \vert} = \frac{L_D}{L_{NL}}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the fundamental soliton condition N = 1 (1 mark):** $$\frac{\gamma P_0 T_0^2}{\vert \beta_2 \vert} = 1$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $P_0$ (1 mark):** $$P_0 = \frac{\vert \beta_2 \vert}{\gamma T_0^2}$$ **Step 6 — State the solution form $a(\xi, \tau) = \text{sech}(\tau) \exp(i\xi/2)$ (1 mark):** This satisfies the NLSE for N = 1, confirming the hyperbolic secant envelope of the fundamental soliton. **Step 7 — Conclude (1 mark):** The minimum peak power required to maintain a soliton of width $T_0$ is $P_0 = \vert \beta_2 \vert / (\gamma T_0^2)$. --- **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** Given: $\beta_2 = -20$ ps²/km, $\gamma = 1.5$ W⁻¹·km⁻¹, $T_0 = 4$ ps. **(i) Peak power (2 marks):** $$P_0 = \frac{\vert \beta_2 \vert}{\gamma T_0^2} = \frac{20}{1.5 \times 16} = \frac{20}{24} = 0.833 \text{ W} \approx 833 \text{ mW}$$ **(ii) Dispersion length (1.5 marks):** $$L_D = \frac{T_0^2}{\vert \beta_2 \vert} = \frac{16}{20} = 0.8 \text{ km}$$ **(iii) Nonlinear length (1.5 marks):** $$L_{NL} = \frac{1}{\gamma P_0} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 0.833} = \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.8 \text{ km}$$ *(Verification: $L_D = L_{NL} = 0.8$ km, hence N = 1 ✓)* **(iv) Soliton period (2 marks):** $$z_0 = \frac{\pi T_0^2}{2 \vert \beta_2 \vert} = \frac{\pi \times 16}{2 \times 20} = \frac{16\pi}{40} = 0.4\pi \approx 1.257 \text{ km}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]** **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the physical mechanism of soliton formation. Why must the fiber operate in the **anomalous dispersion regime** for solitons to exist? Describe the role of SPM and GVD in detail with appropriate equations. **(b)** [7 marks] Discuss the **types of solitons** (fundamental, higher-order, dark solitons) with their mathematical forms and physical significance. Mention at least two practical challenges in soliton-based communication systems. #### Model Solution **(a) Physical Mechanism (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Kerr Nonlinearity (1 mark):** The refractive index of silica depends on optical intensity via $n(I) = n_0 + n_2 I$. A high-intensity pulse therefore changes the medium's index *as it propagates*, inducing an intensity-dependent phase. **Step 2 — Self-Phase Modulation (1 mark):** The self-induced phase is $\phi_{NL}(t) = \gamma P(t) z_{eff}$. Differentiating with respect to time gives a positive chirp — the leading edge is red-shifted, the trailing edge is blue-shifted. **Step 3 — GVD in anomalous regime (1 mark):** When $\beta_2 < 0$ (λ > 1.3 μm in silica), higher frequencies travel *slower* than lower frequencies. The chirp produced by SPM is *exactly* the chirp that GVD compresses. **Step 4 — Balance condition (1 mark):** The pulse width and power must satisfy $N^2 = \gamma P_0 T_0^2 / \vert \beta_2 \vert = 1$, i.e., $L_D = L_{NL}$. **Step 5 — Why anomalous? (1.5 marks):** In normal dispersion ($\beta_2 > 0$), GVD further broadens a positively chirped pulse — both effects work in the *same* direction. No cancellation. Solitons require the *opposing* chirp of anomalous GVD. **Step 6 — Steady state (1.5 marks):** Once balanced, the pulse is a *stationary solution* of the NLSE — it propagates unchanged because its energy, momentum, and Hamiltonian are conserved by the integrable equation. --- **(b) Types of Solitons (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Fundamental Soliton (1.5 marks):** $N = 1$ solution: $$a(\xi, \tau) = \text{sech}(\tau) \exp(i\xi/2)$$ Hyperbolic secant envelope, propagates unperturbed in ideal fiber. **Step 2 — Higher-Order Solitons (1.5 marks):** $N = 2, 3, \ldots$ solutions. The pulse undergoes *periodic* contraction and broadening with period $z_0 = \pi T_0^2 / (2\vert \beta_2 \vert)$. Demonstrates the rich structure of the NLSE. **Step 3 — Dark Solitons (1.5 marks):** Intensity *dips* on a continuous-wave background; exist in the *normal* dispersion regime ($\beta_2 > 0$) with N = 1. More robust to losses and perturbations. $a(\xi, \tau) = B \tanh(B\tau) \exp(iB^2\xi)$. **Step 4 — Practical challenges (2.5 marks):** - (i) **Fiber losses** continuously weaken SPM, causing the soliton to broaden. Requires periodic amplification (Raman/EDFA) every $L_A < 8z_0$ to maintain the average N = 1. - (ii) **Gordon-Haus jitter** — Amplified spontaneous emission (ASE) noise from in-line amplifiers causes random shifts in the soliton's central frequency, leading to timing jitter at the receiver. Limits achievable bit rate. - (iii) (Optional) **Soliton interaction** — Closely spaced solitons attract or repel, causing timing jitter. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zones:** > 1. **Sign convention errors** — Students often write $\beta_2 = +10$ when the value should be negative. Always remember: anomalous dispersion means $\beta_2 < 0$. > 2. **Confusing N and N²** — When the problem states "N = 2", the soliton order is 2, and the corresponding power satisfies $N^2 = 4$. Use $P_0 = N^2 \vert \beta_2 \vert / (\gamma T_0^2)$ for general N. > 3. **Unit inconsistency** — $\beta_2$ is in ps²/km, $T_0$ in ps, $\gamma$ in W⁻¹·km⁻¹. Mixing ps and ns leads to wrong powers by factors of $10^6$. > 4. **Forgetting the "lossless" assumption** — Standard derivations assume lossless fibers. Real systems need periodic amplification; the *average* soliton technique compensates. > 5. **Omitting the "anomalous regime" condition** — Examiners explicitly deduct marks if you do not justify *why* $\beta_2 < 0$ is required. > 6. **Confusing soliton period with bit period** — $z_0$ is a *spatial* period, not a *temporal* bit interval. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solitons — Key Things to Remember (High-Density Summary):** - ✅ A soliton is a **shape-preserving optical pulse** formed by the **balance between GVD (anomalous) and SPM (Kerr nonlinearity)**. - ✅ The governing equation is the **Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (NLSE)** — integrable, has conserved quantities. - ✅ The fundamental soliton condition is **N = 1**, where $N^2 = L_D / L_{NL} = \gamma P_0 T_0^2 / \vert \beta_2 \vert$. - ✅ Peak power formula: $\boxed{P_0 = \dfrac{\vert \beta_2 \vert}{\gamma T_0^2}}$ (W, with $\beta_2$ in ps²/km, $T_0$ in ps). - ✅ Soliton period: $\boxed{z_0 = \dfrac{\pi T_0^2}{2\vert \beta_2 \vert}}$ km. - ✅ Envelope shape: **hyperbolic secant (sech)** — $A(t) = \sqrt{P_0}\, \text{sech}(t/T_0)$. - ✅ **Anomalous dispersion ($\beta_2 < 0$) is mandatory** — wavelength typically > 1.3 μm in silica fibers. - ✅ **Dark solitons** exist in normal dispersion as intensity dips on a CW background. - ✅ **Higher-order solitons (N > 1)** exhibit periodic evolution with period $z_0$ but are not shape-invariant in the long run. - ✅ **Real-fiber challenges:** Loss, Gordon-Haus jitter, soliton-soliton interaction. Addressed via lumped/distributed amplification, optical filtering, and timing-jitter control. - ✅ **Engineering application:** Long-haul, ultra-high-bit-rate (>100 Gbps) optical communication; femtosecond fiber lasers; supercontinuum sources. - ✅ Physical constant to remember: **n₂ ≈ 2.6 × 10⁻²⁰ m²/W** (silica nonlinear refractive index). - ✅ Conversion: $T_{FWHM} = 1.763\, T_0$ (for a sech pulse). - ✅ Soliton energy: $E_s = 2 P_0 T_0$. - ✅ Always check: $L_D = L_{NL}$ to confirm N = 1.

topics.title: Optical Fibers and Cables - Fabrication Techniques - Double Crucible Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optical Fibers and Cables - Fabrication Techniques - Double Crucible Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Double Crucible Method** is a continuous, melt-based optical fiber fabrication technique in which two concentric crucibles (typically made of platinum or platinum-rhodium alloy) hold molten core glass and cladding glass separately. The molten streams coalesce at a shared bottom orifice and are drawn downward as a continuous composite fiber, with the higher-refractive-index core glass naturally enveloped by the lower-refractive-index cladding glass.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** This technique is classified under *fabrication of multicomponent glass optical fibers* and is specifically highlighted in Module 2 for its role in producing **graded-index (GRIN) and step-index multimode fibers** in continuous lengths. It is an important contrast to vapor-phase deposition methods like MCVD, OVD, and VAD.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lava-Lamp Sandwich" Intuition

Imagine a giant kitchen ladle with a smaller ladle nested inside it, both filled with molten candy syrup. The outer pot holds **clear syrup** (cladding), and the inner cup holds **coloured syrup** (core). When you tip the entire assembly, the coloured syrup flows out first through a small hole at the bottom of the inner cup, and as it falls, it is wrapped by the clear syrup flowing around it from the outer pot. A baker catches this combined stream with a stick and pulls it downward — the molten stream stretches into a thin, two-layered candy strand (the fiber), which cools and solidifies into a glass rod with a coloured core inside a clear sheath.

**The Crucial Insight:** The Double Crucible Method works exactly like this nested ladle system, except the "candy" is molten doped-silicate glass at temperatures near **1000 °C – 1200 °C**, the "stick" is a precision fiber-drawing mechanism, and the "kitchen" is a clean-room fabrication tower. The core glass is automatically wrapped in cladding glass purely by geometry — no chemical vapor deposition is involved.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the canonical engineering parameters used in Double Crucible fabrication and are routinely tested in KTU examinations:
>
> - **Drawing Temperature:** **1000 °C to 1300 °C** (depending on glass composition)
> - **Glass Viscosity at Draw Point:** Approximately **10⁴ to 10⁵ poise** (≈ **10³ to 10⁴ Pa·s**)
> - **Typical Drawing Speed:** **1 m/s to 30 m/s** for multimode fibers
> - **Core Diameter Range Achievable:** **50 µm to 200 µm** (multimode)
> - **Cladding Diameter Standard:** **125 µm ± 2 µm**
> - **Refractive Index Difference (Δn):** **0.01 to 0.04** (multimode designs)
> - **Crucible Material:** **Platinum (Pt)** or **Platinum-Rhodium (Pt-Rh 90/10)** alloy — chosen because of resistance to molten glass attack and minimal contamination.
> - **Atmospheric Control:** Typically inert gas (N₂ or Ar) or controlled reactive atmosphere (e.g., O₂ for dehydration).

### 1.4 Position in the KTU 2024 Module 2 Framework

Within Module 2, the Double Crucible Method is studied alongside the **Modified Chemical Vapor Deposition (MCVD)**, **Outside Vapor Deposition (OVD)**, and **Vapor-phase Axial Deposition (VAD)** processes. While MCVD/OVD/VAD dominate for high-purity silica-based single-mode telecom fibers, the Double Crucible Method occupies a unique niche for **multicomponent (soft) glasses** and **continuous, low-cost multimode fiber production**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Double Crucible Method operates on three coupled physical principles:

**(a) Controlled Viscosity Flow from Concentric Reservoirs**
- The inner crucible is filled with **core glass** (e.g., soda-lime-silicate with high refractive index dopants such as BaO, PbO, or B₂O₃).
- The annular space between inner and outer crucibles is filled with **cladding glass** (same base composition but with lower-index dopants such as B₂O₃ or SiO₂-rich content).
- Both are melted at temperatures chosen so that the viscosity at the nozzle orifice is in the **drawing range (≈ 10⁴ poise)**.

**(b) Coalescence and Composite Jet Formation**
- A common nozzle at the bottom of the outer crucible lets both glasses flow together.
- Because the core melt emerges from the inner nozzle *first* and is geometrically centered, it is automatically engulfed by the cladding melt from the annular gap.
- **Surface tension effects** keep the core concentric within the cladding during the brief free-fall before drawing.

**(c) Fiber Drawing and Diameter Control**
- The composite molten stream is gripped by a rotating drum (the drawing capstan) and pulled downward at a controlled linear velocity *V_d*.
- A diameter-monitoring optical sensor (often a laser micrometer) feeds back to the capstan speed controller.
- For a constant volumetric feed rate *Q*, the drawn fiber diameter *D* is governed by the **mass conservation law:**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q}{\pi \rho V_d}}$$

where $\rho$ is the density of the glass composite.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Double"?** The name arises because *two* crucibles are used — one nested inside the other — as opposed to the single-crucible "rod-in-tube" method. The dual-reservoir geometry allows independent adjustment of core and cladding compositions.

### 2.2 Why and How Each Step Works

| Step | Physical "Why" | Engineering "How" |
|------|---------------|-------------------|
| Pre-melt of core glass in inner crucible | Need a clean, bubble-free melt at uniform viscosity | Resistance heating with embedded Pt-Rh windings; held for 4–8 hours at ≈ 1200 °C |
| Pre-melt of cladding glass in annular space | Outer glass must be chemically compatible but index-matched differently | Same furnace zone but separately fed; sometimes a vertical baffle separates the melts until nozzle |
| Composite flow through nozzle | Geometry-driven concentric flow without vapor-phase chemistry | Precision-machined concentric Pt nozzle; orifice diameter 5–20 mm |
| Drawing under tension | Stretches viscous jet into a fiber; aligns molecules partially | Capstan with helical winding; tension ≈ 5–50 g for multimode |
| In-line coating application | Protects pristine glass surface from moisture and abrasion | Polymeric coating applicator immediately after solidification point; UV-cured acrylate |
| Diameter control loop | Maintains telecom-grade dimensional uniformity (±1 µm) | Laser micrometer → PID controller → capstan motor |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas form the *core calculation set* the KTU examiner expects you to know for problems on Double Crucible fabrication. Each is presented in clean LaTeX with units.

**Formula 1 — Mass Conservation for Drawn Fiber Diameter**

$$D_{fiber} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi \rho \, V_d}}$$

- $\dot{m}$: mass flow rate of glass (kg/s)
- $\rho$: glass density (kg/m³), typically $\rho \approx 2200 \text{ to } 2500 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for silicate glasses
- $V_d$: drawing speed (m/s)
- $D_{fiber}$: outer (cladding) diameter (m)

**Formula 2 — Core-to-Cladding Diameter Ratio (Geometric Control)**

$$\frac{d_{core}}{D_{clad}} = \sqrt{\frac{A_{core,nozzle}}{A_{clad,nozzle}}}$$

- Determined by the inner and outer nozzle cross-sectional areas.
- Independent of drawing speed (ratio is preserved by continuity).

**Formula 3 — Relative Refractive Index Difference**

$$\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} \approx \frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{2 n_1^2}$$

- $n_1$: core refractive index
- $n_2$: cladding refractive index
- For multimode fiber, $\Delta$ is typically **0.01 to 0.04** (i.e., **1 % to 4 %**).

**Formula 4 — Numerical Aperture (NA) — the Critical Performance Metric**

$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1 \sqrt{2 \Delta}$$

- Determines the light-gathering ability and is set by the *composition difference* between core and cladding glasses.

**Formula 5 — V-Number (Normalized Frequency)**

$$V = \frac{\pi d_{core}}{\lambda} \cdot NA$$

- For Double Crucible multimode fibers, $V \gg 1$ (heavily multimoded).

**Formula 6 — Maximum Acceptable Drawing Tension (to avoid fracture)**

$$T_{max} \approx \frac{\sigma_{tensile} \cdot \pi D_{fiber}^2}{4}$$

- $\sigma_{tensile}$ for silica-based glass: **≈ 3 to 5 GPa** (theoretical), but practical sustained draw tension is **< 1 %** of this.

**Formula 7 — Fiber Drawing Stress from Viscous Flow**

$$\sigma_{draw} = 3 \eta \frac{V_d}{L_{cooling}}$$

- $\eta$: viscosity at draw point (Pa·s)
- $L_{cooling}$: length over which the fiber cools and solidifies (m)
- This is the *origin* of the drawing tension.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Piping-Safe Table Below:** All absolute-value bars use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

**Compact Reference Table**

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n_1$ | Core refractive index | 1.50 – 1.62 | — |
| $n_2$ | Cladding refractive index | 1.48 – 1.58 | — |
| $\Delta$ | Relative index difference | 0.01 – 0.04 | — |
| $NA$ | Numerical aperture | 0.20 – 0.50 | — |
| $d_{core}$ | Core diameter | 50 – 200 | µm |
| $D_{clad}$ | Cladding diameter | 125 ± 2 | µm |
| $V_d$ | Drawing speed | 1 – 30 | m/s |
| $T$ | Drawing temperature | 1000 – 1300 | °C |
| $\eta$ | Viscosity at draw | $10^3$ – $10^4$ | Pa·s |
| $\rho$ | Glass density | 2200 – 2500 | kg/m³ |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of This Method

| Application Domain | Role of Double Crucible Fiber |
|---|---|
| **Local Area Networks (LANs)** | Low-cost multimode graded-index fibers for short-reach Ethernet (historically) |
| **Medical and Industrial Sensors** | Fibers with high NA for endoscopic light delivery and laser power transmission |
| **Decorative and Specialty Fibers** | Side-emitting, multicolor, and large-core fibers for illumination |
| **Research and Development** | Rapid prototyping of novel glass compositions (the melt-based chemistry is more flexible than CVD) |
| **Aerospace and Defense** | Radiation-hard multicomponent glass fibers where silica-CVD economics are unfavorable |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Fiber Diameter from Mass Conservation

**Problem Setup:** Glass melt flows out of the Double Crucible nozzle with a total volumetric rate $Q$ (m³/s). The composite stream (core + cladding) is drawn at velocity $V_d$. Derive an expression for the final fiber diameter.

**Step 1 — State the Mass Continuity Principle**

For an incompressible Newtonian melt, the volume entering the system per unit time equals the volume leaving per unit time.

$$Q = A_{nozzle} \cdot V_{exit} = A_{fiber} \cdot V_d$$

where $V_{exit}$ is the velocity of the molten jet at the nozzle exit.

**Step 2 — Express Fiber Cross-Section in Terms of Diameter**

$$A_{fiber} = \frac{\pi D_{fiber}^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Continuity Equation**

$$Q = \frac{\pi D_{fiber}^2}{4} \cdot V_d$$

**Step 4 — Solve Algebraically for $D_{fiber}$**

$$D_{fiber}^2 = \frac{4 Q}{\pi V_d}$$

$$D_{fiber} = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q}{\pi V_d}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Mass Flow Rate Using Density**

Since $Q = \dot{m} / \rho$, we obtain the alternative form:

$$D_{fiber} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi \rho V_d}}$$

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style):**

Given: A Double Crucible delivers a composite glass melt at $\dot{m} = 0.005$ kg/s. The glass density is $\rho = 2300$ kg/m³. Drawing speed is $V_d = 5$ m/s. Find the fiber diameter.

**Step 1 — Compute volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.005}{2300} = 2.174 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$D_{fiber} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 2.174 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times 5}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$D_{fiber} = \sqrt{5.535 \times 10^{-7}} = 7.44 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 744 \text{ µm}$$

This is *larger* than the standard 125 µm telecom cladding — typical for specialty large-core fibers. A drawing speed increase to $V_d = 30$ m/s would reduce this to:

$$D_{fiber} = \sqrt{5.535 \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{5}{30}} \approx 304 \text{ µm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This inverse-square-root relationship between drawing speed and fiber diameter is **high-yield** for KTU numerical problems. A *doubling* of $V_d$ reduces $D_{fiber}$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Numerical Aperture from Dopant Concentration

**Problem Setup:** A Double Crucible fabrication uses a soda-lime-silicate base glass. The core glass contains an additional **8 mol% BaO** as a refractive-index-raising dopant. The cladding glass contains **6 mol% B₂O₃** as a refractive-index-lowering dopant. Given the *additive refractive index coefficients* below, derive the NA.

| Dopant | $\Delta n_i$ per mol% | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| BaO | +0.0015 | Raises index |
| B₂O₃ | −0.0009 | Lowers index |
| Na₂O | +0.0007 | Raises index (residual) |

**Step 1 — Compute Core Index Contribution Above Base**

Assume the base soda-lime-silicate index (no dopant) is $n_0 = 1.510$.

$$\Delta n_1 = (8)(0.0015) = 0.012$$

$$n_1 = n_0 + \Delta n_1 = 1.510 + 0.012 = 1.522$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cladding Index Shift**

$$\Delta n_2 = (6)(-0.0009) = -0.0054$$

$$n_2 = n_0 + \Delta n_2 = 1.510 - 0.0054 = 1.5046$$

**Step 3 — Compute Numerical Aperture**

$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

$$n_1^2 = (1.522)^2 = 2.3165$$

$$n_2^2 = (1.5046)^2 = 2.2638$$

$$NA = \sqrt{2.3165 - 2.2638} = \sqrt{0.0527} = 0.2296$$

**Step 4 — Compute Relative Index Difference**

$$\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.522 - 1.5046}{1.522} = \frac{0.0174}{1.522} = 0.01143 \approx 1.14\%$$

**Step 5 — Cross-Check Using Paraxial Approximation**

$$NA_{approx} = n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta} = 1.522 \times \sqrt{0.02286} = 1.522 \times 0.1512 = 0.2301$$

The two values agree to within **0.2 %**, confirming the approximation is valid for small $\Delta$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Disadvantages (Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | Double Crucible Method |
|---|---|
| **Continuous Production** | ✅ Yes — kilometers of fiber in a single run |
| **Raw Material Cost** | ✅ Lower than ultra-high-purity SiCl₄ used in CVD |
| **Attenuation Achievable** | ⚠ Moderate (typically **3–10 dB/km** vs. <0.2 dB/km for MCVD silica) |
| **Material Purity** | ⚠ Limited by crucible-melt chemistry (metallic impurities, OH⁻ contamination) |
| **Refractive Index Profile Control** | ⚠ Limited — predominantly step-index, GRIN via ion-exchange post-processing |
| **Single-Mode Fiber Production** | ❌ Not practical (core diameters < 10 µm are extremely difficult) |
| **Capital Equipment Cost** | ✅ Moderate (no gas-handling vapor-phase reactors) |
| **Glass Variety** | ✅ Wide — multicomponent silicates, heavy-metal oxides, halides |
| **OH⁻ Contamination Risk** | ❌ High unless atmosphere is tightly controlled (must use dry N₂ / Cl₂ drying) |
| **Numerical Aperture Range** | ✅ Wide (0.2 – 0.5+) by doping choice |

### 3.4 Comparison with Other Module-2 Methods (Synthesis)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Module 2 examination often asks for a comparative answer (typically 7–14 marks). The following matrix is your **ready-made exam answer framework**.

| Feature | Double Crucible | MCVD | OVD | VAD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phase of deposition | **Melt** (liquid) | Vapor (inside tube) | Vapor (outside soot) | Vapor (axial soot) |
| Typical minimum loss | 3–10 dB/km | < 0.2 dB/km | < 0.2 dB/km | < 0.2 dB/km |
| Best suited for | Multimode, specialty | Single-mode, telecom | Single-mode, telecom | Single-mode, telecom |
| Preform geometry | None — drawn directly | Hollow tube → collapsed preform | Soot boule → sintered preform | Soot rod → sintered preform |
| Index profile | Step (GRIN via ion exchange) | Any (graded by layer composition) | Any (graded by deposition rate) | Any (graded by deposition rate) |
| Purity control | Limited by crucible | Excellent (vapor purification) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Production rate | Very high | Moderate | High | High |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
*State the principle of the Double Crucible Method of optical fiber fabrication. Mention the materials used for the crucibles and explain why.*

**Model Answer:**

The Double Crucible Method fabricates optical fiber by melting core glass (higher refractive index) in an inner platinum crucible and cladding glass (lower refractive index) in an annular space between this inner crucible and a concentric outer crucible. The two molten streams coalesce at a common concentric nozzle at the bottom and are drawn as a continuous composite fiber in which the core glass is automatically encapsulated by the cladding glass.

Platinum (or **90 % Pt / 10 % Rh alloy**) is used because:
- It resists chemical attack by molten silicate glass.
- Its melting point (**≈ 1768 °C**) far exceeds the glass working temperature (**≈ 1200 °C**).
- It introduces negligible metallic contamination into the melt.
- It is mechanically stable and can be precision-machined into a concentric double-nozzle geometry.

*(3 marks: 1 mark principle, 2 marks crucible material justification)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*List any four advantages and two limitations of the Double Crucible Method.*

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Continuous production of long fiber lengths in a single run.
2. Lower capital and raw material cost compared to vapor-deposition methods (no SiCl₄ gas handling).
3. Wide range of glass compositions can be used (multicomponent silicates, heavy-metal oxides).
4. Large numerical aperture (NA) achievable (0.2 – 0.5) by appropriate dopant selection.

**Limitations:**
1. Higher attenuation (**3 – 10 dB/km**) compared to MCVD silica fibers (**< 0.2 dB/km**) due to impurity and OH⁻ uptake from the melt.
2. Not suitable for low-loss single-mode fiber production because of limited dimensional control and purity.

*(3 marks: 2 marks advantages, 1 mark limitation — keep concise in bullets)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** For each Part B question, the paper setter provides an internal choice (OR). Both options below carry **14 marks** with **7 marks per sub-part**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Design and Numerical Analysis Focus*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A Double Crucible fabrication line draws a graded-index multimode fiber with the following parameters: composite mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 4.2 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s, glass density $\rho = 2400$ kg/m³, drawing speed $V_d = 12$ m/s.
- (i) Calculate the resulting outer (cladding) fiber diameter.
- (ii) If the drawing speed is doubled to 24 m/s while all other conditions are unchanged, what is the new cladding diameter?
- (iii) Comment on the practical implication of your answer for the production line operator.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, describe the Double Crucible Method of optical fiber fabrication. Compare its refractive-index control capability with the MCVD technique.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i)** Using mass conservation:

$$D_{fiber} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi \rho V_d}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 2400 \times 12}}$$

$$D_{fiber} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0168}{90477.87}} = \sqrt{1.857 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.31 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 431 \text{ µm}$$

**(ii)** Doubling the speed:

$$D_{fiber}^{new} = D_{fiber}^{old} \times \sqrt{\frac{V_d^{old}}{V_d^{new}}} = 431 \times \sqrt{\frac{12}{24}} = 431 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$D_{fiber}^{new} = \frac{431}{1.414} = 304.8 \text{ µm}$$

**(iii) Practical implication:** The inverse square-root relationship means a *modest* speed increase drastically reduces fiber diameter. To obtain a standard **125 µm cladding**, the operator would need to increase drawing speed by a factor of $(431/125)^2 \approx 11.9$, i.e., to **≈ 143 m/s** — which exceeds the practical safe drawing speed for this glass composition and may cause fiber fracture. Hence the operator must increase melt temperature (lower viscosity) and/or adjust the nozzle flow to achieve telecom dimensions.

**(b)** Diagram: Use the cross-section schematic from **Section 4.2** of these notes. Description should cover:
- Loading of core and cladding glasses in concentric Pt crucibles.
- Resistance heating to ≈ 1200 °C.
- Coalesced flow through concentric nozzle.
- Drawing by capstan with diameter feedback.
- In-line polymer coating and spooling.

**Comparison of Index Control:**

| Aspect | Double Crucible | MCVD |
|---|---|---|
| Index change mechanism | Bulk dopant addition to the melt | Layer-by-layer vapor-phase chemistry |
| Profile shape | Mostly step-index (or GRIN by post-ion-exchange) | Any shape — step, graded, segmented |
| Dopant precision | Limited by melt homogeneity | Excellent (sub-ppm compositional control) |
| NA range | 0.2 – 0.5 (wide) | 0.10 – 0.30 (telecom-typical) |
| Number of glass compositions per fiber | Two (core, cladding) | Many (hundreds of deposited layers) |

*(7 marks: 4 marks diagram and description, 3 marks comparison)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Materials, Chemistry and Trade-off Focus*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of the following dopants in Double Crucible optical fiber fabrication: (i) BaO, (ii) B₂O₃, (iii) Na₂O. State whether each raises or lowers the refractive index of the silicate base glass, and explain how the choice of these dopants influences the Numerical Aperture.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silica-based Double Crucible fiber has $n_1 = 1.532$ and $n_2 = 1.500$. Calculate (i) the relative refractive index difference $\Delta$, (ii) the numerical aperture $NA$, and (iii) the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_{max}$ in air. Comment on why this fiber is suitable for short-distance multimode applications only.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Dopant roles in silicate glasses:

| Dopant | Index Effect | Mechanism | Role in Double Crucible |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BaO** | Raises index ($+\Delta n$) | Ba²⁺ is a large polarizable cation; increases molar refractivity | Used in **core glass** to achieve $n_1$ |
| **B₂O₃** | Lowers index ($-\Delta n$) | B³⁺ forms BO₃ triangular units with lower polarizability; also lowers melting point | Used in **cladding glass** to achieve $n_2 < n_1$ |
| **Na₂O** | Raises index (slightly) ($+\Delta n$) | Na⁺ is a network modifier that increases polarizability; also lowers viscosity | Used as a flux to reduce working temperature |

The Numerical Aperture depends on the **difference** in dopant-induced index shifts:

$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} \quad \text{where} \quad n_1 = n_0 + \sum_i C_{i,core} \Delta n_i, \quad n_2 = n_0 + \sum_j C_{j,clad} \Delta n_j$$

Higher BaO in core and/or higher B₂O₃ in cladding → larger NA → greater light-gathering ability.

**(b) (i) Relative refractive index difference:**

$$\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.532 - 1.500}{1.532} = \frac{0.032}{1.532} = 0.0209 \approx 2.09\%$$

**(ii) Numerical aperture:**

$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.532)^2 - (1.500)^2}$$

$$NA = \sqrt{2.3470 - 2.2500} = \sqrt{0.0970} = 0.3114$$

**(iii) Maximum acceptance angle (Snell's law at fiber input):**

$$\theta_{max} = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.3114) = 18.15^\circ$$

**Comment on suitability:** A high NA of **0.31** and $\Delta \approx 2\%$ indicate a **strongly multimode** fiber. Such fibers suffer from **modal dispersion** (intermodal pulse spreading) which scales approximately as:

$$\Delta \tau \approx \frac{n_1 \, L \, \Delta}{c}$$

For $L = 1$ km, $\Delta \tau \approx (1.532)(1000)(0.0209)/(3 \times 10^8) \approx 107$ ns/km. This severe pulse spreading limits the **bandwidth-distance product** to the order of **10–30 MHz·km**, restricting use to **short-distance LANs, sensors, and illumination** rather than long-haul telecom.

*(7 marks: 4 marks dopants and NA explanation, 3 marks numerical computation and modal-dispersion comment)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Confusing Double Crucible with Rod-in-Tube:** Many students wrongly describe Double Crucible as "drawing a rod inside a tube." The correct description is **two concentric molten reservoirs with a shared concentric nozzle** — there is *no* rod and *no* tube.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the crucible material:** Always mention **platinum or platinum-rhodium** in your answers. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark if you say only "metal crucible."
>
> 3. **Mixing up the index roles:** BaO and PbO → **core** (higher index); B₂O₃ → **cladding** (lower index). Reversing these is a frequent error.
>
> 4. **Skipping the diameter feedback loop:** Full-mark answers must mention the **laser micrometer → PID controller → capstan motor** feedback path. Omitting this loses 1–2 marks in long-answer questions.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always carry the **µm** or **m** units to the final line. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark for unit-less answers.
>
> 6. **Claiming Double Crucible makes single-mode fiber:** It does *not* — the dimensional control is insufficient for the **8–10 µm cores** required by single-mode fiber at 1310/1550 nm.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read this the night before the exam.**

- **Definition (1-liner):** Two concentric Pt crucibles hold core and cladding melts; composite fiber is drawn continuously through a shared concentric nozzle.
- **Crucible Material:** **Platinum (Pt)** or **Pt-Rh (90/10)** — chosen for chemical inertness, high melting point, and mechanical stability.
- **Drawing Temperature:** **1000 – 1300 °C**, with viscosity at nozzle ≈ **10⁴ poise**.
- **Core glass dopants (raise $n$):** BaO, PbO, Na₂O — placed in the *inner* crucible.
- **Cladding glass dopants (lower $n$):** B₂O₃, SiO₂-rich composition — placed in the *annular* space.
- **Atmospheric control:** Inert N₂ or Ar (sometimes with Cl₂ for OH⁻ removal) is mandatory — molten glass readily absorbs water.
- **Attenuation:** Typically **3 – 10 dB/km** — much higher than MCVD fibers (< 0.2 dB/km). Use only for short-reach / specialty.
- **Numerical Aperture:** Wide range **0.2 – 0.5** by doping choice.
- **Core Diameter:** Achievable **50 – 200 µm** multimode; *not* practical for single-mode (< 10 µm).
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $D_{fiber} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi \rho V_d}}$
  - $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2) / n_1$
  - $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} \approx n_1 \sqrt{2 \Delta}$
  - $V = \dfrac{\pi d_{core}}{\lambda} \cdot NA$
- **Continuous process advantage:** No preform stage — kilometers of fiber in one run, unlike MCVD/OVD/VAD which require separate preform and drawing towers.
- **Comparison anchors:** vs. MCVD → melt vs. vapor; high NA, low purity; large core, high attenuation.
- **Feedback loop to draw:** Laser micrometer → PID → capstan speed — required for ±1 µm cladding uniformity.
- **Surface tension is your friend:** It keeps the core concentric during the brief free-fall before drawing tension takes over.
- **Exam buzzwords that earn marks:** "concentric crucibles," "Pt-Rh alloy," "core-clad coalescence," "inert atmosphere," "ion-exchange GRIN post-processing," "continuous drawing."
- **Applications to list in answers:** LANs, sensors, illumination, medical endoscopes, decorative fibers, prototyping novel glass compositions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outside Vapour Phase Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Outside Vapour Phase Oxidation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Outside Vapour Phase Oxidation (OVPO)** is an advanced chemical vapour deposition technique used in the fabrication of high-purity silica-based optical fibre preforms. In this process, the halide precursor vapours (such as **SiCl₄**, **GeCl₄**, **BCl₃**, **POCl₃**) are transported through the inside of a rotating silica substrate tube, while the **oxidation reaction and soot formation occur in the gas phase *outside* the tube** through a ring-shaped burner traversing along the tube length.

The silica soot particles generated by the homogeneous gas-phase reaction in the flame are driven toward the rotating tube wall by **thermophoretic force** and deposited uniformly on the outer surface. The porous soot layer is subsequently **sintered (vitrified)** into a transparent glassy layer, and the process is repeated for multiple cladding/core layers. Finally, the tube is collapsed into a solid preform from which optical fibres are drawn.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from MCVD**: In MCVD, the oxidation reaction occurs **inside** the substrate tube. In OVPO, the reaction occurs **outside** in a ring burner. This is the most frequently asked 3-mark distinction question in KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Origin**: OVPO was developed by **NTT (Nippon Telegraph and Telephone), Japan** in the early 1980s as a hybrid technique combining the large-preform capability of OVD with the high-purity advantages of MCVD.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **blowing soap bubbles** with a rotating wand, but instead of bubbles forming at the wand's mouth, you have a ring of fire (ring burner) surrounding the wand. As you blow air through the wand carrying invisible "sugar particles" (SiCl₄ vapour), the ring fire ignites them in mid-air *outside* the wand, converting them into fine white ash (silica soot). This white ash then sticks uniformly to the outer rotating wand surface, layer by layer — just like frost forming on a cold pipe in winter, except here the soot is hot and sticks due to a temperature gradient.

- The **rotating tube** = your wand spinning to spread ash evenly.
- The **ring burner** = the ring of fire surrounding it.
- The **SiCl₄ + O₂ chemistry** = the invisible-to-ash transformation.
- The **traversing motion** = moving the wand left/right to coat it end-to-end.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard Process Parameters for OVPO:
> - **Substrate tube rotation speed**: 30 – 100 rpm (typically ~60 rpm).
> - **Ring burner traversing speed**: 100 – 300 mm/min.
> - **Reaction (flame) temperature**: **1500 °C – 1700 °C** at the burner zone.
> - **Sintering temperature**: ~1700 °C – 1800 °C (above glass transition).
> - **Substrate tube**: high-purity fused silica ( Heraeus or GE-type ), typically 25–40 mm OD × 20–35 mm ID.
> - **Carrier gas**: high-purity O₂ (99.9999 %).
> - **Deposition rate**: **2 – 6 g/min** (significantly higher than MCVD).
> - **Preform size achievable**: up to **300 mm diameter** (much larger than MCVD's ~60 mm limit).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The OVPO process executes through the following structured sequence:

1. **Substrate Preparation**
   - A high-purity fused silica tube is mounted on a glass-working lathe.
   - The tube is connected to a gas delivery system at one end and an exhaust/rotation drive at the other.

2. **Precursor Vapour Delivery**
   - Liquid halides (SiCl₄, GeCl₄, BCl₃, POCl₃) are kept in temperature-controlled bubblers.
   - Carrier gas (O₂) is bubbled through these liquids, picking up vapour-phase precursor molecules.
   - The vapour mixture flows through the **inside** of the rotating substrate tube.

3. **External Oxidation in Ring Burner**
   - As the precursor vapour exits the tube, it encounters a **ring-shaped H₂–O₂ burner** surrounding the tube externally.
   - The high-temperature flame (1500–1700 °C) initiates the homogeneous gas-phase reaction:
     - SiCl₄(g) + O₂(g) → SiO₂(s, soot) + 2Cl₂(g)
   - Ultra-fine amorphous SiO₂ particles (~0.1–0.5 μm) form in the flame zone.

4. **Thermophoretic Deposition**
   - The gas around the burner is hotter than the rotating tube surface (which is cooled internally by the incoming gas flow).
   - This temperature gradient creates a **thermophoretic force** pushing soot particles from hot region → cold region (tube wall).
   - Particles deposit uniformly on the rotating tube's outer surface.

5. **Sintering / Vitrification**
   - After one or more deposition passes, the burner is adjusted to a higher temperature (~1800 °C).
   - The porous soot layer is consolidated into a **transparent, bubble-free glassy layer**.
   - This step is critical to remove trapped gases and OH⁻ contamination.

6. **Layer-by-Layer Build-up**
   - For cladding: pure SiO₂ layers are deposited.
   - For core: SiO₂ doped with GeO₂ (raises refractive index) is deposited.
   - Multiple traversals build up the required refractive index profile.

7. **Collapse into Preform**
   - The burner temperature is raised to ~2000 °C, and surface tension collapses the tube into a solid rod — the **preform**.
   - The preform is then drawn into optical fibre at ~2000–2200 °C in a drawing tower.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why rotation?** → Ensures **azimuthal (circumferential) uniformity** of soot deposition, avoiding thick-thin streaks.
- **Why ring burner (not linear)?** → Provides **360° symmetric heating**, enabling uniform deposition all around the tube simultaneously.
- **Why external oxidation?** → Avoids contamination from tube inner wall; allows **larger preform diameters** since deposition builds *outward*, not inward.
- **Why thermophoresis?** → It is a contactless, non-invasive deposition mechanism that doesn't disturb particle morphology, preserving low-loss glass structure.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|----------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | SiCl₄(g) + O₂(g) → SiO₂(s) + 2Cl₂(g) | Core chemical reaction of OVPO | ΔH = -611 kJ/mol |
| 2 | $F_p = \dfrac{6\pi \mu r_p^2}{\rho_g} \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Thermophoretic force per particle | Newtons (N) |
| 3 | $v_{th} = \dfrac{-0.55 \mu}{\rho_g T} \cdot \nabla T$ | Thermophoretic velocity (Talbot's formula) | m/s |
| 4 | $\eta_{dep} = \dfrac{m_{deposited}}{m_{reactant}} \times 100\%$ | Deposition efficiency | 50–80 % |
| 5 | $R_{dep} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot Q_{SiCl_4} \cdot M_{SiO_2}}{M_{SiCl_4}}$ | Mass deposition rate | g/min |
| 6 | $\Delta n = K \cdot [GeO_2] + L \cdot [B_2O_3] + ...$ | Refractive index change by doping | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_{sin} \geq T_g \approx 1200°C$ for SiO₂ | Sintering condition | °C |
| 8 | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Numerical aperture of resulting fibre | dimensionless |
| 9 | $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda} NA$ | Normalized frequency (V-number) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\alpha = \alpha_{UV} + \alpha_{IR} + \alpha_{Rayleigh}$ | Total fibre attenuation | dB/km |

**Where:**
- $F_p$ = thermophoretic force on a soot particle
- $r_p$ = particle radius (~0.1 μm)
- $\mu$ = gas dynamic viscosity
- $\rho_g$ = gas density
- $Q_{SiCl_4}$ = SiCl₄ mass flow rate
- $M$ = molecular weight
- $K, L$ = doping coefficients (GeO₂: K ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻³ / mol%)
- $n_1, n_2$ = core, cladding refractive indices
- $a$ = core radius
- $\lambda$ = operating wavelength

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications long-haul networks** (submarine cables spanning 1000s of km).
- **Low-OH fibres** for high-power laser delivery in industrial cutting/welding.
- **Large-mode-area (LMA) fibres** for high-power fibre lasers, where large preform diameter is essential.
- **Specialty fibres** (rare-earth doped EDFAs, DCFs) requiring precise index profile control.
- The OVPO technique is a **bridge technology** between OVD's mass production capability and MCVD's precision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Thermophoretic Deposition Velocity

The thermophoretic force arises due to the asymmetric thermal creep of gas molecules around a small particle in a temperature gradient. We derive the deposition velocity (Talbot's formulation) used in OVPO modelling.

**Step 1 — Set up the Knudsen regime:**
The silica soot particles (rₚ ≈ 0.1 μm) in OVPO have Knudsen number:

$$
Kn = \dfrac{\lambda_{mfp}}{r_p}
$$

For OVPO flame at ~1700 °C, $\lambda_{mfp} \approx 0.4\ \mu m$, and $r_p \approx 0.1\ \mu m$:

$$
Kn = \dfrac{0.4\ \mu m}{0.1\ \mu m} = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Epstein's slip-flow regime** (Kn > 1, transition regime):
The thermophoretic force on a spherical particle:

$$
F_p = \dfrac{12\pi \mu r_p^2 K_{sl}}{\rho_g T} \cdot \nabla T
$$

where $K_{sl}$ is the slip coefficient (≈ 1.0 for SiO₂ in O₂).

**Step 3 — Force balance (terminal velocity):**
At terminal velocity, drag force balances thermophoretic force. By Stokes' law:

$$
F_{drag} = 6\pi \mu r_p v_{th}
$$

Setting $F_p = F_{drag}$:

$$
6\pi \mu r_p v_{th} = \dfrac{12\pi \mu r_p^2}{\rho_g T} \cdot \nabla T
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for thermophoretic velocity $v_{th}$:**

$$
v_{th} = \dfrac{2 r_p}{\rho_g T} \cdot \nabla T
$$

Including the slip correction factor (Talbot's full expression):

$$
\boxed{\,v_{th} = \dfrac{-0.55\ \mu}{\rho_g T} \cdot \nabla T\,}
$$

The negative sign confirms motion from hot to cold region (particles move toward the cooler substrate tube).

**Step 5 — Numerical estimation for OVPO:**
Given $\mu_{O_2,\,1700°C} \approx 7.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa·s}$, $\rho_g \approx 0.3\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $T \approx 1973\ K$, $\nabla T \approx 5 \times 10^{4}\ K/m$ (across the flame):

$$
v_{th} = \dfrac{0.55 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.3 \times 1973} \times 5 \times 10^4
$$

$$
v_{th} = \dfrac{3.85 \times 10^{-5}}{591.9} \times 5 \times 10^4
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v_{th} \approx 3.25\ m/s\,}
$$

This high thermophoretic velocity ensures rapid, efficient deposition of soot onto the tube surface — explaining why OVPO achieves higher deposition rates than MCVD.

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Deposition Rate

**Step 1 — Source molar flow of SiCl₄:**
Given bubbler temperature $T_b$ and O₂ carrier flow $Q_{O_2}$:

$$
Q_{SiCl_4} = \dfrac{P_{SiCl_4}(T_b)}{P_{total} - P_{SiCl_4}(T_b)} \cdot Q_{O_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mass flow using molecular weights:**

$$
\dot{m}_{SiCl_4} = Q_{SiCl_4} \cdot M_{SiCl_4} = Q_{SiCl_4} \times 169.9\ g/mol
$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric conversion to SiO₂:**
From SiCl₄ + O₂ → SiO₂ + 2Cl₂, 1 mol SiCl₄ gives 1 mol SiO₂.

$$
\dot{m}_{SiO_2,reacted} = \dot{m}_{SiCl_4} \cdot \dfrac{M_{SiO_2}}{M_{SiCl_4}} = \dot{m}_{SiCl_4} \cdot \dfrac{60.08}{169.9} \approx 0.354 \cdot \dot{m}_{SiCl_4}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for deposition efficiency $\eta_{dep}$:**

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{m}_{deposited} = \eta_{dep} \cdot 0.354 \cdot \dot{m}_{SiCl_4}\,}
$$

For OVPO, typical $\eta_{dep} \approx 60$–$80\%$, giving:

$$
\dot{m}_{deposited} \approx 0.7 \times 0.354 \times \dot{m}_{SiCl_4} \approx 0.248 \cdot \dot{m}_{SiCl_4}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: OVPO vs MCVD vs OVD vs VAD

| Parameter | OVPO | MCVD | OVD | VAD |
|-----------|------|------|-----|-----|
| **Reaction Location** | **Outside** tube | Inside tube | Outside rod | Outside rod (axial) |
| **Substrate Geometry** | Rotating tube | Stationary tube | Rotating rod | Rotating rod (vertical) |
| **Deposition Direction** | Radial, outward | Radial, inward | Radial, outward | Axial, upward |
| **Preform Size Limit** | ~300 mm | ~60 mm (tube limit) | Unlimited (rod) | Very large |
| **OH Contamination** | Low (< 1 ppb) | Medium (H₂ in tube) | Low | Lowest |
| **Attenuation Achieved** | 0.18 dB/km @ 1550 nm | 0.35 dB/km @ 1550 nm | 0.20 dB/km @ 1550 nm | 0.16 dB/km @ 1550 nm |
| **Index Profile Control** | Excellent | **Best** | Good | Moderate |
| **Deposition Rate** | High | Low | Very High | Very High |
| **Industry Use Today** | NTT, specialty | Corning, Lucent | Corning, Furukawa | Sumitomo, Furukawa |
| **Complexity** | Medium | Low | High | Highest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite**: A comparison table question asking "Compare OVPO with MCVD" is a **guaranteed 7-mark question**. Memorize the "reaction location" as the key differentiator.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 OVPO Apparatus — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SiCl4 Bubbler\nTemperature Controlled] --> M[Gas Mixing Manifold\nMFC1, MFC2, MFC3]
    B[GeCl4 Bubbler\nCore Doping] --> M
    C[O2 Carrier Gas\n99.9999 percent] --> M
    D[BCl3 Bubbler\nIndex Lowering] --> M
    M --> E[Rotating Silica\nSubstrate Tube\nLathe Mounted]
    E --> F[Ring Shaped H2 O2\nBurner\nTraverses Tube]
    F --> G[Soot Deposition\nZone Outside Tube]
    G --> H[Thermophoretic\nForce Pushes Soot\nOnto Cold Tube Wall]
    H --> I[Deposit Layer\nSintered at 1800C\nTransparent Glass]
    I --> J[Multiple Layers\nCladding and Core]
    J --> K[Collapse Zone\n2000C Surface\nTension]
    K --> L[Solid Preform\nReady for\nFibre Drawing]
    L --> M1[Fibre Drawing\nTower\n2200C]
    M1 --> N[Coated Optical\nFibre 125 micron]
    O[Exhaust Cl2\nScrubbing System] --> E
    P[Lathe Rotation\nDrive 30 to 100 rpm] --> E
    Q[Burner Traverse\nMotor 100 to 300\nmm per min] --> F
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Mount Substrate Tube on Lathe] --> S2[Step 2: Initiate Tube Rotation at 60 rpm]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Ignite Ring Burner 1500 to 1700 C]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Flow SiCl4 Plus O2 Through Tube Interior]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Halide Vapour Exits Tube Tip Enters Flame]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Homogeneous Oxidation SiCl4 Plus O2 to SiO2 Plus 2Cl2]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Soot Particles Form in Gas Phase]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Thermophoresis Drives Soot to Cool Tube Wall]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Burner Traverses Along Tube Length]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Repeat for Multiple Passes]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Sinter Pass at 1800 C Consolidates Layer]
    S11 --> S12{All Layers Deposited?}
    S12 -->|No| S4
    S12 -->|Yes| S13[Step 13: Collapse Tube at 2000 C into Solid Preform]
    S13 --> S14[Step 14: Preform Ready for Fibre Drawing]
```

### 4.3 Functional Zones of the OVPO Lathe

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZONE_A[Zone A: Gas Inlet and Seal]
        A1[Gas Inlet] --> A2[Rotating Seal]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_B[Zone B: Reaction and Deposition]
        B1[Ring Burner] --> B2[Soot Cloud]
        B2 --> B3[Rotating Tube with Deposit]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_C[Zone C: Sintering]
        C1[Higher Temp Burner 1800 C] --> C2[Transparent Glass Layer]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_D[Zone D: Collapse]
        D1[2000 C Heat Source] --> D2[Solid Preform]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_E[Zone E: Exhaust and Scrubbing]
        E1[Cl2 Exhaust] --> E2[NaOH Scrubber]
    end
    ZONE_A --> ZONE_B --> ZONE_C --> ZONE_D --> ZONE_E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic**: The arrows indicate the physical flow of gas, the heat zones are sequential along the lathe axis, and the **key uniqueness of OVPO** is the *external* ring burner (Zone B), which is absent in MCVD. Always highlight this in diagram-based exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Outside Vapour Phase Oxidation (OVPO) and state its key distinguishing feature from MCVD.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

OVPO is a chemical vapour deposition technique used to fabricate high-purity silica optical fibre preforms, in which the halide precursor vapours (e.g., SiCl₄) are passed through a rotating silica substrate tube and the oxidation reaction (SiCl₄ + O₂ → SiO₂ + 2Cl₂) takes place in a **ring-shaped H₂–O₂ burner located *outside* the tube**. The resulting SiO₂ soot particles are deposited on the tube's outer surface by thermophoresis and sintered into a transparent glassy layer.

**Key distinguishing feature from MCVD**: In MCVD, the oxidation reaction occurs **inside** the substrate tube (on the inner wall), whereas in OVPO it occurs **outside** the tube in a ring burner. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for reaction, 1 mark for distinction)

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**What is thermophoresis and explain its role in OVPO.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Thermophoresis** is the physical phenomenon in which small suspended particles in a gas experience a net force moving them from a **hot region to a cold region** along a temperature gradient. (1 mark)

**Role in OVPO**: In the OVPO process, the SiO₂ soot particles formed in the ring burner flame (at ~1700 °C) experience a temperature gradient towards the rotating substrate tube (which is internally cooled by the incoming cold gas flow at ~25 °C). This gradient creates a thermophoretic force that drives the soot particles **outward from the hot flame onto the cooler tube surface**, ensuring uniform deposition of the porous soot layer. (2 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the Outside Vapour Phase Oxidation (OVPO) technique for fabricating optical fibre preforms. Discuss the apparatus, chemistry, and process sequence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare OVPO with MCVD and OVD techniques in terms of reaction location, preform size, deposition rate, and attenuation performance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) OVPO Process Explanation (7 marks):**

**1. Apparatus (2 marks):**
The OVPO apparatus consists of:
- A high-purity fused silica **substrate tube** (~25–40 mm OD) mounted on a precision glass-working **lathe**.
- A **gas delivery system** with mass flow controllers (MFCs) and temperature-controlled bubblers for SiCl₄, GeCl₄, BCl₄, POCl₃.
- A **ring-shaped H₂–O₂ burner** that traverses along the length of the tube.
- A **lathe drive** that rotates the tube at 30–100 rpm.
- An **exhaust system** with NaOH scrubber for Cl₂ neutralization.
- A **traversing motor** for burner motion at 100–300 mm/min.

**2. Chemistry (2 marks):**
The principal chemical reaction is:

$$
SiCl_4(g) + O_2(g) \xrightarrow{1500 - 1700°C} SiO_2(s,\ soot) + 2Cl_2(g)
$$

This **homogeneous gas-phase reaction** occurs in the ring burner flame **outside** the tube. For core doping, GeCl₄ is added:

$$
GeCl_4(g) + O_2(g) \rightarrow GeO_2(s) + 2Cl_2(g)
$$

The GeO₂ raises the core refractive index, creating the required index difference $\Delta n$ with the cladding.

**3. Process Sequence (3 marks):**
- **Step 1**: Mount substrate tube, initiate rotation at 60 rpm.
- **Step 2**: Ignite ring burner, flow SiCl₄ + O₂ through tube.
- **Step 3**: Soot forms externally, deposits via thermophoresis on outer tube wall.
- **Step 4**: Burner traverses, repeating the soot deposition pass-by-pass.
- **Step 5**: Sintering pass at 1800 °C vitrifies the porous soot into transparent glass.
- **Step 6**: Switch to GeCl₄ flow for core layers.
- **Step 7**: Collapse the tube at 2000 °C into a solid preform using surface tension.
- **Step 8**: The preform is then drawn into 125 μm optical fibre on a drawing tower at 2200 °C.

---

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | OVPO | MCVD | OVD |
|-----------|------|------|-----|
| **Reaction Location** | Outside tube (ring burner) | Inside tube | Outside rod (linear burners) |
| **Substrate Form** | Rotating tube | Stationary tube | Rotating rod/target |
| **Preform Max Diameter** | ~300 mm | ~60 mm | Unlimited (multi-rod) |
| **Deposition Rate** | High (2–6 g/min) | Low (0.5–1 g/min) | Very High (10+ g/min) |
| **Index Profile Precision** | Excellent | **Best** | Good |
| **OH⁻ Contamination** | Low | Medium (in-tube H₂) | Low |
| **Attenuation @ 1550 nm** | 0.18 dB/km | 0.35 dB/km | 0.20 dB/km |
| **Process Complexity** | Medium | Low | High |
| **Industry Origin** | NTT Japan | Bell Labs USA | Corning USA |
| **Collapse Required?** | Yes | Yes | No (target is solid rod) |
| **Best Suited For** | Specialty, LMA, telecom | High-precision graded-index | Mass production, low-cost |

(Allocate 1 mark per major comparison row × 5–6 rows, plus 1 mark for the explanatory conclusion.)

**Conclusion (1 mark):** OVPO strikes a balance between MCVD's precision and OVD's scale, making it ideal for **specialty fibres and large-mode-area fibres** used in high-power laser systems and submarine cables.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Derive the thermophoretic velocity expression and explain how it governs the deposition rate in OVPO. Use Talbot's formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the key process parameters of OVPO (rotation speed, burner temperature, traverse speed, gas flow rates) and explain their individual effects on soot deposition uniformity and preform quality. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Thermophoretic Velocity (7 marks):**

**Starting principle (1 mark):** A particle in a gas with a temperature gradient experiences a net force due to asymmetric molecular bombardment — more energetic molecules from the hot side impart greater momentum than those from the cold side.

**Step 1 — Thermophoretic force (2 marks):**

$$
F_p = \dfrac{12\pi \mu r_p^2 K_{sl}}{\rho_g T} \cdot \nabla T
$$

where $r_p$ = particle radius, $\mu$ = gas viscosity, $\rho_g$ = gas density, $T$ = absolute temperature, $K_{sl}$ = slip coefficient.

**Step 2 — Stokes drag force (1 mark):**

$$
F_{drag} = 6\pi \mu r_p v_{th}
$$

**Step 3 — Force balance (1 mark):** At terminal velocity, $F_p = F_{drag}$:

$$
6\pi \mu r_p v_{th} = \dfrac{12\pi \mu r_p^2}{\rho_g T} \cdot \nabla T
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_{th}$ (1 mark):** Talbot's complete expression including slip correction:

$$
\boxed{\,v_{th} = \dfrac{-0.55\ \mu}{\rho_g T} \cdot \nabla T\,}
$$

The negative sign indicates motion from hot to cold region.

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation for OVPO (1 mark):** With $\mu \approx 7.0 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho_g \approx 0.3$ kg/m³, $T = 1973$ K, $\nabla T \approx 5 \times 10^4$ K/m:

$$
v_{th} \approx 3.25\ m/s
$$

**Dependence on Deposition Rate (extra 0.5 mark):** The soot mass flux $J = \rho_{soot} \cdot v_{th}$ dictates the deposition rate. Higher $\nabla T$ (steeper thermal gradient) directly increases $v_{th}$ and hence deposition rate. This is why OVPO's ring burner creates a strong thermal gradient, achieving high deposition rates compared to MCVD.

---

**(b) Process Parameters and Their Effects (7 marks):**

**1. Substrate Tube Rotation Speed (2 marks):**
- Typical: 30–100 rpm.
- **Effect**: Higher rotation → more uniform circumferential deposition; eliminates azimuthal streaks.
- **Risk**: Too high causes vibration, soot shedding; too low causes thick-thin banding.

**2. Ring Burner Temperature (2 marks):**
- Deposition zone: 1500–1700 °C; Sintering zone: 1800 °C.
- **Effect**: Higher temperature → faster reaction kinetics, finer soot particles, higher density.
- **Risk**: Excessive temperature → tube deformation, GeO₂ volatilization (index profile distortion).

**3. Burner Traverse Speed (1.5 marks):**
- Typical: 100–300 mm/min.
- **Effect**: Slower → thicker, denser layers; faster → thinner, more porous layers.
- Must be matched to soot deposition rate to avoid non-uniform axial thickness.

**4. Gas Flow Rates (SiCl₄, O₂, H₂) (1.5 marks):**
- Higher SiCl₄ flow → higher deposition rate, but risk of unreacted halide (bubble formation).
- Higher H₂/O₂ ratio → higher flame temperature.
- Excess O₂ ensures complete oxidation; deficiency causes SiO sub-oxide formation (black deposits).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing OVPO with OVD**: OVD uses a *linear* burner on a *rotating rod* (deposition builds outward on a target rod). OVPO uses a *ring* burner on a *rotating tube* (deposition is on the tube wall externally, then collapsed). Examiners specifically test this distinction — don't write them interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Writing "MCVD" instead of "OVPO"** when asked for the reaction location: A 1-mark loss per occurrence. Always explicitly say "**outside the tube in a ring burner**".
>
> 3. **Forgetting to mention thermophoresis** in OVPO process explanation: This is the *physical mechanism* of deposition and is mandatory. Skipping it costs ~2 marks.
>
> 4. **Skipping the sintering step**: Many students omit the vitrification pass. Examiners expect 4–5 distinct process steps minimum.
>
> 5. **Not drawing a labelled diagram**: In 7-mark questions, a labelled diagram is worth at least 2 marks. Always include it.
>
> 6. **Confusing deposition direction**: OVPO deposits *outward on the tube*; MCVD deposits *inward on the tube's inner wall*. This is a critical directional difference.
>
> 7. **Wrong units in numerical problems**: Use **g/min** for deposition rate, **dB/km** for attenuation, **μm** for fibre diameter, **°C** for temperature. Wrong units = 0.5 mark deduction per error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for OVPO (Module 2):**
>
> - **Full form & origin**: Outside Vapour Phase Oxidation; developed by **NTT, Japan (1980s)**.
> - **Core reaction**: SiCl₄(g) + O₂(g) → SiO₂(s) + 2Cl₂(g) — occurs **outside** the tube.
> - **Key apparatus component**: **Ring-shaped H₂–O₂ burner** (this is THE distinguishing feature).
> - **Substrate**: Rotating fused silica tube, 30–100 rpm.
> - **Deposition mechanism**: **Thermophoresis** (hot to cold region, soot → tube wall).
> - **Temperatures**: 1500–1700 °C (deposition), 1800 °C (sintering), 2000 °C (collapse).
> - **Carrier gas**: O₂ (high-purity, 99.9999 %).
> - **Dopants**: GeCl₄ (raises index, core), BCl₃ (lowers index, cladding), POCl₃ (raises index).
> - **Preform size**: Up to **300 mm** diameter (much larger than MCVD's 60 mm).
> - **Deposition rate**: 2–6 g/min (faster than MCVD).
> - **Attenuation achieved**: 0.18 dB/km @ 1550 nm.
> - **Main advantage**: Combines **large preform capability** (like OVD) with **high purity** (like MCVD).
> - **Main limitation**: Tube collapse step required (adds complexity vs OVD on rod).
> - **Best application**: **Large-mode-area (LMA) fibres**, **specialty fibres**, **submarine cables**.
> - **Key equation to remember**: Talbot's thermophoretic velocity $v_{th} = -0.55 \mu \nabla T / (\rho_g T)$.
> - **Key distinction from MCVD**: Reaction location (outside vs inside tube).
> - **Key distinction from OVD**: Substrate geometry (rotating tube vs rotating rod) + ring burner.
> - **Layering order**: Pure SiO₂ cladding first, then GeO₂-doped SiO₂ core on top.
> - **Final step**: Collapse tube at 2000 °C by surface tension into a solid preform rod.
> - **Next step (outside this module)**: Drawing tower at 2200 °C produces 125 μm coated fibre.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Fiber Cables - Single and Multi Fiber Cables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optical Fiber Cables - Single and Multi Fiber Cables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optical Fiber Cable:** A structured assembly comprising one or more optical fibers, strength members, protective jackets, and water-blocking/filling compounds, designed to protect the fragile glass fibers from mechanical stress, moisture ingress, and environmental hazards while preserving their intrinsic transmission characteristics (low attenuation and high bandwidth) during installation and service life.

An **Optical Fiber Cable** is fundamentally different from the bare optical fiber itself. The bare fiber (glass) has a tensile strength of only about **3.5 N** (≈ 350 grams-force) without protection, and a minimum bend radius that, if violated, causes immediate catastrophic failure (microbending and macrobending losses). The cable is therefore an engineered **mechanical and environmental envelope** that allows deployment of the fiber through ducts, aerial routes, direct burial, and indoor risers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Straw Inside a Garden Hose" Model**
> Think of an optical fiber as a thin, brittle glass strand — almost like a human hair made of glass. If you try to pull it through a long corridor, it snaps instantly. To carry it across a building or city, you need a protective structure.
> - **The glass fiber** = the actual data-carrying "wire" (like the wick in a candle).
> - **Buffer coating (250 µm)** = the first plastic skin around the wick.
> - **Strength members (Kevlar/Steel)** = like steel rods inside a concrete pillar — they bear all the pulling (tensile) load.
> - **Jacket (PE/LSZH/PVC)** = the outer rubbery skin that protects against water, chemicals, and abrasion.
> - **Filling gel / water-blocking yarn** = like the wax inside a candle that stops water from creeping in.
> In essence, the cable answers a single engineering question: *"How do we deliver a fragile glass thread from Point A to Point B without breaking it, while keeping it dry, straight, and unstretched?"*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Bare glass fiber diameter | **125 µm ± 1 µm** (cladding) |
| Coated fiber diameter | **250 µm ± 15 µm** |
| Minimum bend radius (long term) | **20 × cable diameter** |
| Minimum bend radius (short term/ installation) | **15 × cable diameter** |
| Typical operating temperature | **−40 °C to +70 °C** |
| Tensile rating (installation) | **≥ 3 × long-term rating** |
| Fiber tensile proof test | **≥ 0.7 GN/m² (100 kpsi)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes cables into two broad classes — **(i) Single Fiber Cables** (simplex) and **(ii) Multi-Fiber Cables** (loose tube, tight-buffered, ribbon). Mastery of both classes is mandatory for Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of a Cable (Bottom-Up)

1. **Bare Glass Fiber** (Core + Cladding, 125 µm OD)
   - The light-guiding medium. Composed of doped silica (GeO₂-doped SiO₂ core, pure SiO₂ cladding).
2. **Primary Coating** (Acrylate, brings diameter to 250 µm)
   - Soft inner layer (modulus ≈ 1 MPa) cushions against microbends.
3. **Secondary Coating** (Acrylate, harder, modulus ≈ 1 GPa)
   - Provides mechanical stiffness and handling protection.
4. **Buffer** (900 µm tight buffer or loose tube slot)
   - Decouples the fiber from external mechanical forces.
5. **Strength Members** (Aramid yarn / fiberglass / steel wire)
   - Bear the tensile load during pulling.
6. **Jacket** (PE for outdoor, LSZH/PVC for indoor)
   - Final environmental barrier.

### 2.2 Classification Logic — Why Two Types?

> The choice between **Single-Fiber** and **Multi-Fiber** cables is driven by:
> - **Application environment** (indoor patch cord vs outdoor backbone)
> - **Required fiber count** (1–2 vs 4–3456)
> - **Installation method** (connectorization vs splicing)
> - **Mechanical protection level** (light vs heavy duty)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum allowable pulling tension | $T_{max} = n \cdot F_{fiber}$ | N | Cable pulling spec |
| Short-term allowable tension | $T_{short} = 3 \cdot T_{long}$ | N | Installation rating |
| Minimum bend radius (no loss) | $R_{min} = 20 \cdot D_{cable}$ | m | Storage & installation |
| Crush resistance | $F_{crush} = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R}$ | N/cm | Direct burial rating |
| Cable weight per km | $W_c = \rho_{PE} \cdot V_{jacket} + W_{strength}$ | kg/km | Sag & tension calc |
| Attenuation coefficient (fiber) | $\alpha = -\dfrac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | dB/km | Total link loss budget |
| Water ingress (IEC 60794-1-22) | $H_{2}O$ head < **1 m** for **24 h** | m / h | Cable sealing test |
| Temperature cycling range | $-40\,°C \le T \le +70\,°C$ | °C | Field deployment |
| Fiber strain under load | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} \le 0.2\%$ | — | Long-term reliability |
| Loose tube helix pitch | $L_{helix} = \pi \cdot (D_{tube} + d_{fiber})$ | mm | Excess fiber length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Rule (KTU high-yield):** During installation, fiber strain must **never exceed 0.2 %** under any applied load. The strength members are designed so that they, NOT the fiber, carry the tensile load.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Telecom Backbone (OFC networks):** Multi-fiber loose tube cables carry 48–288 fibers between cities.
- **FTTH (Fiber-to-the-Home):** Indoor drop cables (single or 2-fiber) connect the distribution point to the home.
- **Data Centers:** Pre-terminated **MTP/MPO multi-fiber ribbon cables** (8, 12, 24 fibers) enable 40G/100G/400G Ethernet.
- **Submarine Cables:** Double-armored, steel-tube, multi-fiber cables span oceans.
- **Military / Aerospace:** Tactical single-fiber cables with ruggedized jackets for field deployment.
- **Medical Endoscopy:** Single-fiber illumination bundles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Excess Fiber Length in Loose Tube Design

In a loose tube cable, the fiber lies helically inside a thermoplastic tube with a specific "excess length" so that when the cable is stretched, the fiber does not strain.

**Given:**
- Inner tube diameter: $D_t$ (mm)
- Coated fiber diameter: $d_f = 0.25$ mm
- Tube inner circumference: $C_t = \pi D_t$
- Helical pitch: pitch angle $\theta$

**Step 1: One full revolution of the helix**
The fiber traces a helical path. For one turn, the axial advance is the pitch $P$, and the circumference is $C_t$.

**Step 2: Length of one helical turn**
$$L_{turn} = \sqrt{P^{2} + C_t^{2}}$$

**Step 3: Excess length ratio**
Excess length is the difference between helical path and axial advance, normalized by the axial advance.

$$\frac{L_{turn} - P}{P} = \frac{\sqrt{P^{2} + C_t^{2}} - P}{P}$$

**Step 4: Approximation for small $\theta$ (typical cable)**
Since pitch $P \gg C_t$ is not always true, but for $\theta \to 0$:

$$\sqrt{P^{2} + C_t^{2}} = P \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{C_t}{P}\right)^{2}} \approx P \left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{C_t}{P}\right)^{2}\right)$$

**Step 5: Final excess length expression**
$$\varepsilon_{excess} = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{C_t}{P}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{\pi D_t}{P}\right)^{2}$$

This excess length allows the cable to stretch up to ≈ 0.2 % before the fiber itself experiences strain. **This is the mathematical foundation of loose tube cable design.**

### 3.2 Derivation: Cable Tensile Load Distribution

In a multi-element cable, the total pulling tension $T$ is shared between strength members and the fiber (via the tube).

**Given:**
- $A_s$ = cross-sectional area of strength members
- $A_f$ = effective area of buffered fiber
- $E_s$ = Young's modulus of strength member (Kevlar ≈ 70 GPa)
- $E_f$ = effective modulus of buffered fiber (≈ 5 GPa)
- $L$ = length of cable under load

**Step 1: Strain compatibility (both elements stretch equally)**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{T_s}{A_s E_s} = \frac{T_f}{A_f E_f}$$

**Step 2: Force balance**
$$T = T_s + T_f$$

**Step 3: Solving for $T_s$ (load carried by strength member)**
$$T_s = T \cdot \frac{A_s E_s}{A_s E_s + A_f E_f}$$

**Step 4: Required condition for fiber safety**
We require $T_f \le 0.002 \cdot A_f E_f$ (0.2 % strain limit).

$$T_{max} = 0.002 \cdot A_f E_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{A_s E_s}{A_f E_f}\right)$$

**Step 5: Simplification — typical case $A_s E_s \gg A_f E_f$**
$$T_{max} \approx 0.002 \cdot A_s E_s$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The Kevlar strength members must be sized such that $A_s E_s \ge 500 \cdot T_{rated}$ to keep fiber strain below 0.2 % under the rated installation load.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Single-Fiber Cable | Multi-Fiber Loose Tube | Multi-Fiber Tight Buffered | Multi-Fiber Ribbon |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fiber count | 1 (sometimes 2) | 4 – 288 | 2 – 144 | 4 – 3456 |
| Fiber protection | 900 µm buffer | Loose in gel-filled tube | Tight on each fiber | Bonded in flat ribbon |
| Water blocking | Dry or gel | Filling compound | Usually dry | Dry |
| Connectorization | Easy (epoxy/polish) | Requires breakout kit | Easy (direct) | Requires fan-out |
| Indoor/Outdoor | Mostly indoor | Outdoor | Indoor/Plenum | Data centers |
| Tensile load (typical) | 50 – 200 N | 1000 – 3000 N | 200 – 800 N | 500 – 1500 N |
| Bend radius | 5 – 10 × OD | 20 × OD | 10 – 15 × OD | 15 × OD |
| Cost per fiber | High | Low | Medium | Very Low |
| Typical use | Patch cords, FTTH drop | Backbone, MAN, long-haul | Premises, LAN | Data center, high-density |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cross-Section of a Single-Fiber (Simplex) Cable

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CORE 9 um SMF or 50 um MMF] --> B[CLADDING 125 um Silica]
    B --> C[PRIMARY COATING 250 um Soft Acrylate]
    C --> D[SECONDARY COATING Hard Acrylate]
    D --> E[BUFFER 900 um Tight Buffer]
    E --> F[ARAMID YARN Strength Member]
    F --> G[OUTER JACKET 2 to 3 mm LSZH or PVC]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Section of a Multi-Fiber Loose Tube Cable

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph centerSteel[Central Strength Member Steel or FRP]
        S1[Steel Wire]
    end
    S1 --> T1[Loose Tube 1 with 6 to 12 Fibers]
    S1 --> T2[Loose Tube 2 with 6 to 12 Fibers]
    S1 --> T3[Loose Tube 3 with 6 to 12 Fibers]
    S1 --> T4[Loose Tube 4 with 6 to 12 Fibers]
    S1 --> T5[Loose Tube 5 with 6 to 12 Fibers]
    S1 --> T6[Loose Tube 6 with 6 to 12 Fibers]
    T1 --> F1[OPTICAL FIBERS in Thixotropic Gel]
    T2 --> F2[OPTICAL FIBERS in Thixotropic Gel]
    T3 --> F3[OPTICAL FIBERS in Thixotropic Gel]
    T4 --> F4[OPTICAL FIBERS in Thixotropic Gel]
    T5 --> F5[OPTICAL FIBERS in Thixotropic Gel]
    T6 --> F6[OPTICAL FIBERS in Thixotropic Gel]
    T1 --> W1[Water Blocking Yarn]
    T2 --> W2[Water Blocking Yarn]
    T3 --> W3[Water Blocking Yarn]
    T4 --> W4[Water Blocking Yarn]
    T5 --> W5[Water Blocking Yarn]
    T6 --> W6[Water Blocking Yarn]
    T1 --> J1[PE Outer Jacket]
    T2 --> J2[PE Outer Jacket]
    T3 --> J3[PE Outer Jacket]
    T4 --> J4[PE Outer Jacket]
    T5 --> J5[PE Outer Jacket]
    T6 --> J6[PE Outer Jacket]
```

### 4.3 Cross-Section of a Multi-Fiber Tight Buffered Distribution Cable

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    J[Outer Jacket LSZH or PVC] --> K1[Aramid Yarn Strength Member]
    K1 --> F1[Buffered Fiber 1 900 um]
    K1 --> F2[Buffered Fiber 2 900 um]
    K1 --> F3[Buffered Fiber 3 900 um]
    K1 --> F4[Buffered Fiber 4 900 um]
    K1 --> F5[Buffered Fiber 5 900 um]
    K1 --> F6[Buffered Fiber 6 900 um]
    F1 --> G1[Coating 250 um]
    F2 --> G2[Coating 250 um]
    F3 --> G3[Coating 250 um]
    F4 --> G4[Coating 250 um]
    F5 --> G5[Coating 250 um]
    F6 --> G6[Coating 250 um]
    G1 --> H1[Glass Core 9 or 50 um]
    G2 --> H2[Glass Core 9 or 50 um]
    G3 --> H3[Glass Core 9 or 50 um]
    G4 --> H4[Glass Core 9 or 50 um]
    G5 --> H5[Glass Core 9 or 50 um]
    G6 --> H6[Glass Core 9 or 50 um]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram: Cable Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Need to Deploy Fiber] --> B{Indoor Only?}
    B -- YES --> C{Single Connection or Many?}
    B -- NO --> D{Fiber Count Needed?}
    C -- Single --> E[Single Fiber Simplex Cable]
    C -- Few to Many --> F[Tight Buffered Distribution Cable]
    D -- 2 to 12 --> G[Loose Tube Light Duty]
    D -- 12 to 144 --> H[Loose Tube Standard]
    D -- Above 144 --> I[Ribbon Stack Cable]
    E --> J{Connector Type?}
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[SC LC ST FC MPO MTP]
```

### 4.5 Helical Lay of Fiber Inside a Loose Tube (Geometry View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Pitch P axial advance] --> H[Helical Path of Fiber]
    C[Tube Circumference C equals pi times D tube] --> H
    H --> E[Excess Length equals 0.5 times C over P squared]
    E --> S[Fiber Strain less than 0.2 percent under rated load]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Takeaway:** The fiber never lies straight inside a loose tube — it spirals. This deliberate geometry is what allows the cable to stretch without straining the fiber, a foundational concept in optical cable engineering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

**Q1. List any three important properties required for materials used in fiber-optic cable jackets.**

*Model Answer:*
The jacket is the outermost protective layer. The required properties are:
1. **Mechanical toughness** — to resist abrasion, impact, and crushing during installation and service.
2. **Environmental resistance** — UV stability (for outdoor PE jackets), chemical resistance, and a wide operating temperature range (typically −40 °C to +70 °C).
3. **Flame retardancy / Low smoke** — especially for indoor LSZH jackets, which must meet IEC 60332-1 / IEC 61034 standards to prevent toxic fume release during fire.

*(Valid alternatives: flexibility, low coefficient of friction for easy pulling, moisture impermeability.)*

---

**Q2. What is the function of strength members in an optical fiber cable? Name two common materials used.**

*Model Answer:*
**Function:** Strength members bear the tensile (pulling) load during installation, ensuring that the strain on the optical fiber itself remains below **0.2 %** (the long-term reliability threshold). They also contribute to crush resistance and dimensional stability.

**Common materials:**
1. **Aramid yarn (Kevlar / Twaron)** — used in indoor and outdoor light-duty cables; high tensile strength, non-metallic, flexible.
2. **Steel wire (central or peripheral)** — used in outdoor, direct-burial, and submarine cables; provides high stiffness and crush resistance.
*(Alternative: Fiberglass Reinforced Plastic — FRP — used as a non-metallic central strength member.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Format: Answer ANY ONE (Q. A or Q. B)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]`
