**(a)** With neat cross-sectional diagrams, explain the construction of a **Single-Fiber (Simplex) Cable** and a **Multi-Fiber Loose Tube Cable**. Compare their applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **maximum allowable tensile load** on an optical fiber cable such that the fiber strain does not exceed 0.2 %. Discuss how strength members are designed to satisfy this constraint. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Single-Fiber (Simplex) Cable Construction:**

A simplex cable contains one buffered optical fiber (250 µm coated, upgraded to 900 µm tight buffer) surrounded by:
- **Aramid yarn (Kevlar) strength members** wrapped longitudinally around the buffer.
- **Outer jacket** of 2–3 mm diameter, typically LSZH for indoor or PE for outdoor use.

The 900 µm tight buffer allows direct connectorization with epoxy-and-polish or pre-polished connectors. Typical applications include patch cords, equipment interconnects, FTTH drop cables, and test leads.

**Multi-Fiber Loose Tube Cable Construction:**

A loose tube cable contains 4 to 288 fibers. Construction features:
- **Central strength member (CSM)** — steel wire or FRP running along the axis.
- **Multiple loose tubes (typically 6)** helically stranded (SZ or regular lay) around the CSM.
- Each tube contains **6 to 12 fibers** immersed in **thixotropic gel** (water-blocking filling compound).
- **Water-blocking yarns** between tubes and under the jacket.
- **Outer PE jacket** (with optional armor for direct burial).

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Single-Fiber | Multi-Fiber Loose Tube |
|---|---|---|
| Application | Patch cords, drop cables | Backbone, long-haul |
| Tensile rating | 50–200 N | 1000–3000 N |
| Connectorization | Direct | Requires fan-out / breakout |
| Indoor/Outdoor | Both | Predominantly outdoor |

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

The derivation is identical to **Section 3.2** above.

**Starting condition:** Fiber strain must satisfy $\varepsilon_f \le 0.002$ (i.e., 0.2 %).

**Tensile equilibrium:**
$$T = T_s + T_f$$

**Strain compatibility:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{T_s}{A_s E_s} = \frac{T_f}{A_f E_f}$$

**Fiber safety condition:**
$$T_f \le 0.002 \, A_f E_f$$

**Substituting and solving:**
$$T_{max} = 0.002 \, A_f E_f \left(1 + \frac{A_s E_s}{A_f E_f}\right) \approx 0.002 \, A_s E_s$$

**Design rule for strength members:** Since Kevlar has $E_s \approx 70$ GPa, and the typical required installation load is 1500 N:

$$A_s \ge \frac{T_{max}}{0.002 \cdot E_s} = \frac{1500}{0.002 \cdot 70 \times 10^9} = 1.07 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2} = 10.7\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

This is achieved by selecting an appropriate count of aramid yarn strands or steel wire diameter.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the constructional features of **Tight-Buffered Distribution Cables** and **Ribbon Cables**. State two applications for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An optical fiber cable is to be designed for an installation tensile load of 2000 N. The strength member is Kevlar (E = 70 GPa). The fiber-buffer system has an effective stiffness of 1 GPa and effective area 0.5 mm². Calculate: (i) the load carried by the fiber, and (ii) the required cross-sectional area of the Kevlar strength member so that the fiber strain does not exceed 0.2 %. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Tight-Buffered Distribution Cable:**
- Each fiber has a **900 µm tight buffer** of hard polymer (typically LSZH or PVC) bonded directly to the 250 µm coating.
- 2 to 144 such buffered fibers are bundled together, surrounded by **Aramid yarn** and a **single outer jacket**.
- **Applications:** (1) Premises cabling inside buildings, (2) Indoor riser/plenum runs, (3) LAN backbone, (4) Direct connectorization at outlets.

**Ribbon Cable:**
- 4 to 12 fibers are bonded side-by-side using a UV-curable acrylate matrix to form a **flat ribbon**.
- Multiple ribbons (up to 36 ribbons × 12 fibers = 432 fibers, or higher in stacked designs) are stacked inside a slotted core or loose tube.
- **Applications:** (1) Data center high-density interconnects (with MTP/MPO connectors), (2) Mass-fusion splicing (12 fibers spliced in one operation), (3) FTTH high-density distribution.

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Given:**
- $T = 2000$ N
- $E_s = 70 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- $E_f = 1 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- $A_f = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m²
- $\varepsilon_{max} = 0.002$

**Step 1: Maximum permissible load on fiber**
$$T_{f,max} = \varepsilon_{max} \cdot A_f \cdot E_f = 0.002 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 10^{9}$$
$$T_{f,max} = 1\,\text{N}$$

**Step 2: Load carried by Kevlar**
$$T_s = T - T_{f,max} = 2000 - 1 = 1999\,\text{N}$$

**Step 3: Required Kevlar cross-section**
$$A_s = \frac{T_s}{\varepsilon_{max} \cdot E_s} = \frac{1999}{0.002 \times 70 \times 10^{9}}$$
$$A_s = \frac{1999}{1.4 \times 10^{8}} = 1.428 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2} = 14.28\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

**Verification:**
$$\varepsilon_{fiber} = \frac{T_{f,max}}{A_f E_f} = \frac{1}{0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{9}} = 0.002 = 0.2\,\% \;\;\checkmark$$

**Answer:** Fiber carries 1 N, Kevlar needs ≈ 14.3 mm² cross-section.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.2 % strain rule:** Many students write the tensile formula but skip the strain limit. KTU examiners award 2 marks specifically for the **"fiber strain must be < 0.2 %"** statement. Missing it costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in mm² vs m²:** The cross-sectional area conversion is the **#1 mark-loss area** in numericals. Always convert mm² → m² by multiplying by 10⁻⁶, or convert N/mm² → N/m² consistently.
> 3. **Confusing tight buffer with loose tube:** Examiners specifically check whether you know the **900 µm tight buffer** is bonded to the coating, whereas in **loose tube** the fiber floats in gel. Vague answers lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Not drawing diagrams:** A 7-mark construction question with no diagram fetches only 3–4 marks. Always include a labeled cross-section, even if hand-drawn.
> 5. **Ribbon cable fan-out:** Students often forget that ribbon cables require a **ribbon fan-out (breakout kit)** to connectorize individual fibers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024**

- **Optical Fiber Cable = Fiber + Buffer + Strength Members + Jacket + Water Block** (5-element structure).
- **Two broad classes:** Single-Fiber (Simplex) and Multi-Fiber (Loose Tube, Tight Buffered, Ribbon).
- **Single-Fiber Cable:** One 900 µm buffered fiber, aramid yarn, 2–3 mm jacket. Used for patch cords and FTTH drop.
- **Loose Tube Cable:** Multiple tubes (each with 6–12 fibers in gel) stranded around central strength member. Used outdoors.
- **Tight Buffered Cable:** Each fiber individually buffered to 900 µm. Used indoors for premises/LAN.
- **Ribbon Cable:** 4–12 fibers bonded flat; enables mass fusion splicing. Used in data centers.
- **0.2 % rule:** Fiber strain must NEVER exceed 0.2 % under any load.
- **Tensile design rule:** $T_{max} \approx 0.002 \cdot A_s E_s$ — strength members carry the load, not the fiber.
- **Excess fiber length:** $\varepsilon_{excess} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\pi D_t}{P}\right)^2$ — the helical slack that protects the fiber.
- **Bend radius:** ≥ 20 × cable diameter (long term), ≥ 15 × (short term). Violation → macrobending loss.
- **Jacket types:** PE (outdoor, UV resistant), LSZH (indoor, low smoke), PVC (general indoor).
- **Strength members:** Aramid yarn (Kevlar, 70 GPa) or Steel wire (200 GPa). FRP is the non-metallic alternative.
- **Standards to remember:** IEC 60794 (cable), IEC 60332 (flame), ITU-T G.652/G.651 (fiber specs), Telcordia GR-20 (US outdoor).
- **KTU favorite numerical:** "Calculate Kevlar area for given load" — uses $A_s = \dfrac{T}{0.002 \cdot E_s}$.
- **Always draw a labeled cross-section** in 7-mark construction questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Fiber Connections: Splices, Connectors and Couplers
Optical Fiber Measurements: Attenuation and Dispersion Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optical Fiber Measurements: Attenuation and Dispersion Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 Optical Fiber Measurements: Attenuation and Dispersion

## 1.1 What Are We Actually Measuring?

In any optical communication link, the quality of the transmitted signal is degraded by **two fundamental physical phenomena**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attenuation** is the *loss of optical power* as light travels through the fiber (the signal gets *weaker* but is not necessarily *distorted*). **Dispersion** is the *spreading of optical pulses* as they propagate (the signal gets *distorted* and broadened, even if the total power is preserved).

These two impairments are measured using **distinct experimental techniques** because they affect different attributes of the optical signal — *amplitude* versus *temporal shape*.

## 1.2 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Attenuation Measurement** is defined as the experimental determination of the *optical power loss per unit length* of an optical fiber, expressed in **dB/km**, performed by comparing input and output optical powers at one or more specified wavelengths (typically **850 nm**, **1300 nm**, and **1550 nm** windows).

**Dispersion Measurement** is defined as the experimental quantification of the *temporal broadening of an optical pulse* after propagating through a known length of fiber, used to compute the *pulse spread (σ)*, *fiber bandwidth (B)*, and the *wavelength-dependent dispersion parameter D(λ)* in **ps/(km·nm)**.

## 1.3 The Real-World Analogy 🚰

Imagine a long garden hose carrying water (the optical signal):

- **Attenuation ≈ Water pressure drop.** As water flows through a 100 m hose, friction against the walls reduces the pressure at the outlet. The longer the hose, the weaker the flow. This is exactly how silica absorbs and scatters light — by **kilometer**.
- **Dispersion ≈ A water gun firing bullets of different sizes.** Imagine injecting perfectly timed, identical droplets at the inlet. By the outlet, because the hose path is slightly different for each droplet, they arrive at *different times*, smearing the pulse. The signal is still as strong, but the "drops" overlap — symbols collide and the receiver cannot tell them apart.

> [!NOTE]
> A fiber can have **low attenuation but high dispersion** (good for analog power delivery, terrible for digital communication), or vice versa. Both must be measured *independently* because they are physically unrelated phenomena.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Bold

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects every student to recognize the following standard quantities:

- **Attenuation Coefficient:** $\alpha$ in **dB/km**
- **Total Attenuation:** $A$ in **dB**
- **Insertion Loss:** in **dB**
- **Pulse Broadening (RMS):** $\sigma$ in **ps** or **ns**
- **Dispersion Parameter:** $D(\lambda)$ in **ps/(km·nm)**
- **Fiber Bandwidth:** $B_{opt}$ in **MHz·km**
- **Reference Wavelengths:** **850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Three Principal Methods of Attenuation Measurement

### Method 1 — The Cut-Back Method (Reference / Most Accurate)

This is the **destructive but most accurate** laboratory method, considered the benchmark against which all other methods are calibrated.

**Operating Principle:**
1. A stable, narrow-linewidth optical source (LED or laser) is launched into a long fiber (typically **1 km to several km**).
2. The output power $P_{out}$ is measured at the far end using a calibrated optical power meter.
3. *Without disturbing the launch conditions*, the fiber is cut back to a short length (typically **1–2 m**, removing the fiber near the input end).
4. The output power from this short reference length is recorded as $P_{in}$ (the input power to the long fiber, since attenuation in a 2 m fiber is negligible).
5. The attenuation is computed.

**Why it is accurate:** The same source, same launch optics, same wavelength, same connector alignment are reused — only the fiber length changes. This eliminates all systematic launch errors.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner expects you to mention that the **cut-back method destroys the fiber** and is therefore used for **factory characterization**, not field testing.

### Method 2 — The Insertion Loss Method (Non-Destructive)

A **non-destructive** technique suitable for installed cable.

- A reference power $P_{in}$ is measured by connecting the source directly to the power meter.
- The fiber under test is then inserted between source and meter, and $P_{out}$ is measured.
- The difference gives the total loss of the fiber plus the two connectors.

**Limitation:** Includes connector losses; less accurate than cut-back; sensitive to launch variability.

### Method 3 — Optical Time Domain Reflectometry (OTDR)

The most powerful **non-destructive, single-ended** field method.

- A high-power optical pulse is launched into one end of the fiber.
- The fiber backscatters a tiny fraction of the light (via **Rayleigh backscatter**) and reflects at any discontinuities (connectors, splices, breaks).
- The OTDR measures the returned power as a function of time. Because the speed of light in fiber is known, time is converted to distance.

The OTDR trace is a graph of **backscattered power (dB) vs. distance (km)**, from which one can read:
- The *slope* of the trace → attenuation coefficient.
- *Sudden step drops* → connector or splice loss.
- *Abrupt loss of signal* → fiber break.
- *Reflections (peaks)* → Fresnel reflections at connectors or end of fiber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> OTDR can measure attenuation, locate faults, identify splices, and estimate fiber length — all from **one end of the fiber**.

---

## 2.2 Two Principal Methods of Dispersion Measurement

### Method 1 — Time-Domain Pulse Broadening Measurement

A narrow optical pulse (width $\sigma_{in} \approx$ tens of ps) is launched into the fiber, and the broadened output pulse width $\sigma_{out}$ is recorded by a fast photodetector and sampling oscilloscope.

If the input and output pulses are **Gaussian**, the broadening is given by:

$$\sigma_{broadening}^2 = \sigma_{out}^2 - \sigma_{in}^2$$

The fiber bandwidth is then approximated by the **Gaussian approximation**:

$$B_{opt} = \frac{0.44}{\sigma_{broadening}}$$

**Limitation:** Requires ultra-short pulses, high-speed detectors, and is sensitive to chromatic effects if the source is not narrow-line.

### Method 2 — Frequency-Domain (Swept-Frequency / Phase-Shift) Measurement

The fiber is excited by an **intensity-modulated sinusoidal source** whose frequency is swept over a wide range. The modulation envelope is detected at the output, and the **frequency response $H(f)$** is measured.

The **−3 dB optical bandwidth** $f_{3dB}$ is read directly from $H(f)$. The fiber bandwidth is then:

$$B_{opt} = f_{3dB}$$

This is the most commonly used method for **multimode fibers** and gives directly the **baseband bandwidth-length product** in **MHz·km**.

### Method 3 — Interferometric Method (for Chromatic Dispersion in Single-Mode Fibers)

For single-mode fibers, dispersion is dominated by **chromatic dispersion**, measured by launching light at different wavelengths and detecting the **group delay** variation. A Michelson or Mach–Zehnder interferometer compares a reference path with the fiber under test. The shift in fringe position with wavelength gives $\tau(\lambda)$, from which:

$$D(\lambda) = \frac{1}{L} \frac{d\tau}{d\lambda}$$

is computed. Units: **ps/(km·nm)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Total Attenuation | $A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | **dB** | Insertion loss method |
| 2 | Attenuation Coefficient | $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | **dB/km** | $L$ = fiber length |
| 3 | Power After Length | $P_{out} = P_{in}\, 10^{-\alpha L/10}$ | **W or dBm** | Loss budgeting |
| 4 | OTDR Two-Point Loss | $A_{AB} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\big(P_A - P_B\big)$ | **dB** | Between points A and B on trace |
| 5 | RMS Pulse Broadening | $\sigma^2 = \sigma_{out}^2 - \sigma_{in}^2$ | **ns$^2$** | Gaussian pulse assumption |
| 6 | Fiber Bandwidth (Gaussian) | $B_{opt} = \dfrac{0.44}{\sigma}$ | **Hz** | −3 dB optical bandwidth |
| 7 | Bandwidth–Length Product | $BL = B_{opt} \cdot L$ | **MHz·km** | Multimode fiber figure of merit |
| 8 | Dispersion Parameter | $D(\lambda) = \dfrac{1}{L} \dfrac{d\tau}{d\lambda}$ | **ps/(km·nm)** | Single-mode chromatic dispersion |
| 9 | Total Dispersion | $\tau_{total} = D(\lambda) \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot L$ | **ps** | Pulse spread due to source linewidth |
| 10 | Refractive Index via OTDR | $n = \dfrac{c}{2\,v_{group}}$ | dimensionless | $c$ = **3 × 10$^8$ m/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> Always express powers in **linear units (watts)** when inserting into the formulas, then convert to dB/dBm only for the final result. Mixing linear and logarithmic units is a common KTU valuation pitfall.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Operators (BSNL, Reliance Jio, etc.)** use OTDR trucks to certify newly laid fiber routes and pinpoint breaks within centimeters.
- **Fiber Manufacturers** (Corning, OFS, Sterlite) use the cut-back method on every production batch to certify $\alpha$ at 1310 nm and 1550 nm.
- **System Designers** use measured $D(\lambda)$ to compute the maximum bit-rate–distance product $(BL)$ for a given link budget and dispersion budget.
- **DWDM System Planners** use single-mode dispersion measurements to select the correct **dispersion compensation fiber (DCF)** length for 10 Gbps / 40 Gbps / 100 Gbps channels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Solved Problems

## 3.1 Derivation of the Attenuation Coefficient from First Principles

Consider a differential slice of fiber of length $dz$ at position $z$. The power loss in this slice is proportional to the power present:

$$dP = -\alpha\, P(z)\, dz$$

where $\alpha$ is the linear attenuation coefficient in **per meter** (we will convert to dB/km later). Separating variables:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\alpha\, dz$$

Integrating from $z = 0$ (where $P(0) = P_{in}$) to $z = L$ (where $P(L) = P_{out}$):

$$\int_{P_{in}}^{P_{out}} \frac{dP}{P} = -\alpha \int_{0}^{L} dz$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right) = -\alpha L$$

$$P_{out} = P_{in}\, e^{-\alpha L}$$

To express in decibels, multiply by 10 and convert natural log to base-10:

$$A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(e^{\alpha L}) = 10 \cdot \alpha L \cdot \log_{10}(e)$$

Since $\log_{10}(e) = 0.4343$, define $\alpha_{dB} = 4.343\,\alpha$, giving:

$$\boxed{\;A = \alpha_{dB} \cdot L\;} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{\;\alpha_{dB} = \frac{10}{L}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)\;}$$

This is the **fundamental attenuation-coefficient equation** in dB/km used in every KTU problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Dispersion-Bandwidth Relation (Gaussian Pulse)

Assume a Gaussian input pulse:

$$P_{in}(t) = P_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{t^2}{2\sigma_{in}^2}\right)$$

After propagating through a linear dispersive channel, the output pulse remains Gaussian (a unique property of the Gaussian) but is broadened:

$$P_{out}(t) = P_0' \exp\!\left(-\frac{t^2}{2\sigma_{out}^2}\right)$$

For a linear, time-invariant, dispersion-only channel, variances add in quadrature (this is the central limit theorem applied to uncorrelated broadening mechanisms):

$$\sigma_{out}^2 = \sigma_{in}^2 + \sigma_{fiber}^2$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{fiber} = \sqrt{\sigma_{out}^2 - \sigma_{in}^2}$$

The transfer function of the fiber is the Fourier transform of a Gaussian, which is also Gaussian:

$$H(f) = \exp\!\left(-2\pi^2 \sigma_{fiber}^2 f^2\right)$$

The −3 dB bandwidth $f_{3dB}$ is the frequency at which $\vert H(f)\vert^2 = 1/2$:

$$\exp\!\left(-4\pi^2 \sigma_{fiber}^2 f_{3dB}^2\right) = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$-4\pi^2 \sigma_{fiber}^2 f_{3dB}^2 = -\ln 2 = -0.693$$

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{\sqrt{0.693}}{2\pi\,\sigma_{fiber}} = \frac{0.8326}{2\pi\,\sigma_{fiber}} = \frac{0.1325}{\sigma_{fiber}}$$

A more common engineering approximation, widely accepted in KTU solutions, is:

$$\boxed{\;B_{opt} = f_{3dB} = \frac{0.44}{\sigma_{fiber}}\;}$$

where $\sigma_{fiber}$ is in nanoseconds for $B$ in GHz, and the bandwidth-length product is:

$$\boxed{\;B_{opt}\cdot L = \frac{0.44\,L}{\sigma_{fiber}}\;}\quad \text{[MHz·km]}$$

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Problem 1 — Cut-Back Attenuation

> **Problem:** A 10 km fiber is measured with the cut-back method. Output power from the 10 km fiber is **0.5 mW**. After cutting back to 2 m, the output power is **5 mW**. Calculate the attenuation per km at the test wavelength.

**Solution:**

Given: $P_{out,long} = 0.5$ mW, $P_{out,short} = 5$ mW (≈ $P_{in}$), $L = 10$ km.

$$\alpha = \frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$$

$$\alpha = \frac{10}{10} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5}{0.5}\right)$$

$$\alpha = 1 \cdot \log_{10}(10) = 1 \cdot 1 = 1.0\ \text{dB/km}$$

**Result:** $\alpha = 1.0$ dB/km (typical for a multimode graded-index fiber at 850 nm).

---

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Problem 2 — Loss Budget

> **Problem:** A 50 km link uses fiber with $\alpha = 0.4$ dB/km at 1550 nm. There are **4 splices** each of loss **0.1 dB**, and **2 connectors** each of loss **0.5 dB**. Source launches **+3 dBm** into the fiber. Find the received power.

**Solution:**

$$A_{fiber} = \alpha \cdot L = 0.4 \times 50 = 20\ \text{dB}$$

$$A_{splices} = 4 \times 0.1 = 0.4\ \text{dB}$$

$$A_{connectors} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0\ \text{dB}$$

$$A_{total} = 20 + 0.4 + 1.0 = 21.4\ \text{dB}$$

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} - A_{total} = (+3) - 21.4 = -18.4\ \text{dBm}$$

**Result:** Receiver sees **−18.4 dBm** (well within the sensitivity of a typical PIN-TIA receiver at 1550 nm).

---

## 3.5 Solved Numerical Problem 3 — Dispersion-Limited Bandwidth

> **Problem:** A 6 km multimode fiber has a measured output pulse width of **14 ns** for an input pulse of **5 ns** width. Compute the fiber bandwidth and the bandwidth-length product.

**Solution:**

$$\sigma_{fiber} = \sqrt{\sigma_{out}^2 - \sigma_{in}^2} = \sqrt{14^2 - 5^2} = \sqrt{196 - 25} = \sqrt{171}$$

$$\sigma_{fiber} = 13.08\ \text{ns}$$

$$B_{opt} = \frac{0.44}{\sigma_{fiber}} = \frac{0.44}{13.08 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$B_{opt} = 33.6 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz} = 33.6\ \text{MHz}$$

$$B_{opt} \cdot L = 33.6 \times 6 = 201.6\ \text{MHz·km}$$

**Result:** $B_{opt} \approx 33.6$ MHz, $BL \approx 201.6$ MHz·km.

---

## 3.6 Solved Numerical Problem 4 — Chromatic Dispersion in Single-Mode Fiber

> **Problem:** A 100 km single-mode link uses a source of spectral width $\Delta\lambda = 0.2$ nm centered at 1550 nm. The fiber dispersion parameter is $D = 17$ ps/(km·nm). Compute the total pulse spread and the bit-rate limit assuming NRZ signaling.

**Solution:**

$$\tau_{total} = D \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot L = 17 \times 0.2 \times 100 = 340\ \text{ps}$$

For NRZ, the bit-period must be at least $4 \cdot \sigma$ to avoid ISI:

$$T_b \ge 4 \cdot \tau_{total} = 4 \times 340\ \text{ps} = 1360\ \text{ps} = 1.36\ \text{ns}$$

$$B_{max} = \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{1}{1.36 \times 10^{-9}} = 735\ \text{Mbps}$$

**Result:** Pulse spread is **340 ps**, dispersion-limited bit rate is approximately **735 Mbps** over 100 km.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📊 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cut-Back Attenuation Measurement — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceA[Optical Source 850 or 1300 or 1550 nm] --> launchA[Launch Optics Connectorized]
    launchA --> longF[Long Fiber Under Test e.g. 10 km]
    longF --> pmLong[Optical Power Meter Reading P out Long]
    pmLong --> recordA[Record P out Long value]

    sourceA --> cutbackA[Cut Fiber Back to 2 m Reference Length]
    cutbackA --> pmShort[Optical Power Meter Reading P in]
    pmShort --> computeA[Compute alpha dB per km]
    recordA --> computeA
    pmShort --> computeA

    computeA --> resultA[Attenuation Coefficient alpha in dB per km]
```

## 4.2 OTDR Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pulseGen[Pulse Generator] --> laserDrive[Laser Diode Driver]
    laserDrive --> laser[Laser Diode 1550 nm]
    laser --> coupler[Directional Coupler 3 dB]
    fiberIn[Fiber Under Test] --> coupler
    coupler --> apd[APD Photodetector]
    apd --> amp[Low Noise Transimpedance Amplifier]
    amp --> adc[High Speed ADC]
    adc --> dsp[DSP Signal Averaging Unit]
    dsp --> display[Display Trace Power dB vs Distance km]
    display --> events[Sudden Steps Connectors Splices Breaks]
```

## 4.3 Time-Domain Dispersion Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pulseSrc[Narrow Pulse Source Sub ns] --> fiberIn2[Fiber Under Test Length L]
    fiberIn2 --> fastPD[High Speed Photodetector]
    fastPD --> scope[Sampling Oscilloscope]
    scope --> measureOut[Measure sigma out]
    pulseSrc --> refPD[Reference Photodetector]
    refPD --> refScope[Reference Scope]
    refScope --> measureIn[Measure sigma in]
    measureIn --> computeDisp[Compute sigma fiber and B opt]
    measureOut --> computeDisp
```

## 4.4 Frequency-Domain Swept Measurement Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sweeper[RF Sweep Generator] --> mod[External Modulator]
    laserSrc[CW Laser] --> mod
    mod --> fibIn3[Fiber Under Test]
    fibIn3 --> detPD[Photodetector]
    sweeper --> refIn[Reference RF Path]
    detPD --> mixer[RF Mixer]
    refIn --> mixer
    mixer --> lockin[Lock In Amplifier or Network Analyzer]
    lockin --> plot[Plot H f Response]
    plot --> bwRead[Read f 3dB Bandwidth]
```

## 4.5 Attenuation vs Wavelength — Typical Silica Fiber Signature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a850[850 nm Window alpha approx 3 dB per km] --> humpOH[OH Peak at 1383 nm Water Absorption]
    humpOH --> a1300[1300 nm Window alpha approx 0.5 dB per km]
    a1300 --> low1550[1550 nm Window alpha approx 0.2 dB per km Lowest Loss]
    low1550 --> pure[Intrinsic Rayleigh Scattering Limit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above models the **spectral signature** of a silica fiber. The OH⁻ (water) absorption peak near 1383 nm is a critical feature in OTDR traces at 1550 nm wavelength — KTU questions often ask students to identify this peak.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
**Explain the cut-back method for measurement of attenuation in optical fibers. Why is it considered the most accurate method?**

**Model Answer:**

The cut-back method involves launching a stable optical signal of known wavelength into a long length of fiber under test and measuring the output power $P_{out}$. The fiber is then cut back to a short reference length (≈ 2 m) near the input end, and the output power from this short length is measured as $P_{in}$ (since attenuation over 2 m is negligible). The attenuation coefficient is:

$$\alpha = \frac{10}{L}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)\ \text{dB/km}$$

**Why it is most accurate:** The same source, same wavelength, same launch optics, and same connectors are used for both measurements. Only the fiber length changes. This eliminates errors due to source fluctuation, launch variability, and detector spectral response. *(3 marks: definition + formula + reason for accuracy)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is OTDR? List any four applications.**

**Model Answer:**

OTDR (Optical Time Domain Reflectometer) is a single-ended, non-destructive instrument that characterizes an optical fiber by analyzing the **Rayleigh backscattered light** and **Fresnel reflections** from a launched optical pulse.

**Four applications:**
1. Measurement of fiber attenuation (from slope of the trace).
2. Location and identification of fiber breaks / faults.
3. Measurement of splice and connector loss.
4. Estimation of fiber length.

*(3 marks: definition + 4 applications)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> ⚠️ **KTU Pattern Note:** In the University ESE, Module 2 questions are typically asked as a *single 14-mark question with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each*. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice).

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — Attenuation Measurements

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **OTDR**. Derive the relation between backscattered power and fiber attenuation.  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A fiber of length 12 km is tested using the cut-back method. The output power at 12 km is **2 μW**, and after cutting back to 1 m, the output power is **200 μW** at 1300 nm. Calculate:
   (i) Total attenuation in dB.
   (ii) Attenuation coefficient in dB/km.
   (iii) Received power at the end of a 30 km link launched with **0 dBm** input (use same fiber). *(7 marks)*

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Dispersion Measurements

**(a)** Explain the **time-domain pulse broadening method** for measuring dispersion in multimode optical fibers. Derive the relation between the fiber bandwidth and the RMS pulse width. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** In a time-domain dispersion measurement on a 5 km fiber, the input pulse width is **2 ns** and the output pulse width is **11 ns**.
   (i) Compute the RMS pulse broadening $\sigma_{fiber}$.
   (ii) Calculate the fiber bandwidth.
   (iii) Calculate the bandwidth-length product.
   (iv) If the source spectral width is 1 nm and the fiber has $D = 18$ ps/(km·nm) at 1550 nm, find the chromatic-dispersion-induced pulse spread for a 50 km link. *(7 marks)*

---

## ✅ Model Solutions — Question A

### (a) OTDR Working + Derivation — 7 marks

**Working (4 marks):**

A high-power, narrow optical pulse is launched into the fiber via a directional coupler. As the pulse travels down the fiber, two phenomena occur continuously:

- **Rayleigh backscatter:** A tiny fraction of the pulse (typically −40 to −50 dB below the forward power) is scattered in all directions; a small portion is captured back into the fiber and returns to the source end.
- **Fresnel reflections:** At any index discontinuity (connector, splice, break, fiber end), a much larger reflection (≈ −14 dB for a glass–air break, or ≈ −40 dB at a fusion splice) returns.

The returning light is separated by the directional coupler, detected by an APD, and averaged over many pulses. Time-of-flight (using $t = 2nz/c$) gives distance. The trace is a plot of **backscattered power (dB) vs distance (km)**.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Consider a pulse of width $\tau$ and peak power $P_0$ launched at $z=0$. The pulse travels to position $z$ where its power is:

$$P(z) = P_0\, 10^{-\alpha z/10}$$

The backscatter capture coefficient is $S$ (a fiber property, typically $\approx -50$ dB for silica). The captured backscatter at position $z$ is:

$$P_{bs}(z) = S \cdot \frac{c\tau}{2n} \cdot P_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha z/10}$$

The factor $c\tau/(2n)$ accounts for the spatial length of the pulse (the scattering region). The detected return power is:

$$P_{ret}(z) = S \cdot \frac{c\tau}{2n} \cdot P_0 \cdot 10^{-2\alpha z/10}$$

(the factor of 2 in the exponent arises because light travels to $z$ and back). Taking $10\log_{10}$:

$$P_{ret}(z)\ [\text{dB}] = P_0\ [\text{dB}] + \text{const} - 2\alpha z$$

The **slope of the OTDR trace** in dB/km is therefore $\mathbf{-2\alpha}$, hence:

$$\alpha = -\frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{slope}\ \text{(in dB/km)}$$

---

### (b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks

**Given:** $L = 12$ km, $P_{out}(12\,\text{km}) = 2\ \mu\text{W}$, $P_{in} = 200\ \mu\text{W}$, $\lambda = 1300$ nm.

**(i) Total attenuation:**

$$A = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{200}{2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 20\ \text{dB}$$

**(ii) Attenuation coefficient:**

$$\alpha = \frac{20}{12} = 1.667\ \text{dB/km} \approx 1.67\ \text{dB/km}$$

**(iii) For a 30 km link with 0 dBm input:**

$$A_{30} = \alpha \cdot L = 1.667 \times 30 = 50\ \text{dB}$$

$$P_{rx} = 0 - 50 = -50\ \text{dBm}$$

**Result:** $P_{rx} = -50$ dBm (a typical multimode fiber attenuation at 1300 nm).

---

## ✅ Model Solutions — Question B

### (a) Time-Domain Pulse Broadening Method — 7 marks

A narrow optical pulse (sub-nanosecond) from a pulsed laser source is injected into the fiber under test. The output pulse is detected by a high-speed photodetector (e.g., avalanche photodiode) and displayed on a **sampling oscilloscope** (or a streak camera for ps resolution). The input pulse width $\sigma_{in}$ is measured by bypassing the fiber (or measuring the source directly). The output pulse width $\sigma_{out}$ is measured at the far end. The fiber-induced RMS broadening is:

$$\sigma_{fiber} = \sqrt{\sigma_{out}^2 - \sigma_{in}^2}$$

**Derivation of bandwidth relation (Gaussian approximation):**

The fiber transfer function is the Fourier transform of a Gaussian, hence also Gaussian:

$$H(f) = \exp\!\left(-2\pi^2 \sigma_{fiber}^2 f^2\right)$$

Setting $\vert H(f_{3dB})\vert^2 = 1/2$:

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{0.44}{\sigma_{fiber}} = B_{opt}$$

Multiplying by $L$ gives the bandwidth–length product $BL$ in **MHz·km**, the standard figure of merit for multimode fibers.

---

### (b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks

**Given:** $L = 5$ km, $\sigma_{in} = 2$ ns, $\sigma_{out} = 11$ ns, $\Delta\lambda = 1$ nm, $D = 18$ ps/(km·nm), new link $L' = 50$ km.

**(i) RMS broadening:**

$$\sigma_{fiber} = \sqrt{11^2 - 2^2} = \sqrt{121 - 4} = \sqrt{117} = 10.82\ \text{ns}$$

**(ii) Fiber bandwidth:**

$$B_{opt} = \frac{0.44}{10.82 \times 10^{-9}} = 40.7 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz} = 40.7\ \text{MHz}$$

**(iii) Bandwidth-length product:**

$$BL = 40.7 \times 5 = 203.5\ \text{MHz·km}$$

**(iv) Chromatic dispersion pulse spread for 50 km:**

$$\tau_{cd} = D \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot L' = 18 \times 1 \times 50 = 900\ \text{ps}$$

**Result:** $\sigma_{fiber} = 10.82$ ns, $B_{opt} = 40.7$ MHz, $BL = 203.5$ MHz·km, $\tau_{cd} = 900$ ps.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Writing $P_{in}$ in dBm directly inside $\log_{10}$. Always convert dBm → mW first, OR use $A = P_{in}(\text{dBm}) - P_{out}(\text{dBm})$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in OTDR slope:** OTDR measures **two-way** loss; the slope is $-2\alpha$, not $-\alpha$. The attenuation is **half** the magnitude of the slope.
> 3. **Confusing $B_{opt}$ and $B_{elec}$:** $B_{opt}$ is the **optical (intensity) bandwidth**; $B_{elec}$ is the **electrical bandwidth** $= B_{opt}/\sqrt{2}$ for Gaussian pulses.
> 4. **Subtracting instead of variance-subtracting for pulse widths:** Pulse widths do **not** subtract linearly — they subtract in **quadrature** because of the Gaussian assumption.
> 5. **Wrong Rayleigh-scattering physics:** $S$ is wavelength-dependent as $\lambda^{-4}$; do not assume a constant.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Attenuation** is a *power-loss* phenomenon; **dispersion** is a *pulse-shape* phenomenon. They are measured by **completely different setups**.
- **Cut-back method** is destructive but most accurate; **OTDR** is non-destructive and field-friendly.
- **OTDR** measures backscattered light; the trace is **logarithmic (dB)** vs **distance (km)**.
- The OTDR slope is **$-2\alpha$** because light travels forward and back.
- **Three windows of operation:** 850 nm (multimode LANs), 1300 nm (zero-dispersion silica), 1550 nm (lowest loss, long-haul).
- **Pulse broadening** in multimode fibers arises mainly from **modal dispersion**; in single-mode fibers from **chromatic dispersion**.
- **Variances add in quadrature**, not widths — always compute $\sigma^2$ first.
- The **Gaussian approximation** gives $B_{opt} = 0.44/\sigma$; valid for most KTU problems.
- **Bandwidth-length product $BL$ (MHz·km)** is a *figure of merit* — it is approximately **constant** for a given fiber type, so doubling $L$ halves the available bandwidth.
- **Dispersion parameter $D(\lambda)$** has units **ps/(km·nm)**; it is zero near 1310 nm for standard silica single-mode fiber.
- **OTDR dead zone** (the recovery time after a strong reflection) is a real-world limitation for measuring closely spaced events.
- Standard reference fibers for OTDR calibration use a known length and a known $\alpha$ trace.
- **OTDR trace features to remember:** linear slope = uniform attenuation, sudden step down = loss event, peak = reflection, abrupt end = break.
- **Rayleigh scattering coefficient** $S_R \propto \lambda^{-4}$ — explains why attenuation is lower at longer wavelengths.
- **OH⁻ absorption peak** at ~1383 nm is a fingerprint of water contamination in silica.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MZ Interferometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** MZ Interferometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI)** is a fundamental photonic integrated device used to manipulate the amplitude, phase, and intensity of optical signals by exploiting the principle of two-beam optical interference. In the context of KTU's Optical Communication syllabus, the MZ interferometer is primarily studied as an **external electro-optic modulator** in which an incoming optical carrier is split into two coherent beam paths, undergoes a controllable phase shift (typically via the linear electro-optic Pockels effect), and is recombined to produce amplitude-modulated output light.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An MZI consists of **two 3 dB directional couplers** interconnected by **two dissimilar optical waveguide arms** of differing optical path lengths. By precisely varying the relative phase difference between the two arms, the device can be switched continuously between **constructive interference** (full throughput) and **destructive interference** (null output), thereby performing high-speed optical intensity modulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a railway track that **splits into two parallel tracks** at a junction (first coupler), runs side-by-side for a stretch with one track being **slightly longer** than the other, and then **merges back together** at a second junction (second coupler).

- If two trains start together at the splitter, the one taking the longer track arrives **slightly delayed**.
- When they meet again at the merger, two outcomes are possible:
  - **Both trains arrive at the same time** → they merge smoothly, and a strong combined output leaves the station. (Constructive interference — **ON state**)
  - **One train arrives exactly half a cycle later** → they crash into each other and **cancel out** completely. (Destructive interference — **OFF state**)

The MZ interferometer behaves identically with **light waves**: light is split, given a controllable delay (phase shift) along one arm, and recombined. The relative delay decides whether the output is bright or dark.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for students:** A tiny change in the refractive index of one arm (induced by an applied voltage) is enough to flip the device from "fully ON" to "fully OFF." This makes the MZI an ultra-sensitive **optical switch and modulator**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard parameters govern MZI design and frequently appear in KTU numerical problems:

- **Operating wavelength λ ≈ 1550 nm** (C-band standard for optical fiber communication)
- **Free-space speed of light c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Refractive index of Lithium Niobate (LiNbO₃) substrate n ≈ 2.2** (most common MZI material)
- **Electro-optic coefficient r₃₃ ≈ 30.8 pm/V** for LiNbO₃
- **Half-wave voltage V_π (typically 3–10 V)** — the voltage required to switch from ON to OFF
- **Extinction ratio ER > 20 dB** (industry standard for high-quality MZI modulators)
- **Insertion loss ≈ 3–6 dB**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown — Operational Logic

The MZ interferometer is composed of four physical regions:

1. **Input Optical Waveguide** — delivers the continuous-wave (CW) laser light into the device.
2. **First 3 dB Directional Coupler (Splitter)** — divides the incoming optical power equally (50:50) into two coherent beams traveling along the two interferometer arms.
3. **Two Parallel Waveguide Arms** — one is the *reference arm* (passive), and the other is the *signal/modulation arm* onto which electrodes are deposited to apply an electric field.
4. **Second 3 dB Directional Coupler (Recombiner)** — merges the two phase-shifted beams, producing complementary outputs at its two output ports.

The **operating logic** proceeds as follows:

- **Step 1 — Splitting:** The 3 dB coupler introduces a 90° relative phase shift between the two output arms while dividing the optical power equally.
- **Step 2 — Phase Accumulation:** Light in arm-1 picks up phase φ₁ = (2π/λ)·n₁·L₁, and light in arm-2 picks up phase φ₂ = (2π/λ)·n₂·L₂.
- **Step 3 — Phase Modulation:** Applying a voltage V across the electrodes of arm-2 changes its refractive index via the Pockels effect: Δn = -(1/2)·n³·r₃₃·(V/d).
- **Step 4 — Recombination:** The phase difference Δφ = φ₂ − φ₁ determines the interference outcome at the output.
- **Step 5 — Detection:** Constructive interference (Δφ = 2mπ) → maximum output intensity; destructive interference (Δφ = (2m+1)π) → minimum output.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cos^2(\Delta\phi / 2)$ | Output power (constructive port) | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $P_{bar} = P_{in} \sin^2(\Delta\phi / 2)$ | Complementary output (cross port) | Watts (W) |
| 3 | $\Delta\phi = (2\pi/\lambda) \cdot n \cdot \Delta L$ | Phase difference due to path-length difference | radians |
| 4 | $\Delta\phi = \pi V / V_\pi$ | Phase shift due to applied voltage (Pockels effect) | radians |
| 5 | $V_\pi = (\lambda \cdot d) / (n^3 \cdot r_{33} \cdot L)$ | Half-wave (switching) voltage | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $\Delta n = -\frac{1}{2} n^3 r_{33} E$ | Pockels-effect refractive index change | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{ext} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{max}/P_{min})$ | Extinction ratio | dB |
| 8 | $f_{3dB} \approx c / (n \cdot \Delta L)$ | Free Spectral Range / Bandwidth limit | Hz |
| 9 | $I_{out} = I_{in}\left[1 + m\cos(\Delta\phi)\right]/2$ | General interferometric intensity | W/m² |
| 10 | $E_{out} = \tfrac{1}{2}E_{in}\left[e^{-j\phi_1} \pm e^{-j\phi_2}\right]$ | Output field after recombination | V/m |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote the boundary conditions — maximum throughput occurs at $\Delta\phi = 0, 2\pi, 4\pi, ...$ and null throughput occurs at $\Delta\phi = \pi, 3\pi, 5\pi, ...$. Most mark-losing errors occur because students confuse which port is the "bar" and which is the "cross."

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Systems

The MZ interferometer is the **workhorse of high-speed optical modulation** in modern telecommunication systems. Its real-world applications include:

- **External Modulators for Long-Haul DWDM Links:** Replaces direct laser modulation to avoid chirp, enabling 100 Gbps+ and beyond data rates.
- **Optical Add-Drop Multiplexers (OADMs):** Used as wavelength-selective switches in Reconfigurable Optical Add-Drop Multiplexers (ROADMs).
- **Quantum Key Distribution (QKD):** MZI forms the heart of interferometric quantum cryptography setups (e.g., BB84 protocol).
- **Fiber-Optic Gyroscopes (FOG):** The Sagnac-loop MZI variant senses rotation by detecting tiny phase shifts induced by the rotating frame.
- **Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT):** Medical imaging uses MZI to map tissue reflectivity with micrometer resolution.
- **Integrated Photonic Logic Gates:** Mach-Zehnder-based switches form building blocks of all-optical signal processing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Function of the MZ Interferometer (KTU High-Yield)

Let the input optical field entering the first coupler be a monochromatic plane wave:

$$E_{in}(t) = E_0 \, e^{j\omega t}$$

The first 3 dB directional coupler splits the field equally between the two arms while introducing a relative 90° (π/2 rad) phase shift between them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{a}(t) &= \frac{E_0}{\sqrt{2}} \, e^{j\omega t} \quad \text{(arm A — reference)} \\
E_{b}(t) &= \frac{j E_0}{\sqrt{2}} \, e^{j\omega t} \quad \text{(arm B — modulation, with } j = e^{j\pi/2} \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The optical fields travel through arms A and B of length L, accumulating phase shifts φ₁ and φ₂ respectively. With propagation constants β_A and β_B:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_1 &= \beta_A L = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} n_A L \\
\phi_2 &= \beta_B L = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} n_B L
\end{aligned}
$$

The phase-shifted fields at the input of the second coupler are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_A &= \frac{E_0}{\sqrt{2}} \, e^{j(\omega t - \phi_1)} \\
E_B &= \frac{j E_0}{\sqrt{2}} \, e^{j(\omega t - \phi_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The second 3 dB coupler recombines the two fields. Applying the same 50:50 splitting matrix with a 90° phase bias:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{out,1}(t) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left( E_A + j E_B \right) \\
E_{out,2}(t) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left( j E_A + E_B \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the expressions for E_A and E_B:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{out,1} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left[ \frac{E_0}{\sqrt{2}} e^{-j\phi_1} + j \cdot \frac{j E_0}{\sqrt{2}} e^{-j\phi_2} \right] e^{j\omega t} \\
&= \frac{E_0}{2} \left[ e^{-j\phi_1} - e^{-j\phi_2} \right] e^{j\omega t} \quad (\text{since } j \cdot j = -1)
\end{aligned}
$$

The output intensity is proportional to the squared magnitude of the field. For port 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{out,1} &= \left\vert E_{out,1} \right\vert^2 \\
&= \frac{\vert E_0 \vert^2}{4} \left\vert e^{-j\phi_1} - e^{-j\phi_2} \right\vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding the modulus using the identity $\vert e^{j\alpha} - e^{j\beta} \vert^2 = 2 - 2\cos(\alpha - \beta)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{out,1} &= \frac{\vert E_0 \vert^2}{4} \left[ 2 - 2\cos(\phi_2 - \phi_1) \right] \\
&= \frac{I_{in}}{2} \left[ 1 - \cos(\Delta\phi) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the half-angle trigonometric identity $1 - \cos(\theta) = 2\sin^2(\theta/2)$:

$$
\boxed{I_{out,1} = I_{in} \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)}
$$

Following identical algebra for port 2 (which yields the complementary result):

$$
\boxed{I_{out,2} = I_{in} \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)}
$$

where the **phase difference** between the two arms is:

$$\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,(n_2 - n_1)\,L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Conservation Check:** $I_{out,1} + I_{out,2} = I_{in}\left[\sin^2(\Delta\phi/2) + \cos^2(\Delta\phi/2)\right] = I_{in}$. ✔ Power is conserved across both output ports.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voltage-Induced Phase Shift via the Pockels Effect

When a DC voltage V is applied across electrodes deposited on arm B, the induced electric field $E = V/d$ (where d is the electrode gap) modifies the refractive index of the electro-optic material (e.g., LiNbO₃) via the linear Pockels effect:

$$\Delta n = -\frac{1}{2}\, n^3 \, r_{33} \, E = -\frac{n^3 r_{33} V}{2d}$$

The resulting change in optical path length induces a phase shift:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\phi &= \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \, L \, \Delta n \\
&= \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \, L \cdot \left( -\frac{n^3 r_{33} V}{2d} \right) \\
&= -\frac{\pi n^3 r_{33} L}{\lambda d}\, V
\end{aligned}
$$

Defining the **half-wave voltage** $V_\pi$ as the voltage required to produce $\Delta\phi = \pi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi &= \frac{\pi n^3 r_{33} L}{\lambda d}\, V_\pi \\[4pt]
V_\pi &= \frac{\lambda d}{n^3 r_{33} L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the phase shift can be expressed compactly as:

$$\boxed{\Delta\phi = \pi \cdot \frac{V}{V_\pi}}$$

Substituting into the transfer function gives the **modulation equation**:

$$
\boxed{P_{out}(V) = P_{in} \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\pi V}{2 V_\pi}\right)}
$$

This is the foundational equation KTU expects students to derive for any MZI-based modulation problem.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Extinction Ratio (ER) in dB

The extinction ratio quantifies the contrast between maximum and minimum transmission. Using the maximum ($P_{max} = P_{in}$ at $V = 0$) and minimum ($P_{min}$ at $V = V_\pi$, ideally zero but realistically $P_{min} > 0$ due to fabrication imperfections):

$$\eta_{ext} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{max}}{P_{min}}\right) \quad [\text{dB}]$$

A perfectly symmetric MZI gives $P_{min} \to 0$, implying $\eta_{ext} \to \infty$. In practice, an ER of **20–30 dB** is achievable and sufficient for most telecom applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates the **functional architecture flow** of the MZ interferometer, mapping the interactions between its constituent blocks rather than its physical lithographic geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL[Laser Source CW 1550nm] --> node1
    node1[Input Waveguide] --> node2
    node2[Coupler 1 3dB Splitter 50:50] --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node3[Arm A Reference Waveguide Length LA] --> node5
    node4[Arm B Modulation Waveguide Length LB with Electrodes] --> node5
    node5[Coupler 2 3dB Recombiner] --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node6[Output Port 1 Cross Bar Port] --> node8
    node7[Output Port 2 Through Port] --> node9
    node8[Photodetector or Fiber Output]
    node9[Photodetector or Fiber Output]
    node10[DC Bias Voltage Source Vbias] --> node4
    node11[RF Modulation Signal Vrf] --> node4
    node10 --> node4
    node11 --> node4
```

## 4.2 Operating-State Decision Flow (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Apply Voltage V to MZI] --> nodeB{Compute Phase Shift}
    nodeB --> nodeC[Delta phi equals pi V divided by V pi]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Delta phi equal to 2m pi}
    nodeD --> nodeYES[YES Constructive Interference]
    nodeD --> nodeNO[NO]
    nodeYES --> nodeE[Output Port 2 Maximum ON state]
    nodeNO --> nodeF{Delta phi equal to 2m plus 1 times pi}
    nodeF --> nodeYES2[YES Destructive Interference]
    nodeF --> nodeNO2[NO Partial Transmission]
    nodeYES2 --> nodeG[Output Port 2 Minimum OFF state]
    nodeNO2 --> nodeH[Output Port 2 Intermediate Value]
```

## 4.3 Annotated Block Diagram — Single-Drive vs Dual-Drive MZI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SD[Single Drive MZI Configuration]
        SD1[Arm A Passive] --> SD2[Arm B has only one electrode pair]
        SD2 --> SD3[Single applied voltage controls full phase shift]
    end
    subgraph DD[Dual Drive MZI Configuration]
        DD1[Arm A has electrode pair VA] --> DD2[Arm B has electrode pair VB]
        DD2 --> DD3[Independent voltages allow chirp control and SSB modulation]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for KTU students:** In the Single-Drive MZI, only one arm carries electrodes — this is the simplest and most common configuration. In the Dual-Drive MZI, **both arms** carry electrodes, allowing the user to control the phase difference $\Delta\phi$ as a function of *two independent voltages*, enabling advanced modulation formats such as SSB (Single-Sideband), DPSK, and QAM.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Mach-Zehnder Interferometer. Briefly explain its principle of operation.**

**Model Answer:**

A Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI) is an integrated photonic device consisting of two 3 dB directional couplers connected by two optical waveguide arms of unequal path length. Its operation is based on the principle of **two-beam optical interference**.

Light from a coherent source is split into two equal parts by the first coupler. These two beams traverse separate paths, acquiring a relative phase difference $\Delta\phi$. Upon recombination at the second coupler, they interfere either **constructively** (producing maximum output, ON state) or **destructively** (producing minimum output, OFF state), depending on whether $\Delta\phi$ equals an even or odd multiple of $\pi$.

MZI finds extensive application in optical communication as an **external electro-optic modulator**, where voltage applied to electrodes on one arm modulates the output intensity.

*(3 marks — full marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark operating principle + 1 mark application)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the transfer function of an MZI in terms of applied voltage.**

**Model Answer:**

The transfer function of a single-drive Mach-Zehnder Interferometer relating output optical power to the applied modulating voltage V is:

$$P_{out}(V) = P_{in} \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\pi V}{2 V_\pi}\right)$$

where $P_{in}$ is the input optical power and $V_\pi$ is the **half-wave voltage** — the voltage required to switch the MZI from maximum transmission to zero transmission.

**Explanation:** When $V = 0$, $\cos^2(0) = 1$, and the output is maximum. When $V = V_\pi$, $\cos^2(\pi/2) = 0$, and the output is minimum. For a small sinusoidal modulation $V = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, the output intensity is varied between these extremes, producing an intensity-modulated optical signal.

*(3 marks — full marks: 1 mark formula + 1 mark V_π definition + 1 mark boundary behaviour)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of ½ inside the cosine squared** — a classic 2-mark deduction trap. Always re-derive from the field equations rather than memorising.
> 2. **Confusing bar and cross ports** — the formulas $P_{out} = P_{in}\cos^2(\Delta\phi/2)$ and $P_{bar} = P_{in}\sin^2(\Delta\phi/2)$ refer to **different physical output ports**. Label your diagram explicitly.
> 3. **Not stating boundary conditions explicitly** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if you do not verify $V = 0$ gives $P_{out} = P_{in}$ and $V = V_\pi$ gives $P_{out} = 0$.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the transfer function of a Mach-Zehnder Interferometer starting from the input optical field. Show that the output power is $P_{out} = P_{in}\cos^2(\Delta\phi/2)$.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

Let the input optical field be $E_{in}(t) = E_0 e^{j\omega t}$.

**Step 1 — Splitting at Coupler 1:** The 3 dB directional coupler divides the input power equally and introduces a 90° relative phase shift:

$$E_A = \frac{E_0}{\sqrt{2}}e^{j\omega t}, \quad E_B = \frac{jE_0}{\sqrt{2}}e^{j\omega t}$$

**Step 2 — Phase Accumulation:** The two fields acquire phase shifts φ₁ and φ₂ in the two arms:

$$E_A' = \frac{E_0}{\sqrt{2}}e^{j(\omega t - \phi_1)}, \quad E_B' = \frac{jE_0}{\sqrt{2}}e^{j(\omega t - \phi_2)}$$

**Step 3 — Recombination at Coupler 2:** Applying the coupler transfer matrix:

$$E_{out} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(E_A' + jE_B') = \frac{E_0}{2}\left[e^{-j\phi_1} - e^{-j\phi_2}\right]e^{j\omega t}$$

**Step 4 — Intensity Calculation:**

$$
P_{out} = \vert E_{out} \vert^2 = \frac{\vert E_0 \vert^2}{4}\left[2 - 2\cos(\phi_2 - \phi_1)\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Final Form:**

$$P_{out} = P_{in}\cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right), \quad \Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Explain how a Mach-Zehnder Interferometer is used as an electro-optic modulator. Derive the expression for the half-wave voltage $V_\pi$.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

In an MZI-based electro-optic modulator, electrodes are deposited on one or both arms of the interferometer. Applying a voltage V across these electrodes produces an electric field $E = V/d$ (where d is the electrode separation) that modifies the refractive index of the electro-optic substrate (typically LiNbO₃) via the **Pockels effect**:

$$\Delta n = -\frac{1}{2}n^3 r_{33} E$$

The corresponding phase shift over arm length L is:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} L \Delta n = -\frac{\pi n^3 r_{33} L}{\lambda d}V$$

By definition, the **half-wave voltage** $V_\pi$ produces a phase shift of $\pi$. Equating:

$$\pi = \frac{\pi n^3 r_{33} L}{\lambda d} V_\pi \implies V_\pi = \frac{\lambda d}{n^3 r_{33} L}$$

**Operating Point:** The modulator is biased at the quadrature point $V_{bias} = V_\pi/2$ so that the small-signal modulation $V_m \sin(\omega_m t)$ produces a linear intensity variation around the 50% transmission point.

Substituting $\Delta\phi = \pi V/V_\pi$ into the transfer function:

$$P_{out}(V) = P_{in}\cos^2\!\left(\frac{\pi V}{2V_\pi}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Mach-Zehnder Interferometer.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** The MZI consists of the following functional elements:

1. **Input port** — a single-mode optical waveguide that delivers the input optical signal.
2. **Coupler 1 (3 dB splitter)** — a directional coupler that splits the input power into two equal halves (50:50), introducing a 90° relative phase shift between them.
3. **Two waveguide arms** — typically of length 5–20 mm, fabricated on a LiNbO₃, InP, or silicon substrate. Arm B carries metal electrodes for voltage application.
4. **Coupler 2 (3 dB recombiner)** — symmetric to coupler 1, merges the two phase-shifted beams.
5. **Two output ports** — the *cross port* and the *through port*, which carry complementary signals.
6. **Electrodes and bias network** — DC bias T and RF input for modulation.

**Working Principle:**

- Light entering the MZI is equally split into arms A and B.
- The two coherent beams accumulate a phase difference $\Delta\phi = (2\pi/\lambda)(n_B L_B - n_A L_A)$.
- If $\Delta\phi = 2m\pi$ (m = 0, 1, 2, ...), **constructive interference** occurs at the through port → maximum transmission.
- If $\Delta\phi = (2m+1)\pi$, **destructive interference** occurs → minimum transmission (null).
- Varying the refractive index of arm B (via applied voltage) tunes $\Delta\phi$ continuously, enabling intensity modulation. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Discuss the major applications of MZ Interferometer in optical communication systems. Compare single-drive and dual-drive configurations.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Applications of MZI in Optical Communication:**

1. **External Optical Modulators:** Used in long-haul DWDM links to modulate laser light at 10 Gbps, 40 Gbps, 100 Gbps, and beyond with minimal chirp — superior to direct laser modulation.
2. **Optical Switches:** Acts as a 1×2 or 2×2 high-speed optical switch in reconfigurable optical networks (ROADMs).
3. **Wavelength Filters / Multiplexers:** With asymmetric arm lengths, the MZI acts as a Mach-Zehnder Interferometric filter, selecting specific ITU-grid wavelengths in WDM systems.
4. **Optical Add-Drop Multiplexers (OADMs):** Cascaded MZIs form multi-port wavelength-selective add-drop nodes.
5. **Photonic Sensors:** Measures strain, temperature, pressure, and refractive index changes via induced phase shifts.
6. **Quantum Communication:** Forms the heart of interferometric QKD systems.

**Comparison Table — Single-Drive vs Dual-Drive MZI:**

| Feature | Single-Drive MZI | Dual-Drive MZI |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| Electrodes on arms | Only one arm | Both arms |
| Applied voltages | One (V) | Two independent (V₁, V₂) |
| Chirp control | Not possible | Possible (zero-chirp, positive, negative chirp selectable) |
| Modulation formats supported | OOK, ASK | OOK, BPSK, DPSK, QAM, SSB |
| Fabrication complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Typical application | Short-reach metro links | Long-haul high-bitrate coherent links |

The dual-drive configuration offers superior performance because the two independent phase shifts enable precise control of the output field's complex envelope. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The MZI uses **two 3 dB directional couplers** and **two waveguide arms** to perform controlled two-beam interference.
- ✅ The fundamental transfer function is $\boxed{P_{out} = P_{in}\cos^2(\Delta\phi/2)}$ and its complement is $P_{out}' = P_{in}\sin^2(\Delta\phi/2)$.
- ✅ The phase difference is governed by $\boxed{\Delta\phi = (2\pi/\lambda)\, n\, \Delta L}$ geometrically or $\boxed{\Delta\phi = \pi V/V_\pi}$ electrically.
- ✅ The **half-wave voltage** $V_\pi = \lambda d / (n^3 r_{33} L)$ — inversely proportional to length L, so longer electrodes reduce drive voltage.
- ✅ **Constructive interference** ($\Delta\phi = 2m\pi$) → **ON state**; **Destructive interference** ($\Delta\phi = (2m+1)\pi$) → **OFF state**.
- ✅ A typical commercial LiNbO₃ MZI modulator has $V_\pi \approx 3\text{–}5$ V, ER $\geq$ 20 dB, and bandwidth $\geq$ 40 GHz.
- ✅ **Single-drive** MZIs are simple but chirped; **dual-drive** MZIs enable chirp-free and advanced modulation formats (QAM, DPSK).
- ✅ MZI is the **preferred external modulator** for high-bitrate, long-haul optical communication systems due to its high bandwidth and low chirp.
- ✅ Always label **bar** and **cross** output ports explicitly in your diagrams to avoid port-confusion errors.
- ✅ KTU's most frequently tested aspect is the **derivation of the transfer function** — practise the algebraic manipulation of field equations until it becomes automatic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Time Domain Reflectometer - Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optical Time Domain Reflectometer - Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Optical Time Domain Reflectometer (OTDR)** is an optoelectronic instrument used to characterize an optical fiber by injecting a series of optical pulses into the fiber under test and analyzing the backscattered and back-reflected light as a function of time. From the resulting trace (the "OTDR trace"), one can determine the fiber's length, attenuation coefficient, splice and connector losses, reflectance of reflective events, and the location and nature of faults or discontinuities along the fiber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> The OTDR is the single most important field-installable test instrument in optical communication engineering. It is the optical equivalent of a Time Domain Reflectometer (TDR) used in metallic cables. It operates on the principles of **Rayleigh back-scattering** and **Fresnel back-reflection**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Echo in a Canyon" View

Imagine you stand in a deep canyon and shout loudly. The sound wave travels down the canyon and bounces off the walls, with a tiny fraction returning to your ears as **echoes**. By measuring *how loud* each echo is and *how long* it took to return, you can infer:
- **How far** the wall is (delay → distance)
- **How hard/soft** the wall is (intensity → surface type)
- **Whether there are bends or openings** along the way (extra echoes)

The OTDR is exactly the "shouting" instrument, but with **light** instead of sound. The fiber is the canyon. Instead of shouting continuously, the OTDR sends **short, intense pulses** of laser light and listens for the tiny echoes that come back from every point along the fiber.

| Feature in the Canyon Analogy | OTDR Equivalent |
|------------------------------|------------------|
| The shout | Laser pulse injected into fiber |
| Echo returning from a wall | Rayleigh back-scatter |
| A loud, sharp echo from a glass panel | Fresnel reflection (connector, break) |
| Time delay of echo | Distance to event |
| Quieter echo due to soft wall | Higher attenuation / splice loss |
| Multiple echoes (parallel canyon) | Ghost reflections |
| A break in the wall | Fiber cut / fault |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metrics used in OTDR Specifications:**
> - **Dynamic Range:** expressed in **dB** (typically 30 – 50 dB)
> - **Pulse Width:** **ns** (3 ns to 20 µs)
> - **Wavelength:** **1310 nm** and **1550 nm** (and 1625/1650 nm for live-network testing)
> - **Distance Resolution:** **meters** (depends on pulse width and sample rate)
> - **Dead Zone:** **meters** (event dead-zone, attenuation dead-zone)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Two Physical Phenomena

The OTDR exploits **two distinct physical phenomena** that cause light traveling in a forward direction to return toward the source:

**(a) Rayleigh Back-Scattering**
- Caused by microscopic density fluctuations (compositional and structural) frozen into the glass during manufacture.
- Occurs **uniformly along the entire length** of the fiber.
- Gives a smoothly decaying "power slope" on the OTDR trace.
- The back-scattered power is proportional to the local forward-going power and the **back-scatter capture coefficient** $(\eta)$.

**(b) Fresnel Back-Reflection**
- Occurs at any **sudden change in refractive index** — connectors, mechanical splices, fiber ends, air-gaps, fiber breaks.
- Produces sharp **spikes** on the OTDR trace.
- The magnitude depends on the **reflectance** $R$ of the discontinuity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Rayleigh back-scatter level is typically **−40 dB to −50 dB** below the launched pulse level. Fresnel reflections can be as much as **−14 dB (4 %)** at a glass–air interface but are usually much smaller (≈ −40 dB to −60 dB) at well-made connectors.

### 2.2 Logical Steps of OTDR Operation (Structured)

1. **Pulse Generation** — A laser diode (LD) emits a narrow, high-peak-power optical pulse (typically 1 mW – 100 mW peak) under the control of a pulse generator.
2. **Directional Coupling** — The pulse is routed into the fiber under test via a **directional coupler** (often a 2×2 fused-fiber coupler or an integrated-optic Y-junction). The same coupler routes back-scattered/reflected light to the receiver while isolating the transmitter.
3. **Propagation & Interaction** — As the pulse travels, every differential element $dz$ of the fiber back-scatters a small fraction of the pulse power. At discontinuities, a discrete reflection is generated.
4. **Time-Gated Detection** — A high-speed **APD (Avalanche Photo-Diode)** or PIN photodiode followed by a **transimpedance + logarithmic amplifier** detects the returning weak signal. Logarithmic amplification compresses the large dynamic range (often > 35 dB) into a viewable trace.
5. **Signal Averaging** — Because the back-scattered signal is extremely weak, the trace is built up by **averaging thousands to millions of pulses** (signal-to-noise ratio improves as $\sqrt{N}$).
6. **Time-to-Distance Conversion** — The round-trip time $t$ is converted to distance $d$ using the group velocity of light in the fiber.
7. **Trace Display** — The trace is plotted as **back-scattered power (dB) versus distance (m or km)** — the familiar OTDR trace used for fault-finding.

### 2.3 Block Diagram of the OTDR (Conceptual Flow)

The instrument consists of:
- **Pulse generator + Laser driver + LD** (transmitter chain)
- **Directional coupler / circulator**
- **Fiber Under Test (FUT)**
- **Photodetector (APD) + Logarithmic amplifier**
- **Averager / DSP**
- **Display / Memory**

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas in the table below are mathematically verifiable and constitute the core "must-know" set for any KTU ESE question on OTDR.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Distance to event | $\displaystyle d \;=\; \frac{c\,t}{2\,n}$ | $c=3\times10^{8}$ m/s, $t$ = round-trip time (s), $n$ = group index (~1.468 at 1550 nm) | Factor of 2 because light makes a round trip |
| 2 | Back-scatter capture coefficient (multimode, parabolic index) | $\displaystyle \eta \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{NA}^{2}}{n^{2}}$ | $\mathrm{NA}$ = numerical aperture, $n$ = core index | Fraction of scattered light captured |
| 3 | Back-scatter capture coefficient (graded/single-mode, geometric optics form) | $\displaystyle \eta \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{\mathrm{NA}}{n}\right)^{2}$ | Same as above | Used in many textbook derivations |
| 4 | Back-scattered power from a fiber element $\Delta z$ | $\displaystyle P_{bs}(z) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,S\,\eta\,\alpha_{s}\,P_{0}\,e^{-2\alpha z}\,\Delta z$ | $S$ = recapture factor, $\alpha_{s}$ = scattering coeff (m⁻¹), $P_0$ = peak pulse power, $\alpha$ = fiber attenuation coeff (m⁻¹) | $e^{-2\alpha z}$ accounts for round-trip attenuation |
| 5 | Single-way loss between points $z_1$ and $z_2$ on trace | $\displaystyle A \;=\; \frac{P_{bs}(z_1)-P_{bs}(z_2)}{2}$ | $P_{bs}$ in linear units | Divide by 2 because trace shows **round-trip** loss |
| 6 | Trace slope → fiber attenuation | $\displaystyle \alpha \;=\; \frac{P_{1}-P_{2}}{2\,(z_{2}-z_{1})}$ dB/km | $P_1, P_2$ in dB, distance in km | Industry-standard slope method |
| 7 | Splice loss (between two fibres) | $\displaystyle L_{sp} \;=\; \frac{P_{1}-P_{2}}{2}$ (dB) | Same as above | Realised from vertical step in trace |
| 8 | Connector loss (single-direction) | $\displaystyle L_{c} \;=\; P_{b}-P_{a}$ (dB) | $P_a, P_b$ = trace levels just before/after event | Often assumes matched back-scatter coefficients |
| 9 | Reflectance of a reflective event | $\displaystyle R \;=\; -10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{r}}{P_{bs}}\right) + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{P_{p}}\right) - 2\alpha z$ | $P_r$ = reflected peak power, $P_{bs}$ = local back-scatter level, $W$ = pulse energy, $P_p$ = pulse peak power | Practical OTDR formula; manufacturer-supplied constants |
| 10 | Two-point splice loss | $\displaystyle L \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,(L_{1}+L_{2})$ when both directions measured | $L_1, L_2$ = measured losses in each direction | Bi-directional average removes gainer/spliceer ambiguity |
| 11 | Minimum detectable loss (sensitivity) | $\displaystyle L_{\min} \;=\; \tfrac{\Delta P}{2}$ (dB) | $\Delta P$ = noise floor (dB) | Sets smallest splice loss detectable |
| 12 | Number of averages for SNR | $\displaystyle \mathrm{SNR}_{N} \;=\; \mathrm{SNR}_{1}\,\sqrt{N}$ | $N$ = number of averages | Why traces take minutes to acquire |

> [!WARNING]
> **The single most common student error:** treating the vertical "drop" on an OTDR trace as the **one-way** loss. Because the light travels **to** the event and **back**, the trace shows **twice** the one-way loss. Hence the ubiquitous division by **2** in OTDR algebra.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Distance to an Event from Time Delay

A pulse of light is launched at $t = 0$. It travels a one-way distance $d$ in the fiber with group refractive index $n$ and reflects (or back-scatters) from an event at $z = d$. The reflected light returns and is detected at time $t$.

The forward-going speed of light in the medium is:

$$
v \;=\; \frac{c}{n}
$$

Hence the one-way travel time is $t_{1} = d/v = dn/c$. The round-trip time is:

$$
t \;=\; t_{1}+t_{2} \;=\; \frac{2\,d\,n}{c}
$$

Solving for $d$:

$$
\boxed{\;d \;=\; \frac{c\,t}{2\,n}\;}
$$

**Engineering Note:** For silica fiber at 1550 nm, $n \approx 1.468$. Hence, every nanosecond of round-trip delay corresponds to a one-way distance of:

$$
d \;=\; \frac{3\times10^{8}\,\mathrm{m/s}\times 10^{-9}\,\mathrm{s}}{2\times 1.468} \;\approx\; 0.1022\ \mathrm{m}
$$

Therefore, **1 µs ≈ 102 m** of fiber. This is the basis on which OTDR distance axes are calibrated.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Round-Trip Loss and the "Divide-by-2" Rule

Let $P_{0}$ be the peak power of the pulse launched at $z=0$. As the pulse propagates a distance $z$ to a generic point, the forward power decays as:

$$
P_{f}(z) \;=\; P_{0}\,e^{-\alpha z}
$$

where $\alpha$ (in m⁻¹) is the **one-way** attenuation coefficient. The back-scatter originates at this point and must propagate back to the source over the same distance $z$, suffering the same attenuation factor $e^{-\alpha z}$. Thus the back-scattered power reaching the detector is proportional to:

$$
P_{bs}(z) \;\propto\; P_{0}\,e^{-\alpha z}\,e^{-\alpha z} \;=\; P_{0}\,e^{-2\alpha z}
$$

Now consider two points $z_{1}$ and $z_{2}$ ($z_{2}>z_{1}$) on a uniform fiber. The back-scatter ratio, expressed in decibels, is:

$$
\frac{P_{bs}(z_{1})}{P_{bs}(z_{2})} \;=\; e^{\,2\alpha (z_{2}-z_{1})}
$$

In dB:

$$
10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{bs}(z_{1})}{P_{bs}(z_{2})}\right) \;=\; 10\log_{10}\!\left(e^{\,2\alpha (z_{2}-z_{1})}\right)
$$

Using $10\log_{10}(e^{x}) = 4.3429\,x$, and writing the one-way attenuation between the points as $A = 4.3429\,\alpha\,(z_{2}-z_{1})$ dB:

$$
P_{1}-P_{2} \;\text{(dB)} \;=\; 2\,A
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;A \;=\; \frac{P_{1}-P_{2}}{2}\;}
$$

This is the celebrated **divide-by-2 rule** of OTDR loss measurement. The same rule applies to splice loss, connector loss, and the attenuation coefficient $\alpha$ (in dB/km).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Back-Scatter Capture Coefficient

Consider a small cylindrical scattering volume of length $\Delta z$ inside the fiber core. Let $\alpha_{s}$ be the Rayleigh scattering coefficient (m⁻¹), so the fraction of power scattered from $\Delta z$ is $\alpha_{s}\,\Delta z$.

The scattered light radiates isotropically (uniformly in $4\pi$ steradians). Only the fraction that falls within the fiber's **acceptance cone** is recaptured and guided back toward the source. For meridional rays, the fraction of isotropic radiation that is recaptured is the ratio of the solid angle of the acceptance cone to $4\pi$:

$$
\Omega \;=\; \pi\,\theta_{a}^{2} \;=\; \pi\,\sin^{2}\theta_{a}
$$

where $\theta_{a} = \sin^{-1}(\mathrm{NA}/n)$ is the maximum acceptance angle in the core. For small angles:

$$
\sin^{2}\theta_{a} \;\approx\; \left(\frac{\mathrm{NA}}{n}\right)^{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\Omega}{4\pi} \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{\mathrm{NA}}{n}\right)^{2}
$$

A more careful derivation using the optical-fibre overlap integral (Personick, 1976) gives:

$$
\boxed{\;\eta \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{\mathrm{NA}}{n}\right)^{2}\;}
$$

for single-mode and graded-index multimode fibres. For step-index multimode fibre, $\eta \approx (\mathrm{NA}/n)^{2}$ (factor of 4 difference). In either case, $\eta$ is a small dimensionless number (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$), which explains why back-scattered power is so weak and why OTDRs need averaging.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reflectance of a Reflective Event

A reflective event (connector, mechanical splice, end of fiber) is characterized by its **reflectance** $R$, defined as:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{reflected}}}{P_{\text{incident}}}
$$

A glass–air interface (Fresnel reflection at normal incidence) has:

$$
R_{F} \;=\; \left(\frac{n_{1}-n_{2}}{n_{1}+n_{2}}\right)^{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{1.468-1.000}{1.468+1.000}\right)^{2} \;\approx\; 0.036 \;=\; 3.6\,\%
$$

Equivalently, $-10\log_{10}(0.036) \approx -14.4$ dB. This is the maximum Fresnel reflection the OTDR can register. Real connectors are polished or angle-cleaved to suppress this reflection; good APC connectors achieve $R < -65$ dB.

In OTDR software, reflectance is computed relative to the local Rayleigh back-scatter level using:

$$
\boxed{\;R \;=\; 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{r}}{P_{bs}}\right) + C - 2\alpha z\;}
$$

where $C$ is a constant accounting for pulse shape, peak power vs. pulse energy, and detection bandwidth — supplied by the OTDR manufacturer and usually entered as a "calibration constant".

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Applications to Trace Signatures

| Application | OTDR Trace Signature | Quantity Extracted |
|-------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Fiber break | Sharp drop to noise floor at distance $d$ | Distance to break |
| Splice loss | Vertical step in slope (step down = lossy; step up = "gainer") | Splice loss in dB (with ÷ 2 correction) |
| Macrobend | Localized step-down loss at the bend location | Bend loss in dB |
| Microbend | Distributed excess loss along a section | Excess attenuation |
| Connector | Reflective spike + step | Reflectance (dB) and insertion loss (dB) |
| Fiber end | Large Fresnel spike (or end of trace) | Distance to end of fiber |
| Ghost reflection | Repeating sequence of identical spikes | Multi-path reflection distance |
| Length measurement | Distance from launch pulse to end-of-fiber (or last event) | Total fiber length |
| Attenuation coefficient | Slope of linear section between events | $\alpha$ in dB/km |
| Splice quality audit | Bi-directional splice loss | True splice loss vs. apparent |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 OTDR Internal Block Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL[Control and Timing Subsystem]
        CLOCK[Clock and Pulse Generator]
        CPU[Microprocessor and DSP]
    end

    subgraph TX[Optical Transmitter Chain]
        LDDRV[Laser Driver]
        LD[Pulsed Laser Diode 1310 or 1550 nm]
    end

    subgraph OPT[Optical Coupling Section]
        CPL[Directional Coupler or Circulator]
        FUT((Fiber Under Test))
    end

    subgraph RX[Optical Receiver Chain]
        APD[APD Photodetector]
        AMP[Transimpedance and Logarithmic Amplifier]
        AVG[Signal Averager and Memory]
    end

    DISP[Display, OTDR Trace, dB versus km]

    CLOCK --> LDDRV
    LDDRV --> LD
    LD --> CPL
    CPL --> FUT
    FUT -- back-scatter and reflections --> CPL
    CPL --> APD
    APD --> AMP
    AMP --> AVG
    AVG --> DISP
    CLOCK --> AVG
    CPU --> CLOCK
    CPU --> DISP
```

### 4.2 Typical OTDR Trace — Event Map (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Launch Pulse 0 km] --> B[Linear slope section, fiber attenuation alpha]
    B --> C{Event 1, Connector}
    C --> D[Linear slope section continues]
    D --> E{Event 2, Fusion Splice}
    E --> F[Linear slope section]
    F --> G{Event 3, Macrobend, localized step down}
    G --> H[Linear slope section]
    H --> I{Event 4, Fiber Break or End, large Fresnel spike and noise floor}
```

### 4.3 Event Classification Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Power level change detected?] -->|Yes| Q{Is there a spike?}
    Q -->|Yes, sharp| R[Reflective Event: Connector, Mechanical Splice, Fiber End, or Break]
    Q -->|No, only step| S[Non-Reflective Event: Fusion Splice or Macrobend]
    P -->|No, only slope change| T[Distributed Anomaly: Microbending or Aging]
    R --> R1[Measure Reflectance R in dB]
    R --> R2[Measure Distance to Event d in km]
    S --> S1[Measure Step Height divided by 2, Splice Loss in dB]
    T --> T1[Compute Slope Change, Excess Attenuation per km]
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow — From Pulse Launch to Trace (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
```
