```mermaid
    Idle --> LaunchPulse: Trigger from Clock
    LaunchPulse --> PropagateInFiber: Pulse enters FUT
    PropagateInFiber --> ReceiveBackscatter: Continuous Rayleigh return
    PropagateInFiber --> ReceiveReflection: Fresnel event
    ReceiveBackscatter --> LogarithmicAmplify
    ReceiveReflection --> LogarithmicAmplify
    LogarithmicAmplify --> SampleAndHold
    SampleAndHold --> AverageOverN: Repeat for N pulses
    AverageOverN --> PlotTrace
    PlotTrace --> [*]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. Define OTDR and list its major applications.**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Optical Time Domain Reflectometer (OTDR) is an optoelectronic instrument that injects short optical pulses into an optical fiber and measures the Rayleigh back-scattered and Fresnel-reflected light as a function of time, producing a trace of back-scattered power versus distance.

**Major Applications (any 4 expected for 3 marks):**
1. **Fault/break location** — pinpointing the exact distance to a fiber cut or bend.
2. **Splice-loss measurement** — quantifying the loss introduced by fusion or mechanical splices.
3. **Connector-loss and reflectance measurement** — evaluating mated connector pairs.
4. **Fiber attenuation coefficient ($\alpha$) measurement** — dB/km using the slope method.
5. **Fiber-length measurement** — total length of installed cable.
6. **Network characterization for acceptance testing** — documentation of newly laid cables.
7. **Maintenance and troubleshooting** of live or dark optical networks.

---

#### **Q2. Distinguish between Rayleigh back-scattering and Fresnel reflection as used in OTDR.**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Rayleigh Back-Scattering | Fresnel Reflection |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Origin** | Microscopic refractive-index fluctuations in the glass | Sudden change of refractive index (glass–air, connector, splice) |
| **Spatial distribution** | Continuous along entire fiber length | Localised at discrete events |
| **Magnitude on trace** | Smooth power slope, $\sim -40$ to $-50$ dB below launched pulse | Sharp spike, up to $\sim -14$ dB (4 % of incident) |
| **Information provided** | Fiber attenuation, splice loss, fiber length | Event location, event reflectance, connector quality |
| **Reflectance value** | Distributed; characterized by $\eta$ capture coefficient | Discrete $R$ at a single point |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 marks):**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Explain the working principle of an OTDR with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the distance to a back-scattering event. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss any **five major applications** of the OTDR in optical fiber communication systems with the trace signature observed in each case. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Working Principle and Distance Formula (7 marks):**

The OTDR consists of a **pulsed laser source** (LD + driver), a **directional coupler** (or optical circulator), a **photodetector (APD)** with logarithmic amplifier, a **signal averager**, and a **display unit**. A short optical pulse of width $\tau$ (typically 3 ns – 20 µs) is launched into the Fiber Under Test (FUT). As it propagates, two phenomena send a small fraction of the light back toward the source:

1. **Rayleigh back-scattering** from every infinitesimal element of the fiber (continuous).
2. **Fresnel back-reflection** at discontinuities (connectors, splices, breaks, end-of-fiber).

The returning light is routed by the directional coupler to the APD. Because the back-scattered signal is very weak (∼ 40–50 dB below the launch), the receiver uses **logarithmic amplification** and **extensive averaging** ($N$ pulses) to compress the large dynamic range. The averaged signal is plotted against time, then converted to distance using:

$$
d \;=\; \frac{c\,t}{2\,n}
$$

where $c$ = velocity of light in vacuum, $n$ = group refractive index of the fiber ($\approx 1.468$ at 1550 nm), and $t$ = round-trip delay between launch pulse and event echo. The factor of 2 arises because light traverses the event distance twice.

**Block diagram (to be drawn on answer script):**

Pulse Generator → Laser Diode → Coupler → Fiber Under Test → Coupler → APD → Logarithmic Amplifier → Averager → Display. *(Match with Section 4.1 diagram.)*

**(b) Five Major Applications (7 marks):**

| # | Application | Trace Signature |
|---|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Fiber fault / break location** | Sharp drop to noise floor at distance $d$. Used by maintenance crews to dig/excavate at the right spot. |
| 2 | **Splice-loss measurement** | Step change (down) in the back-scatter slope. Loss $= \tfrac{1}{2}(P_1 - P_2)$. Negative step ("gainer") indicates mode-field-diameter mismatch, not actual gain. |
| 3 | **Connector reflectance and loss** | Reflective spike (Fresnel) at connector position; step indicates insertion loss. Used to certify connector quality per TIA-568 / IEC 61300. |
| 4 | **Fiber attenuation coefficient $\alpha$** | Linear slope between events; $\alpha = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{2(z_2 - z_1)}$ dB/km. Used during cable acceptance testing. |
| 5 | **Fiber length measurement** | Distance from launch pulse to last event or end-of-fiber; provides physical length of installed cable. |

*Optional 6th and 7th applications (for extra credit):*
6. **Macrobend / microbend detection** — localised step or distributed excess loss.
7. **Ghost-reflection identification** — secondary echoes from double-reflection paths.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks):**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** With the help of a schematic diagram, describe the construction and operation of an OTDR. Explain Rayleigh back-scattering and derive an expression for the back-scatter coefficient $\eta$. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain in detail how an OTDR is used for: (i) fault localization in a long-haul link, (ii) splice-loss measurement, and (iii) measurement of fiber attenuation coefficient. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Construction, Operation, and Back-Scatter Coefficient (7 marks):**

**Construction:** The OTDR comprises a **pulse generator**, **laser diode source** (1310 nm / 1550 nm), a **directional coupler** (or optical circulator), a **high-sensitivity APD receiver**, a **logarithmic amplifier**, an **averager / DSP unit**, and a **display**.

**Operation:** A short optical pulse is injected via the coupler into the FUT. Light returns from every point along the fiber due to **Rayleigh back-scattering** (continuous, distributed) and **Fresnel reflection** (discrete, localised). The returning signal is detected, log-amplified, sampled, and averaged. The averaged samples plotted against round-trip time constitute the OTDR trace, whose horizontal axis is converted to distance.

**Derivation of $\eta$:**

Consider a scattering volume of length $\Delta z$. The fraction of forward power back-scattered into all directions is $\alpha_s \Delta z$. Of this isotropic scatter, only the fraction guided by the fiber core reaches the source — the **capture fraction** $\eta$. For meridional rays with numerical aperture NA and core index $n$:

$$
\sin\theta_a \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{NA}}{n}
$$

The solid angle of the acceptance cone is $\Omega = \pi\sin^{2}\theta_a$. The fraction captured is $\Omega/(4\pi)$:

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{\mathrm{NA}}{n}\right)^{2}
$$

This is the **back-scatter capture coefficient** — typically $\sim 10^{-3}$ for SMF-28 fibre at 1550 nm.

**(b) Three Specific Applications (7 marks):**

**(i) Fault Localization (3 marks):**
For a fiber break at distance $d$, the OTDR trace shows a smooth Rayleigh slope until $z = d$, followed by a sharp drop to the noise floor. The distance is read directly from the trace:

$$
d \;=\; \frac{c\,t_{\text{break}}}{2n}
$$

This allows precise pinpointing to within ±1 m on modern units, drastically reducing repair time and cost.

**(ii) Splice-Loss Measurement (2 marks):**
A fusion splice appears as a step discontinuity. Reading trace levels $P_1$ (just before) and $P_2$ (just after) in dB:

$$
L_{\text{splice}} \;=\; \frac{P_1 - P_2}{2} \ \text{dB}
$$

For greater accuracy, bi-directional measurement is performed; the true splice loss is the average $L = (L_1 + L_2)/2$, which eliminates the **gainer/spliceer ambiguity** caused by mode-field-diameter mismatch.

**(iii) Attenuation Coefficient (2 marks):**
Between two markers $z_1$ and $z_2$ placed in a clean linear section, the trace shows a constant negative slope:

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{P_1 - P_2}{2(z_2 - z_1)} \ \text{dB/km}
$$

where $P_1, P_2$ are in dB and distances are in km. This is the **LSA (Least-Squares Approximation)** or **two-point** method used during cable acceptance testing.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on OTDR questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in distance ($d = ct/2n$, not $ct/n$) or in loss (trace slope shows round-trip loss).
> 2. **Confusing Rayleigh back-scatter with Fresnel reflection** — they are distinct phenomena and must be tabulated.
> 3. **Calling "gainer" splices "amplifying"** — a "gainer" trace artefact arises from MFD mismatch between two fibres; it disappears in bi-directional measurement. The student must explicitly mention this.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram** in the working-principle question — KTU examiners award **at least 2 marks** for a clean block diagram with all blocks labelled.
> 5. **Writing "OTDR measures splice loss directly"** — it measures the *change in back-scatter level*; the loss is computed by *dividing by 2*. Always show the derivation.
> 6. **Forgetting units** — distances in km, loss in dB, $\alpha$ in dB/km.
> 7. **Not naming the phenomena (Rayleigh / Fresnel)** explicitly in definitions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OTDR** = Optical Time Domain Reflectometer; the indispensable field instrument for fiber characterization.
- Two physical phenomena: **Rayleigh back-scattering** (distributed) and **Fresnel reflection** (discrete).
- **Distance formula:** $d = ct/(2n)$ — remember the factor of 2 for round-trip.
- **Divide-by-2 rule:** every vertical step on the trace represents **twice** the one-way loss.
- **Attenuation coefficient:** $\alpha = (P_1 - P_2)/(2(z_2-z_1))$ dB/km from trace slope.
- **Back-scatter capture coefficient:** $\eta = \tfrac{1}{4}(\mathrm{NA}/n)^{2}$ — small (≈10⁻³), which is why back-scattered power is weak.
- **Reflectance of glass–air interface:** $R \approx 3.6\,\%$ ($\approx -14.4$ dB).
- **Dynamic range** (dB) determines longest measurable fiber; **pulse width** controls trade-off between range and resolution.
- **Dead zones** (event & attenuation) limit how close two events can be and still be resolved — narrower pulses ⇒ smaller dead zones.
- **Applications:** fault location, splice loss, connector loss, fiber length, attenuation coefficient, macrobend/microbend detection, network acceptance testing, maintenance troubleshooting, ghost-reflection identification.
- **Two wavelength testing:** 1310 nm (loss dominated by scattering) and 1550 nm (loss dominated by bending) — comparing the two reveals bending losses.
- **Live-fiber testing:** 1625 / 1650 nm filtered OTDRs allow in-service testing without disturbing traffic.
- **Bi-directional averaging** is the only way to get the *true* splice loss — never trust a single-direction "gainer".
- Always state the **OTDR wavelength** in the answer; KTU examiners look for this detail.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LEDs and LDs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LEDs and LDs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs)

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is a forward-biased p-n junction semiconductor device that converts electrical current into spontaneous incoherent optical radiation through the process of **electroluminescence**. In optical communication, LEDs are used as optical sources operating typically in the **850 nm** and **1310 nm** spectral windows with a spectral width of **30–100 nm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LEDs are considered *incoherent* sources because the emitted photons have random phase, direction, and frequency. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Firefly Bucket"
Imagine thousands of tiny fireflies trapped inside a translucent box. Each firefly (electron-hole pair) glows independently when stimulated, but they blink at random times and in random directions. The total light is bright but scattered and unfocused. This is exactly how an LED works — many spontaneous emission events, no phase relationship, and the output is spatially and spectrally broad.

---

## 1.2 Laser Diodes (LDs)

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Laser Diode (LD)**, also called a *semiconductor laser* or *injection laser*, is a forward-biased p-n junction device that produces **stimulated emission** of coherent, monochromatic, and highly directional light through population inversion and optical feedback provided by a Fabry–Pérot cavity (or distributed feedback structure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The acronym **LASER** stands for **Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation**. The three pillars of laser action are: **(i) Population Inversion**, **(ii) Optical Feedback**, and **(iii) Threshold Condition**.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Synchronized Marching Band"
Now imagine replacing those random fireflies with a marching band. Every musician (photon) is told exactly when to step, in which direction, and at what beat. The result is a powerful, focused, in-phase beam of light — this is laser emission. A small "starter" (the seed photon) triggers a chain reaction (stimulated emission), and mirrors at both ends (the cavity) bounce the beam back and forth to amplify it.

---

## 1.3 LED vs. LD: Quick Comparison Snapshot

| Parameter | LED | Laser Diode |
|---|---|---|
| Emission Type | Spontaneous | Stimulated |
| Coherence | Incoherent | Coherent |
| Spectral Width | 30–100 nm | 0.1–5 nm |
| Output Power | Low (mW) | High (tens of mW) |
| Modulation Bandwidth | < 200 MHz | Up to tens of GHz |
| Threshold Current | None | Required |
| Beam Divergence | Wide (30°–50°) | Narrow (5°–10°) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |

> [!NOTE]
> For short-distance, cost-sensitive links (e.g., LANs, PONs), **LEDs** dominate. For long-haul, high-bit-rate links (e.g., 10 Gbps, 100 Gbps backbone), **LDs** are mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LED — Operational Principle

### 2.1.1 The Recombination Process
When the p-n junction is forward biased with voltage $V_f \geq E_g / e$ (where $E_g$ is the bandgap energy and $e$ is the electron charge), electrons from the n-region and holes from the p-region flood into the **depletion (active) region**. They recombine radiatively, releasing a photon of energy:

$$h\nu = E_g \quad \text{(ideal case)}$$

The emitted wavelength is given by the **peak emission wavelength**:

$$\lambda_p = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1.24 \; \mu\text{m·eV}}{E_g \text{ (eV)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a typical InGaAsP LED used in fiber optics, $E_g \approx 0.96$ eV gives $\lambda \approx 1310$ nm. This is the bandgap-wavelength relationship you **must memorize** for KTU derivations.

### 2.1.2 Internal Quantum Efficiency
Not every electron produces a photon. Some recombine non-radiatively (heat, defects, Auger recombination). The **internal quantum efficiency** is:

$$\eta_{int} = \frac{\text{Rate of radiative recombination}}{\text{Total recombination rate}} = \frac{R_r}{R_r + R_{nr}}$$

### 2.1.3 External Quantum Efficiency
Only a fraction of generated photons escape the semiconductor due to total internal reflection (high refractive index of semiconductor). The **external quantum efficiency** accounts for this:

$$\eta_{ext} = \eta_{int} \cdot \frac{1}{4n^2} \cdot \left(1 - \cos\theta_c\right)$$

where $n$ is the refractive index of the semiconductor and $\theta_c$ is the critical angle.

### 2.1.4 Output Optical Power
The optical power emitted by an LED is:

$$P_{out} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \frac{h\nu}{e} \cdot I$$

where $I$ is the forward current. This is a **linear** relationship (one of the LED's key advantages).

---

## 2.2 LED Structures

### 2.2.1 Surface-Emitting LED (SLED / Burrus LED)
- Light is emitted perpendicular to the junction plane.
- Has a well etched in the substrate to allow fiber coupling from the top.
- Large emission area, isotropic pattern.
- **Coupling efficiency** to a multimode fiber is typically **1–2%**.

### 2.2.2 Edge-Emitting LED (ELED)
- Light is emitted from the edge of the junction (parallel to it).
- Uses a striped geometry to confine the beam.
- Better coupling to fiber (5–10%) and higher modulation bandwidth.
- Emission is more directional (Lambertian-like in the horizontal plane).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite:** "Compare SLED and ELED" is a classic 7-mark question. Memorize: *ELED > SLED in bandwidth and coupling efficiency, but SLED is cheaper to fabricate.*

---

## 2.3 Laser Diode — Operational Principle

### 2.3.1 Three Key Conditions for Lasing

1. **Population Inversion:** $N \geq N_{th}$ — more electrons in the conduction band than in the valence band. Achieved by heavy forward bias (current $I \geq I_{th}$).

2. **Optical Feedback:** Provided by cleaved crystal facets acting as mirrors. The reflectivity $R$ of each facet is:

$$R = \left(\frac{n-1}{n+1}\right)^2$$

For GaAs ($n = 3.6$), $R \approx 0.32$. This forms a **Fabry–Pérot cavity**.

3. **Threshold Condition:** The round-trip gain must equal the round-trip loss:

$$G_{th} = \alpha_{int} + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

where $L$ is cavity length, $\alpha_{int}$ is internal loss, and $R_1, R_2$ are facet reflectivities.

### 2.3.2 Output Power vs. Current (P–I) Characteristic

Below threshold ($I < I_{th}$): LED-like spontaneous emission, low power, broad spectrum.
Above threshold ($I > I_{th}$): stimulated emission dominates, power rises steeply:

$$P_{out} = \eta_d \cdot \frac{h\nu}{e} \cdot (I - I_{th})$$

where $\eta_d$ is the *external differential quantum efficiency*.

### 2.3.3 Threshold Current
The minimum current required to initiate lasing:

$$I_{th} = \frac{q V N_{th}}{\tau_{sp}}$$

A key practical figure of merit is the **characteristic temperature** $T_0$:

$$I_{th}(T) = I_{th}(T_0) \cdot \exp\left(\frac{T - T_0}{T_0}\right)$$

Higher $T_0$ means better temperature stability.

---

## 2.4 Advanced LD Structures

### 2.4.1 Fabry–Pérot (FP) Laser
- Simplest structure: cleaved facets form cavity.
- **Multi-longitudinal mode** operation — multiple wavelengths oscillate simultaneously.
- Spectral width: 1–5 nm. Suitable for moderate-bit-rate systems.

### 2.4.2 Distributed Feedback (DFB) Laser
- A Bragg grating is etched into the active region.
- **Single longitudinal mode** operation — one wavelength dominates.
- Spectral width: < 0.1 nm. Essential for **DWDM** systems (100 GHz / 50 GHz channel spacing).

### 2.4.3 Distributed Bragg Reflector (DBR) Laser
- Bragg gratings at the cavity ends, active region in between.
- Combines tunability with single-mode operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFB lasers are the workhorse of modern high-speed optical communication systems** (10 Gbps, 40 Gbps, 100 Gbps). The Bragg condition is:
> $$\lambda_{Bragg} = \frac{2 n_{eff} \Lambda}{m}$$
> where $\Lambda$ is the grating period, $n_{eff}$ is effective index, $m$ is order (typically 1).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda_p = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g\text{(eV)}}$ | Peak emission wavelength | $\mu$m |
| 2 | $\eta_{ext} = \eta_{int} \cdot \dfrac{1}{4n^2}(1 - \cos\theta_c)$ | External quantum efficiency | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P_{out} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{h\nu}{e} \cdot I$ | LED output power | W |
| 4 | $R = \left(\dfrac{n-1}{n+1}\right)^2$ | Facet reflectivity | dimensionless |
| 5 | $G_{th} = \alpha_{int} + \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$ | Threshold gain | cm$^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $P_{out} = \eta_d \cdot \dfrac{h\nu}{e} \cdot (I - I_{th})$ | LD output above threshold | W |
| 7 | $\lambda_{Bragg} = \dfrac{2 n_{eff} \Lambda}{m}$ | Bragg wavelength of DFB/DBR | m |
| 8 | $\Delta\lambda_{FP} = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{2nL}$ | FP mode spacing | nm |
| 9 | $\eta_d = \dfrac{\ln(1/\sqrt{R_1 R_2})}{2\alpha_{int} L + \ln(1/\sqrt{R_1 R_2})}$ | External differential efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $I_{th}(T) = I_{th}(T_0) \exp\!\left(\dfrac{T - T_0}{T_0}\right)$ | Threshold vs. temperature | A |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LEDs**: Used in 1 Gbps Ethernet (1000BASE-SX), optical mice, short-reach interconnects, and **PON (Passive Optical Network)** downstream links like GPON and XG-PON.
- **FP Lasers**: Used in 1–2.5 Gbps links, CD/DVD pickup heads, and low-cost short-haul systems.
- **DFB Lasers**: Backbone of metro and long-haul WDM/DWDM networks; CATV distribution; coherent 100G/400G transponders.
- **Tunable DBR Lasers**: Reconfigurable optical add-drop multiplexers (ROADMs) in software-defined networks (SDN).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Peak Emission Wavelength from Bandgap Energy

We want to show that the peak wavelength of emission is determined by the semiconductor bandgap.

**Step 1 — Energy of a photon:**
The energy of a single emitted photon is:
$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — In a direct bandgap semiconductor, electron-hole recombination releases energy equal to $E_g$:**
$$E_{photon} = E_g$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for $\lambda$:**
$$\frac{hc}{\lambda_p} = E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_p = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute constants in convenient units:**
$h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.

$$\lambda_p (\mu m) = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot E_g (\text{eV})} \cdot 10^6$$

$$\lambda_p (\mu m) = \frac{1.2408}{E_g (\text{eV})}$$

**Step 5 — For InGaAsP at 1310 nm, find bandgap:**
$$E_g = \frac{1.2408}{1.310} = 0.947 \text{ eV} \approx 0.95 \text{ eV}$$

> This result (≈ 0.95 eV) is what you'd expect for a quaternary InGaAsP alloy tuned for the second telecommunications window.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Threshold Gain Condition in a Fabry–Pérot Laser

A photon of intensity $I_0$ traveling through a cavity of length $L$ experiences:
- Gain $G$ per unit length (amplification).
- Internal loss $\alpha_{int}$ per unit length (absorption, scattering).

**Step 1 — Intensity after one pass:**
$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{(G - \alpha_{int})L}$$

**Step 2 — Reflection at each facet (multiplicative factors $R_1$ and $R_2$):**
$$I_{round-trip} = I_0 \cdot e^{(G - \alpha_{int})2L} \cdot R_1 R_2$$

**Step 3 — For sustained oscillation (lasing threshold), round-trip intensity must equal initial intensity:**
$$1 = e^{(G_{th} - \alpha_{int})2L} \cdot R_1 R_2$$

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**
$$0 = 2L(G_{th} - \alpha_{int}) + \ln(R_1 R_2)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to solve for $G_{th}$:**
$$G_{th} = \alpha_{int} + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

> This is the **fundamental lasing threshold equation**. The term $\frac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1 R_2)$ is called the *mirror loss* $\alpha_m$. So: $G_{th} = \alpha_{int} + \alpha_m$.

**Numerical Example:**
For a GaAs laser with $L = 300\,\mu$m, $R_1 = R_2 = 0.32$, $\alpha_{int} = 10$ cm$^{-1}$:
$$\alpha_m = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.03}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{(0.32)^2}\right) = \frac{1}{0.06}\ln(9.77) = 37.93 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$
$$G_{th} = 10 + 37.93 = 47.93 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: External Differential Quantum Efficiency

The external differential efficiency $\eta_d$ is defined as the slope of the P–I curve above threshold:

$$\eta_d = \frac{dP_{out}/dI}{(h\nu/e)} = \frac{\text{slope of P-I curve}}{h\nu/e}$$

**Step 1 — Consider the photon flux generated per second inside the cavity:**
Photon generation rate = $\eta_i (I - I_{th}) / e$ where $\eta_i$ is the internal efficiency (assume ≈ 1 for simplicity).

**Step 2 — The fraction that escapes through both mirrors vs. fraction lost to internal absorption:**
$$\eta_d = \eta_i \cdot \frac{\alpha_m}{\alpha_{int} + \alpha_m}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $\alpha_m$:**
$$\eta_d = \eta_i \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1 R_2)}{\alpha_{int} + \frac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1 R_2)}$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging (and dropping $\eta_i$ for brevity):**
$$\eta_d = \frac{\ln(1/\sqrt{R_1 R_2})}{2\alpha_{int} L + \ln(1/\sqrt{R_1 R_2})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** $\eta_d$ approaches 1 when mirror loss dominates internal loss (short cavity, high-reflectivity facets), and approaches 0 when internal loss dominates (long cavity, poor material quality).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Longitudinal Mode Spacing in FP Laser

A Fabry–Pérot cavity supports standing waves when an integer number of half-wavelengths fit in the optical path:

$$m\lambda = 2nL$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate both sides with respect to mode number $m$ (treating $\lambda$ as the variable):**
$$\lambda = 2nL/m \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = 2nL/\lambda$$

**Step 2 — Mode spacing $\Delta\lambda$ between adjacent modes (m and m+1):**
$$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_{m+1} - \lambda_m = \frac{2nL}{m} - \frac{2nL}{m+1} = \frac{2nL}{m(m+1)} \approx \frac{2nL}{m^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $m \approx 2nL/\lambda$ back:**
$$\Delta\lambda \approx \frac{2nL}{(2nL/\lambda)^2} = \frac{\lambda^2}{2nL}$$

> **Numerical Example:** For a 1310 nm FP laser with $n = 3.5$, $L = 300\,\mu$m:
> $$\Delta\lambda = \frac{(1.31)^2}{2(3.5)(0.0003)} = \frac{1.7161}{0.0021} \approx 817 \text{ pm} = 0.817 \text{ nm}$$
> This means modes are spaced by less than 1 nm — explaining why FP lasers have a *comb* of wavelengths in their output.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Application Scenario | Best Source | Why |
|---|---|---|
| 100 Mbps, 50 m LAN | LED | Cheapest, sufficient bandwidth |
| 1 Gbps, 500 m LAN | LED (1300 nm) or VCSEL | Multimode fiber, cost-effective |
| 10 Gbps, 10 km | DFB Laser | High bandwidth, low dispersion |
| 100 Gbps coherent, 1000 km | External Modulated Laser (EML) | Narrow linewidth, stable wavelength |
| CWDM (18 channels) | DFB laser | One per wavelength channel |
| DWDM (80+ channels, 50 GHz spacing) | DFB or EML | Tight spectral packing |
| Analog CATV (RF over fiber) | DFB + external modulator | High linearity required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: LED Operational Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forward Bias Voltage Source] --> B[P-N Junction Active Region]
    B --> C[Electron-Hole Injection]
    C --> D[Radiative Recombination]
    D --> E[Isotropic Photon Emission]
    E --> F[Critical Angle Filtering]
    F --> G[Optical Fiber Coupling]
    H[Heat Sink] --> B
    I[Current Driver] --> A
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The current driver supplies a forward-bias voltage across the LED. Charge carriers flood the active region where they recombine radiatively. Most emitted photons are trapped by total internal reflection; only those within the escape cone (defined by the critical angle) are coupled into the fiber. The heat sink is critical because LED efficiency drops with temperature.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Laser Diode Operational Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current Driver] --> B[Above Threshold Bias I ≥ I_th]
    B --> C[Active Region Population Inversion]
    C --> D[Stimulated Emission Cascade]
    D --> E[Fabry-Perot Cavity Resonance]
    F[Cleaved Facet Mirror 1] --> E
    G[Cleaved Facet Mirror 2] --> E
    E --> H[Coherent Optical Output]
    H --> I[Single-mode or Multi-mode Beam]
    J[Thermoelectric Cooler] --> C
    K[Optical Isolator] --> I
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The current must exceed $I_{th}$ to achieve population inversion. Within the cavity, photons bounce between the two cleaved facets, triggering stimulated emission on each pass. The optical output is coherent and highly directional. The TEC stabilizes temperature (since $I_{th}$ varies with T), and the isolator prevents back-reflections from destabilizing the cavity.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Comparison of Source Emission Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electron in Conduction Band] -->|Spontaneous| B[LED Output]
    A -->|Stimulated Emission| C[Laser Output]
    D[Photon Stimulus] --> A
    B --> E[Random Phase, Broad Spectrum]
    C --> F[Identical Phase, Narrow Spectrum]
    E --> G[Incoherent Source]
    F --> H[Coherent Source]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: DFB Laser Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Left Bragg Grating] --> B[Active Gain Region]
    B --> C[Right Bragg Grating]
    C --> D[Phase Section Optional]
    D --> E[Output Facet]
    B --> F[Single Longitudinal Mode Wavelength]
    F --> E
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The Bragg gratings act as wavelength-selective mirrors. Only the wavelength satisfying $\lambda_{Bragg} = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda / m$ is reflected constructively, forcing single-mode operation. A phase section can fine-tune the exact emission wavelength.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: LED Structure Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LED Source] --> B[Surface Emitter SLED]
    A --> C[Edge Emitter ELED]
    B --> B1[Light emitted perpendicular to junction]
    B --> B2[Large emission area]
    B --> B3[Lambertian pattern]
    C --> C1[Light emitted parallel to junction]
    C --> C2[Striped geometry]
    C --> C3[Directional beam, higher bandwidth]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Differentiate between spontaneous and stimulated emission. Which mechanism is responsible for LED and LD operation?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Spontaneous Emission:** An electron in the excited state drops to a lower energy state *randomly* without any external stimulus. The emitted photon has *random phase, direction, and polarization*. This is the dominant mechanism in **LEDs**.

- **Stimulated Emission:** An incoming photon of energy $h\nu = E_g$ triggers an excited electron to drop to a lower state, emitting a *second photon identical* in phase, frequency, direction, and polarization. This is the mechanism behind **Laser Diodes** and *requires population inversion*.

> Spontaneous emission is fundamentally random and incoherent; stimulated emission is coherent and amplifies the input field.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Define external quantum efficiency of an LED. Why is it much lower than internal quantum efficiency?**

**Model Answer:**

The **external quantum efficiency** is the ratio of photons emitted into free space (or coupled to fiber) to the number of electrons injected into the device.

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{\text{Number of photons emitted out of the device per second}}{\text{Number of electrons injected per second}}$$

**Why it's low:** The semiconductor has a high refractive index ($n \approx 3.5$ for GaAs). The critical angle $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(1/n) \approx 17°$ is very small, so by Snell's law, only a tiny cone of light (about 2% for GaAs) can escape the semiconductor-air interface. The rest is trapped by total internal reflection and reabsorbed.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a **Fabry–Pérot semiconductor laser diode**. Derive the threshold condition for lasing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GaAs laser diode has cavity length $L = 400\,\mu$m, facet reflectivities $R_1 = 0.32$, $R_2 = 0.32$, and internal loss $\alpha_{int} = 15$ cm$^{-1}$. Calculate:
  (i) Threshold gain $G_{th}$
  (ii) External differential quantum efficiency $\eta_d$
  (iii) Output power at $I = 2I_{th}$ (take $I_{th} = 50$ mA, $h\nu = 1.42$ eV)
  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle & Threshold Derivation (7 marks)**

The FP laser consists of a p-n junction with cleaved facets acting as mirrors. Under heavy forward bias above $I_{th}$:

1. Electrons and holes flood the **active (depletion) region**, achieving **population inversion**.
2. A spontaneously emitted photon of energy $h\nu = E_g$ triggers **stimulated emission**, producing a clone photon.
3. Photons travel along the cavity, amplified by gain $G$ and attenuated by internal loss $\alpha_{int}$.
4. At each facet, fraction $R_1$ and $R_2$ are reflected, providing **positive feedback**.
5. Lasing begins when round-trip gain equals round-trip loss:

$$\boxed{G_{th} = \alpha_{int} + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)}$$

The output is coherent, monochromatic, and emerges from one of the partially transmitting facets.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $L = 400\,\mu\text{m} = 0.04$ cm, $R_1 = R_2 = 0.32$, $\alpha_{int} = 15$ cm$^{-1}$, $I_{th} = 50$ mA, $h\nu = 1.42$ eV.

**(i) Threshold gain:**
$$G_{th} = 15 + \frac{1}{2 \times 0.04}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{(0.32)^2}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.1024}\right) = \ln(9.766) = 2.279$$

$$G_{th} = 15 + \frac{2.279}{0.08} = 15 + 28.49 = 43.49 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**(ii) External differential efficiency:**
$$\eta_d = \frac{\ln(1/\sqrt{R_1 R_2})}{2\alpha_{int} L + \ln(1/\sqrt{R_1 R_2})}$$

$$\sqrt{R_1 R_2} = 0.32, \quad \ln(1/0.32) = \ln(3.125) = 1.139$$

$$2\alpha_{int} L = 2 \times 15 \times 0.04 = 1.2$$

$$\eta_d = \frac{1.139}{1.2 + 1.139} = \frac{1.139}{2.339} = 0.487 = 48.7\%$$

**(iii) Output power at $I = 2I_{th} = 100$ mA:**
$$P_{out} = \eta_d \cdot \frac{h\nu}{e} \cdot (I - I_{th})$$

$$\frac{h\nu}{e} = 1.42 \text{ V}$$

$$P_{out} = 0.487 \times 1.42 \times (0.100 - 0.050) = 0.487 \times 1.42 \times 0.050$$

$$P_{out} = 0.0346 \text{ W} = 34.6 \text{ mW}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the structure and performance of **surface-emitting and edge-emitting LEDs**. Which is preferred for optical communication and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LED has bandgap energy $E_g = 1.42$ eV (GaAs), internal quantum efficiency $\eta_{int} = 0.7$, refractive index $n = 3.6$, and is driven by $I = 100$ mA. Calculate:
  (i) Peak emission wavelength
  (ii) Critical angle
  (iii) External quantum efficiency
  (iv) Optical output power
  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks)**

| Parameter | SLED | ELED |
|---|---|---|
| Emission direction | Perpendicular to junction | Parallel to junction (from edge) |
| Beam pattern | Lambertian (cos θ) | Directional, lower divergence |
| Active area | Larger | Smaller (striped) |
| Coupling efficiency to MMF | 1–2% | 5–10% |
| Modulation bandwidth | Lower (~50 MHz) | Higher (~200 MHz) |
| Fabrication | Simple | More complex |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

**Preferred for optical communication:** **ELEDs** are preferred because of their higher modulation bandwidth (essential for high bit rates) and better fiber coupling efficiency. SLEDs are used in low-cost, low-speed applications.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $E_g = 1.42$ eV, $\eta_{int} = 0.7$, $n = 3.6$, $I = 100$ mA.

**(i) Peak emission wavelength:**
$$\lambda_p = \frac{1.24}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{1.42} = 0.873 \,\mu\text{m} = 873 \text{ nm}$$

**(ii) Critical angle:**
$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{3.6}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.2778) = 16.13°$$

**(iii) External quantum efficiency:**
$$\eta_{ext} = \eta_{int} \cdot \frac{1}{4n^2}(1 - \cos\theta_c)$$

$$4n^2 = 4(3.6)^2 = 51.84$$

$$\cos(16.13°) = 0.9606 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - 0.9606 = 0.0394$$

$$\eta_{ext} = 0.7 \times \frac{0.0394}{51.84} = 0.7 \times 7.6 \times 10^{-4} = 5.32 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0532\%$$

**(iv) Output power:**
$$\frac{h\nu}{e} = E_g = 1.42 \text{ V}$$

$$P_{out} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \frac{h\nu}{e} \cdot I = 5.32 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.42 \times 0.100$$

$$P_{out} = 7.55 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 75.5 \,\mu\text{W}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in $\lambda_p$ calculation:** The formula $\lambda_p = 1.24/E_g$ gives $\mu$m **only** when $E_g$ is in eV. Writing nm without conversion loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $G_{th}$ with $\eta_d$:** Students often mix the threshold gain equation with the efficiency equation. Memorize: $G_{th}$ uses $\ln(1/R_1 R_2)$ and **only** in the denominator format.
> 3. **Using $I$ instead of $(I - I_{th})$** in the laser power formula above threshold. This is the most common error.
> 4. **Ignoring $\eta_d$ in $P_{out}$ calculation:** Always multiply by $\eta_d$, not by 1.
> 5. **Conflating LED and LD in spontaneous vs. stimulated questions:** LEDs use *only* spontaneous emission in their active region, even though some stimulated emission can occur at very high currents (this is *not* the dominant mechanism).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **LED = Spontaneous, Incoherent, Broadband (30–100 nm), Cheap, Low Power, No Threshold.** ✓
- **LD = Stimulated, Coherent, Narrowband (<5 nm), Costly, High Power, Requires $I_{th}$.** ✓
- **Memorize:** $\lambda_p = 1.24/E_g$ (in $\mu$m). ✓
- **Memorize:** $G_{th} = \alpha_{int} + \frac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1 R_2)$ — the master threshold equation. ✓
- **Memorize:** $P_{out} = \eta_d (h\nu/e)(I - I_{th})$ for lasers, $P_{out} = \eta_{ext}(h\nu/e) I$ for LEDs. ✓
- **ELED beats SLED** in bandwidth and coupling efficiency — favorite 7-mark question. ✓
- **DFB = single longitudinal mode** (one wavelength), **FP = multi-mode** (comb of wavelengths). ✓
- **Bragg condition:** $\lambda_{Bragg} = 2n_{eff}\Lambda / m$ — DFB and DBR lasers. ✓
- **External quantum efficiency is small** (~0.05–2%) due to high refractive index. ✓
- **Facet reflectivity** for GaAs: $R \approx 0.32$; for InP: $R \approx 0.27$. ✓
- **Threshold current increases with temperature** — characterized by $T_0$. ✓
- **Three conditions for lasing**: Population inversion, optical feedback, threshold gain. ✓
- **Mode spacing** in FP laser: $\Delta\lambda = \lambda^2 / (2nL)$ — small spacing, multiple modes. ✓
- **LEDs** are used in 850/1310 nm multimode systems; **DFB LDs** in 1310/1550 nm single-mode DWDM. ✓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** General Structures ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **General Structure of an Optical Fiber** refers to the complete cylindrical, layered physical construction of a dielectric waveguide designed to confine and guide optical (light) signals over long distances with minimal loss. It is composed of concentric cylindrical layers: the **Core**, the **Cladding**, the **Primary (Buffer) Coating**, the **Secondary Coating**, and the protective **Outer Jacket**. The optical fiber is essentially a **dielectric cylindrical waveguide** in which light propagates through the phenomenon of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**. The structure is engineered so that the refractive index of the core ($n_1$) is marginally higher than that of the cladding ($n_2$), satisfying the condition $n_1 > n_2$. **Key Structural Components (Inner to Outer):** | Layer | Function | Typical Material | |---|---|---| | **Core** | Carries the optical signal | SiO₂ doped with GeO₂ | | **Cladding** | Confines light via TIR | SiO₂ doped with B₂O₃ / F | | **Primary Coating** | Mechanical cushioning | Soft acrylate polymer | | **Secondary Coating** | Structural rigidity | Hard acrylate polymer | | **Outer Jacket** | Environmental protection | PVC, LSZH, Hytrel | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Aquarium Pipe Analogy"** — Imagine a transparent acrylic pipe carrying a column of water inside a tank of slightly less dense oil. A flashlight shone into the pipe will bounce along its length without leaking out, *because* the pipe's medium is optically "denser" than its surroundings. The optical fiber is just this principle scaled down to micron-level precision using ultra-pure glass! - **Core** = Water column (where light travels). - **Cladding** = Oil surrounding the pipe (reflective boundary). - **Jacket** = Outer protective rubber sheath preventing physical damage. The light doesn't travel in a straight line — it zigzags down the core, hitting the core-cladding boundary at angles greater than the **critical angle**, which causes **Total Internal Reflection**, just like a coin dropped in a deep well keeps reflecting off the walls. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Critical Engineering Constants used in optical fiber design:** > - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$ (must be quoted as exact **3 × 10⁸ m/s** in KTU answers). > - **Standard Cladding Diameter:** $\mathbf{125 \, \mu m}$ (industry-wide standard). > - **Single-Mode Core Diameter:** $\mathbf{8 \text{ to } 10 \, \mu m}$. > - **Multimode Core Diameter:** $\mathbf{50 \, \mu m}$ (OM2/OM3) or $\mathbf{62.5 \, \mu m}$ (OM1). > - **Operating Wavelengths:** 850 nm, **1310 nm**, and **1550 nm** (the three KTU-mandated transmission windows). ### 1.4 Two Generic Structural Families > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU syllabus mandates awareness of the following two generic fiber structures: > 1. **Step-Index Fiber:** Refractive index changes **abruptly** at the core-cladding interface. > 2. **Graded-Index Fiber:** Refractive index **varies parabolically** (or smoothly) from the center of the core outward to the cladding boundary. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Layered Architecture — Detailed Functional Breakdown **Step 1: The Core (Diameter = 2a)** - The innermost cylindrical region where the optical signal physically propagates. - Manufactured from **fused silica (SiO₂)** doped with materials like **germanium dioxide (GeO₂)** or **phosphorus pentoxide (P₂O₅)** to elevate its refractive index. - Diameter determines the fiber's **modal behavior** (single-mode vs multi-mode). **Step 2: The Cladding (Outer Diameter = 2b)** - Surrounds the core with a slightly **lower** refractive index. - Provides the optical "mirror" effect via Total Internal Reflection. - Outer diameter is universally standardized at **125 µm** to ensure compatibility with connectors and splicing equipment. - Doped with **boron (B₂O₃)** or **fluorine (F)** to lower its refractive index. **Step 3: Primary Coating / Buffer Layer (Diameter ≈ 250 µm)** - A soft, elastomeric polymer (typically acrylate) applied directly over the cladding. - **Function:** Absorbs mechanical shocks and microbends; provides a cushion that prevents stress-induced losses. - Critical for preserving the fiber's optical performance under bending and temperature variations. **Step 4: Secondary Coating (Diameter ≈ 400 µm)** - A harder polymer layer that gives the fiber its mechanical stiffness. - Prevents the soft primary coating from sticking to the jacket. **Step 5: Outer Jacket (Diameter ≈ 900 µm)** - A robust external sheath made of materials like **PVC**, **LSZH** (Low Smoke Zero Halogen), or **Hytrel**. - Provides protection from moisture, abrasion, chemicals, and crushing forces during installation. - For multi-fiber cables, multiple jacketed fibers are bundled inside a central strength member (Kevlar) wrapped in an outer polyethylene sheath. ### 2.2 Refractive Index Profile — The Heart of Fiber Design The **refractive index profile** is the mathematical function $n(r)$ that describes how the refractive index varies radially outward from the fiber's center axis ($r=0$). **For Step-Index Fiber:** $$n(r) = \begin{cases} n_1, & 0 \leq r \leq a \quad \text{(core)} \\ n_2, & r > a \quad \text{(cladding)} \end{cases}$$ The transition is a **sharp discontinuity** at $r = a$. **For Graded-Index Fiber:** $$n(r) = \begin{cases} n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta \left(\frac{r}{a}\right)^{\alpha}}, & 0 \leq r \leq a \\ n_2, & r > a \end{cases}$$ where $\alpha$ is the **profile parameter** (typically $\alpha = 2$ for parabolic index). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Optical Communication problem paper.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Numerical Aperture (NA) | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | NA (approximate, for small $\Delta$) | $NA \approx n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$ | Valid when $\Delta \ll 1$ | | 3 | Relative Refractive Index Difference | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ | Typically $\sim 0.01$ | | 4 | Acceptance Angle (in air) | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ | In **radians or degrees** | | 5 | Acceptance Cone Half-Angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\right)$ | — | | 6 | Critical Angle (at core-cladding boundary) | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | — | | 7 | Normalized Frequency (V-Number) | $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot NA$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | Condition for Single-Mode Operation | $V < 2.405$ | Cutoff condition | | 9 | Fractional Power in Core (Step-Index, approx.) | $P_{core} \approx 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2a^2}{w_0^2}\right)$ | For Gaussian mode | | 10 | Solid Acceptance Angle | $\Omega = \pi (NA)^2$ | In **steradians** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Pitfall:** When asked for the acceptance angle, *always* provide the value of $\theta_a$ (the external half-angle in air), NOT the internal angle inside the fiber. Many students lose marks by quoting $\sin^{-1}(n_1 \sin \theta_{internal})$. ### 2.4 Why These Structures Matter in Engineering - **Telecommunications:** Modern long-haul systems (e.g., undersea cables connecting continents) use **single-mode step-index fibers** at 1550 nm for minimum dispersion and loss. - **Data Centers:** **Multimode graded-index fibers (OM3/OM4)** are preferred for short-reach links due to inexpensive VCSEL sources. - **Medical Endoscopes:** Coherent fiber bundles with carefully controlled **step-index structures** carry images into the human body. - **Structural Health Monitoring:** Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensors use specialty fiber structures to monitor strain, temperature, and pressure in bridges, aircraft, and pipelines. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Numerical Aperture from Snell's Law We derive the **Numerical Aperture (NA)** — the most fundamental parameter characterizing an optical fiber's light-gathering ability. **Step 1: Consider a light ray entering the fiber core from air ($n_0 = 1$).** At the air-core boundary, **Snell's Law** governs refraction: $$n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$ Since $n_0 = 1$ (air), this simplifies to: $$\sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$ This relates the external angle of incidence $\theta_0$ to the internal refraction angle $\theta_1$. **Step 2: Apply the geometric relationship at the core-cladding interface.** The refracted ray inside the core strikes the core-cladding interface at an angle $\theta_2$. By geometry of the ray path (a right-angled triangle formed by the ray, the normal, and the axis): $$\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 90^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 90^\circ - \theta_1$$ So the angle of incidence at the core-cladding boundary is the **complement** of the refraction angle at the air-core entry. **Step 3: Apply the Total Internal Reflection (TIR) condition.** For TIR to occur at the core-cladding interface, the angle of incidence $\theta_2$ must be **greater than or equal to the critical angle** $\theta_c$, where: $$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$ Substituting $\theta_2 = 90^\circ - \theta_1$: $$90^\circ - \theta_1 \geq \theta_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 \leq 90^\circ - \theta_c$$ **Step 4: Combine the two Snell's Law equations to eliminate $\theta_1$.** From the first Snell's equation: $\sin \theta_1 = \sin \theta_0 / n_1$. Substituting: $$\frac{\sin \theta_0}{n_1} \leq \sin(90^\circ - \theta_c) = \cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_c}$$ Using $\sin \theta_c = n_2 / n_1$: $$\frac{\sin \theta_0}{n_1} \leq \sqrt{1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$ Cancelling $n_1$ from both sides: $$\sin \theta_0 \leq \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$ **Step 5: Define the Numerical Aperture.** The **maximum acceptance angle** $\theta_a$ in air satisfies: $$\boxed{\sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = NA}$$ This is the formal definition of Numerical Aperture. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Approximate NA for Small $\Delta$ For typical silica fibers, the difference $n_1 - n_2$ is very small (around 0.01), so we can derive a simpler approximation. **Step 1: Square the exact expression for NA.** $$NA^2 = n_1^2 - n_2^2 = (n_1 - n_2)(n_1 + n_2)$$ **Step 2: Use the small-difference approximation.** Since $n_1 \approx n_2$, we can write $n_1 + n_2 \approx 2n_1$. Therefore: $$NA^2 \approx (n_1 - n_2)(2 n_1) = 2 n_1^2 \cdot \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = 2 n_1^2 \Delta$$ **Step 3: Take the square root of both sides.** $$\boxed{NA \approx n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This approximation is **essential** for KTU numerical problems where $\Delta$ is given in percent (e.g., $\Delta = 1\%$) and direct substitution of the full formula is cumbersome. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A step-index optical fiber has a core refractive index $n_1 = 1.48$ and a cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.46$. The core diameter is $50 \, \mu m$. Find: 1. The Numerical Aperture (NA). 2. The acceptance angle $\theta_a$. 3. The relative refractive index difference $\Delta$. 4. The normalized frequency $V$ at $\lambda = 1310$ nm. **Solution:** **Part (a): Numerical Aperture** $$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2}$$ Calculate $n_1^2 = 1.48 \times 1.48 = 2.1904$ Calculate $n_2^2 = 1.46 \times 1.46 = 2.1316$ $$NA = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} \approx 0.2425$$ **Part (b): Acceptance Angle** $$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.2425) \approx 14.03^\circ$$ **Part (c): Relative Refractive Index Difference** $$\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.48 - 1.46}{1.48} = \frac{0.02}{1.48} \approx 0.01351$$ $$\Delta \approx 1.351\%$$ **Part (d): Normalized Frequency (V-Number)** The core radius is $a = 25 \, \mu m = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m. The wavelength is $\lambda = 1310 \, nm = 1.31 \times 10^{-6}$ m. $$V = \frac{2\pi a \cdot NA}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi \times (25 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.2425}{1.31 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$V = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 25 \times 0.2425}{1.31} = \frac{38.09}{1.31} \approx 29.08$$ Since $V = 29.08 \gg 2.405$, this fiber is **multimode** at 1310 nm. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Cross-Sectional Architecture of a Step-Index Optical Fiber ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Axis[Optical Fiber Cross-Section] direction TB nodeA["CORE
Diameter 2a
Refractive Index n1
Fused Silica + GeO2"] nodeB["CLADDING
Diameter 2b = 125 micrometer
Refractive Index n2
Fused Silica + B2O3 or F"] nodeC["PRIMARY COATING
Diameter approx 250 micrometer
Soft Acrylate Polymer
Cushion and microbend protection"] nodeD["SECONDARY COATING
Diameter approx 400 micrometer
Hard Acrylate Polymer
Mechanical stiffness"] nodeE["OUTER JACKET
Diameter approx 900 micrometer
PVC or LSZH or Hytrel
Environmental protection"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE end style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#4682B4,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px style nodeE fill:#2F4F4F,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Flow of Light Propagation in the Fiber ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeSrc["Light Source
LED or LD
Lambda = 850 or 1310 or 1550 nm"] -->|Emit| nodeLaunch["Launching Optics
Lens or Butt Coupling
Angle less than Theta_a"] nodeLaunch -->|Inject| nodeCoreFiber["OPTICAL FIBER CORE
Propagation by TIR
Step or Graded Index"] nodeCoreFiber -->|Transport| nodeBends["Microscopic and Macroscopic Bends
Cause Radiation Loss"] nodeBends -->|Arrive| nodeDet["Photodetector
PIN or APD
Converts Photons to Current"] nodeDet --> nodeOut["Output Electrical Signal"] style nodeSrc fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000 style nodeLaunch fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000 style nodeCoreFiber fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style nodeBends fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style nodeDet fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style nodeOut fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Refractive Index Profile Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BlockStep["Step-Index Fiber Profile"] nodeA1["Center r = 0
n = n1 (constant)"] -->|"Sharp drop at r = a"| nodeA2["Region r greater than a
n = n2 (constant)"] end subgraph BlockGraded["Graded-Index Fiber Profile"] nodeB1["Center r = 0
n = n1 (maximum)"] -->|"Smooth parabolic decrease"| nodeB2["Edge r = a
n = n2 (minimum)"] end style nodeA1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style nodeA2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000 style nodeB1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style nodeB2 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **step-index profile** is *piecewise constant* — a vertical cliff at $r = a$. The **graded-index profile** is *parabolic* — a smooth, continuous curve. Both achieve light confinement, but the graded-index design dramatically reduces **intermodal dispersion** by equalizing the path lengths of different modes. ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A Standard Fiber-Optic Communication Link ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TxBlock["Transmitter Module"] txE["Electrical Input Signal
e.g. Voice or Data"] --> txD["Driver Circuit
Current Modulation"] txD --> txO["Optical Source
LED 850 nm or LD 1310 or 1550 nm"] end TxBlock -->|Coupling into fiber| FiberBlock["OPTICAL FIBER CHANNEL
General Structure
Core plus Cladding plus Coatings plus Jacket"] FiberBlock -->|Spliced or Connectorized| RxBlock["Receiver Module"] subgraph RxBlock["Receiver Module"] rxO["Photodetector
PIN or APD"] --> rxA["Amplifier and Equalizer"] rxA --> rxD["Decision Circuit and Clock Recovery"] rxD --> rxE["Electrical Output Signal"] end style FiberBlock fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:4px style txO fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style rxO fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **Numerical Aperture (NA)**. Derive its expression in terms of the core and cladding refractive indices. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Aperture (NA)** is defined as the sine of the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$ of an optical fiber, measured in air. It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the light-gathering ability of the fiber. **Derivation:** At the air-core interface, applying Snell's law: $$\sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$ For Total Internal Reflection (TIR) at the core-cladding interface, the internal angle must exceed the critical angle. Using the geometric relation $\theta_2 = 90^\circ - \theta_1$ and the critical angle condition $\sin \theta_c = n_2 / n_1$, we obtain: $$\sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$ --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List the **various layers** of a general optical fiber structure with their **primary function**. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > A standard optical fiber consists of the following layers (from inside to outside): > 1. **Core ($n_1$):** Carries the optical signal; made of fused silica doped with GeO₂. > 2. **Cladding ($n_2 < n_1$):** Confines light via Total Internal Reflection; standard outer diameter 125 µm. > 3. **Primary Coating (~250 µm):** Soft polymer that absorbs mechanical shocks and microbends. > 4. **Secondary Coating (~400 µm):** Hard polymer providing mechanical stiffness. > 5. **Outer Jacket (~900 µm):** PVC/LSZH sheath that protects against moisture, abrasion, and crushing. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE) follow the **Module Internal Choice** pattern. Students answer **ONE** full question from a choice of two. The 14 marks are split equally: **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – July 2024] **(a)** With the aid of a neat diagram, describe the **general structure of an optical fiber** and explain the function of each layer. Discuss the typical materials used in the core and cladding. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An optical fiber has a core refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$ and a cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.45$. If the operating wavelength is $\lambda = 1550$ nm and the core diameter is $8 \, \mu m$, calculate: (i) Numerical Aperture, (ii) Acceptance Angle, (iii) Relative Refractive Index Difference, and (iv) Normalized Frequency. Identify whether the fiber is single-mode or multi-mode. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** The general structure of an optical fiber consists of **five concentric cylindrical layers**: **Layer 1 — Core:** - Innermost cylinder of diameter **2a** (typically 8–10 µm for single-mode, 50/62.5 µm for multimode). - Made of **fused silica (SiO₂)** doped with **germanium dioxide (GeO₂)** or **phosphorus pentoxide (P₂O₅)** to raise its refractive index $n_1$. - Light propagates through the core via TIR. **Layer 2 — Cladding:** - Surrounds the core with outer diameter **2b = 125 µm** (industry standard). - Made of **fused silica doped with boron trioxide (B₂O₃)** or **fluorine (F)** to lower its refractive index $n_2$. - Provides the optical boundary for TIR. **Layer 3 — Primary (Buffer) Coating:** - Diameter approximately **250 µm**. - Made of a **soft, elastomeric acrylate polymer**. - **Function:** Absorbs mechanical shocks; prevents microbending losses; cushions the fiber against lateral stress. **Layer 4 — Secondary Coating:** - Diameter approximately **400 µm**. - Made of a **harder acrylate polymer**. - **Function:** Provides mechanical rigidity and prevents the primary coating from sticking to the jacket. **Layer 5 — Outer Jacket:** - Diameter approximately **900 µm**. - Made of **PVC, LSZH (Low Smoke Zero Halogen), or Hytrel**. - **Function:** Protects the fiber from environmental hazards like moisture, chemicals, abrasion, and crushing. > A cross-sectional diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes) clearly shows these five concentric layers with the core at the center. **Part (b):** Given: $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.45$, $\lambda = 1550 \, nm = 1.55 \times 10^{-6} \, m$, and core diameter $2a = 8 \, \mu m \Rightarrow a = 4 \, \mu m = 4 \times 10^{-6} \, m$. **(i) Numerical Aperture (NA):** $$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475} \approx 0.384$$ **(ii) Acceptance Angle ($\theta_a$):** $$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.384) \approx 22.58^\circ$$ **(iii) Relative Refractive Index Difference ($\Delta$):** $$\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.50 - 1.45}{1.50} = \frac{0.05}{1.50} = 0.0333$$ $$\Delta \approx 3.33\%$$ **(iv) Normalized Frequency (V-Number):** $$V = \frac{2\pi a \cdot NA}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi \times (4 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.384}{1.55 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$V = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 4 \times 0.384}{1.55} = \frac{9.649}{1.55} \approx 6.225$$ **Conclusion:** Since $V = 6.225 > 2.405$, the fiber supports **multiple modes** at 1550 nm and is therefore a **multimode step-index fiber**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Differentiate between **step-index and graded-index optical fibers** with the help of refractive index profile diagrams. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Define **Numerical Aperture** and derive its expression. An optical fiber has $n_1 = 1.52$ and $n_2 = 1.48$. Calculate: (i) NA, (ii) Acceptance Angle, and (iii) the maximum incidence angle (with respect to the fiber axis) of a ray entering the core. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** | Feature | Step-Index Fiber | Graded-Index Fiber | |---|---|---| | **Index Profile** | Refractive index is **constant** in the core and drops **abruptly** at the cladding boundary. | Refractive index is **maximum** at the center and **decreases parabolically** outward. | | **Mathematical Form** | $n(r) = n_1$ for $r < a$, $n(r) = n_2$ for $r > a$. | $n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta (r/a)^\alpha}$ with $\alpha \approx 2$. | | **Modal Behavior** | Supports many modes with widely varying path lengths. | Supports many modes but equalizes their transit times. | | **Intermodal Dispersion** | **High** (different modes arrive at different times). | **Low** (parabolic profile acts as a lens). | | **Bandwidth** | Limited (~10–50 MHz·km). | Higher (~500 MHz·km to a few GHz·km). | | **Typical Application** | Short-distance, low-cost links. | Medium-distance LAN, data-center interconnects. | | **Profile Diagram** | Rectangular step shape. | Smooth bell-shaped (parabolic) curve. | > For diagrams, refer to Section 4.3 of these notes. **Part (b):** **Definition:** Numerical Aperture (NA) is the sine of the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$ of an optical fiber, representing its light-collecting capacity. **Derivation (Brief):** Applying Snell's law at the air-core interface and the TIR condition at the core-cladding interface: $$\sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} \Rightarrow \boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$ **Calculations:** Given $n_1 = 1.52$ and $n_2 = 1.48$. **(i) Numerical Aperture:** $$NA = \sqrt{(1.52)^2 - (1.48)^2} = \sqrt{2.3104 - 2.1904} = \sqrt{0.1200} \approx 0.3464$$ **(ii) Acceptance Angle:** $$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.3464) \approx 20.27^\circ$$ **(iii) Maximum Incidence Angle with Respect to Fiber Axis:** The acceptance angle $\theta_a$ is measured with respect to the **fiber axis**. So the maximum incidence angle (measured from the fiber axis) for a ray entering the core is $\theta_a \approx 20.27^\circ$. If, however, the question asks for the angle with respect to the **fiber end-face normal** (which is perpendicular to the axis), the angle would be: $$90^\circ - 20.27^\circ = 69.73^\circ$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU valuation requires that the *reference axis be clearly stated* in the answer. State explicitly: "$\theta_a$ is measured from the fiber axis." --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Forgetting units:** Always quote $\lambda$ in **meters** when computing $V$ (not in nm or µm). Many students compute $V$ using inconsistent units and lose 1–2 marks. > 2. **Misidentifying the Single-Mode Condition:** A fiber is single-mode **only** when $V < 2.405$. Some students mistakenly believe it depends on the *core diameter alone*. > 3. **Confusing $n_1$ and $n_2$:** The core ALWAYS has a *higher* refractive index ($n_1 > n_2$); otherwise TIR cannot occur. Reverse the indices and your answer is wrong. > 4. **Omitting the "approximate" NA formula:** When $\Delta$ is small, KTU expects the *exact* expression or the *approximate* one — but not both mashed together. Choose one and state the assumption. > 5. **Forgetting to identify single-mode vs. multi-mode:** After computing $V$, *always* state whether $V < 2.405$ (single-mode) or $V \geq 2.405$ (multi-mode). This is a 1-mark easy point that students often miss. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — "What to Remember"** - **Structure:** Core → Cladding → Primary Coating → Secondary Coating → Outer Jacket (5 layers, each with a distinct function). - **Standard Sizes:** Cladding = **125 µm**; Buffer = **250 µm**; Jacket = **900 µm**. - **Materials:** Core = **SiO₂ + GeO₂**; Cladding = **SiO₂ + B₂O₃ / F**; Coatings = **Acrylate polymers**; Jacket = **PVC / LSZH**. - **Key Condition for TIR:** $n_1 > n_2$ (core index must be **strictly greater** than cladding index). - **Numerical Aperture:** $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} \approx n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$ (for small $\Delta$). - **Acceptance Angle:** $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ — measured **with respect to the fiber axis**. - **Critical Angle:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2 / n_1)$ — measured **with respect to the normal** at the core-cladding interface. - **V-Number:** $V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot NA$ — Single-mode cutoff is $V < 2.405$. - **Two Generic Structures:** **Step-Index** (abrupt transition) and **Graded-Index** (parabolic transition). - **Three Standard Wavelengths:** 850 nm, **1310 nm**, and **1550 nm**. - **Relative Refractive Index Difference:** $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$ — typically **0.5% to 3%** for standard silica fibers. - **Solid Acceptance Angle:** $\Omega = \pi (NA)^2$ steradians — represents the 3D "cone" of light the fiber can accept. - **Physical Constants:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum; $n_{air} \approx 1$. - **KTU-Favorite Topic:** NA, acceptance angle, and V-number calculations appear in **almost every ESE** paper — practice at least 3 numerical problems before the exam.

topics.title: Coupling with Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coupling with Fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coupling with Fibres** is defined as the systematic process of efficiently transferring optical power either from a **light source** (LED or Laser Diode) into an **optical fiber**, or from one **fiber end to another fiber end**, with minimum insertion loss and maximum power transfer efficiency.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, fibre coupling is classified into two broad engineering domains:

1. **Source-to-Fiber Coupling (Launching):** Injecting light from an optoelectronic source into the core of an optical fiber.
2. **Fiber-to-Fiber Coupling (Joints/Connectors/Splices):** Joining two fiber segments mechanically or permanently.

The **Coupling Efficiency ($\eta$)** is formally defined as the ratio of optical power successfully entering the fiber's guided mode region to the total optical power radiated by the source.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{fiber}}}{P_{\text{source}}} \quad \text{(dimensionless, } 0 \le \eta \le 1\text{)}$$

And the corresponding **Insertion Loss ($L_i$)** in decibels is:

$$L_i = -10 \log_{10}(\eta) \quad \text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** Typical target values in KTU laboratory standards aim for $\eta \ge 0.4$ for direct LED-to-fiber coupling and $\eta \ge 0.8$ for laser-to-fiber with lensing schemes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pouring water from a **wide garden hose** (source) into a **narrow drinking straw** (fiber core):

- If the straw is held **far away** (longitudinal misalignment), water splashes.
- If the straw is **tilted** (angular misalignment), water spills off the side.
- If the straw is **offset sideways** (lateral misalignment), the stream misses entirely.
- If the straw's **opening is bigger** (larger NA / core area), more water can enter.
- A **funnel** (lens) placed at the hose mouth helps gather and direct the water.

> **Optical coupling works on the exact same principle.** The light source emits a cone of light; the fiber's **Numerical Aperture (NA)** defines a cone of acceptance. Only the rays falling within the fiber's acceptance cone AND within the core cross-section can be guided. Everything else is lost as **radiation loss**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (Multimode) | Standard Value (Single-mode) |
|---|---|---|
| **Fiber Core Diameter** | **50 µm / 62.5 µm** | **8 µm – 10 µm** |
| **Fiber Numerical Aperture (NA)** | **0.20 – 0.40** | **0.10 – 0.14** |
| **Source Active Area (LED)** | **50 µm – 100 µm** | N/A |
| **Source Divergence Angle (Laser)** | Narrow (≈ 10°–30°) | Narrow (≈ 10°–30°) |
| **Fresnel Reflection Coefficient (R)** | **≈ 0.04** (for n=1.5) | **≈ 0.04** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEECT636 - Module 3):** Students must master the derivation of source-to-fiber coupling efficiency, the four lensing schemes, the three classes of fiber misalignment losses, and the difference between LED (Lambertian) and Laser (directional) launching.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Source-to-Fiber Coupling — The Physical Problem

When light leaves an LED, it radiates in **all forward directions** following the **Lambertian emission pattern**, where the radiant intensity falls off as the **cosine of the emission angle**. A laser diode, in contrast, produces a **directional beam** with a small divergence angle.

For a fiber to capture this light, **two simultaneous conditions** must be met:

1. **Spatial condition:** The light ray must strike the fiber **core** (within the core radius $a$).
2. **Angular condition:** The angle of incidence must be **less than the acceptance angle** $\theta_a$, where $NA = \sin \theta_a$.

## 2.2 Coupling Efficiency for a Lambertian Source (LED)

The **radiance** $L$ (W/cm²·sr) of a Lambertian source is constant in all forward directions:

$$L = \frac{P_s}{\pi \, A_s}$$

where $A_s$ is the source emitting area.

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

1. The fiber accepts a maximum solid angle $\Omega_{\max} = \pi \sin^2 \theta_a = \pi (NA)^2$.
2. The fiber presents a capture area equal to its core cross-section $A_f = \pi a^2$.
3. **Case A: Source smaller than fiber core** ($A_s \le A_f$) — limited by **Numerical Aperture**:
4. **Case B: Source larger than fiber core** ($A_s > A_f$) — limited by **core area**:

## 2.3 Coupling Efficiency for a Laser Diode (Directional Source)

Lasers emit a quasi-collimated beam with small divergence. Two cases arise based on the laser spot size relative to the fiber core:

- **If laser spot $\le$ fiber core:** All laser power is captured → $\eta \approx 1$ (ignoring Fresnel).
- **If laser spot $>$ fiber core:** $\eta = A_f / A_s$.
- **If laser divergence $>$ fiber NA:** Angular filtering applies.

## 2.4 Lensing Schemes (Source-to-Fiber)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** Lensing schemes are essential when coupling laser diodes to single-mode fibers, where direct butt-coupling yields less than 10% efficiency.

The four standard schemes are:

| Scheme | Description | Typical $\eta$ Improvement |
|---|---|---|
| **Butt Coupling (Direct)** | Source placed in physical contact with fiber end-face | Baseline (≈ 10–40%) |
| **Microlens on Fiber Tip** | Tiny spherical or hemispherical lens etched/melted on fiber end | 5×–10× improvement |
| **Spherical (Ball) Lens** | External glass sphere focuses light into core | 30%–50% |
| **Cylindrical (Cigar) Lens** | Compensates laser astigmatism; focuses elliptical beam | 40%–60% |
| **GRIN (Gradient-Index) Lens** | Quarter-pitch GRIN rod for collimation/focusing | 50%–80% |

## 2.5 Fiber-to-Fiber Coupling — Misalignment Losses

Three canonical mechanical misalignments exist at a fiber joint:

| Type | Symbol | Physical Origin | Loss Dependence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lateral (Transverse)** Offset | $x$ | Core axes displaced sideways | $\propto (x/a)^2$ |
| **Angular (Tilt) Misalignment | $\theta$ | Fiber axes cross at an angle | $\propto (n_1 \, a \, \theta / \lambda)^2$ |
| **Longitudinal (End-Separation) Gap | $z$ | Fibers do not touch; air gap | $\propto (z \, \text{NA} / a)^2$ |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all exam-critical formulas** for Module 3 — Coupling with Fibres. Memorize these before entering the exam hall.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coupling Efficiency (General) | $\eta = P_{\text{fiber}} / P_s$ | dimensionless | All coupling problems |
| 2 | Insertion Loss (dB) | $L_i = -10 \log_{10}(\eta)$ | dB | All coupling problems |
| 3 | Fiber Power (LED, $A_s \le A_f$) | $P_{\text{fiber}} = P_s \cdot (NA)^2$ | Watts (W) | Small LED into MMF |
| 4 | Fiber Power (LED, $A_s > A_f$) | $P_{\text{fiber}} = P_s \cdot (A_f/A_s) \cdot (NA)^2$ | W | Large LED or small core |
| 5 | Fiber Power (Laser, area-limited) | $P_{\text{fiber}} = P_s \cdot (A_f / A_s)$ | W | LD into SMF/MMF |
| 6 | Lambertian Source Radiance | $L = P_s / (\pi A_s)$ | W·sr$^{-1}$·cm$^{-2}$ | LED analysis |
| 7 | Fiber Solid Angle | $\Omega_{\max} = \pi (NA)^2$ | sr (steradians) | Acceptance geometry |
| 8 | Fresnel Reflection (Normal Incidence) | $R = [(n_1 - n_2)/(n_1 + n_2)]^2$ | dimensionless | Air-glass interface |
| 9 | Fresnel Loss (dB) | $L_F = -10 \log_{10}(1 - R)$ | dB | End-face reflection |
| 10 | Lateral Offset Loss (MMF, step-index) | $\eta_{\text{lat}} \approx 1 - (4 x)/(3\pi a)$ | dimensionless | Step-index MMF |
| 11 | Angular Tilt Loss | $\eta_{\theta} \approx 1 - 0.637 \, \theta \, a / \text{NA} \cdot (2/\lambda)$ | dimensionless | Small-angle approx. |
| 12 | Longitudinal Gap Loss | $\eta_z = 1 - (z \cdot \text{NA}) / (4 a)$ | dimensionless | For $z \ll a/\text{NA}$ |
| 13 | Maximum Coupling (Lambertian) | $\eta_{\max} = (NA)^2$ | dimensionless | LED ceiling |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Optical coupling is the **make-or-break engineering step** in every fibre-optic system. In **undersea telecom cables** (e.g., trans-Pacific links), even a **0.1 dB loss per joint** accumulates to many dB across 100+ repeater spans, costing millions of dollars. In **medical endoscopy** and **fiber laser delivery**, efficient coupling enables high-power laser surgery. In **data centres**, MT-RJ and MPO connectors with sub-micron alignment tolerances achieve thousands of reliable insertions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Coupling Efficiency of a Lambertian LED into a Step-Index Multimode Fiber

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU exams. The setup is an LED with emitting area $A_s$ and a step-index fiber with core radius $a$ and numerical aperture $NA$.

### Step 1 — Define the Source Radiance

A Lambertian source has a constant radiance $L$ (W·cm$^{-2}$·sr$^{-1}$) such that the radiant exitance (power emitted per unit area into the forward hemisphere) is:

$$M = \int\!\!\int_{\text{hemisphere}} L \cos\theta \, d\Omega = L \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$M = L \cdot (2\pi) \cdot \left[\frac{\sin^2\theta}{2}\right]_0^{\pi/2} = L \cdot (2\pi) \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \pi L$$

Therefore the **total power emitted by the source** is:

$$P_s = M \cdot A_s = \pi \, L \, A_s$$

Solving for the radiance:

$$L = \frac{P_s}{\pi \, A_s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Power Accepted by the Fiber

The fiber core presents a capture area $A_f = \pi a^2$. The fiber accepts all rays whose angle with the fiber axis is $\le \theta_a$, i.e., within the solid angle:

$$\Omega_{\max} = \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{\theta_a} \sin\theta \, d\theta = 2\pi \cdot (1 - \cos\theta_a)$$

For small $\theta_a$ (paraxial approximation), $1 - \cos\theta_a \approx \sin^2\theta_a / 2$, giving:

$$\Omega_{\max} \approx \pi \sin^2\theta_a = \pi (NA)^2$$

The power coupled into the fiber is the radiance times the area times the solid angle of acceptance:

$$P_{\text{fiber}} = L \cdot A_f \cdot \Omega_{\max}$$

Substituting $L$:

$$P_{\text{fiber}} = \frac{P_s}{\pi A_s} \cdot A_f \cdot \pi (NA)^2 = P_s \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_s} \cdot (NA)^2$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Two Limiting Cases

**Case A: Source smaller than fiber core ($A_s \le A_f$)**

The capture is limited by the fiber's acceptance NA. The factor $A_f / A_s \ge 1$, but only the portion of the fiber core directly illuminated contributes. Effectively:

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{fiber}} = P_s \cdot (NA)^2 \quad \text{(NA-limited)}\,}$$

$$\eta_A = \frac{P_{\text{fiber}}}{P_s} = (NA)^2$$

For a typical MMF with $NA = 0.2$: $\eta_A = 0.04$ (only 4%!) — explaining the need for lensing.

**Case B: Source larger than fiber core ($A_s > A_f$)**

The capture is limited by the small fiber core area. The factor $A_f / A_s < 1$:

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{fiber}} = P_s \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_s} \cdot (NA)^2 \quad \text{(Area-limited)}\,}$$

$$\eta_B = (NA)^2 \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_s}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Verification (Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example for Exam Practice:**
> LED with $A_s = 10^{-4}$ cm², $P_s = 1$ mW, fiber with $a = 25\,\mu$m, $NA = 0.2$.
> - $A_f = \pi (25 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²
> - Since $A_s > A_f$, use Case B
> - $\eta_B = (0.2)^2 \times (1.963 \times 10^{-5} / 10^{-4}) = 0.04 \times 0.1963 = 7.85 \times 10^{-3}$
> - $P_{\text{fiber}} = 7.85 \,\mu W$
> - $L_i = -10 \log_{10}(7.85 \times 10^{-3}) = 21.05$ dB → **very lossy!**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fresnel Reflection Loss at a Fiber End-Face

When light travels from a medium of refractive index $n_1$ into a medium of $n_2$ (e.g., glass-to-air or air-to-glass), part of the power is reflected at normal incidence.

The amplitude reflection coefficient is:

$$r = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}$$

The **power reflection coefficient** is $R = r^2$:

$$R = \left(\frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$$

For silica fiber ($n_1 = 1.46$) and air ($n_2 = 1.0$):

$$R = \left(\frac{1.46 - 1.00}{1.46 + 1.00}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.46}{2.46}\right)^2 = 0.035 \approx 3.5\%$$

The fraction **transmitted** is $T = 1 - R = 0.965$, so the loss is:

$$L_F = -10 \log_{10}(1 - R) = -10 \log_{10}(0.965) = 0.156 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Implication:** This 0.156 dB per end-face loss is why **index-matching gel** is used in fiber connectors — it eliminates the air-glass interface, reducing $R$ to nearly zero.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lateral Misalignment Loss Between Two Multimode Fibers

Consider two identical step-index fibers with core radius $a$, NA, and a lateral offset $x$ between the two core axes.

By **geometric optics**, the overlap area between the two circular cores is reduced. The power transmission coefficient for two identical step-index MMF with lateral offset $x$ is approximately:

$$\eta_{\text{lat}} = \frac{2}{\pi} \left[ \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x}{2a}\right) - \frac{x}{2a}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{x}{2a}\right)^2} \right]$$

For small offsets ($x \ll a$), a Taylor series expansion yields the simple approximation:

$$\eta_{\text{lat}} \approx 1 - \frac{4x}{3\pi a}$$

The loss in dB is:

$$L_{\text{lat}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 - \frac{4x}{3\pi a}\right)$$

**Numerical example:** For $a = 50\,\mu$m, $x = 5\,\mu$m (10% offset):
- $\eta_{\text{lat}} \approx 1 - (4 \times 5)/(3\pi \times 50) = 1 - 0.0424 = 0.9576$
- $L_{\text{lat}} = 0.187$ dB

> [!NOTE]
