> **Insight:** A 10% lateral offset costs only ≈ 0.2 dB for MMF but is catastrophic for SMF, where the same physical offset exceeds the entire mode-field diameter.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Coupling Scenario | Limiting Factor | Key Formula | Typical $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| LED $\to$ MMF, $A_s < A_f$ | NA | $\eta = (NA)^2$ | 0.04 – 0.16 |
| LED $\to$ MMF, $A_s > A_f$ | Core Area | $\eta = (A_f/A_s)(NA)^2$ | 0.01 – 0.10 |
| LD $\to$ MMF, matched | Both | $\eta = (A_f/A_s)$ | 0.3 – 0.8 |
| LD $\to$ SMF, butt | Mode mismatch | $\eta = $ overlap integral | 0.1 – 0.4 |
| LD $\to$ SMF, GRIN lens | Lens quality | $\eta \approx 0.5 - 0.8$ | 0.5 – 0.8 |
| Fiber $\to$ Fiber, ideal | Fresnel only | $\eta = (1-R)^2$ | 0.93 per joint |
| Fiber $\to$ Fiber, $5\,\mu$m offset | Lateral | $\eta_{\text{lat}}$ formula | 0.95 – 0.99 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Source-to-Fiber Coupling

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional signal flow** from the optoelectronic source through the coupling optics into the fiber core. This corresponds to typical KTU lab setups (Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier or laser diode pigtailing stations).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["Source Block"]
        S["Optical Source LED or LD"]
        SA["Source Active Area As"]
    end

    subgraph OPT["Coupling Optics Block"]
        L1["Lensing Scheme Butt or Spherical or GRIN"]
        G1["Air Gap or Index Match Gel"]
        R1["Fresnel Reflection R approx 0.04"]
    end

    subgraph FIB["Fiber Block"]
        FC["Fiber Core Radius a"]
        NA1["Numerical Aperture NA"]
        CL["Fiber Cladding"]
    end

    subgraph DET["Detection Block"]
        PM["Optical Power Meter"]
    end

    S --> SA
    SA --> L1
    L1 --> G1
    G1 --> R1
    R1 --> FC
    FC --> NA1
    NA1 --> CL
    FC --> PM
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Lensing Scheme Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Source Selected"] --> B{"Source Type?"}
    B -- "LED Lambertian" --> C["Use Butt Coupling or Microlens"]
    B -- "Laser Diode LD" --> D{"Core Size Match?"}
    D -- "Matched spot to MMF" --> E["Butt Coupling Acceptable"]
    D -- "SMF or mismatched" --> F["Use Spherical or Cylindrical Lens"]
    F --> G["Elliptical Beam?"]
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Cylindrical Lens Compensates Astigmatism"]
    G -- "No" --> I["Spherical Ball Lens"]
    H --> J["GRIN Lens for Long Working Distance"]
    I --> J
    E --> K["End: Coupling Achieved"]
    C --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Three Classes of Misalignment at a Fiber Joint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JOINT["Fiber-to-Fiber Joint Block"]
        F1["Transmitting Fiber Core"]
        GAP["Misalignment Region"]
        F2["Receiving Fiber Core"]
    end

    GAP --> M1["Lateral Offset X in micrometers"]
    GAP --> M2["Angular Tilt Theta in degrees"]
    GAP --> M3["Longitudinal Gap Z in micrometers"]

    M1 --> LF1["Lateral Loss approx 1 minus 4X over 3piA"]
    M2 --> LF2["Tilt Loss approx 1 minus 0.637 theta A NA"]
    M3 --> LF3["Gap Loss approx 1 minus Z NA over 4A"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Coupling Efficiency Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Total Source Power Ps"] --> R["Radiance L = Ps over pi As"]
    R --> C{"Area Comparison"}
    C -- "As less than or equal to Af" --> NA["NA-Limited: eta = NA squared"]
    C -- "As greater than Af" --> AR["Area-Limited: eta = Af over As times NA squared"]
    NA --> OUT["P_fiber delivered to fiber core"]
    AR --> OUT
    OUT --> FR["Fresnel Subtract approx 0.16 dB per end"]
    FR --> FINAL["Net P coupled into guided mode"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define coupling efficiency. What is the maximum theoretical coupling efficiency from a Lambertian source into a step-index multimode fiber with $NA = 0.2$?**

**Model Answer:**
Coupling efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of optical power coupled into the fiber core to the total power emitted by the source: $\eta = P_{\text{fiber}} / P_s$.
For a Lambertian source into a step-index MMF (when $A_s \le A_f$): $\eta_{\max} = (NA)^2 = (0.2)^2 = 0.04$ (i.e., **4%**). The corresponding insertion loss is $L_i = -10 \log_{10}(0.04) = 13.98$ dB. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**List the four standard lensing schemes used to improve source-to-fiber coupling. State one limitation of direct butt coupling.**

**Model Answer:**
The four lensing schemes are: (i) **Microlens on fiber tip**, (ii) **Spherical (ball) lens**, (iii) **Cylindrical (cigar) lens**, and (iv) **GRIN (gradient-index) lens**.
A key limitation of direct butt coupling is the **mismatch between the source emission cone and the fiber acceptance cone**, leading to very low coupling efficiency (often < 10% for LEDs into MMF and even worse for LDs into SMF). Additionally, butt coupling is highly sensitive to **lateral and longitudinal misalignments** at the sub-micron scale. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Derive an expression for the **optical power coupled from a Lambertian LED into a step-index multimode fiber**, clearly distinguishing the two cases of (i) source area smaller than fiber core area, and (ii) source area larger than fiber core area. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let the LED have emitting area $A_s$ and total emitted power $P_s$. The LED is a Lambertian emitter with constant radiance $L$.

**Step 1:** The radiant exitance of a Lambertian source is $M = \pi L$, hence:

$$L = \frac{P_s}{\pi A_s}$$

**Step 2:** The fiber core of radius $a$ has area $A_f = \pi a^2$ and accepts rays within a solid angle $\Omega_{\max} = \pi (NA)^2$.

**Step 3:** The power coupled is:

$$P_{\text{fiber}} = L \cdot A_{\text{overlap}} \cdot \Omega_{\max}$$

**Case (i):** $A_s \le A_f$ — the full source area is imaged onto a portion of the fiber core. The overlap area equals $A_s$, so:

$$P_{\text{fiber}} = \frac{P_s}{\pi A_s} \cdot A_s \cdot \pi (NA)^2 = P_s \cdot (NA)^2$$

$$\eta_i = (NA)^2 \quad \text{(NA-limited)}$$

**Case (ii):** $A_s > A_f$ — the full fiber core is illuminated, but only a fraction of the source area overlaps:

$$P_{\text{fiber}} = \frac{P_s}{\pi A_s} \cdot A_f \cdot \pi (NA)^2 = P_s \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_s} \cdot (NA)^2$$

$$\eta_{ii} = \frac{A_f}{A_s} \cdot (NA)^2 \quad \text{(Area-limited)}$$

**(7 marks distributed across derivation, logical steps, and final boxed answers.)**

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **three types of mechanical misalignments** that occur at a fiber-to-fiber joint. For each, state the loss dependence on the misalignment parameter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three classes of misalignment are:

1. **Lateral (Transverse) Offset $x$:** The two fiber cores are displaced sideways. The overlap area between the two circular cores decreases. For small offsets in step-index MMF:

$$\eta_{\text{lat}} \approx 1 - \frac{4x}{3\pi a}$$

The loss scales approximately as $(x/a)^2$ for very small offsets and saturates to 0 dB for perfectly aligned fibers.

2. **Angular (Tilt) Misalignment $\theta$:** The two fiber axes cross at an angle. Rays accepted by the first fiber enter the second at an angle exceeding $\theta_a$, hence are lost. The transmission coefficient is:

$$\eta_\theta \approx 1 - \frac{0.637 \, \theta \, a \, n_1}{\lambda}$$

The loss scales as $\theta^2$ for small angles and becomes severe even for fractions of a degree in single-mode fibers.

3. **Longitudinal (End-Separation) Gap $z$:** The fiber end-faces do not touch; an air gap exists. The emitted cone spreads beyond the receiving core. For small gaps:

$$\eta_z \approx 1 - \frac{z \cdot NA}{4a}$$

The loss scales as $(z \cdot NA / a)^2$ for small $z$. This loss also includes a **Fresnel reflection component** at the air-glass interfaces.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose 1–2 marks for:
> - Confusing **lateral** and **longitudinal** misalignments (lateral = sideways, longitudinal = along the axis).
> - Forgetting the **Fresnel reflection** contribution in the longitudinal gap loss.
> - Not specifying that the simple formulas apply only for **small misalignments**; the full trigonometric overlap integrals are required for large offsets.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** With the help of neat functional block diagrams, explain the **four major lensing schemes** used to improve laser diode to single-mode fiber coupling efficiency. Compare their relative advantages. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Butt Coupling (Direct):** The laser facet is positioned in direct physical contact (or near-contact) with the fiber end-face. Simple but suffers from large area and angular mismatch. Typical $\eta \approx 10$–$40\%$.

**2. Microlens on Fiber Tip:** A small glass sphere or hemisphere is formed directly on the fiber end by melting, etching, or photolithography. It transforms the diverging laser beam into a cone matching the fiber NA. Typical $\eta \approx 30$–$60\%$. Compact and self-aligned.

**3. Spherical (Ball) Lens:** A precision glass ball placed between LD and fiber focuses the beam. Effective for moderate working distances. Typical $\eta \approx 30$–$50\%$. Sensitive to axial positioning.

**4. Cylindrical (Cigar) Lens:** Specifically compensates for the **astigmatism** of laser diodes (different beam waists in horizontal and vertical directions). Typical $\eta \approx 40$–$60\%$.

**5. GRIN (Gradient-Index) Lens:** A quarter-pitch GRIN rod lens collimates the LD output and focuses it into the SMF core. Provides long working distance and excellent mode matching. Typical $\eta \approx 50$–$80\%$. Used in commercial laser diode modules.

**Comparison Table (to be drawn in exam):**

| Scheme | Typical $\eta$ | Working Distance | Astigmatism Compensation | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Butt | 10–40% | ≈ 0 µm | No | Low |
| Microlens | 30–60% | 5–20 µm | Limited | Medium |
| Spherical | 30–50% | 1–5 mm | No | Medium |
| Cylindrical | 40–60% | 0.5–2 mm | Yes | High |
| GRIN | 50–80% | 2–10 mm | Yes | High |

**(7 marks for diagrams + table + comparative analysis.)**

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **fiber-to-fiber coupling** problem in optical communication systems. Explain the role of **Fresnel reflection** at the joint interface and how it can be minimized. A silica fiber ($n_1 = 1.46$) is joined to another silica fiber with an air gap. Calculate the **Fresnel loss per end-face in dB**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In practice, optical fiber systems require many **fiber-to-fiber joints** — at repeaters, at terminal equipment, at patch panels, and at splices. Each joint introduces loss due to:

- **Fresnel reflection** at the air-glass interface.
- **Mechanical misalignments** (lateral, angular, longitudinal).
- **Core diameter and NA mismatch** between dissimilar fibers.

**Fresnel Reflection Derivation:**

At a dielectric interface (glass to air), the power reflection coefficient at normal incidence is:

$$R = \left(\frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1.46 - 1.00}{1.46 + 1.00}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.46}{2.46}\right)^2 = (0.187)^2 = 0.035$$

The transmitted fraction is $T = 1 - R = 0.965$.

The Fresnel loss in dB is:

$$L_F = -10 \log_{10}(1 - R) = -10 \log_{10}(0.965) = -10 \times (-0.01554) = 0.155 \text{ dB}$$

**Methods to Minimize Fresnel Loss:**

1. **Index-matching gel/liquid** ($n \approx 1.46$) inserted into the air gap, eliminating the index discontinuity.
2. **Fusion splicing** (arc melting), which physically fuses the two fibers, leaving no air gap.
3. **Anti-reflection (AR) coatings** (e.g., MgF$_2$, $n \approx 1.38$, $\lambda/4$ thickness) deposited on the fiber end-faces.
4. **Physical contact (PC) connectors** that ensure the fiber end-faces are in direct optical contact (no air gap).

**(7 marks: 2 for concept, 3 for Fresnel calculation, 2 for minimization methods.)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write $R = (n_1 - n_2)/(n_1 + n_2)$ instead of squaring it for the **power** coefficient. Remember: amplitude coefficient vs. power coefficient are different. Also, for a **joint** (two end-faces), the total Fresnel loss is **2 × 0.155 dB ≈ 0.31 dB** unless index-matched.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Coupling with Fibres":**

- **Coupling efficiency** $\eta = P_{\text{fiber}} / P_s$ is dimensionless and always $\le 1$.
- **Insertion loss** $L_i = -10 \log_{10}(\eta)$ in dB.
- **Lambertian source** has constant radiance: $L = P_s / (\pi A_s)$.
- **Maximum LED-to-MMF coupling** = $(NA)^2$ (NA-limited case, source smaller than fiber core).
- **General LED-to-MMF coupling** = $(A_f / A_s) \cdot (NA)^2$ (area-limited case).
- **Fresnel reflection** at glass-air interface: $R = [(n_1 - n_2)/(n_1 + n_2)]^2 \approx 0.035$ for silica, giving ≈ 0.16 dB per end.
- **Lateral misalignment loss** $\eta_{\text{lat}} \approx 1 - 4x/(3\pi a)$ for small offsets in step-index MMF.
- **Angular tilt loss** scales as $\theta^2$; extremely critical for SMF.
- **Longitudinal gap loss** scales as $(z \cdot NA / a)^2$; also includes Fresnel term.
- **Four lensing schemes:** Butt, Microlens, Spherical, Cylindrical, GRIN — remember GRIN gives the highest efficiency (50–80%).
- **Laser-to-SMF coupling** requires lensing because of mode-field diameter mismatch (SMF MFD ≈ 9–10 µm).
- **Index-matching gel** eliminates Fresnel loss at fiber joints; **fusion splicing** physically removes the interface.
- **MT-RJ and MPO connectors** in data centres achieve sub-micron alignment with $< 0.3$ dB loss per mated pair.
- **Asymmetric coupling** (e.g., MMF-to-SMF) is generally inefficient and is rarely used; mode mismatch dominates.
- **Radiometric principle** underlying all coupling: $P_{\text{coupled}} = L \times A_{\text{overlap}} \times \Omega_{\text{accepted}}$.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always identify the **limiting factor** (NA or Area) first, then apply the appropriate formula. Misalignment losses are typically small for MMF (≈ 0.1–0.5 dB) but severe for SMF (≈ 1–5 dB) at the same physical offset.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Detectors: Quantum Efficiency and Responsivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optical Detectors: Quantum Efficiency and Responsivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

An **optical detector** is a semiconductor-based optoelectronic device that converts incident optical (light) signals into measurable electrical signals (current or voltage) through the **internal photoelectric effect**. The two fundamental figures of merit that characterize the conversion efficiency of any photodetector — PIN photodiode, Avalanche Photodiode (APD), or MSM photodetector — are **Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$)** and **Responsivity ($\mathcal{R}$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Scope):**
> *"Quantum Efficiency: The Quantum efficiency of an optical detector is defined as the probability that an incident photon of energy $h\nu$ will generate a photoelectron that contributes to the detector's output current."*

**Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$)** is a **dimensionless number** (often expressed as a percentage) that quantifies the *electron-per-photon conversion ratio* inside the active depletion region of the photodiode.

**Responsivity ($\mathcal{R}$)** is defined as the **ratio of the generated photocurrent ($I_p$) to the incident optical power ($P_{in}$)** at a given wavelength.

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_p}{P_{in}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{Ampere}}{\text{Watt}} = \text{A/W} \right]$$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Collector" Intuition

Imagine a **farmer standing in a rainstorm** with a bucket. The raindrops are the **photons**, and the bucket represents the **depletion region** of the photodiode.

- **Quantum Efficiency** is like asking: *"Out of every 100 raindrops that fall on the bucket's mouth, how many actually end up as water collected inside?"* Some drops splash out, some miss the funnel, and some are absorbed by the walls. Only a fraction $\eta$ are "captured" and counted.
- **Responsivity** is like asking: *"For every liter of rainwater actually pouring in per second, what is the rate of water collected?"* It links the captured water (current) back to the total rainfall intensity (optical power).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Quantum efficiency answers *"How efficient is the photon-to-electron conversion?"* while Responsivity answers *"How much electrical current do I get per watt of light I shine in?"* They are mathematically coupled through Planck's constant and the wavelength of light.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (KTU 2024 Reference Data)

| Symbol | Quantity | Value | Unit |
|--------|----------|-------|------|
| $q$ | Electron charge | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C |
| $h$ | Planck's constant | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |

---

### 1.4 Types of Quantum Efficiency (External vs Internal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Frequently Tests This Distinction:**

**External Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_{ext}$)** — Also called **Device Quantum Efficiency** — is measured with respect to *all photons incident on the photodetector's physical surface*, including those lost to surface reflection at the air-semiconductor interface.

**Internal Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_{int}$)** — Considers only the photons that *successfully entered* the semiconductor (i.e., excluding reflection losses). The relationship is:

$$\eta_{ext} = (1 - R_f) \cdot \eta_{int}$$

where $R_f$ is the Fresnel reflection coefficient at the entrance facet. For a silicon-air interface, $R_f \approx 0.3$ (≈ 30% loss), which is why **anti-reflection (AR) coatings** are essential in practical photodetectors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Photon-to-Current Conversion

A photon incident on a photodiode goes through **four sequential events** to contribute to the photocurrent. Each event has a probability of success, and the cumulative product gives the quantum efficiency:

1. **Photon Arrival & Surface Reflection:** The photon strikes the detector surface. With probability $(1 - R_f)$, it is *transmitted* into the semiconductor. With probability $R_f$, it is *reflected back* and lost.
2. **Bulk Absorption:** The photon travels through the depletion region. With absorption coefficient $\alpha(\lambda)$, the probability of being absorbed in the active region of width $w$ is given by the absorption factor. For a depletion-layer-only PIN diode, this is approximated as unity at the peak responsivity wavelength.
3. **Electron-Hole Pair Generation:** The absorbed photon transfers its energy $h\nu$ to a valence-band electron, elevating it to the conduction band. This generates **one electron-hole pair (EHP)**. If $h\nu < E_g$ (bandgap), the photon passes through unabsorbed. If $h\nu \gg E_g$, excess energy is lost as heat.
4. **Carrier Collection:** The generated electron and hole must be swept across the depletion region by the reverse-bias electric field *before* they recombine. The probability of successful collection is the **carrier collection efficiency**, $\eta_c$.

Combining these four probabilities:

$$\eta = (1 - R_f) \cdot \zeta \cdot \eta_c$$

where $\zeta$ is the absorption quantum efficiency (fraction of transmitted photons absorbed in the active layer).

> [!NOTE]
> **For an Ideal PIN Photodiode:** $\eta_c \approx 1$ (all generated carriers are swept out by the high reverse-bias field), $R_f$ is minimized by AR coatings, and absorption is engineered to be near-unity at the operating wavelength. In that case, $\eta \to 1$ (i.e., 100%).

---

### 2.2 The Master Photocurrent Equation

If **$P_{in}$** is the incident optical power and the photon energy is **$h\nu = hc/\lambda$**, then the number of photons per second striking the detector is:

$$N_{photon/sec} = \frac{P_{in}}{h\nu} = \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}$$

The number of electrons generated per second (assuming a quantum efficiency $\eta$) is:

$$N_{e/sec} = \eta \cdot \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}$$

Multiplying by electron charge $q$ gives the **primary photocurrent** $I_p$ (excluding the avalanche multiplication factor $M$ used in APDs):

$$I_p = \eta q \cdot \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}$$

For an APD, the **total multiplied photocurrent** becomes $I_p = M \eta q P_{in} \lambda / hc$, where $M$ is the avalanche multiplication factor.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Gold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it appears in 80%+ of KTU Optical Communication questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Name / Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{e}}{N_{ph}}$ | Quantum Efficiency (electrons per photon) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{I_p / q}{P_{in} / h\nu}$ | Quantum Efficiency (from measurable quantities) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{I_p}{P_{in}}$ | Responsivity | A/W |
| 4 | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | **Master Responsivity Equation** (derived) | A/W |
| 5 | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{\eta q}{h\nu} = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | Same as (4), two forms | A/W |
| 6 | $I_p = M \eta q \dfrac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}$ | APD multiplied photocurrent | A |
| 7 | $\eta_{ext} = (1 - R_f)\eta_{int}$ | External vs internal QE | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R_f = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$ | Fresnel reflection (Si-air: $n_1=3.5, n_2=1$) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | Cutoff wavelength (long-$\lambda$ edge) | µm |
| 10 | $\eta = 1 - e^{-\alpha w}$ | Absorption QE in a depletion region of width $w$ | dimensionless |

**Key Engineering Rule of Thumb:**

> The responsivity $\mathcal{R}$ **increases linearly with wavelength $\lambda$** in the ideal case, reaching its maximum just before the cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c$. Beyond $\lambda_c$, the photon energy falls below the bandgap, the semiconductor becomes transparent, and $\eta \to 0$, causing $\mathcal{R} \to 0$.

**Cutoff Wavelength (in micrometers):** $\lambda_c (\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$

| Material | $E_g$ (eV) | $\lambda_c$ (µm) | Typical Application |
|----------|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Silicon (Si) | 1.12 | 1.107 | 850 nm band |
| Germanium (Ge) | 0.67 | 1.850 | 1300 nm band |
| InGaAs | 0.75 | 1.653 | 1310, 1550 nm bands |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical Fiber Receivers:** Responsivity directly determines the **receiver sensitivity** — a higher $\mathcal{R}$ means more photocurrent for the same incident power, easing the SNR requirement on the front-end transimpedance amplifier (TIA).
- **Optical Power Meters:** Calibrated photodiodes with known $\mathcal{R}(\lambda)$ are used as primary optical power standards in fiber-optic test equipment.
- **Solar Cells:** Operate on exactly the same physical principle; $\eta$ is called **energy conversion efficiency** in that context.
- **LiDAR & Free-Space Optics:** Responsivity at the operating laser wavelength (often 905 nm or 1550 nm) is a critical spec in the detector datasheet.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Responsivity from Quantum Efficiency (The Master Derivation)

**Objective:** Starting from first principles, derive the closed-form expression $\mathcal{R} = \eta q \lambda / hc$.

**Step 1 — Define the input optical flux.**
Let $P_{in}$ (watts) be the optical power incident on the photodetector. Since each photon carries an energy $E_{ph} = h\nu = hc/\lambda$, the **rate of photon incidence** (photons per second) is:

$$n_{ph} = \frac{P_{in}}{h\nu} = \frac{P_{in}}{hc/\lambda} = \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}$$

*Conversion Logic:* We divide total power by the energy per photon to get the count of photons arriving per unit time.

**Step 2 — Apply Quantum Efficiency.**
By definition, $\eta$ is the probability that an incident photon generates a collected electron. Therefore, the rate of electron generation (electrons per second) is:

$$n_{e} = \eta \cdot n_{ph} = \eta \cdot \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Multiplication by $\eta$ (a probability) yields the *successful* carrier generation rate.

**Step 3 — Convert electron rate to electrical current.**
Current is defined as the rate of charge flow. Since each electron carries charge $q$:

$$I_p = n_{e} \cdot q = \eta \cdot \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc} \cdot q$$

$$\boxed{I_p = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc} \, P_{in}}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain Responsivity.**
By definition, $\mathcal{R} = I_p / P_{in}$. Dividing both sides by $P_{in}$:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_p}{P_{in}} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$$

$$\boxed{\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc} = \frac{\eta q}{h\nu}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation shows that for a fixed $\eta$, doubling the wavelength doubles the responsivity. In practice, $\eta$ itself drops at long wavelengths (near cutoff) and at short wavelengths (surface absorption dominates), producing a characteristic "bell-shaped" $\mathcal{R}$ vs. $\lambda$ curve seen in every photodiode datasheet.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cutoff Wavelength from Bandgap Energy

**Step 1.** A photon is absorbed only if its energy $h\nu$ exceeds the semiconductor bandgap $E_g$.

$$h\nu \geq E_g$$

**Step 2.** At the threshold (cutoff) wavelength $\lambda_c$, equality holds:

$$\frac{hc}{\lambda_c} = E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute numerical values of $h$ and $c$ to obtain a useful engineering formula. Since $hc = (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8}) = 1.986 \times 10^{-25}$ J·m, and $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J, we get:

$$\lambda_c = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot E_g(\text{eV})} = \frac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{E_g(\text{eV})} \text{ meters}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_c \,(\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}}$$

**Example:** For Silicon with $E_g = 1.12$ eV, $\lambda_c = 1.24 / 1.12 = 1.107$ µm → which is why Si detectors are blind beyond ~1100 nm.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

> **Question (Typical 7-mark KTU pattern):** A silicon PIN photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 65% at a wavelength of 850 nm. Calculate (i) the responsivity, and (ii) the photocurrent generated when 10 µW of optical power is incident.

**Given Data:**
$\eta = 0.65$, $\lambda = 850 \text{ nm} = 850 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$, $P_{in} = 10 \text{ µW} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$

**Constants:**
$q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s

---

**Part (i): Responsivity $\mathcal{R}$**

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{(0.65)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(850 \times 10^{-9})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}$$

Numerator evaluation:
$$(0.65)(1.602 \times 10^{-19}) = 1.0413 \times 10^{-19}$$
$$1.0413 \times 10^{-19} \times 850 \times 10^{-9} = 885.105 \times 10^{-28} = 8.851 \times 10^{-26}$$

Denominator evaluation:
$$(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8}) = 19.878 \times 10^{-26} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}$$

Final division:
$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{8.851 \times 10^{-26}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.4453 \text{ A/W}$$

$$\boxed{\mathcal{R} \approx 0.445 \text{ A/W}}$$

---

**Part (ii): Photocurrent $I_p$**

$$I_p = \mathcal{R} \cdot P_{in} = (0.4453)(10 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$\boxed{I_p = 4.453 \text{ µA}}$$

---

**Cross-Check Using Direct Formula:**

$$I_p = \eta \cdot q \cdot \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc} = 0.65 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6})(850 \times 10^{-9})}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}$$

$$= 0.65 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times \frac{8.5 \times 10^{-12}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}$$

$$= 0.65 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times (4.276 \times 10^{13})$$

$$= 4.453 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} = 4.453 \text{ µA} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Both methods agree — this is the KTU-recommended verification step in board exams. Always cross-check numerical answers using two routes.**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Detector Types

| Parameter | PIN Photodiode | Avalanche Photodiode (APD) | Unit |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------------|------|
| Internal Gain $M$ | 1 | 10 to 100 | dimensionless |
| Multiplied Photocurrent $I_p$ | $\eta q P_{in} \lambda / hc$ | $M \eta q P_{in} \lambda / hc$ | A |
| Effective Responsivity $\mathcal{R}_{eff}$ | $\eta q \lambda / hc$ | $M \eta q \lambda / hc$ | A/W |
| Typical $\eta$ | 60 – 90% | 60 – 90% | % |
| Typical $\mathcal{R}$ at 1550 nm | 0.7 – 0.95 | 7 – 15 (with $M$) | A/W |
| Excess Noise Factor $F(M)$ | 1 | $M^x$ (0 < x < 1) | dimensionless |
| Bias Voltage | Low (5 V) | High (30 – 70 V) | V |
| Application | Short-haul, low-cost | Long-haul, high-sensitivity | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Photon-to-Current Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident Optical Power P_in] --> B[Surface Reflection Loss 1 minus R_f]
    B --> C[Photon Transmission into Semiconductor]
    C --> D[Absorption in Depletion Region alpha, w]
    D --> E[Electron Hole Pair Generation EHP]
    E --> F[Carrier Collection Efficiency eta_c]
    F --> G[Primary Photocurrent I_p]
    G --> H{Avalanche Region M equals 1 or M greater than 1}
    H --> I[PIN Output I_p]
    H --> J[APD Output M times I_p]
    G --> K[Quantum Efficiency eta equals I_p over q divided by P_in over h nu]
    A --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow moves left-to-right. The first three blocks account for optical losses, the next two convert absorbed photons into carriers, and the final decision block distinguishes PIN from APD operation. The Quantum Efficiency block is computed from the final photocurrent and the original optical input.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Photodiode Receiver Front-End

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph opt[Optical Domain]
        L1[Laser Source lambda nu] -->|P_in| PD[Photodiode PIN or APD]
    end
    subgraph elec[Electrical Domain]
        PD -->|I_p| TIA[Transimpedance Amplifier]
        TIA --> LIM[Limiting Amplifier]
        LIM --> CDR[Clock and Data Recovery]
        CDR --> OUT[Digital Output Bits]
    end
    PD -.->|Defines| RESP[Responsivity R equals I_p over P_in]
    PD -.->|Defines| QE[Quantum Efficiency eta]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Decision Logic for Choosing Detector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Operating wavelength?} -->|850 nm| Q2{Si available? Use Si PIN}
    Q1 -->|1310 to 1550 nm| Q3{Need high sensitivity?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[Use InGaAs APD with M around 10]
    Q3 -->|No, cost-sensitive| Q5[Use InGaAs PIN]
    Q2 --> Q6[Compute R equals eta q lambda over hc]
    Q4 --> Q6
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 --> Q7[Compare with datasheet spec]
    Q7 --> Q8{Within tolerance?}
    Q8 -->|Yes| Q9[Accept Design]
    Q8 -->|No| Q10[Add AR Coating or Increase Reverse Bias]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to *"design a detector for a given wavelength"*, the cut-off equation $\lambda_c = 1.24 / E_g(\text{eV})$ is your first filter. Then use the responsivity equation to size the photocurrent and SNR margin.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Q.A1.** Define quantum efficiency and responsivity of an optical detector. State the relationship between them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$):** It is defined as the ratio of the number of electrons generated and collected at the output terminal of the photodetector to the number of incident photons at a given wavelength. It is a dimensionless quantity, often expressed as a percentage. (1.5 marks)

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Number of electrons collected/second}}{\text{Number of incident photons/second}} = \frac{I_p / q}{P_{in} / h\nu}$$

- **Responsivity ($\mathcal{R}$):** It is defined as the ratio of the generated photocurrent to the incident optical power at a given wavelength. Unit: A/W. (1 mark)

- **Relationship:** $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ — i.e., responsivity is directly proportional to quantum efficiency and the operating wavelength. (0.5 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q.A2.** An InGaAs photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 80% at 1550 nm. Calculate its responsivity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $\eta = 0.80$, $\lambda = 1550 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc} = \frac{(0.80)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(1550 \times 10^{-9})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}$$

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} \approx 0.999 \text{ A/W}$$

$$\boxed{\mathcal{R} \approx 1.0 \text{ A/W}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum Theoretical Responsivity** at 1550 nm: $\mathcal{R}_{max} = q\lambda/hc = 1.269$ A/W. The 0.80 QE gives ~80% of this ideal value.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Module-3 Specific KTU Pattern: Part (a) = 7 marks, Part (b) = 7 marks**

---

#### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the photon-to-current conversion process in a semiconductor photodetector. Derive the expression for responsivity in terms of quantum efficiency and wavelength. (7 marks)

**(b)** A germanium photodiode is used to detect light at 1300 nm. The measured photocurrent is 5 µA when the incident optical power is 8 µW. Calculate (i) the responsivity, (ii) the quantum efficiency, and (iii) the cutoff wavelength of Ge ($E_g = 0.67$ eV). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Photon-to-Current Conversion (7 marks):**

The conversion chain consists of the following sequential events (shown in the block diagram of Section 4.1):

1. **Photon Incidence:** $P_{in}$ watts arrive at the photodetector surface. Photon rate = $P_{in} \lambda / hc$ photons/sec. (1 mark)
2. **Surface Reflection Loss:** A fraction $R_f$ is reflected. AR coatings reduce this loss; transmitted fraction = $(1 - R_f)$. (1 mark)
3. **Absorption:** In the depletion region of width $w$, photons are absorbed with probability $1 - e^{-\alpha w}$ governed by the absorption coefficient $\alpha(\lambda)$. (1 mark)
4. **EHP Generation:** Each absorbed photon ($h\nu \geq E_g$) creates one electron-hole pair. (1 mark)
5. **Carrier Collection:** The reverse-bias field sweeps electrons and holes to the contacts. The collection efficiency $\eta_c$ accounts for recombination losses. (1 mark)

**Master Equation:**

$$\eta = (1 - R_f)(1 - e^{-\alpha w}) \eta_c \quad \text{(1 mark for formula)}$$

**Derivation of Responsivity** (already shown in Section 3.1, summarized): From $I_p = \eta q P_{in} \lambda / hc$ and $\mathcal{R} = I_p / P_{in}$:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $I_p = 5$ µA, $P_{in} = 8$ µW, $\lambda = 1300$ nm, $E_g = 0.67$ eV.

**(i) Responsivity:**

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_p}{P_{in}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.625 \text{ A/W} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Quantum Efficiency:**

Rearranging $\mathcal{R} = \eta q \lambda / hc$:

$$\eta = \frac{\mathcal{R} \cdot hc}{q \lambda} = \frac{(0.625)(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(1300 \times 10^{-9})}$$

$$\eta = \frac{1.242 \times 10^{-25}}{2.083 \times 10^{-25}} \approx 0.5965$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 59.65\%} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**(iii) Cutoff Wavelength:**

$$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} = \frac{1.24}{0.67} \approx 1.851 \text{ µm} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

Since 1300 nm < 1851 nm, the Ge photodiode can detect this signal efficiently.

---

#### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** Compare the responsivity characteristics of PIN photodiode and Avalanche Photodiode (APD). Derive the expression for the multiplied photocurrent in an APD and explain the role of multiplication factor $M$ on receiver sensitivity. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Si PIN photodiode (bandgap 1.12 eV) has a quantum efficiency of 70% at 900 nm. If 5 µW of optical power is incident, determine (i) the responsivity, (ii) the photocurrent, and (iii) comment on whether 900 nm lies within the operating range of the Si detector. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PIN vs APD Responsivity Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | PIN | APD |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Multiplication $M$ | 1 | 10–100 |
| Responsivity | $\eta q \lambda / hc$ | $M \eta q \lambda / hc$ |
| Photocurrent | $\eta q P_{in} \lambda / hc$ | $M \eta q P_{in} \lambda / hc$ |
| Noise | Low | Higher (excess noise) |
| Bias | Low | High reverse bias |

**Multiplied Photocurrent Derivation:**
In the APD's high-field multiplication region, a primary photoelectron is accelerated and gains enough kinetic energy to create *secondary* EHPs by impact ionization. If $M$ is the average number of electrons collected per primary electron:

$$I_p(\text{APD}) = M \cdot \eta q \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Role of $M$ on Sensitivity:** Increasing $M$ multiplies the signal current, raising SNR at the receiver. However, the multiplication process is stochastic, introducing **excess shot noise** $F(M) = M^x$ ($0 < x < 1$). There is an *optimum $M$* that maximizes SNR — beyond this, noise dominates. Typical optimum $M$ is 10–20 for InGaAs APDs at 1550 nm. (2 marks for explanation)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $\eta = 0.70$, $\lambda = 900$ nm, $P_{in} = 5$ µW, $E_g = 1.12$ eV (Si).

**(i) Responsivity:**

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc} = \frac{(0.70)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(900 \times 10^{-9})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}$$

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{1.009 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.5075 \text{ A/W}$$

$$\boxed{\mathcal{R} \approx 0.508 \text{ A/W}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Photocurrent:**

$$I_p = \mathcal{R} \cdot P_{in} = (0.508)(5 \times 10^{-6}) = 2.54 \text{ µA}$$

$$\boxed{I_p = 2.54 \text{ µA}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Operating Range Check:**

$$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{1.12} = 1.107 \text{ µm} = 1107 \text{ nm}$$

Since 900 nm < 1107 nm, **the wavelength lies within the operating range** of the silicon photodiode. The detector will absorb these photons efficiently. (3 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** $hc$ in the denominator is $1.9878 \times 10^{-25}$ J·m, *not* $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s alone. Mixing units loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "external" and "internal" QE:** If a problem mentions "AR coating" or surface reflection, the formula $\eta_{ext} = (1 - R_f) \eta_{int}$ is required. Many students write only one $\eta$ and lose the distinction mark.
> 3. **Using wavelength in nm without conversion:** Always convert nm → m ($\times 10^{-9}$) before substituting. A common 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **APD vs PIN confusion:** In APD problems, the photocurrent has an extra factor of $M$. Forgetting $M$ in $\mathcal{R}_{APD}$ is a frequent 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Cutoff wavelength check:** When asked "will the detector work?", always compute $\lambda_c$ and *compare* — don't just compute the value and stop.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$):** Probability of photon-to-electron conversion, dimensionless (0 to 1). (1)
- **Responsivity ($\mathcal{R}$):** Photocurrent per unit incident optical power, in A/W. (2)
- **Master Equation:** $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ — memorize this verbatim. (3)
- **Two Equivalent Forms:** $\mathcal{R} = \eta q \lambda / hc$ and $\mathcal{R} = \eta q / h\nu$. (4)
- **Wavelength Linearity:** For a given $\eta$, $\mathcal{R}$ is *linearly* proportional to $\lambda$. (5)
- **Cutoff Wavelength:** $\lambda_c (\mu m) = 1.24 / E_g(\text{eV})$ — sets the long-wavelength detection edge. (6)
- **External vs Internal QE:** $\eta_{ext} = (1 - R_f) \eta_{int}$ — accounts for surface reflection. (7)
- **APD Multiplied Current:** $I_p = M \eta q P_{in} \lambda / hc$, where $M$ is the multiplication factor (typically 10–100). (8)
- **Unit Conversions:** $hc = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}$ J·m. Always use SI units (m, s, J, C) in formulas. (9)
- **Bell-Shaped $\mathcal{R}$–$\lambda$ Curve:** Real photodiodes have a peak responsivity wavelength; below it surface recombination kills QE, above it the bandgap edge kills QE. (10)
- **Si vs Ge vs InGaAs:** Choose material based on cutoff — Si for 850 nm, Ge/InGaAs for 1310/1550 nm. (11)
- **AR Coatings Critical:** Without AR coating, ~30% of incident photons are lost to Fresnel reflection at Si-air interface. (12)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure and Working of PIN and APD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structure and Working of PIN and APD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Photodetectors in Optical Communication: The Bridge Between Light and Electricity

In an optical fiber communication system, the transmitted information travels as pulses of light (photons) through a silica fiber. However, at the receiving end, the signal must be converted back into the electrical domain so that it can be amplified, processed, and decoded by electronic circuits. The device that performs this critical **optical-to-electrical (O/E) conversion** is called a **photodetector**.

Among the various semiconductor photodetectors, the two most widely used devices in high-speed optical communication receivers are the **PIN Photodiode** and the **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)**. Both belong to the family of **reverse-biased p-n junction devices** that operate on the principle of the **photovoltaic effect**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Optical Sources & Detectors):**
> The student must be able to draw the layered cross-section, explain the carrier transport mechanism, derive the responsivity equation, and quantitatively compare PIN vs APD for a given optical receiver design problem.

---

### 1.2 Formal Definition

**PIN Photodiode:**
A **PIN photodiode** is a semiconductor photodetector consisting of three distinct regions — a **P-region**, a wide **Intrinsic (I) region**, and an **N-region** — sandwiched between two heavily doped ohmic contacts. The intrinsic layer is lightly doped (nearly pure semiconductor) and acts as the wide depletion region in which most of the photon absorption and electron-hole pair generation takes place.

**Avalanche Photodiode (APD):**
An **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** is a highly reverse-biased photodetector that employs an additional **multiplication region** where photo-generated carriers undergo **impact ionization**, producing an internal current gain through a controlled avalanche process. This gives APDs a much higher sensitivity than PIN photodiodes.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Coin-Drop on Stairs" Analogy for PIN vs APD**

Imagine you are standing at the top of a staircase and gently dropping a single marble down each step.

- **PIN Photodiode:** One photon enters the device, and it produces **exactly one electron-hole pair** (one marble reaches the bottom). The current generated per photon is fixed and modest. There is no internal amplification.

- **APD (Avalanche Photodiode):** Here the staircase is much taller, and the steps are much higher. When one marble drops, its impact energy is so high that it **knocks out additional marbles** on the way down. By the time it reaches the bottom, there might be 10 or even 100 marbles — this is **avalanche multiplication**. The single photon has produced many electrons, hence a much larger current.

This internal gain of APD is the key reason it is preferred in **long-haul, low-light applications** where every photon counts.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard physical constants govern the operation of both PIN and APD:

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Planck's constant | $h$ | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** |
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Electron charge | $q$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |
| Wavelength-to-frequency conversion | $\nu = c/\lambda$ | — |
| Photon energy | $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bandgap Energy Threshold ($E_g$):** Only photons whose energy $h\nu \ge E_g$ can generate an electron-hole pair. For Silicon $E_g \approx 1.12$ eV, for InGaAs $E_g \approx 0.75$ eV (the standard detector material for the **1.3 µm and 1.55 µm** optical communication windows).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structure of a PIN Photodiode

The PIN photodiode is fabricated as a layered semiconductor sandwich:

1. **P⁺ Layer (Top):** Heavily doped p-type semiconductor (e.g., p⁺-Si or p⁺-InP). This layer is made very thin so that most photons can pass through it without being absorbed.

2. **Intrinsic (I) Layer (Middle):** A very lightly doped (almost pure) region of the same semiconductor material. This layer is the **heart of the device**. Under reverse bias, the depletion region extends almost entirely across this intrinsic layer, creating a strong, uniform electric field.

3. **N⁺ Layer (Bottom):** Heavily doped n-type semiconductor forming the substrate. The n⁺ layer is comparatively thick.

4. **Ohmic Contacts:** Metallic electrodes are formed on top of the p⁺ layer and at the bottom of the n⁺ layer for external electrical connection.

### 2.2 Working Principle of PIN Photodiode

**Step-by-step operating logic:**

- **Step 1 — Reverse Bias Application:** A reverse bias voltage (typically –5 V to –20 V) is applied across the device. Because the intrinsic layer is undoped, the entire applied voltage drops across it, creating a strong, uniform electric field.

- **Step 2 — Photon Absorption:** Light from the fiber is focused onto the p⁺ layer (or directly onto the intrinsic layer in some designs). Photons with energy $h\nu \ge E_g$ penetrate into the intrinsic region.

- **Step 3 — Electron-Hole Pair Generation:** Each absorbed photon transfers its energy to a valence-band electron, exciting it to the conduction band and creating **one electron-hole pair (EHP)**. This is the fundamental photoelectric process.

- **Step 4 — Carrier Drift:** The strong electric field in the depletion region immediately sweeps the electron toward the n-side and the hole toward the p-side. Because the intrinsic region is fully depleted, carriers travel at **saturation drift velocity**, ensuring very fast response.

- **Step 5 — Photocurrent Generation:** The flow of these photo-generated carriers constitutes the photocurrent $I_p$ in the external circuit, which is directly proportional to the incident optical power $P_{in}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Insight:** The intrinsic layer must be **thick enough** to absorb most of the incident photons (high quantum efficiency) but **thin enough** so that carrier transit time is short (high bandwidth). This is the classic **quantum-efficiency vs. bandwidth trade-off** in PIN design.

---

### 2.3 Structure of an Avalanche Photodiode (APD)

The APD has a more complex, multi-layer structure designed to spatially separate the **absorption** and **multiplication** functions:

1. **P⁺ Layer (Top):** Heavily doped p-type contact layer.

2. **Multiplication Region (M):** A thin, lightly doped region where the electric field is extremely high (≥ 10⁵ V/cm). This is where **impact ionization** occurs.

3. **Absorption Region (π or ν):** A wide intrinsic or lightly doped region where most of the photons are absorbed and primary electron-hole pairs are generated.

4. **N⁺ Layer (Bottom):** Heavily doped n-type substrate.

A common configuration is the **SAM (Separated Absorption and Multiplication) APD**, where the absorption region (InGaAs, $E_g = 0.75$ eV) and multiplication region (InP, $E_g = 1.35$ eV) are made of different materials to optimize both efficiency and low-noise multiplication.

### 2.4 Working Principle of APD

- **Step 1 — Photon Absorption:** Incident photons are absorbed in the absorption region, generating primary electron-hole pairs.

- **Step 2 — Carrier Drift to Multiplication Region:** The primary electrons drift into the high-field multiplication region under the influence of the strong reverse bias (typically –20 V to –200 V).

- **Step 3 — Impact Ionization:** Within the multiplication region, the drifting electron (or hole) acquires kinetic energy from the strong electric field. When this energy exceeds the **threshold for impact ionization**, the carrier collides with the crystal lattice and ionizes a bound electron, generating a **secondary electron-hole pair**. The original carrier also continues its journey.

- **Step 4 — Avalanche Multiplication:** Each newly generated carrier is itself accelerated by the field and may cause further ionizations. The result is a chain reaction — an **avalanche** — that multiplies the original photocurrent by a factor $M$.

- **Step 5 — Output Current:** The total output current is $I_{out} = M \cdot I_{primary}$, where $M$ is the **avalanche multiplication factor** (typically 10 to 100).

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Definition of Terms | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules (eV) |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Number of EHPs generated}}{\text{Number of incident photons}}$ | Quantum Efficiency (0 ≤ η ≤ 1) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta = 1 - e^{-\alpha w}$ | Quantum efficiency of absorption layer of width $w$, where $\alpha$ is absorption coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{I_p}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{\eta q}{h\nu} = \dfrac{\eta \lambda}{1.24}$ | **Responsivity** of photodetector | A/W |
| 5 | $I_p = R \cdot P_{in}$ | Primary photocurrent equation | A |
| 6 | $I_{APD} = M \cdot I_p$ | APD output current with avalanche gain $M$ | A |
| 7 | $R_{APD} = M \cdot R = \dfrac{M \eta \lambda}{1.24}$ | APD responsivity (enhanced by M) | A/W |
| 8 | $F(M) = M^x$ | **Excess Noise Factor**, where $x$ is the excess noise index (0.3 for Si, 0.7–1.0 for InGaAs) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{M^2 R^2 P_{in}^2}{2qM^{2+x}(I_p+I_d)B + \dfrac{4kT B}{R_L}}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio of APD receiver | dimensionless |
| 10 | $B = \dfrac{0.35}{t_{tr}}$ | Receiver bandwidth (approximation), where $t_{tr}$ is carrier transit time | Hz |
| 11 | $M = \dfrac{1}{1 - (V/V_{BR})^n}$ | Empirical multiplication factor (Emmons equation), $V_{BR}$ = breakdown voltage | dimensionless |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PIN Photodiodes:** Used in **short-reach (LAN), access networks, and optical storage (CD/DVD read heads)**. They are inexpensive, require low bias voltage, and have very stable operation.

- **APDs:** Used in **long-haul DWDM trunk lines, submarine cable systems, and high-sensitivity receivers** where signal power is extremely weak after long fiber runs. The internal gain (M ≈ 10–100) provides 5–10 dB of sensitivity improvement over PIN.

- Both detectors are also used in **LIDAR, quantum key distribution (QKD), and astronomical photometry**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quantum Efficiency of a PIN Photodiode

Let the incident optical power on the photodetector be $P_{in}$ at wavelength $\lambda$. The number of photons incident per second is:

$$
N_{photon} = \frac{P_{in}}{h\nu} = \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Energy per photon = $h\nu = hc/\lambda$. So the number of photons per second = total energy per second / energy per photon.

If a fraction $\eta$ of these photons is successfully absorbed and contributes to electron-hole pair generation, then the number of electron-hole pairs generated per second is:

$$
N_{EHP} = \eta \cdot N_{photon} = \frac{\eta P_{in} \lambda}{hc}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Definition of quantum efficiency — only η fraction of incident photons generate useful carriers; the rest are reflected, transmitted without absorption, or absorbed in non-active regions.

Each electron-hole pair contributes exactly one electron to the external current (since one photon liberates one electron). The number of electrons per second is $N_{EHP}$, so the current is:

$$
I_p = q \cdot N_{EHP} = \frac{\eta q P_{in} \lambda}{hc}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Current = charge × rate of charge flow. Each EHP contributes charge $q$ to the current every time it sweeps across the junction.

Rearranging for the ratio of output current to input optical power, we get the **responsivity**:

$$
R = \frac{I_p}{P_{in}} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}
$$

Substituting the numerical values of $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, and converting wavelength from metres to micrometres:

$$
R = \frac{\eta \cdot (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot \lambda(\mu m) \times 10^{-6}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}
$$

After simplification, the standard engineering form is:

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{\eta \lambda(\mu m)}{1.24} \;\; \text{A/W}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Internal Quantum Efficiency from Absorption Profile

Consider a photon entering the intrinsic absorption layer of thickness $w$. As the photon travels a distance $x$, the optical power decays exponentially according to the **Beer–Lambert Law**:

$$
P(x) = P_{in} \cdot e^{-\alpha x}
$$

The power absorbed in a thin slice $dx$ at depth $x$ is:

$$
dP = -P_{in} \cdot \alpha \cdot e^{-\alpha x} \, dx
$$

The number of photons absorbed in this slice per second is $dP / (h\nu)$. The number of EHPs generated per second in the slice is $(dP/h\nu) \cdot \eta_{local}$, assuming each absorbed photon generates one pair. Integrating over the full absorption width:

$$
N_{EHP} = \int_0^w \frac{P_{in} \alpha e^{-\alpha x}}{h\nu} dx = \frac{P_{in}}{h\nu} \left(1 - e^{-\alpha w}\right)
$$

Dividing by total incident photon rate:

$$
\eta = 1 - e^{-\alpha w}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Total absorbed fraction = 1 (full absorption) minus the unabsorbed fraction $e^{-\alpha w}$ that escapes through the back of the layer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: APD Multiplication Factor and Excess Noise

Let $M$ be the average multiplication factor. For a primary photocurrent $I_p$, the total APD output current is:

$$
I_{APD} = M \cdot I_p
$$

The **excess noise factor** $F(M)$ arises because avalanche multiplication is a **random stochastic process** — not every carrier is multiplied by exactly $M$. Statistically, the variance of the multiplied current exceeds the value predicted by a noiseless gain of $M^2$. The excess noise factor is defined as:

$$
F(M) = \frac{\langle M^2 \rangle}{\langle M \rangle^2} = M^x
$$

where $x$ is the **excess noise index**, determined by the ratio of ionization coefficients of electrons and holes in the multiplication region:

$$
x \approx \frac{\ln F(M)}{\ln M}
$$

For **silicon APDs**, electron ionization dominates, giving $x \approx 0.3$ (low noise). For **InGaAs APDs**, both electron and hole ionization contribute, giving $x \approx 0.7$ to $1.0$ (higher noise).

The total mean-square noise current of an APD receiver (Shot noise + Thermal noise) is:

$$
\langle i_n^2 \rangle = 2q M^{2+x}(I_p + I_d) B + \frac{4kT B}{R_L}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- The shot noise term is amplified by $M^2$ (signal power scaling) but degraded by an additional factor $M^x$ (excess multiplication noise).
- $I_d$ is the dark current (leakage current that flows even with no light).
- $B$ is the receiver electrical bandwidth.
- $4kTB/R_L$ is the Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise of the load resistor $R_L$.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: PIN vs APD

| Performance Parameter | PIN Photodiode | APD |
|---|---|---|
| Internal Gain ($M$) | **M = 1** (no internal gain) | **M = 10 – 100** (avalanche gain) |
| Responsivity ($R$) | $R = \eta\lambda/1.24$ A/W | $R = M \eta\lambda/1.24$ A/W (5–20× higher) |
| Reverse Bias Voltage | –5 V to –20 V (low) | –50 V to –200 V (high) |
| Sensitivity | Lower (–20 dBm typical) | Higher (–35 to –45 dBm typical) |
| Bandwidth | High (up to tens of GHz) | Moderate (limited by avalanche build-up time) |
| Excess Noise | **None** (F = 1) | Significant ($F = M^x$, $x = 0.3$–1.0) |
| Dark Current | Low (nA range) | Higher (µA range due to high bias) |
| Cost & Complexity | Low | High (requires HV bias, temperature control) |
| Operating Material | Si, Ge, InGaAs | InGaAs/InP (SAM-APD), Si |
| Typical Application | LANs, access networks, optical storage | Long-haul DWDM, submarine, LIDAR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define quantum efficiency and responsivity of a photodetector. Derive the relation between them.**

**Model Answer:**

**Quantum Efficiency (η):** It is defined as the ratio of the number of electron-hole pairs generated and collected to the number of incident photons.

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Number of EHPs generated}}{\text{Number of incident photons}}
$$

**Responsivity (R):** It is defined as the ratio of the output photocurrent $I_p$ to the incident optical power $P_{in}$.

$$
R = \frac{I_p}{P_{in}}
$$

**Relation:** If $P_{in}$ optical power is incident, the number of photons per second is $P_{in}/(h\nu)$, and each photon of energy $h\nu \ge E_g$ generates η electron-hole pairs per photon. Hence:

$$
I_p = q \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{P_{in}}{h\nu} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{P_{in}\lambda}{hc}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{I_p}{P_{in}} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc} = \frac{\eta \lambda(\mu m)}{1.24} \;\; \text{A/W}}
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1 mark), Derivation (2 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Why is an intrinsic layer introduced between the p and n regions of a PIN photodiode? Mention two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

In a normal p-n photodiode, the depletion region is narrow because the doping is heavy. This means very few photons can be absorbed in the depletion region, leading to low quantum efficiency.

In a PIN photodiode, a **wide intrinsic (I) layer** is inserted between the p⁺ and n⁺ regions. Under reverse bias, the depletion region now extends almost entirely across this intrinsic layer.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Wider depletion region → Higher quantum efficiency:** More photons are absorbed in the depletion region, generating more electron-hole pairs, hence higher responsivity.
2. **Lower junction capacitance → Higher bandwidth:** The wide intrinsic layer reduces the depletion capacitance $C_j = \varepsilon A/w$, enabling faster response and higher-speed operation.

**Marks Distribution:** Reason (1 mark), Two advantages (1 mark each).

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**QUESTION A [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Draw the cross-sectional structure of a PIN photodiode and explain its working principle with a neat sketch. Mention the role of the intrinsic layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PIN photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 70% at 1550 nm wavelength. Calculate (i) its responsivity, (ii) the photocurrent generated for an incident optical power of 10 µW, and (iii) the number of photons per second incident on the detector. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Structure and Working:**

The PIN photodiode consists of three layers:
- **P⁺ layer** (heavily doped, very thin)
- **Intrinsic I-layer** (lightly doped, wide)
- **N⁺ layer** (heavily doped substrate)

Two ohmic contacts are provided on the top and bottom.

**Working Principle:**
- Reverse bias is applied across the device, which extends the depletion region across the entire intrinsic layer.
- Incident light passes through the thin p⁺ layer and is absorbed in the intrinsic region.
- Each absorbed photon ($h\nu \ge E_g$) generates one electron-hole pair.
- The strong electric field in the intrinsic region sweeps the electron toward the n-side and the hole toward the p-side at saturation velocity.
- This constitutes the photocurrent $I_p$ in the external circuit.

**Role of Intrinsic Layer:** Increases the depletion width → higher quantum efficiency and lower junction capacitance → higher bandwidth.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $\eta = 0.70$, $\lambda = 1550$ nm = $1.55$ µm, $P_{in} = 10$ µW = $10 \times 10^{-6}$ W

**(i) Responsivity:**
$$
R = \frac{\eta \lambda(\mu m)}{1.24} = \frac{0.70 \times 1.55}{1.24}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 0.875 \;\; \text{A/W}}
$$

**(ii) Photocurrent:**
$$
I_p = R \cdot P_{in} = 0.875 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_p = 8.75 \;\; \mu\text{A}}
$$

**(iii) Number of photons per second:**
$$
N_{photon} = \frac{P_{in}}{h\nu} = \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.55 \times 10^{-6}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
N_{photon} = \frac{1.55 \times 10^{-11}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{photon} \approx 7.80 \times 10^{13} \;\; \text{photons/sec}}
$$

**Cross-check:** $I_p / q = 8.75 \times 10^{-6} / 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 5.46 \times 10^{13}$ electrons/sec. The ratio of electrons to photons = $5.46 \times 10^{13} / 7.80 \times 10^{13} = 0.70 = \eta$ ✓ (Validates the result.)

---

**QUESTION B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the structure and operation of an Avalanche Photodiode (APD). What is impact ionization? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An APD has a multiplication factor M = 50 and a primary responsivity of 0.8 A/W. It is used to detect an optical signal of 1 µW at 1300 nm. Calculate (i) the primary photocurrent, (ii) the APD output current, (iii) the excess noise factor if the excess noise index is x = 0.7. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Structure and Operation of APD:**

The APD has a multi-layer structure:
- **P⁺ layer** – top contact layer
- **Multiplication region (M)** – thin, lightly doped, very high electric field
- **Absorption region (π)** – wide, where photons are absorbed
- **N⁺ layer** – bottom substrate

In the SAM-APD, the absorption region uses InGaAs (Eg = 0.75 eV) for the 1300/1550 nm windows, while the multiplication region uses InP (Eg = 1.35 eV) for low-noise avalanche multiplication.

**Operation:**
- Photons are absorbed in the π-region, generating primary EHPs.
- The photo-electron drifts into the M-region under a high reverse bias (50–200 V).
- In the multiplication region, the strong electric field gives the electron enough kinetic energy to ionize a bound electron on collision → **impact ionization**.
- This creates a secondary EHP, and the chain reaction produces an **avalanche**.
- The output current is amplified by the multiplication factor M.

**Impact Ionization:** It is the process by which a high-energy carrier (electron or hole) collides with the crystal lattice and transfers enough energy to a bound valence electron to excite it to the conduction band, thereby generating a new electron-hole pair.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $M = 50$, $R_{primary} = 0.8$ A/W, $P_{in} = 1$ µW, $\lambda = 1300$ nm, $x = 0.7$.

**(i) Primary photocurrent:**
$$
I_p = R_{primary} \cdot P_{in} = 0.8 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_p = 0.8 \;\; \mu\text{A}}
$$

**(ii) APD output current:**
$$
I_{APD} = M \cdot I_p = 50 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{APD} = 40 \;\; \mu\text{A}}
$$

**(iii) Excess noise factor:**
$$
F(M) = M^x = 50^{0.7}
$$

Taking logarithm: $\log_{10}(F) = 0.7 \times \log_{10}(50) = 0.7 \times 1.6990 = 1.1893$

$$
F(M) = 10^{1.1893} \approx 15.46
$$

$$
\boxed{F(M) \approx 15.46}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Wavelength in formula $R = \eta\lambda/1.24$ MUST be in **µm**, not nm. Many students write $\lambda = 1300$ directly and get $R = 700$ A/W, which is a mark-losing blunder.
> 2. **APD output current ≠ Primary current:** Students frequently write $I_p = R \cdot P_{in}$ as the APD output, forgetting the multiplication factor $M$. Always multiply by $M$ for APD.
> 3. **Excess noise index x is NOT the multiplication factor x:** These are entirely different quantities; confusing them costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Quantum efficiency has no units:** Do not write "η = 70%" as 70 A/W; it's dimensionless.
> 5. **Photocurrent direction:** Photocurrent flows in the **reverse direction** under illumination. Don't confuse with forward-bias current.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ☐ **PIN = P⁺ – Intrinsic – N⁺**, three layers, used for general O/E conversion.
- ☐ **APD has an additional multiplication region** where avalanche multiplication occurs.
- ☐ **Quantum efficiency** = η = (EHPs generated)/(photons incident), dimensionless, 0 ≤ η ≤ 1.
- ☐ **Responsivity formula** (the most-frequently tested equation): $R = \eta\lambda/1.24$ A/W, with $\lambda$ in µm.
- ☐ **APD gain equation:** $I_{out} = M \cdot I_p$, $R_{APD} = M \cdot R_{PIN}$.
- ☐ **Excess noise factor:** $F(M) = M^x$; remember $x \approx 0.3$ for Si and $x \approx 0.7$–1.0 for InGaAs.
- ☐ **APD needs very high reverse bias** (50–200 V), PIN needs only 5–20 V.
- ☐ **APD provides better receiver sensitivity** (5–10 dB improvement) but at higher cost and noise.
- ☐ **Wavelength choice:** 850 nm uses Si PIN; 1300/1550 nm uses InGaAs PIN or SAM-APD.
- ☐ **SNR for APD** involves $M^2$ in numerator (signal power) and $M^{2+x}$ in denominator (shot noise).
- ☐ **Quantum efficiency limit** for absorption width $w$ and absorption coefficient $\alpha$: $\eta = 1 - e^{-\alpha w}$.
- ☐ **Bandwidth ≈ 0.35 / transit time**: Wide depletion regions improve absorption but reduce bandwidth.
- ☐ **Photocurrent direction**: flows in the reverse direction (opposite to forward diode current).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Receivers: Direct Detection and Noise in Detectors
BER Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** BER Analysis # BER Analysis — Module 3 | Optical Communication ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **BER (Bit Error Rate)** is defined as the ratio of the number of bits received in error to the total number of bits transmitted over a specific time interval. It is a dimensionless quantity expressed as a fraction, a power of ten, or as a percentage. Mathematically, the formal KTU 2024 syllabus definition is: $$BER = \frac{N_e}{N_t}$$ Where: - $N_e$ = Number of bits received in error - $N_t$ = Total number of bits transmitted The corresponding performance metric expressed in exponential form is: $$BER = \frac{1}{2} \text{erfc}\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx \frac{1}{Q\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-Q^2/2}$$ Where **$Q$** is the Q-factor (electrical SNR per bit in linear units), and **erfc** is the complementary error function. ### Key Standard Metrics Used in BER Analysis | Metric | Standard Value (Industry Reference) | KTU Notation | |--------|-------------------------------------|--------------| | **Typical Receiver BER Threshold** | $10^{-9}$ | Standard telecom benchmark | | **High-Quality BER Threshold** | $10^{-12}$ | SONET/SDH grade | | **Pre-FEC BER Limit (Coherent)** | $2 \times 10^{-2}$ | Soft-decision FEC limit | | **Q-factor for $10^{-9}$ BER** | $\approx 6.0$ | Computed threshold | | **Q-factor for $10^{-12}$ BER** | $\approx 7.03$ | Computed threshold | | **Optical Carrier Frequency** | $\approx 193.1$ **THz** | ITU-T C-band center | | **Planck's Constant** | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s** | Photodetection physics | ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy: The Postal Letter Test** > Imagine you send **1,000 letters** through a postal system. Out of those 1,000, suppose **3 letters** arrive with garbled, unreadable characters. The BER of that postal system is $3/1000 = 3 \times 10^{-3}$. The optical fiber communication system works exactly the same way — instead of letters, we send **photons** as 1s and 0s. Due to noise (thermal noise, shot noise, ASE noise from optical amplifiers), sometimes a "1" is mistaken for "0" or vice versa. BER quantifies how often this confusion occurs per unit of data. A system with $BER = 10^{-9}$ means roughly only **1 error in 1 billion bits** — an extraordinarily reliable system. ### Why BER Analysis is Indispensable BER is the **single most important performance metric** in digital optical communication because it directly answers three engineering questions: 1. **How far can the signal travel** before errors become intolerable (system reach)? 2. **How much optical power is needed** at the receiver to achieve acceptable quality (sensitivity)? 3. **How much margin does the link have** against component degradation over its lifetime (system margin)? > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** BER Analysis bridges the gap between physical-layer impairments (noise, dispersion, nonlinearities) and the Quality of Service (QoS) experienced by end users. Module 3 specifically tests your ability to convert noise statistics into an exact BER figure. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sources of Errors in Optical Receivers Errors in an optical receiver originate from **two fundamental noise processes** that corrupt the decision variable at the sampling instant: ### Step-by-Step Logic of Error Generation 1. **Photon-to-Electron Conversion:** The incoming optical pulse strikes a photodetector (PIN or APD). The mean number of electrons generated is proportional to the incident optical energy. 2. **Noise Introduction:** During and after conversion, the following noise contributions corrupt the signal: - **Shot Noise (Quantum Noise):** Discrete, random nature of photodetection; modeled as Poisson statistics. Variance: $\sigma_{shot}^2 = 2qI B$ - **Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise):** Generated by the receiver's front-end electronics. Variance: $\sigma_{th}^2 = \frac{4k_B T B}{R_L}$ - **Relative Intensity Noise (RIN):** Laser phase/frequency instability. Variance: $\sigma_{RIN}^2 = (RIN) \cdot I^2 B$ 3. **Decision Threshold Crossing:** A binary decision is made by comparing the received signal-plus-noise voltage to a threshold level $D$. If $V_{1} < D$, a "1" is decided as "0" (error). 4. **Error Counting:** Total errors are accumulated over $N_t$ transmitted bits, yielding the BER. ## 2.2 The Q-Factor Concept (KTU High-Yield Topic) The **Q-factor** elegantly combines signal and noise statistics into a single figure of merit. For a binary optical receiver: $$Q = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0}$$ Where: - $\mu_1$ = Mean signal level for bit "1" at decision instant - $\mu_0$ = Mean signal level for bit "0" at decision instant - $\sigma_1$ = Standard deviation of noise on "1" level - $\sigma_0$ = Standard deviation of noise on "0" level > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Meaning:** A larger $Q$ implies a greater separation between the "1" and "0" distributions relative to their noise widths. Hence, errors are exponentially less likely. ### KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Conditions | |---|--------------------|------------|---------------------| | 1 | Bit Error Rate (BER) | $BER = N_e / N_t$ | Dimensionless, $\le 10^{-9}$ typical | | 2 | Q-factor Definition | $Q = (\mu_1 - \mu_0) / (\sigma_1 + \sigma_0)$ | Linear, dimensionless | | 3 | Exact BER from Q | $BER = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(Q/\sqrt{2})$ | Gaussian assumption | | 4 | BER Approximation | $BER \approx \frac{1}{Q\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-Q^2/2}$ | Asymptotic for large $Q$ | | 5 | Photocurrent Mean | $I_p = \Re P_{in}$ | Amperes, $\Re$ responsivity | | 6 | Shot Noise Variance | $\sigma_{shot}^2 = 2q(I_p + I_d)B$ | $A^2$, $I_d$ dark current | | 7 | Thermal Noise Variance | $\sigma_{th}^2 = 4k_B T B / R_L$ | $A^2$, $R_L$ load resistance | | 8 | SNR (Electrical) | $SNR = (I_p R_L)^2 / \sigma_{total}^2$ | Dimensionless | | 9 | OSNR | $OSNR = P_{signal} / P_{ASE}$ (per 0.1 nm) | dB | | 10 | OSNR–Q Relation (ASE-limited) | $Q^2 \approx \frac{OSNR \cdot B_o}{B_e}$ | $B_o$ optical, $B_e$ electrical | | 11 | Optical Power Penalty | $\delta = 10\log_{10}(Q_{req}/Q_{actual})$ | dB | | 12 | Receiver Sensitivity | $P_{sen} = \frac{Q\hbar\nu}{\Re}$ (shot-noise limited) | Watts or dBm | | 13 | Eye Diagram Q-measure | $Q = \frac{V_{top} - V_{base}}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0}$ | From eye diagram | | 14 | Q for BER $= 10^{-9}$ | $Q \approx 6.0$ | Computed threshold | | 15 | Q for BER $= 10^{-12}$ | $Q \approx 7.03$ | Computed threshold | | 16 | Boltzmann Constant | $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** | Standard physical constant | | 17 | Electronic Charge | $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** | Standard physical constant | | 18 | Decision Threshold | $D = \frac{\sigma_0 \mu_1 + \sigma_1 \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0}$ | Optimal (Gaussian) | > [!WARNING] > In the formula table above, the vertical bar (e.g., for absolute value) has been replaced with the LaTeX-safe `\vert` to avoid breaking Markdown table rendering. **KTU Examiners strictly use these standard forms — memorize them.** ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility BER Analysis is the **cornerstone of optical link budget design** in: - **Long-haul submarine cable systems** (BER $\le 10^{-12}$ for 10,000+ km spans) - **Data center interconnects** (BER $\le 10^{-15}$ with FEC) - **5G fronthaul networks** (BER $\le 10^{-9}$ without FEC) - **Coherent 100G/400G/800G transponders** (Pre-FEC BER $\le 2 \times 10^{-2}$) - **PON (Passive Optical Networks)** — FTTH deployments # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Probability Density Functions to BER ### Step 1 — Modeling the Received Signal as a Random Variable At the decision instant, the sampled voltage $V$ is a random variable. For each bit value, $V$ follows a Gaussian distribution (by Central Limit Theorem, since shot + thermal + RIN combine). For bit "1": $$p(V \mid 1) = \frac{1}{\sigma_1 \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(V - \mu_1)^2}{2\sigma_1^2}\right]$$ For bit "0": $$p(V \mid 0) = \frac{1}{\sigma_0 \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(V - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2}\right]$$ ### Step 2 — Defining Probability of Error Assuming equiprobable bits (50% "1" and 50% "0"), and an optimal decision threshold $D$: $$P_e = \frac{1}{2}P(0 \mid 1) + \frac{1}{2}P(1 \mid 0)$$ $$P_e = \frac{1}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{D} p(V \mid 1)\, dV + \frac{1}{2}\int_{D}^{\infty} p(V \mid 0)\, dV$$ ### Step 3 — Changing Variables to Standard Normal Form Substitute $x = (V - \mu_1)/\sigma_1$ for the first integral: $$\int_{-\infty}^{D} p(V \mid 1)\, dV = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\mu_1 - D}{\sigma_1 \sqrt{2}}\right)$$ Similarly for the second integral: $$\int_{D}^{\infty} p(V \mid 0)\, dV = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{D - \mu_0}{\sigma_0 \sqrt{2}}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Optimal Threshold Selection To minimize total error, set $\partial P_e / \partial D = 0$: $$\frac{1}{\sigma_1}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(\mu_1 - D)^2}{2\sigma_1^2}\right] = \frac{1}{\sigma_0}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(D - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2}\right]$$ Solving this equation gives the optimal threshold: $$D_{opt} = \frac{\sigma_0 \mu_1 + \sigma_1 \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0}$$ ### Step 5 — Substituting the Optimal Threshold Substituting $D_{opt}$ back, both error arguments become equal to a single quantity: $$\frac{\mu_1 - D_{opt}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{D_{opt} - \mu_0}{\sigma_0} = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0} \equiv Q$$ ### Step 6 — The Final BER Expression Combining both contributions: $$BER = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$ This is the **fundamental BER-Q relationship** demanded in every KTU exam. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — BER and Q Pairs (Numerical Table for KTU) | BER | Q-factor (approx) | Application / Standard | |------|---------------------|------------------------| | $10^{-3}$ | 3.09 | Pre-FEC threshold (some) | | $10^{-6}$ | 4.75 | Early Ethernet systems | | $10^{-9}$ | 6.00 | Standard telecom requirement | | $10^{-12}$ | 7.03 | SONET/SDH long-haul | | $10^{-15}$ | 7.94 | Ultra-reliable links | ## 3.3 Derivation: BER for Shot-Noise Limited Receiver For a PIN photodiode receiver dominated by shot noise (thermal negligible): $$\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_0^2 = \sigma_{shot}^2 = 2q(I_1 + I_0)B/2 \approx qI_1 B$$ since $I_0 \approx 0$ (no light → negligible photocurrent, only dark current neglected). The signal current for "1": $I_1 = \Re P_1$ The signal current for "0": $I_0 = 0$ Therefore: $$Q = \frac{I_1 - 0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0} = \frac{\Re P_1}{2\sigma_{shot}} = \frac{\Re P_1}{2\sqrt{q\Re P_1 B}}$$ Simplifying: $$Q = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\Re P_1}{qB}}$$ Solving for the **receiver sensitivity** (minimum optical power to achieve BER = $10^{-9}$, i.e., Q = 6): $$P_{sen} = \frac{4 Q^2 q B}{\Re}$$ For $B = 10$ GHz, $\Re = 0.8$ A/W: $$P_{sen} = \frac{4 \times 36 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{10}}{0.8} \approx 28.8 \text{ nW} \approx -45.4 \text{ dBm}$$ This matches textbook quantum limits for ideal PIN receivers. ## 3.4 Derivation: ASE-Limited OSNR-to-BER Conversion (Coherent Systems) In optically amplified systems, **ASE (Amplified Spontaneous Emission)** noise dominates. The Q-factor relates to OSNR as: $$Q^2 = \frac{OSNR \cdot B_{opt}}{B_{elec}}$$ Where $B_{opt}$ is the optical bandwidth (typically 0.1 nm = 12.5 GHz) and $B_{elec}$ is the electrical bandwidth (≈ bit rate / 2). Given OSNR $= 14$ dB (factor of 25.12), $B_{opt} = 12.5$ GHz, $B_{elec} = 7$ GHz (for 14 Gbps NRZ): $$Q^2 = 25.12 \times \frac{12.5}{7} = 44.86$$ $$Q = 6.70 \implies BER = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(6.70/\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-11}$$ This satisfies the $10^{-9}$ benchmark with healthy margin. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 BER Measurement Test Setup (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pattern Generator
PRBS 2^31-1] --> B[Optical Transmitter
Laser + Modulator] B --> C[Device Under Test
DUT: Fiber + Amplifiers] C --> D[Optical Receiver
PIN/APD + TIA] D --> E[Clock Recovery
CDR Unit] E --> F[Decision Circuit
Comparator + Threshold] F --> G[Error Detector
Bit Error Rate Tester BERT] A -.Reference PRBS.-> G H[Optical Attenuator
