VOA] -.-> C I[Optical Power Meter] -.-> D J[Eye Diagram
Oscilloscope] -.-> F style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333 style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333 style G fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — BER Estimation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Received Optical Signal] --> B[Photodetection
O/E Conversion] B --> C[Amplification
TIA + LA] C --> D[Noise Sources Accumulate
Shot + Thermal + ASE + RIN] D --> E[Decision Variable V
at Sampling Instant] E --> F[Statistical Distribution
pV1 and pV0] F --> G[Compute Q-factor
Q = mu1 - mu0 / sigma1 + sigma0] G --> H[Apply BER Equation
BER = 0.5 erfc Q over root 2] H --> I[Output BER Figure
e.g. 10 to power -9] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333 style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 style H fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Q-Factor Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph NoiseDistributions["Received Voltage Distributions"] Z[Bit 0 Distribution
Mean mu0 Std sigma0] ---|Overlap Region| O[Bit 1 Distribution
Mean mu1 Std sigma1] end D[Decision Threshold D] -.-> O Z -.-> D E1[Error: 0 misread as 1] -.-> Z E2[Error: 1 misread as 0] -.-> O Q[Q factor mu1 minus mu0 over sigma1 plus sigma0] --- NoiseDistributions style Z fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style O fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style D fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Q-Diagram:** The Q-factor is essentially a measure of **separation distance** between the "1" and "0" Gaussian bells, normalized by their noise widths. Higher separation = lower BER. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define Bit Error Rate (BER) in optical communication. Mention the typical acceptable BER value for standard telecommunication systems.** **Model Answer:** BER is the ratio of the number of bits received in error to the total number of bits transmitted within a specified time interval. $$BER = \frac{N_e}{N_t}$$ The typical acceptable BER for standard telecom-grade optical systems is **$10^{-9}$**, corresponding to a Q-factor of approximately **6.0**. Higher-grade links (SONET/SDH) operate at $10^{-12}$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse BER with SER (Symbol Error Rate). For binary systems they coincide, but for M-ary signaling they differ. Also, do not write BER in percentage for telecom — always express as a power of ten. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **What is Q-factor? Write its mathematical relation to BER.** **Model Answer:** The Q-factor is a measure of the signal-to-noise ratio at the decision circuit, defined as: $$Q = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0}$$ Where $\mu_1, \mu_0$ are mean signal levels and $\sigma_1, \sigma_0$ are noise standard deviations for bits "1" and "0" respectively. The BER is related to Q by: $$BER = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$ A higher Q-factor implies lower BER (fewer errors). For BER = $10^{-9}$, Q ≈ 6.0. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Internal Choice Mandate:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Both questions are 14 marks each, typically split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive the expression for BER in terms of Q-factor for an optical receiver assuming Gaussian noise statistics. **[7 marks]** **(b)** An optical receiver detects a "1" pulse with mean photocurrent $\mu_1 = 100$ nA and a "0" with mean photocurrent $\mu_0 = 0$ nA. The noise standard deviations are $\sigma_1 = 12$ nA and $\sigma_0 = 10$ nA. Compute: (i) the Q-factor, (ii) the BER, and (iii) the optical power penalty if the required Q is 6.0. **[7 marks]** ### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Refer to **Section 3.1 (Steps 1–6)** of these notes for the full derivation. Briefly summarized for writing: - **Step 1:** Model the sampled voltage at the decision instant as a Gaussian random variable for both bit "1" and bit "0". - **Step 2:** Define total error probability: $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{D} p(V \mid 1)dV + \frac{1}{2}\int_{D}^{\infty} p(V \mid 0)dV$ - **Step 3:** Apply variable substitution to obtain erfc form. - **Step 4:** Optimize threshold: $D_{opt} = \frac{\sigma_0 \mu_1 + \sigma_1 \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0}$ - **Step 5:** Recognize that both arguments equal $Q = (\mu_1 - \mu_0)/(\sigma_1 + \sigma_0)$. - **Step 6:** Conclude: $$BER = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx \frac{1}{Q\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-Q^2/2}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Q-factor calculation:** $$Q = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0} = \frac{100 - 0}{12 + 10} = \frac{100}{22} \approx 4.545$$ **(ii) BER calculation:** $$BER = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{4.545}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(3.214)$$ Using erfc(3.214) ≈ $1.106 \times 10^{-5}$: $$BER = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.106 \times 10^{-5} \approx 5.53 \times 10^{-6}$$ **(iii) Optical power penalty:** The required Q is 6.0, but actual Q is 4.545. The penalty is the additional power needed to raise Q from 4.545 to 6.0: $$\delta_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{Q_{req}}{Q_{actual}}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{6.0}{4.545}\right)$$ $$\delta_{dB} = 20 \times \log_{10}(1.320) = 20 \times 0.1206 = 2.41 \text{ dB}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** The penalty factor uses **$20\log_{10}$** because Q relates to *electrical* (voltage or current) SNR, not optical power directly. Using $10\log_{10}$ would yield **half the correct answer** — a common 1–2 mark loss. --- ### Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the sources of noise in an optical receiver. Derive the expression for total noise variance. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A 10 Gbps optical link uses a PIN receiver with responsivity $\Re = 0.85$ A/W. The receiver bandwidth is 7 GHz. Compute the receiver sensitivity (in dBm) for a target BER of $10^{-9}$, assuming shot-noise-limited operation at 1550 nm. **[7 marks]** ### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Sources of noise (7 marks):** Three primary noise sources corrupt the decision variable in an optical receiver: 1. **Shot Noise (Quantum Noise):** Arises from the discrete, random arrival of photons at the photodetector. Variance: $$\sigma_{shot}^2 = 2q(I_p + I_d)B$$ 2. **Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise):** Generated in the load resistor and front-end electronics: $$\sigma_{th}^2 = \frac{4k_B T B}{R_L}$$ 3. **Relative Intensity Noise (RIN):** Power fluctuations of the laser source: $$\sigma_{RIN}^2 = (\text{RIN}) \cdot I_p^2 B$$ The **total noise variance** (assuming independent noise processes) is: $$\sigma_{total}^2 = \sigma_{shot}^2 + \sigma_{th}^2 + \sigma_{RIN}^2 + \sigma_{dark}^2$$ Where $\sigma_{dark}^2 = 2qI_d B$ is the dark current shot noise contribution. **(b) Sensitivity computation (7 marks):** Given: $R_b = 10$ Gbps, $\Re = 0.85$ A/W, $B = 7$ GHz, target BER $= 10^{-9}$ → $Q = 6.0$. **Step 1 — Wavelength and photon energy:** $\lambda = 1550$ nm → $\hbar\omega = \frac{1.2414}{1.55}$ eV = 0.8009 eV = $1.282 \times 10^{-19}$ J **Step 2 — Shot-noise limited Q-factor expression:** $$Q = \frac{\Re P_{sen}}{2\sqrt{q\Re P_{sen} B}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\Re P_{sen}}{qB}}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $P_{sen}$:** $$P_{sen} = \frac{4Q^2 qB}{\Re} = \frac{4 \times 36 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 7 \times 10^9}{0.85}$$ $$P_{sen} = \frac{4 \times 36 \times 1.6 \times 7 \times 10^{-10}}{0.85} = \frac{1.6128 \times 10^{-7}}{0.85}$$ $$P_{sen} = 1.897 \times 10^{-7} \text{ W} = 189.7 \text{ nW}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to dBm:** $$P_{sen}(\text{dBm}) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.897 \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(1.897 \times 10^{-4})$$ $$P_{sen}(\text{dBm}) = 10 \times (-3.722) = -37.22 \text{ dBm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Forgetting to convert Watts → milliwatts before taking $10\log_{10}$ is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. The denominator must be $10^{-3}$, not $1$. Also, do not confuse electrical bandwidth $B$ with bit rate $R_b$. For NRZ, $B \approx 0.7 R_b$. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Reasons for Marks Deduction in BER Analysis Questions:** > 1. Using $10\log_{10}$ instead of $20\log_{10}$ for power penalties derived from electrical SNR. > 2. Confusing thermal noise variance $\frac{4k_BTB}{R_L}$ with $\frac{k_BT}{R_L}$ (missing the bandwidth factor). > 3. Mixing up $B$ (electrical bandwidth) with $R_b$ (bit rate) — for NRZ-OOK, $B \approx 0.7 R_b$. > 4. Forgetting that the complementary error function $\text{erfc}(x)$ argument is $Q/\sqrt{2}$, not $Q$. > 5. Writing BER in percentage form for telecom values (always use powers of ten). > 6. Failing to mention the Gaussian noise assumption in derivations — this is a 1-mark penalty per KTU valuation key. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **BER Definition:** $BER = N_e / N_t$; standard telecom benchmark is $10^{-9}$. - ✅ **Q-factor Definition:** $Q = (\mu_1 - \mu_0)/(\sigma_1 + \sigma_0)$; larger Q means fewer errors. - ✅ **Master Formula:** $BER = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(Q/\sqrt{2}) \approx \frac{1}{Q\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-Q^2/2}$ - ✅ **Key Q-values:** BER $= 10^{-9} \Rightarrow Q = 6.0$; BER $= 10^{-12} \Rightarrow Q = 7.03$. - ✅ **Shot Noise:** $\sigma^2 = 2q(I_p + I_d)B$ — quantum-limit noise. - ✅ **Thermal Noise:** $\sigma^2 = 4k_BTB/R_L$ — circuit-limit noise. - ✅ **Sensitivity Formula (Shot-limited):** $P_{sen} = 4Q^2qB/\Re$. - ✅ **Power Penalty:** $\delta = 20\log_{10}(Q_{req}/Q_{actual})$ in dB (electrical domain). - ✅ **OSNR-to-Q Relation:** $Q^2 = OSNR \cdot B_{opt}/B_{elec}$ for ASE-limited systems. - ✅ **Constants:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C; $\hbar = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s. - ✅ **Optimal Threshold:** $D_{opt} = (\sigma_0 \mu_1 + \sigma_1 \mu_0)/(\sigma_1 + \sigma_0)$. - ✅ **Gaussian Assumption:** Holds due to Central Limit Theorem when multiple noise sources combine.

topics.title: Optical Amplifiers: EDFA - Principle, Structure and Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Optical Amplifiers: EDFA - Principle, Structure and Working # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier (EDFA)** is an optical amplifier that uses a silica-based optical fiber doped with rare-earth element **Erbium (Er³⁺)** ions as the gain medium, which is optically pumped to achieve **population inversion** and amplifies optical signals in the **C-band (1530 nm – 1565 nm)** and **L-band (1565 nm – 1625 nm)** wavelength regions through **stimulated emission**. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the EDFA belongs to the family of **in-line (or repeater-type) optical amplifiers** that directly amplify an optical signal without converting it back to the electrical domain. It revolutionized long-haul and wavelength-division-multiplexed (WDM) optical networks by replacing costly O-E-O (Optical-Electrical-Optical) regenerators. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a long, dark tunnel where you are shouting to a friend standing at the far end. By the time your voice reaches them, it has become weak and barely audible. Now suppose the tunnel walls are painted with a special material that absorbs light from a flashlight (the **pump laser**) and re-emits an *amplified copy* of your voice every few meters. Your friend's voice not only survives the journey but arrives louder than ever. In this analogy: - The **tunnel** = Erbium-doped fiber - The **flashlight** = Pump laser (980 nm or 1480 nm) - The **special wall paint** = Er³⁺ ions that get excited and release energy - **Your voice** = Weak incoming optical signal (1550 nm) - **Your friend's amplified hearing** = Boosted output signal > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Unlike electrical amplifiers, the EDFA amplifies *any wavelength* in the C-band simultaneously, making it the **backbone of WDM networks**. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | |-----------|--------|---------------| | Speed of light | $c$ | **$3 \times 10^{8} \, m/s$** | | Planck's constant | $h$ | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, J \cdot s$** | | Operating wavelength window | $\lambda_s$ | **1550 nm** (C-band) | | Standard pump wavelengths | $\lambda_p$ | **980 nm** or **1480 nm** | | Typical small-signal gain | $G$ | **20 – 40 dB** | | Typical noise figure | $NF$ | **4 – 5 dB** | | Saturation output power | $P_{sat}$ | **10 – 20 dBm** | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Three-Level Energy System The EDFA operates on the principle of **stimulated emission** using the energy transitions of **Erbium (Er³⁺)** ions doped inside the silica fiber core. The working cycle has three logical phases: ### Phase 1: Optical Pumping (Population Inversion) - A high-power pump laser (980 nm or 1480 nm) injects energy into the doped fiber. - Er³⁺ ions absorb pump photons and jump from the **ground state** $\bigl(^{4}I_{15/2}\bigr)$ to an **excited state**. - For a **980 nm pump**, ions are excited to the $\bigl(^{4}I_{11/2}\bigr)$ level. - These ions undergo rapid **non-radiative decay** to the **metastable state** $\bigl(^{4}I_{13/2}\bigr)$. - The metastable state has a **long lifetime (≈ 10 ms)**, allowing ions to *accumulate* there. - Once enough ions populate the metastable state, **population inversion** is achieved: $$N_2 > N_1$$ where $N_2$ = population of metastable level, $N_1$ = population of ground level. ### Phase 2: Stimulated Emission (Signal Amplification) - A weak input optical signal at wavelength $\lambda_s \approx 1550 \, nm$ enters the fiber. - When the signal photon encounters an excited Er³⁺ ion in the metastable state, it **stimulates** that ion to drop back to the ground state. - This drop releases a *new photon* that is **identical** to the signal photon in: - **Wavelength** - **Phase** - **Polarization** - **Direction of propagation** - The signal beam gains photons → **optical amplification**. ### Phase 3: Spontaneous Emission (Noise Source) - Some excited ions decay *spontaneously* (without an incoming photon). - This produces **Amplified Spontaneous Emission (ASE)** noise in both forward and backward directions. - ASE is the principal contributor to the EDFA's **Noise Figure**. > [!NOTE] > The EDFA is a **three-level system** when pumped at 980 nm and a **two-level (quasi-three-level) system** when pumped at 1480 nm. The 980 nm pump gives **lower noise figure**, while 1480 nm gives **higher power conversion efficiency**. ## 2.2 Why Erbium? — The Physical Justification The **silica fiber** has low loss in the **1550 nm** window (minimum attenuation ≈ **0.2 dB/km**). The energy gap of the Er³⁺ ion between the metastable level $^{4}I_{13/2}$ and the ground level $^{4}I_{15/2}$ corresponds *exactly* to a photon of wavelength **1530 nm**, with a fluorescence bandwidth extending up to 1625 nm. This **spectral coincidence** between Er³⁺ emission and the silica low-loss window makes erbium the perfect dopant. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorise for KTU ESE** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $G = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Linear Gain (ratio) | dimensionless | | 2 | $G_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | Gain in decibels | dB | | 3 | $NF = \dfrac{SNR_{in}}{SNR_{out}}$ | Noise Figure | dimensionless | | 4 | $NF_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(NF)$ | Noise Figure in dB | dB | | 5 | $P_{ASE} = n_{sp} \cdot h \nu \cdot B_0 \cdot (G - 1)$ | ASE noise power at output | W | | 6 | $n_{sp} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_2 - \dfrac{g_2}{g_1} N_1}$ | Population inversion / spontaneous emission factor | dimensionless | | 7 | $\Delta \nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda^2} \cdot \Delta \lambda$ | Optical bandwidth conversion | Hz | | 8 | $E_{photon} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of a photon | Joules | | 9 | $P_{sat}^{out} = \dfrac{P_{sat}^{in}}{G}$ | Saturation output power relation | W | | 10 | $\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{P_{out} - P_{in}}{P_{pump}}$ | Power conversion efficiency | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The gain ($G$) and noise figure ($NF$) jointly determine the **optical SNR** at the receiver, which dictates the **maximum transmission distance** in a long-haul WDM link. EDFAs allow links of 1000+ km without electrical regeneration. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Population Inversion Condition in a Three-Level EDFA In a three-level pumping scheme (980 nm pump), let: - $N_0$ = population of ground state ($^{4}I_{15/2}$) - $N_1$ = population of pump-excited state ($^{4}I_{11/2}$) — usually decays rapidly - $N_2$ = population of metastable state ($^{4}I_{13/2}$) - $N_{tot} = N_0 + N_1 + N_2$ (total Erbium ion concentration) ### Step 1: Total Population Conservation $$N_{tot} = N_0 + N_1 + N_2$$ ### Step 2: Population Inversion Condition For amplification by stimulated emission, we require: $$N_2 > N_0$$ In a true three-level system, this requires that **more than half** of the total ions be pumped into the metastable state: $$N_2 > \dfrac{N_{tot}}{2}$$ ### Step 3: Threshold Pump Power The pump power required to achieve this inversion is: $$P_{pump}^{th} = \dfrac{h c \, N_{tot} \, A_{eff}}{2 \, \tau \, \sigma_p \, \lambda_p}$$ where $\tau$ is the metastable lifetime, $\sigma_p$ is the pump absorption cross-section, and $A_{eff}$ is the effective mode area of the fiber. ### Step 4: Small-Signal Gain Expression When the signal is weak (no saturation), the unsaturated gain is: $$\begin{aligned} G(dB) &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\Gamma \, \sigma_s \, N_2^{inv} \, L\right)\right] \\ G(dB) &= 4.343 \cdot \Gamma \, \sigma_s \, (N_2 - N_0) \, L \end{aligned}$$ where: - $\Gamma$ = overlap (confinement) factor between optical mode and doped region - $\sigma_s$ = stimulated emission cross-section at signal wavelength - $N_2 - N_0$ = population inversion density - $L$ = length of doped fiber --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Noise Figure and ASE Power ### Step 1: Definition of Noise Figure The optical amplifier noise figure is defined as the degradation of optical signal-to-noise ratio: $$NF = \dfrac{SNR_{in}}{SNR_{out}} = \dfrac{P_{in}/N_{in}}{P_{out}/N_{out}}$$ where $N_{in}$ and $N_{out}$ are noise powers referred to the input and output. ### Step 2: Quantum-Limited Noise Figure For an ideal EDFA (lossless, perfect inversion), the minimum noise figure is: $$NF_{min} = 2 \, n_{sp}$$ with $n_{sp} = 1$ (complete inversion), giving the **quantum limit of 3 dB**. ### Step 3: ASE Noise Spectral Density The ASE power per unit bandwidth (spectral density) at the output is: $$\rho_{ASE} = n_{sp} \cdot (G - 1) \cdot h \nu$$ ### Step 4: Total ASE Power over Optical Bandwidth $B_o$ Integrating over the amplifier's optical bandwidth $B_o$: $$\begin{aligned} P_{ASE} &= \rho_{ASE} \cdot B_o \\ P_{ASE} &= n_{sp} \cdot h \nu \cdot B_o \cdot (G - 1) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5: Practical Noise Figure in dB $$NF_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2 n_{sp} \cdot \dfrac{G - 1}{G}\right)$$ For large gain ($G \gg 1$): $$NF_{dB} \approx 10 \log_{10}(2 \, n_{sp})$$ If $n_{sp} = 1.5$ (typical EDFA): $$NF_{dB} \approx 10 \log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77 \, dB$$ This matches the **typical EDFA NF of 4 – 5 dB** seen in practice. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) **Problem:** An EDFA amplifies an input signal of $P_{in} = -30 \, dBm$ to an output of $P_{out} = 10 \, dBm$. The spontaneous emission factor is $n_{sp} = 1.4$. Compute the (a) gain in dB, (b) linear gain, and (c) noise figure. ### (a) Gain in dB $$\begin{aligned} G_{dB} &= P_{out}(dBm) - P_{in}(dBm) \\ G_{dB} &= 10 - (-30) = 40 \, dB \end{aligned}$$ ### (b) Linear Gain $$\begin{aligned} G &= 10^{G_{dB}/10} \\ G &= 10^{40/10} = 10^{4} = 10000 \end{aligned}$$ ### (c) Noise Figure $$\begin{aligned} NF_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(2 \cdot n_{sp}) \quad \text{(since } G \gg 1\text{)} \\ NF_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(2 \times 1.4) \\ NF_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(2.8) \\ NF_{dB} &\approx 4.47 \, dB \end{aligned}$$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Trick:** Whenever $G > 20 \, dB$, you can safely use the approximation $NF \approx 2 n_{sp}$. Examiners love this simplification. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | 980 nm Pump | 1480 nm Pump | |-----------|:-----------:|:------------:| | Mode of operation | Three-level | Quasi-two-level | | Noise Figure | Lower (≈ 4 dB) | Higher (≈ 5–6 dB) | | Power Conversion Efficiency | Lower (~50%) | Higher (~75%) | | Pump Power Required | Higher | Lower | | Preferred application | Long-haul (NF-critical) | High-power booster | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Architecture of an EDFA System The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete EDFA, illustrating how each optical subsystem interacts to amplify the input signal. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Optical Signal
1550 nm, weak power] --> B[Optical Isolator 1
blocks back reflections] B --> C[WDM Coupler
Pump Signal Combiner] D[Pump Laser Source
980 nm or 1480 nm] --> C C --> E[Erbium Doped Fiber
EDF Gain Medium 5 to 30 m] E --> F[Optical Isolator 2
blocks backward ASE] F --> G[Output Optical Signal
1550 nm amplified + ASE noise] E --> H[Tap Coupler 5 percent] H --> I[Photodetector PD] I --> J[Control Electronics
Gain Lock Loop] J --> K[Feedback to Pump Laser Power] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The weak input signal combines with the high-power pump inside the WDM coupler. The combined light travels through the Erbium-doped fiber where energy transfer takes place. The amplified output is then isolated to prevent destabilizing reflections. A 5% tap monitors output power for automatic gain control (AGC). --- ## 4.2 Three-Level Energy Transition Diagram (Mermaid-Adapted) ```mermaid flowchart TB L3["Level 3: Pump Excited State
4I11/2 at 980 nm"] L2["Level 2: Metastable State
4I13/2 - Long lifetime 10 ms"] L1["Level 1: Ground State
4I15/2 - stable"] P1["Pump Photon 980 nm
Ground to Excited"] -.->|absorbed| L3 L3 ==>|"fast non-radiative decay"| L2 L2 ==>|"stimulated emission at 1550 nm"| L1 L2 -.->|"spontaneous emission - ASE noise"| L1 S1["Signal Photon 1550 nm
triggers stimulated emission"] -.->|stimulates| L2 style L3 fill:#ffd6a5 style L2 fill:#caffbf style L1 fill:#a0c4ff style P1 fill:#ffadad style S1 fill:#bdb2ff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Note:** For a 1480 nm pump, the ion goes **directly** from ground state to the metastable level (Level 2) — i.e., L1 → L2. This is why the system becomes a **two-level/quasi-two-level system** and is more power-efficient but produces higher noise. --- ## 4.3 Internal Component Schematic (Block-Level View) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph EDFA_Module["EDFA Sub-System Block"] direction LR M1[Isolator IN] --> M2[WDM Coupler] M2 --> M3[Erbium Doped Fiber Coil] M3 --> M4[Isolator OUT] M3 -.-> M5[Tap Monitor 5 percent] M5 -.-> M6[Photodiode] M6 -.-> M7[AGC Controller] M7 -.-> M2 end IN_Signal["Input Signal Fiber"] --> M1 M4 --> OUT_Signal["Amplified Output Fiber"] Pump_In["Pump Laser Input"] --> M2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Components and Their Roles:** > 1. **Isolators** — Permit light flow in only one direction, protecting the EDF from reflected ASE and laser noise. > 2. **WDM Coupler (980/1550 nm)** — Multiplexes pump and signal wavelengths into the same fiber with minimal loss. > 3. **Erbium-Doped Fiber** — The active gain medium; typical length 5–30 m, doping concentration ~1000 ppm. > 4. **Tap Coupler + Photodiode** — Monitors output power for automatic gain control (AGC). > 5. **Pump Laser** — Semiconductor laser (980 nm or 1480 nm), 50–200 mW power. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is population inversion, and why is it essential for optical amplification in EDFA?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Population inversion is the condition in which the number of atoms/ions in the higher energy (metastable) state exceeds those in the lower (ground) energy state, i.e., $N_2 > N_1$. In an EDFA, erbium ions are optically pumped (typically at 980 nm) so that they accumulate in the metastable $^{4}I_{13/2}$ state. This inversion is essential because **stimulated emission** — the process responsible for signal amplification — only dominates over absorption when $N_2 > N_1$. Without inversion, incoming signal photons would be absorbed rather than amplified. (3 marks) --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]` **List any three advantages of EDFA over electronic repeaters.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Wavelength-independent gain:** A single EDFA simultaneously amplifies all channels in the C-band, ideal for WDM systems, whereas electronic repeaters amplify one channel at a time. 2. **No O-E-O conversion:** Direct optical amplification reduces latency, cost, and complexity. 3. **Bit-rate transparent:** Operates independently of modulation format (NRZ, RZ, QAM) and bit rate up to >100 Gbps. 4. **High gain and low noise figure:** Typical gain 20–40 dB with NF 4–5 dB, enabling 1000+ km links. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks) ### ✅ Question A — 14 Marks **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat energy-level diagram, explain the **principle of operation of an Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier (EDFA)** pumped at 980 nm. Discuss the role of the metastable state. **(b) [7 Marks]** An EDFA receives an input power of $P_{in} = -25 \, dBm$ and delivers an output power of $P_{out} = +15 \, dBm$. The spontaneous emission factor is $n_{sp} = 1.6$ and the optical bandwidth is $B_0 = 30 \, nm$ centred at $\lambda = 1550 \, nm$. Calculate: (i) Gain in dB and as a ratio, (ii) Noise figure in dB, (iii) Total ASE power in mW. --- #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a):** The Erbium ion (Er³⁺) inside the silica fiber has three relevant energy levels: 1. **Ground State ($^{4}I_{15/2}$):** Stable lowest level. 2. **Pump Excited State ($^{4}I_{11/2}$):** ~1.27 eV above ground, corresponds to **980 nm** photon absorption. 3. **Metastable State ($^{4}I_{13/2}$):** ~0.81 eV above ground, corresponds to **1550 nm** photon. Has a long spontaneous lifetime of **~10 ms**, allowing ions to accumulate. **Operating Cycle (Step-by-step):** - **Step 1 (Pumping):** Pump laser injects 980 nm photons. Er³⁺ ions absorb them and jump from $^{4}I_{15/2}$ to $^{4}I_{11/2}$. - **Step 2 (Fast non-radiative decay):** Ions in $^{4}I_{11/2}$ rapidly lose energy via phonon interactions and fall to $^{4}I_{13/2}$. - **Step 3 (Population accumulation):** Because the metastable state has a long lifetime (~10 ms), ions *accumulate* there, eventually achieving **population inversion** ($N_2 > N_0$). - **Step 4 (Stimulated emission):** A 1550 nm signal photon passing through the fiber triggers an excited ion in $^{4}I_{13/2}$ to drop back to $^{4}I_{15/2}$, emitting an **identical** (same wavelength, phase, polarization, direction) photon. This creates **optical amplification**. - **Step 5 (Spontaneous emission):** Some ions decay spontaneously, producing **ASE noise** in all directions. **Role of the Metastable State:** The metastable state acts as a *reservoir* for excited ions due to its long lifetime. Without it, ions would decay back to ground as fast as they are pumped, and population inversion would never occur. Its 10 ms lifetime is critical for achieving the inversion necessary for amplification. (7 marks) --- **Part (b):** **(i) Gain:** $$\begin{aligned} G_{dB} &= P_{out}(dBm) - P_{in}(dBm) \\ G_{dB} &= 15 - (-25) = 40 \, dB \\ G &= 10^{40/10} = 10^{4} = 10{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Noise Figure:** For $G \gg 1$: $$\begin{aligned} NF_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(2 \cdot n_{sp}) \\ NF_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(2 \times 1.6) \\ NF_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(3.2) \\ NF_{dB} &\approx 5.05 \, dB \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Total ASE Power:** Convert quantities: - $G - 1 \approx G = 10^{4}$ - $h \nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{1550 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.282 \times 10^{-19} \, J$ Optical bandwidth: $$\begin{aligned} B_0 &= \dfrac{c}{\lambda^2} \Delta \lambda \\ B_0 &= \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{(1550 \times 10^{-9})^2} \times 30 \times 10^{-9} \\ B_0 &= \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.4025 \times 10^{-12}} \times 30 \times 10^{-9} \\ B_0 &= 1.2487 \times 10^{20} \times 30 \times 10^{-9} \\ B_0 &= 3.746 \times 10^{12} \, Hz = 3.746 \, THz \end{aligned}$$ Total ASE Power: $$\begin{aligned} P_{ASE} &= n_{sp} \cdot h \nu \cdot B_0 \cdot (G - 1) \\ P_{ASE} &= 1.6 \times 1.282 \times 10^{-19} \times 3.746 \times 10^{12} \times 10^{4} \\ P_{ASE} &= 1.6 \times 1.282 \times 3.746 \times 10^{-19+12+4} \\ P_{ASE} &= 7.689 \times 10^{-3} \, W \\ P_{ASE} &\approx 7.69 \, mW \end{aligned}$$ (7 marks) --- ### ✅ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the **block diagram of an EDFA**. Discuss the function of each component. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **980 nm and 1480 nm pumping schemes** of an EDFA in terms of noise figure, power conversion efficiency, and pumping mode. --- #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Block Diagram and Component Functions:** ``` [Input Signal 1550 nm] → [Isolator 1] → [WDM Coupler] ← [Pump Laser 980/1480 nm] ↓ [Erbium Doped Fiber (EDF)] ↓ [Isolator 2] → [Output (amplified)] ↓ [Tap Coupler (5%)] ↓ [Photodetector] ↓ [Control Circuit] → (feedback to Pump Laser) ``` **Functions of each component:** 1. **Optical Isolator (Input):** Permits the input signal to enter the EDFA but blocks any backward-traveling reflections from entering the upstream fiber, preventing laser instability. 2. **WDM Coupler (Wavelength-Division Multiplexer):** Combines the 1550 nm signal and 980/1480 nm pump light into the same fiber. Designed for low insertion loss (< 0.5 dB) at both wavelengths. 3. **Erbium-Doped Fiber (EDF):** The active gain medium. Typically 5–30 m long, doped with ~1000 ppm of Er³⁺ ions. Provides the optical gain through stimulated emission. 4. **Optical Isolator (Output):** Prevents backward-propagating ASE noise from re-entering the EDF and saturating it. 5. **Pump Laser:** Semiconductor laser diode providing high-power (50–200 mW) pump light at 980 nm or 1480 nm. 6. **Tap Coupler + Photodetector + Control Circuit:** Together implement **Automatic Gain Control (AGC)**. The tap diverts ~5% of output to a photodiode; the resulting electrical signal adjusts the pump laser current to maintain a constant gain, independent of input power fluctuations. (7 marks) --- **Part (b) — Comparison of 980 nm vs 1480 nm Pumping:** | Parameter | 980 nm Pumping | 1480 nm Pumping | |-----------|:--------------:|:---------------:| | **Pumping mode** | Three-level system | Quasi-two-level system | | **Excited level** | $^{4}I_{11/2}$ (rapidly decays to metastable) | $^{4}I_{13/2}$ (metastable directly) | | **Noise Figure** | Lower (~4 dB) | Higher (~5–6 dB) | | **Power Conversion Efficiency** | Lower (~50%) | Higher (~75%) | | **Pump power required** | Higher (~100 mW) | Lower (~50 mW) | | **Best use case** | Long-haul pre-amplifier (NF critical) | Power booster amplifier | **Key Explanation:** - At **980 nm**, the pump excites ions into a *higher* level that quickly relaxes to the metastable state. This achieves **complete inversion** ($N_2 \gg N_0$), yielding a low spontaneous emission factor ($n_{sp} \approx 1$) and hence the **lowest noise figure (≈ 3 dB limit)**. - At **1480 nm**, the pump excites ions *directly* into the metastable level. Because the same level is also the upper signal-emitting level, some ions are always in the ground state absorbing — this is the **quasi-two-level** nature. The lower inversion density leads to higher noise but better pump-to-signal power conversion. (7 marks) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE:** > 1. **Confusing pump wavelengths with signal wavelength** — Always state that *signal is at 1550 nm* and *pump is at 980 nm or 1480 nm*. > 2. **Forgetting to mention the metastable lifetime (~10 ms)** — Examiners specifically check for this when awarding full marks for the population inversion explanation. > 3. **Wrong formula for Noise Figure** — Use $NF = 2 n_{sp}$ only when $G \gg 1$. For small gain, use the full expression $NF = 2 n_{sp} (G-1)/G$. > 4. **Not drawing the energy level diagram** — A 7-mark question on principle of operation *always* expects an energy-level diagram with arrows showing pump absorption, non-radiative decay, and stimulated emission. > 5. **Unit errors in $B_o$ calculation** — Convert $\Delta \lambda$ in nm to frequency bandwidth in Hz using $\Delta \nu = (c/\lambda^2) \Delta \lambda$. Mixing units is a frequent error. > 6. **Ignoring ASE noise direction** — ASE propagates in *both* directions; the isolator only suppresses backward ASE at the output. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — EDFA** - ✅ **EDFA** = Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier; amplifies optical signals in the **C-band (1530–1565 nm)**. - ✅ Active medium: **Silica fiber doped with Er³⁺ ions** (~1000 ppm, length 5–30 m). - ✅ Working principle: **Stimulated emission** in a **three-level energy system** (980 nm pump) or quasi-two-level (1480 nm pump). - ✅ Key energy levels: Ground ($^{4}I_{15/2}$), Metastable ($^{4}I_{13/2}$, $\tau \approx 10$ ms), Pump ($^{4}I_{11/2}$). - ✅ Population inversion: $N_2 > N_1$; achieved by pumping at 980 nm or 1480 nm. - ✅ **Gain**: typically **20–40 dB**; linear $G = P_{out}/P_{in}$. - ✅ **Noise Figure**: typically **4–5 dB**; quantum limit = **3 dB** for $n_{sp} = 1$. - ✅ **ASE noise** is the dominant internal noise source; propagates bidirectionally. - ✅ **Isolators** are placed at both ends to suppress reflections and backward ASE. - ✅ **WDM coupler** merges pump (980/1480 nm) and signal (1550 nm) onto the same fiber. - ✅ **Tap coupler + photodiode + control circuit** implement Automatic Gain Control (AGC). - ✅ **980 nm pump** → lower NF, preferred for pre-amplifiers in long-haul systems. - ✅ **1480 nm pump** → higher power efficiency, preferred for power boosters. - ✅ EDFAs enable **WDM-compatible, bit-rate transparent, format-independent** amplification — replacing O-E-O regenerators in modern optical networks. - ✅ Important formulas (must memorise): - $G_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{out}/P_{in})$ - $NF_{dB} \approx 10 \log_{10}(2 n_{sp})$ (for large gain) - $P_{ASE} = n_{sp} \cdot h\nu \cdot B_0 \cdot (G-1)$

topics.title: OTDM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** OTDM ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Optical Time Division Multiplexing (OTDM)** is an ultra-high-speed optical multiplexing technique in which several lower-bit-rate optical data tributaries (channels) are combined in the **time domain** through precise **bit-interleaving** at the optical layer, producing a single aggregate optical stream with a bit rate equal to the sum of the tributary bit rates. The multiplexing is performed in the optical domain (not electronically), typically using very narrow optical pulses (on the order of **picoseconds**) generated by mode-locked lasers and controlled by optical delay lines. Mathematically, if **N** tributaries each carrying data at a bit rate **B** are multiplexed: $$B_{aggregate} = N \times B \;\;[\text{bits/s}]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** OTDM is treated as an enabling technology for **beyond-100 Gb/s** single-channel transmission where direct electronic time-division multiplexing (ETDM) becomes infeasible due to the speed limitations of electronic circuits (silicon and III-V electronic bottleneck ~ 100 Gb/s). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **single-lane highway with a traffic signal** that allows only one car to pass through a toll booth per green flash. The toll booth can flash at, say, **100 times per second**, so only 100 cars/second can pass. Now suppose we upgrade to a **super-fast strobe light** flashing **400 times per second**, and we carefully **stagger 4 lines of cars** (Line 1, Line 2, Line 3, Line 4) so that one car from each line enters the booth during each consecutive flash cycle. The throughput jumps to **400 cars/second**, but only **one car** occupies the booth at any instant. - The **strobe light** = mode-locked picosecond pulse train (the OTDM "clock") - The **4 car lines** = 4 tributary data channels (each at 100 Gb/s) - The **stagger** = optical delay lines (T, 2T, 3T, ...) - The **single output stream** = the OTDM aggregate signal at 400 Gb/s This is exactly how OTDM works: it trades **parallelism in time** for **higher aggregate bit rate**, while the system bandwidth of any individual electronic component only needs to handle the tributary rate, not the aggregate. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from WDM:** WDM separates channels by **wavelength (frequency)**, while OTDM separates channels by **time slots at a single wavelength**. OTDM and WDM can in fact be **combined** (OTDM/WDM hybrid) to reach Tbps capacities. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics in OTDM Systems | Metric | Typical Value | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Tributary bit rate (B)** | **10 – 40 Gb/s** | Per-channel electronic rate | | **Number of tributaries (N)** | **2, 4, 8, 16, ...** | Multiplexing depth | | **Aggregate bit rate** | **40 – 640 Gb/s** | N × B | | **Pulse width (τₚ)** | **1 – 10 ps** | Picosecond optical pulse | | **Bit period (T_b)** | **Aggregator⁻¹** | 1/B_aggregate | | **Time slot per channel** | **T_b = T_frame/N** | Channel separation | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step The OTDM system operates in **two stages**: **Multiplexing (TX)** and **Demultiplexing (RX)**. **Stage A — Optical Multiplexing (Transmitter Side)** 1. **Picosecond Pulse Generation:** A **mode-locked laser (MLL)** — typically an Erbium-Doped Fiber Ring Laser (EDFRL) or a semiconductor mode-locked laser — generates a stable, low-jitter train of optical pulses at a repetition rate equal to the **aggregate bit rate** (or a sub-multiple that is multiplied later). Pulse widths are on the order of **1–10 picoseconds**. 2. **Data Modulation:** The pulse train is split and each branch is fed into a high-speed **external electro-optic modulator (e.g., LiNbO₃ Mach-Zehnder modulator)**. Each modulator imprints one tributary data stream onto its pulse train. 3. **Optical Delay Lines:** Each modulated tributary is given a **precise time delay** of integer multiples of the aggregate bit period: - Channel 1 → delay **0** - Channel 2 → delay **T_b** - Channel 3 → delay **2T_b** - ... - Channel N → delay **(N−1)T_b** 4. **Passive Optical Combiner:** All N delayed pulse trains are combined using a **passive fiber coupler** (e.g., 1×N star coupler) into a single fiber. Because the pulses are interleaved in time, the result is a **single aggregate OTDM signal** at bit rate N·B. **Stage B — Optical Demultiplexing (Receiver Side)** 1. **Clock Recovery (CR):** A high-Q optical resonator (e.g., a fiber Fabry-Perot filter) or an all-optical phase-locked loop extracts the **bit-rate clock** from the aggregate stream. 2. **Optical Gating / Demultiplexing:** An ultrafast **optical gate** (acting as a time-window switch) is opened only during the time slot occupied by the desired tributary. Common gating technologies: - **Nonlinear Optical Loop Mirror (NOLM)** — uses fiber Kerr nonlinearity + asymmetric coupler. - **Semiconductor Optical Amplifier (SOA) — based nonlinear interferometer** (e.g., TOAD, SLALOM, UNI). - **Electro-Absorption Modulator (EAM)** — driven by the recovered clock. - **Four-Wave Mixing (FWM)** in a nonlinear fiber or SOA. 3. **Baseband Detection:** The extracted tributary pulses are detected by a **photodetector** whose bandwidth only needs to match the **tributary** rate, not the aggregate rate — a critical electronic bottleneck relief. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why OTDM?** At ≥ 160 Gb/s per channel, electronic serializers/deserializers (SerDes) become extremely lossy and power-hungry. OTDM moves the speed bottleneck from electronics into the optical domain, where **femtosecond phenomena** are readily exploited. ### 2.2 Frame Structure and Timing Relationships An OTDM **frame** consists of **N consecutive time slots**, one per tributary, spanning a frame period **T_frame**. Within each frame, **one bit from every tributary** is transmitted. Therefore: $$T_{frame} = N \cdot T_{bit,tributary} = T_{bit,aggregate}$$ Each tributary's bit period in the **aggregate** stream becomes **N times shorter** than its original tributary period. Concretely: $$\tau_{slot} = \frac{T_{bit,aggregate}}{N} = \frac{T_{bit,tributary}}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \text{(no, see below)}$$ Clarifying the time-slot math: $$\tau_{slot} = T_{bit,aggregate} = \frac{1}{N \cdot B_{tributary}}$$ For example, with N = 4 tributaries at B = 10 Gb/s: - Tributary bit period = 100 ps - Aggregate bit period = 25 ps - Each tributary bit occupies a 25-ps wide slot in the aggregate stream. ### 2.3 Pulse Width Constraint To avoid **inter-symbol interference (ISI)** between adjacent tributaries, the optical pulse width **τₚ must be significantly less than the time slot** τ_slot. The empirical design rule is: $$\tau_{p} \leq \frac{\tau_{slot}}{4} \quad \text{(conservative)}$$ $$\tau_{p} \leq \frac{\tau_{slot}}{2} \quad \text{(Nyquist limit)}$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Relation | Unit | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---|---| | $N$ | Number of tributaries | — | integer | 2, 4, 8, 16 | | $B_{trib}$ | Tributary bit rate | given | Gb/s | 10 – 40 | | $B_{agg}$ | Aggregate (OTDM) bit rate | $N \cdot B_{trib}$ | Gb/s | 40 – 640 | | $T_{b,agg}$ | Aggregate bit period | $1 / B_{agg}$ | ps | 1.5 – 25 | | $T_{frame}$ | OTDM frame period | $N / B_{agg}$ | ps | equal to $1/B_{trib}$ | | $\tau_{slot}$ | Time slot per channel | $T_{b,agg}$ | ps | 1.5 – 25 | | $\tau_{p}$ | Optical pulse width (FWHM) | $\leq \tau_{slot}/4$ | ps | 0.5 – 5 | | $\Delta t_k$ | Delay for k-th tributary | $(k-1) \cdot T_{b,agg}$ | ps | integer multiple | | $P_{avg}$ | Average transmitted power | $P_{peak} \cdot \tau_p \cdot B_{agg}$ | mW | — | | $OSNR$ | Required OSNR | $\propto B_{opt}$ | dB | ≥ 25 dB for 640 Gb/s | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Core backbone networks** demanding > 100 Gb/s per wavelength. - **High-speed OTDM/WDM super-channels** (Terabit/s aggregate). - **Photonic switching fabrics** for all-optical time-slot interchange. - **Optical sampling** of ultra-fast electrical signals. - **Test & measurement** — OTDM provides reference signals for characterizing high-speed photodetectors and A/D converters. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Aggregate Bit Rate and Time-Slot Geometry **Problem Setup:** An OTDM transmitter combines **N = 8** tributaries, each operating at **B_trib = 10 Gb/s**. **Goal:** Determine the aggregate bit rate, aggregate bit period, frame period, time slot per channel, and the required optical pulse width. **Step 1 — Aggregate bit rate.** The aggregate bit rate is the sum of the tributary rates (since one tributary bit occupies one slot per frame, and a frame contains N slots transmitted in one tributary bit-period): $$B_{agg} = N \cdot B_{trib} = 8 \times 10 \;\text{Gb/s} = 80 \;\text{Gb/s}$$ **Step 2 — Aggregate bit period.** The aggregate bit period is the inverse of the aggregate bit rate: $$T_{b,agg} = \frac{1}{B_{agg}} = \frac{1}{80 \times 10^{9}}\;\text{s} = 12.5\;\text{ps}$$ **Step 3 — Time slot per tributary.** Since there are 8 slots in one tributary bit period (a frame), and they are equally spaced: $$\tau_{slot} = T_{b,agg} = 12.5 \;\text{ps}$$ **Step 4 — Frame period.** A frame carries one bit from each tributary. The frame duration equals one tributary bit period: $$T_{frame} = \frac{1}{B_{trib}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{9}}\;\text{s} = 100\;\text{ps}$$ **Step 5 — Verification.** Number of slots per frame: $$\frac{T_{frame}}{\tau_{slot}} = \frac{100 \;\text{ps}}{12.5 \;\text{ps}} = 8 = N \;\checkmark$$ **Step 6 — Pulse width requirement.** Adopt the conservative rule $\tau_p \leq \tau_{slot}/4$: $$\tau_{p} \leq \frac{12.5}{4} \;\text{ps} = 3.125 \;\text{ps}$$ A mode-locked laser producing **τ_p ≈ 2–3 ps** pulses is therefore appropriate. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Because the pulse must fit in a 12.5-ps slot with margin, dispersion becomes a major issue. **Dispersion-Shifted Fiber (DSF)** or **Non-Zero Dispersion-Shifted Fiber (NZ-DSF)** with **dispersion compensation** is mandatory for 80-Gb/s OTDM transmission beyond a few kilometers. ### 3.2 Derivation: Optical Delay for the k-th Tributary **Goal:** Show the general delay formula and compute the delay for the 5th tributary in a 160-Gb/s, 16-channel system. **Setup:** Each tributary's pulse train must be delayed by an integer multiple of the aggregate bit period so that its bits fall into the k-th slot of the frame. **Step 1 — General formula.** $$\Delta t_{k} = (k-1) \cdot T_{b,agg} = \frac{(k-1)}{B_{agg}}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$ **Step 2 — Compute aggregate period.** For B_agg = 160 Gb/s: $$T_{b,agg} = \frac{1}{160 \times 10^{9}} = 6.25 \;\text{ps}$$ **Step 3 — Delay for the 5th tributary (k = 5).** $$\Delta t_{5} = (5-1) \times 6.25 \;\text{ps} = 25 \;\text{ps}$$ This delay is typically realized using a **coiled single-mode fiber** of precise length, where the group delay per meter is approximately: $$\Delta L = \frac{\Delta t \cdot c}{n_g}$$ With $n_g \approx 1.47$ for silica at 1550 nm: $$\Delta L = \frac{25 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{1.47} \approx 5.1 \;\text{mm}$$ So a **5-mm** change in fiber length corresponds to a **25-ps** delay increment — extraordinarily fine, demanding **picosecond stability** and temperature control. ### 3.3 Derivation: Power Budget — Peak vs. Average Power **Problem:** Compute the peak power required for an average transmitted power of **0 dBm (1 mW)** in a 640-Gb/s OTDM system using **2-ps** pulses. **Step 1 — Duty cycle.** The duty cycle is the fraction of time the laser emits a pulse: $$D = \tau_{p} \cdot B_{agg} = 2 \times 10^{-12} \times 640 \times 10^{9} = 1.28 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 2 — Peak power.** The peak power is the average power divided by the duty cycle: $$P_{peak} = \frac{P_{avg}}{D} = \frac{1 \;\text{mW}}{1.28 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.78 \;\text{W}$$ This shows that OTDM pulses can reach **sub-Watt peak powers** at modest average power — significant for activating fiber nonlinearities in NOLM demultiplexers. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of OTDM Demultiplexing Techniques | Technique | Switching Speed | Contrast Ratio | Power Required | Practical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **NOLM (fiber)** | **< 1 ps** | **20–30 dB** | Peak ~ 100 mW | Lab-scale 160 Gb/s | | **SOA-MZI (UNI, TOAD)** | **1–10 ps** | **20–40 dB** | Few mW average | Commercial 40–160 Gb/s | | **EAM** | **10–30 ps** | **15–25 dB** | Few V drive | Tributary selection | | **FWM in SOA/HNLF** | **< 1 ps** | **15–25 dB** | Tens of mW | Format conversion | | **Electronic (ETDM)** | **> 10 ps** | High | — | Limited to ~100 Gb/s | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 OTDM Transmitter — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Mode-Locked Laser
Picosecond Pulse Train] --> B[1xN Optical Splitter] B --> C1[Modulator 1
LiNbO3 MZM
Tributary 1 Data] B --> C2[Modulator 2
LiNbO3 MZM
Tributary 2 Data] B --> C3[Modulator 3
LiNbO3 MZM
Tributary 3 Data] B --> C4[Modulator 4
LiNbO3 MZM
Tributary 4 Data] C1 --> D1[Optical Delay Line
Delay 0] C2 --> D2[Optical Delay Line
Delay T_b] C3 --> D3[Optical Delay Line
Delay 2T_b] C4 --> D4[Optical Delay Line
Delay 3T_b] D1 --> E[Passive 4x1 Optical Combiner] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E E --> F[Aggregate OTDM Signal
N x B Gb/s Output] ``` ### 4.2 OTDM Receiver — Demultiplexing Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Aggregate OTDM Signal
N x B Gb/s] --> B[Optical Pre-Amplifier
EDFA] B --> C[Clock Recovery
Fabry-Perot Filter] B --> D[Optical Gate
NOLM or SOA-MZI] C --> E[Clock Driver
Synchronization] E --> D D --> F[Photodetector
Tributary Bandwidth] F --> G[Decision Circuit
Threshold Detector] G --> H[Recovered Tributary Data
B Gb/s] ``` ### 4.3 OTDM Frame and Time-Slot Mapping ```mermaid gantt title OTDM Frame Structure (N = 4 tributaries) dateFormat X axisFormat %S section Frame 1 Ch1 bit (delay 0) :a1, 0, 25 Ch2 bit (delay T_b) :a2, 25, 25 Ch3 bit (delay 2T_b) :a3, 50, 25 Ch4 bit (delay 3T_b) :a4, 75, 25 section Frame 2 Ch1 bit :b1, 100, 25 Ch2 bit :b2, 125, 25 Ch3 bit :b3, 150, 25 Ch4 bit :b4, 175, 25 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** Each horizontal block represents a **25-ps slot** in a **100-ps frame** (4 × 25 ps = 100 ps). The aggregate bit rate is 1/25 ps = **40 Gb/s**, while each tributary runs at 1/100 ps = **10 Gb/s**. This visualizes the essence of OTDM: **N tributaries squeezed into one wavelength's time domain**. ### 4.4 OTDM vs WDM — Functional Comparison Block ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph WDM W1[Wavelength 1 Channel] --> WC1[Mux] W2[Wavelength 2 Channel] --> WC1 W3[Wavelength N Channel] --> WC1 WC1 --> WF[Single Fiber Output] end subgraph OTDM O1[Tributary 1 Pulses] --> OC1[Time Interleaver] O2[Tributary 2 Pulses] --> OC1 O3[Tributary N Pulses] --> OC1 OC1 --> OF[Single Wavelength Output] end ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Define Optical Time Division Multiplexing (OTDM). What is its principal advantage over conventional ETDM (Electronic TDM)?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** OTDM is a technique in which several lower-bit-rate optical data streams are **interleaved in the time domain** at the optical layer using picosecond pulses and precise optical delay lines, producing a single high-bit-rate stream at **N × B** where N is the number of tributaries and B is the tributary rate. **Advantage over ETDM (1 mark):** OTDM overcomes the **electronic speed bottleneck** (~100 Gb/s) by performing multiplexing in the optical domain. The receiver electronics only need to operate at the tributary rate B, not at the aggregate rate N·B, enabling ultra-high aggregate bit rates (160 Gb/s, 640 Gb/s, beyond). **Key enabling component (1 mark):** Mode-locked laser + optical delay lines + all-optical demultiplexer (e.g., NOLM). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** **List the three main functional blocks of an OTDM transmitter and state the purpose of the optical delay line.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Mode-locked laser (MLL)** – generates a stable train of picosecond optical pulses acting as the OTDM "carrier clock." (1 mark) 2. **Optical modulator bank** – N external modulators (e.g., LiNbO₃ MZMs) imprint independent tributary data on N split copies of the pulse train. (1 mark) 3. **Optical delay line + passive combiner** – delays each tributary by $(k-1)·T_b$ so that bits from different tributaries interleave without collision; combiner merges them into the aggregate OTDM stream. (1 mark) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently state "delay = tributary bit period" instead of "delay = aggregate bit period." Remember: the delay must shift pulses by the **aggregate** slot width T_b,agg = 1/(N·B). A 1-mark penalty is common for this confusion. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice**: Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both carry equal marks. Each is split into two 7-mark parts. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of an OTDM transmitter**. Clearly identify the role of the mode-locked laser, modulators, and delay lines. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — System overview (1 mark):** An OTDM transmitter combines N low-rate tributary channels in the time domain onto a single wavelength using a pulsed optical source and precisely delayed data-modulated pulse trains. **Step 2 — Mode-locked laser (1 mark):** A mode-locked laser (typically erbium-doped fiber ring laser or semiconductor MLL) emits a periodic train of ultra-short pulses (1–10 ps wide) at a repetition rate equal to the **aggregate bit rate B_agg = N·B**. **Step 3 — Optical splitter (1 mark):** A 1×N passive splitter divides the pulse train into N identical copies. **Step 4 — Modulator array (1 mark):** Each copy is fed into an external electro-optic modulator (LiNbO₃ Mach-Zehnder), driven by one tributary data stream (e.g., 10 Gb/s NRZ or RZ). **Step 5 — Optical delay lines (2 marks):** Each tributary's pulse train is delayed by $(k-1)·T_{b,agg}$ relative to channel 1, where $T_{b,agg} = 1/(N·B)$ is the aggregate bit period. This staggering ensures that the bits from different channels fall into **non-overlapping consecutive time slots** within each OTDM frame. **Step 6 — Passive combiner (1 mark):** A 1×N star coupler or tree coupler combines all delayed pulse trains into one fiber. The output is a **single-wavelength OTDM signal** with bit rate N·B. **Diagram (drawn on answer sheet):** Present the block diagram from §4.1, clearly labeling all blocks and the delay values 0, T_b, 2T_b, ..., (N-1)T_b. --- **(b)** An OTDM system multiplexes **N = 16** tributaries each at **B = 10 Gb/s**. (i) Compute the **aggregate bit rate**, **aggregate bit period**, **frame period**, and **time slot per channel**. (ii) Determine the **required optical pulse width** using the conservative design rule. (iii) Compute the **optical delay** for the **8th tributary**. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(i) Timing parameters (3 marks):** $$B_{agg} = N \cdot B = 16 \times 10 = 160 \;\text{Gb/s}$$ $$T_{b,agg} = \frac{1}{B_{agg}} = \frac{1}{160 \times 10^{9}} = 6.25 \;\text{ps}$$ $$T_{frame} = \frac{1}{B_{trib}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{9}} = 100 \;\text{ps}$$ $$\tau_{slot} = T_{b,agg} = 6.25 \;\text{ps}$$ **Verification:** Slots per frame = 100 / 6.25 = **16** ✓ **(ii) Pulse width (2 marks):** $$\tau_p \leq \frac{\tau_{slot}}{4} = \frac{6.25}{4} = 1.5625 \;\text{ps}$$ A **τ_p ≈ 1.5 ps** pulse train is required. **(iii) Delay for k = 8 (2 marks):** $$\Delta t_8 = (8-1) \cdot T_{b,agg} = 7 \times 6.25 = 43.75 \;\text{ps}$$ Equivalently, with $n_g \approx 1.47$ at 1550 nm: $$\Delta L = \frac{43.75 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{1.47} \approx 8.93 \;\text{mm}$$ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a)** Explain the **working of an OTDM demultiplexer** based on the **Nonlinear Optical Loop Mirror (NOLM)**. Discuss why NOLM is preferred at ultra-high bit rates. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — NOLM topology (2 marks):** A NOLM is a fiber loop closed on itself with a 50:50 coupler. The input OTDM signal enters the loop, splits into two counter-propagating copies. A coupler asymmetry (typically by inserting an **erbium-doped fiber amplifier** near one end of the loop) makes the two counter-propagating beams experience **different gains** and hence different nonlinear phase shifts. **Step 2 — Nonlinear phase shift (2 marks):** The fiber's Kerr nonlinearity causes a **self-phase modulation (SPM)** proportional to instantaneous power: $$\Delta \phi_{NL} = \gamma \cdot P_{in} \cdot L_{eff}$$ Because the two beams see different gains, they accumulate different nonlinear phases. **Step 3 — Switching action (2 marks):** When the control pulse coincides with the target OTDM slot, the asymmetry creates a π phase difference. The two counter-propagating beams then interfere **destructively at the input port** and **constructively at the output port** — the loop "reflects" in the absence of control and "transmits" only the targeted time slot. **Step 4 — Why NOLM at high bit rates (1 mark):** Switching window is set by the **walk-off length** between control and signal (typically < 1 ps), enabling demultiplexing of 160–640 Gb/s OTDM signals where electronics would fail. --- **(b)** Compare OTDM with **WDM** as multiplexing techniques. Mention **two advantages** and **two limitations** of OTDM. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Comparison table (3 marks):** | Parameter | OTDM | WDM | |---|---|---| | Domain | Time | Wavelength | | Channel separation | Picosecond delay | Nanometer wavelength spacing | | Source requirement | Mode-locked picosecond laser | Multiple CW lasers or comb | | Electronics | Tributary rate | Per-wavelength rate | | Capacity scaling | Increase N | Add wavelengths | | Best use | > 100 Gb/s/λ | Multiple 10/40/100 Gb/s channels | **Two advantages of OTDM (2 marks):** 1. **Single-wavelength operation** → avoids need for multiple stabilized laser sources and wavelength-selective components. 2. **Electronics relief** → receiver bandwidth equals tributary rate, easing the SerDes bottleneck. 3. (Bonus) Naturally compatible with **all-optical processing** (logic, regeneration, switching). **Two limitations of OTDM (2 marks):** 1. **Strict synchronization & timing jitter** required at picosecond scale → complex clock recovery, costly. 2. **Strong sensitivity to chromatic & polarization-mode dispersion** because pulses are sub-10 ps. 3. (Bonus) **Nonlinear crosstalk** from fiber Kerr effects (XPM, FWM) becomes severe at high peak powers. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — OTDM Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Confusing N·B with N+B:** Aggregate is **N × B**, not N + B. (Lose 1–2 marks.) > 2. **Delay = aggregate bit period, NOT tributary bit period.** The delay line staggers by $T_{b,agg} = 1/(N \cdot B)$, not $1/B$. > 3. **Forgetting that frame period = tributary bit period** in OTDM. This single relationship is worth 1–2 marks on most derivations. > 4. **Drawing the block diagram without labeling the delays** (0, T, 2T ...). Examiners explicitly look for delay markings. > 5. **Mixing OTDM with WDM** — students often write "OTDM uses different wavelengths" — that is **wrong**. OTDM uses **one wavelength, multiple time slots**. > 6. **No mention of pulse-width constraint** when asked for system design. Always state τ_p ≤ τ_slot/4. --- ### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST - **Definition** — OTDM = bit-interleaving of N tributaries at one wavelength using picosecond pulses ⇒ aggregate rate = **N·B**. - **Why OTDM?** Overcomes the **electronic speed bottleneck** (>100 Gb/s) by performing multiplexing/demultiplexing optically. - **Three TX blocks:** Mode-Locked Laser → 1×N Splitter → Modulators → **Delay Lines (0, T_b, ..., (N-1)T_b)** → 1×N Combiner. - **Three RX blocks:** Optical Pre-Amp → Clock Recovery → **Optical Gate (NOLM/SOA-MZI/EAM)** → Photodetector → Decision. - **Frame period = tributary bit period = N × (aggregate bit period).** - **Time slot = aggregate bit period = 1/(N·B).** - **Pulse width rule:** τ_p ≤ τ_slot / 4 (conservative). - **Delay formula:** Δt_k = (k−1) / B_agg. - **Common demux techs:** NOLM (sub-ps, fiber-based), SOA-MZI (1–10 ps), EAM (10–30 ps), FWM (sub-ps). - **Engineering challenges:** timing jitter, chromatic dispersion, polarization-mode dispersion, fiber nonlinearities (XPM, FWM), clock-recovery complexity. - **Typical numbers:** 4 × 40 Gb/s = 160 Gb/s; 16 × 40 Gb/s = 640 Gb/s; pulses 1–10 ps. - **OTDM vs WDM:** Time vs Frequency (wavelength); OTDM uses **one wavelength, many time slots**; WDM uses **many wavelengths, one time slot**. - **Hybrid OTDM/WDM:** combines both for **Tbps** super-channels in modern backbone networks. - **OTDM Pulse Source:** Mode-locked Erbium-Doped Fiber Ring Laser (EDFRL) is the workhorse. - **Remember:** In all derivations, carry units explicitly — KTU examiners reward correct units and penalize silent unit confusion.

topics.title: WDM and Optical CDMA: Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** WDM and Optical CDMA: Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM)** is a fiber-optic multiplexing technique that simultaneously transmits multiple optical carrier signals on a single optical fiber by assigning each signal a unique wavelength (frequency) band. The channels are combined at the transmit end using a **multiplexer (MUX)** and separated at the receive end using a **demultiplexer (DEMUX)**, typically conforming to the **ITU-T G.694.1** standard frequency grid.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Multi-Lane Highway"
Imagine a single-lane road (one optical fiber) carrying cars (data bits). WDM is like turning that road into a **multi-lane highway** where each lane carries cars moving independently. The "lanes" are actually different **colors (wavelengths)** of light, all travelling together inside the same glass fiber without colliding because light waves of different frequencies do not interfere with each other linearly. A **prism (the MUX/DEMUX)** is used to merge or split these colored beams.

---

## 1.2 Optical Code Division Multiple Access (Optical CDMA / OCDMA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Optical CDMA (OCDMA)** is a spread-spectrum multiple-access technique in which each user is assigned a unique high-rate **optical code sequence** (a pseudo-random pattern of optical pulses). All users transmit simultaneously over the same wavelength band; the receiver recovers a specific user's data by correlating the received composite signal with that user's own code. The technique relies on **Optical Orthogonal Codes (OOC)** or spectral phase encoding to maintain low cross-talk between users.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Secret Knock"
Imagine many people speaking in the same room. Without rules, no one understands anything. Now imagine each speaker whispers a unique **rhythmic pattern of taps** (their "secret knock") along with their words. Listeners can **filter out** everyone except the speaker whose knock matches their own — that's exactly how OCDMA works. Each user's bits are "tagged" with a high-speed pseudo-random optical code (the knock), and the decoder uses **correlation** to extract only the matching user.

---

## 1.3 Why These Technologies Matter (KTU Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Drivers:**
> - **Bandwidth Explosion:** A single-mode fiber has a theoretical low-loss window of ~**50 THz** (around the **1550 nm** band). A single wavelength uses only a tiny fraction of this — WDM/OCDMA unlock the rest.
> - **Cost per bit:** Multiplexing many low-rate channels over one fiber is far cheaper than laying new fibers (**CAPEX/OPEX** reduction).
> - **Transparency:** WDM is **bit-rate and protocol agnostic**, supporting Ethernet, SDH/SONET, OTN, etc.

### 1.4 Classification of WDM (Board-Favorite Question)

| Parameter | **CWDM** (Coarse WDM) | **DWDM** (Dense WDM) |
|---|---|---|
| Channel Spacing | **20 nm** (often 2.5 nm grid) | **0.8 / 0.4 / 0.2 / 0.1 nm** |
| Number of Channels | Up to **18** | Up to **96 / 128 / 160+** |
| Spectral Band | **1270–1610 nm** (O–L bands) | **1525–1565 nm** (C-band), extended to L-band |
| Source Specification | Uncooled lasers allowed | **Temperature-stabilized / wavelength-locked** lasers |
| Cost | Low (no EDFA typically) | High (EDFA + precise filters required) |
| Typical Reach | Metro / Access (< 80 km) | Long-haul / Ultra-long-haul |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 3-mark question is to **compare CWDM vs DWDM**. Always remember that *CWDM is for short reach / low cost*, while *DWDM is for high capacity / long haul*.

---

## 1.5 Optical CDMA — Coherent vs Incoherent

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Major OCDMA Families:**
> 1. **Incoherent OCDMA** — uses **time-spreading** of broadband optical pulses (e.g., **Optical Orthogonal Codes**, **Prime Codes**). Detection is based on optical intensity (direct detection).
> 2. **Coherent OCDMA** — uses **Spectral Phase Encoding (SPE)** of ultra-short optical pulses with a phase mask. Detection requires coherent processing and is more complex but supports many more users.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 WDM System Architecture — Components

A WDM link consists of the following functional blocks (memorize the block diagram; it appears in nearly every KTU paper):

1. **Optical Transmitters** — N lasers tuned to N distinct wavelengths (λ₁, λ₂, …, λ_N). In modern systems, these may be replaced by a single multi-wavelength source (e.g., a comb laser).
2. **Optical Multiplexer (OMUX)** — Combines all wavelengths onto a single fiber. Realized using **thin-film filters (TFF)**, **arrayed waveguide gratings (AWG)**, or **fiber Bragg gratings (FBG)**.
3. **Transmission Fiber** — Typically **single-mode fiber (SMF)** with low attenuation in the **1550 nm** window (~0.2 dB/km). Long-haul links insert **EDFAs** (Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifiers) periodically.
4. **In-Line Amplifiers / Dispersion Compensators** — **EDFA** (gain band ~**C-band: 1525–1565 nm**) or **Raman amplifiers** for long distances; **DCF (Dispersion Compensating Fiber)** or **DCM (Dispersion Compensation Module)** to combat chromatic dispersion.
5. **Optical Demultiplexer (ODEMUX)** — Separates wavelengths back into individual fibers. Symmetrical counterpart of the OMUX.
6. **Optical Receivers** — One per channel (PIN / APD photodiodes with O/E converters).

### 2.2 Working Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Multiplexing (MUX):** The OMUX is essentially a **wavelength-selective combiner**. Each port passes only one wavelength with minimal insertion loss, while reflecting/transmitting all others. This works because of the **interference of thin dielectric layers**.
- **EDFA Amplification:** WDM's killer advantage over TDM is that a single EDFA can amplify **all wavelengths simultaneously**, since the gain spectrum of erbium ions covers the entire C-band. This makes WDM scale economically.
- **Demultiplexing (DEMUX):** Reverse of MUX. AWGs use the **diffraction principle** in integrated silica waveguides to spatially separate wavelengths.

---

## 2.3 Optical Orthogonal Codes (OOC) — The Heart of Incoherent OCDMA

An **Optical Orthogonal Code** is a family of binary sequences with special correlation properties. Each code is characterized by four parameters: **(F, w, λₐ, λ_c)** where:

- **F** = code length (number of chips per bit)
- **w** = code weight (number of "1"s per code; "1" means a pulse is sent)
- **λₐ** = maximum out-of-phase **autocorrelation**
- **λ_c** = maximum **cross-correlation** between any two distinct codes

### Correlation Conditions for an OOC (F, w, 1, 1)

> [!NOTE]
> For a code `a` of length F, the autocorrelation and cross-correlation are:
>
> $\Phi_{aa}(\tau) = \sum_{i=0}^{F-1} a_i \cdot a_{(i+\tau) \bmod F}$
>
> $\Phi_{ab}(\tau) = \sum_{i=0}^{F-1} a_i \cdot b_{(i+\tau) \bmod F}$

The OOC conditions are:

$$\Phi_{aa}(\tau) \le \lambda_a \quad \text{for } \tau \ne 0$$

$$\Phi_{ab}(\tau) \le \lambda_c \quad \text{for all } \tau \text{ and } a \ne b$$

For **λₐ = λ_c = 1**, the well-known **cardinality bound** (maximum number of users) is:

$$|C| \le \left\lfloor \frac{F - 1}{w(w - 1)} \right\rfloor$$

This bound is a *direct consequence* of the Johnson-type combinatorial limit and is the most important OOC result tested in KTU.

---

## 2.4 Coherent OCDMA — Spectral Phase Encoding (Spectral Phase Encoding, SPE)

In coherent OCDMA, an ultrashort optical pulse (bandwidth Δf) is spread across **M spectral bins**, each of width **δf**. A **phase mask** assigns a phase of either **0** or **π** to each bin. The encoded signal becomes:

$$E_{\text{enc}}(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} e^{j \phi_k} \cdot p(t) \cdot e^{j 2\pi f_k t}$$

where `φ_k ∈ {0, π}` is the phase applied by the k-th mask element and `p(t)` is the original pulse envelope. Only the **matched receiver** (using the conjugate phase mask) reconstructs the autocorrelation peak (a narrow pulse), while unmatched users produce low-intensity noise.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in ≥ 70% of WDM/OCDMA numerical questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of WDM channels | $N = \dfrac{\lambda_{\max} - \lambda_{\min}}{\Delta\lambda} + 1$ | λ in **nm**, Δλ in **nm** |
| 2 | Frequency ↔ Wavelength | $\Delta f \approx \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta\lambda}{\lambda^2}$ | c = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**, Δf in **Hz** |
| 3 | Aggregate bit rate | $B_{\text{agg}} = N \times B_{\text{ch}}$ | **bps** |
| 4 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{B_{\text{agg}}}{\Delta f_{\text{total}}}$ | **bps/Hz** |
| 5 | EDFA gain (small signal) | $G_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{out}} / P_{\text{in}})$ | **dB** |
| 6 | Amplifier spacing | $L_{\text{span}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}} - P_{\text{in}} - \text{margin}}{\alpha}$ | km |
| 7 | OOC cardinality (λₐ = λ_c = 1) | $\vert C \vert \le \left\lfloor \dfrac{F - 1}{w(w - 1)} \right\rfloor$ | pure number |
| 8 | OOC max weight (for |C| ≥ 2) | $w \le \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 4(F-1)}}{2}$ | — |
| 9 | Autocorrelation (in-phase) | $\Phi_{aa}(0) = w$ | — |
| 10 | SNR penalty (OCDMA MAI) | $P_{\text{pen}} \approx \dfrac{(N-1) w^2}{2 F}$ | linear units |
| 11 | OCDMA chip rate | $R_{\text{chip}} = R_{\text{data}} \times F$ | **chips/s** |
| 12 | Dispersion limit (approx) | $L_{\max} \approx \dfrac{1}{4 \cdot \beta_2 \cdot B^2 \cdot D_\text{tol}}$ | km |

### Real-World Utility

- **Telecom backbones:** Modern DWDM systems carry **≥ 10 Tbps** per fiber pair using coherent detection and 64-QAM (e.g., 80 × 200 Gbps channels over the C-band).
- **5G Xhaul:** CWDM and DWDM form the optical underlay for **fronthaul / midhaul** networks.
- **Sensor Networks:** OCDMA's asynchronous, soft-capacity nature makes it attractive for **fiber-to-the-home (FTTH)** and **avionics / defense** secure LANs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Channel Count from Wavelength Grid

**Problem:** A WDM system operates in the C-band from **1530 nm to 1565 nm** with a channel spacing of **0.8 nm** (DWDM). Calculate the **maximum number of channels** N.

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{\lambda_{\max} - \lambda_{\min}}{\Delta\lambda} + 1 \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{1565~\text{nm} - 1530~\text{nm}}{0.8~\text{nm}} + 1 \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{35}{0.8} + 1 \\[6pt]
  &= 43.75 + 1 \\[6pt]
  &= 44.75
\end{aligned}
$$

Since N must be an **integer**, we take the **floor**:

$$N_{\text{actual}} = \lfloor 44.75 \rfloor = 44 \text{ channels}$$

> **Interpretation:** The +1 term accounts for the fact that we count both endpoints — N intervals produce N+1 grid points. A real system would report either 43 or 44 channels depending on whether both edges are usable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — OCDMA Code Cardinality (Johnson Bound)

**Problem:** Design an OOC with code length **F = 31**, weight **w = 3**, and λₐ = λ_c = 1. Find the **maximum number of users** (code cardinality).

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
|C| &\le \left\lfloor \frac{F - 1}{w(w - 1)} \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
    &\le \left\lfloor \frac{31 - 1}{3 \cdot (3 - 1)} \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
    &\le \left\lfloor \frac{30}{6} \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
    &\le \lfloor 5 \rfloor \\[6pt]
    &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A code family of length 31 and weight 3 can support at most **5 simultaneous users** while keeping the cross-correlation bounded by 1. To increase capacity, we either **increase F** (longer codes → more chip slots) or **increase w** (heavier codes) — both at the cost of bandwidth.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aggregate Capacity of a WDM Link with EDFA Spacing

**Problem:** A long-haul DWDM link has **40 channels** each carrying **100 Gbps** QPSK data. The launch power per channel is **0 dBm**, fiber loss is **0.2 dB/km**, EDFA gain is selected to compensate exactly for span loss with a **3 dB margin**. Determine:
1. The **aggregate bit rate**.
2. The **EDFA spacing (span length)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part 1 — Aggregate Bit Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{agg}} &= N \times B_{\text{ch}} \\[4pt]
              &= 40 \times 100~\text{Gbps} \\[4pt]
              &= 4000~\text{Gbps} = 4~\text{Tbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 2 — EDFA Spacing:**

Let P_out = P_in (saturation ignored); the EDFA must compensate span loss plus 3 dB margin.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{span}} &= \frac{P_{\text{out,dBm}} - P_{\text{in,dBm}} + \text{margin}}{\alpha} \\[6pt]
                &= \frac{0 - 0 + 3}{0.2} \\[6pt]
                &= 15~\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Realistic EDFA spacing for 100 Gbps coherent systems is **80–100 km**, but this assumes high-power EDFAs (~+17 dBm) and Raman assistance. The example above uses 0 dBm for clarity — in practice, P_in and P_out are very different.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Spectral Efficiency of a DWDM Grid

**Problem:** A DWDM system carries **80 channels** at **200 Gbps** each (DP-16QAM), with channel spacing of **50 GHz**. Compute the **spectral efficiency**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{agg}} &= 80 \times 200~\text{Gbps} = 16,000~\text{Gbps} = 16~\text{Tbps} \\[6pt]
\Delta f_{\text{total}} &= 80 \times 50~\text{GHz} = 4000~\text{GHz} = 4~\text{THz} \\[6pt]
\eta &= \frac{B_{\text{agg}}}{\Delta f_{\text{total}}} = \frac{16,000~\text{Gbps}}{4000~\text{GHz}} = 4~\text{bps/Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** A real DP-16QAM signal achieves **4 bps/Hz per polarization × 2 pol = 8 bps/Hz**, but our number accounts for the guard band between channels.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — OOC Trade-offs

| F (length) | w (weight) | Bound λ=1 | Bandwidth expansion | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 31 | 3 | 5 | 31× | Low capacity, low rate loss |
| 63 | 5 | 3 | 63× | Higher SNR, fewer users |
| 127 | 7 | 3 | 127× | Used in research systems |
| 1000 | 31 | 1 | 1000× | Single user, very low MAI |

> **Takeaway:** Increasing F improves **orthogonality** but expands the **chip rate** required from the transmitter.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End WDM System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Tx1 lambda1] --> MUX
    T2[Tx2 lambda2] --> MUX
    T3[TxN lambdaN] --> MUX
    MUX[Optical MUX OMUX] --> FIBER[SMF Transmission Fiber]
    FIBER --> EDFA1[In-line EDFA 1]
    EDFA1 --> FIBER2[SMF Span 2]
    FIBER2 --> EDFA2[In-line EDFA 2]
    EDFA2 --> FIBER3[SMF Span N]
    FIBER3 --> DEMUX[Optical DEMUX ODMUX]
    DEMUX --> R1[Rx1 lambda1]
    DEMUX --> R2[Rx2 lambda2]
    DEMUX --> R3[RxN lambdaN]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Coherent OCDMA Encoder / Decoder Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[Ultrashort Optical Pulse Source] --> FBS[Fiber Bragg Grating Spectral Decomposer]
    FBS --> MASK1[Phase Mask Encoder user A]
    FBS --> MASK2[Phase Mask Encoder user B]
    MASK1 --> COUPLER[Optical Star Coupler Shared Medium]
    MASK2 --> COUPLER
    COUPLER --> FBSRX[Spectral Recombiner at Receiver]
    FBSRX --> DECMASK[Matched Phase Mask Decoder]
    DECMASK --> PHOTO[Photodetector]
    PHOTO --> THRESH[Threshold Decision]
    THRESH --> DATAOUT[Recovered Data Bit]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Incoherent OCDMA (Time-Spreading) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    USER1[User 1 Bit] --> ENC1[Encoder OOC Code C1]
    USER2[User 2 Bit] --> ENC2[Encoder OOC Code C2]
    USER3[User K Bit] --> ENCK[Encoder OOC Code CK]
    ENC1 --> STAR[Optical Star Coupler]
    ENC2 --> STAR
    ENCK --> STAR
    STAR --> FIBER[Shared Fiber Channel]
    FIBER --> DEC1[Decoder matched C1]
    FIBER --> DEC2[Decoder matched C2]
    FIBER --> DECK[Decoder matched CK]
    DEC1 --> TH1[Threshold photodetector]
    DEC2 --> TH2[Threshold photodetector]
    DECK --> THK[Threshold photodetector]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Comparison Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MUXTECH[WDM and OCDMA Multiplexing Family] --> WDM[WDM Wavelength Division]
    MUXTECH --> OCDMA[OCDMA Code Division]
    WDM --> CWDM[CWDM Coarse 20 nm]
    WDM --> DWDM[DWDM Dense 0.8 nm]
    WDM --> UDWDM[UDWDM Ultra Dense 0.05 nm]
    OCDMA --> INCOH[Incoherent Time Spreading]
    OCDMA --> COH[Coherent Spectral Phase Encoding]
    INCOH --> OOC[Optical Orthogonal Codes OOC]
    INCOH --> PRIME[Prime Codes]
    COH --> SPE[Spectral Phase Encoding]
    COH --> WHC[Wavelength Hopping Codes]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q:** Define WDM. List **two advantages** and **one disadvantage** of WDM compared to TDM.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM) is the technique of transmitting multiple optical carrier signals simultaneously over a single optical fiber by using different wavelengths (frequencies) of light, in accordance with the ITU-T G.694.1 frequency grid.

**Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Huge Bandwidth:** A single EDFA can amplify all channels at once, enabling Tbps-scale aggregate rates without proportional cost increase.
2. **Protocol Transparency:** Each wavelength can carry any data format (Ethernet, OTN, SDH), giving operators great flexibility.

**Disadvantage (0.5 mark):** WDM requires **precise wavelength control** (especially DWDM), **optical add-drop multiplexers (OADMs)**, and dispersion management — making it more expensive and complex than simple TDM for low-capacity links.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`
**Q:** What is an Optical Orthogonal Code (OOC)? Why is it important in OCDMA?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** An OOC is a family of binary sequences of length **F**, weight **w**, and bounded auto/cross-correlation (λₐ, λ_c), used as signature codes for each user in an incoherent OCDMA system.

**Importance (2 marks):**
1. **Low Cross-Correlation (1 mark):** OOC ensures that interference between users (Multiple Access Interference, MAI) remains bounded even when many users transmit simultaneously on the same wavelength.
2. **Sharp Autocorrelation Peak (1 mark):** The in-phase autocorrelation equals `w` while off-phase is bounded by `λₐ ≤ 1`. This sharp peak at the matched decoder enables reliable bit detection.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**) with **internal choice** between Question A and Question B.

---
