### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q:** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **DWDM system**. List the functions of the **multiplexer, demultiplexer, and EDFA** in the link.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The DWDM system consists of `N` optical transmitters (each at a distinct wavelength λ₁, λ₂, …, λ_N) feeding an **Optical Multiplexer (OMUX)**. The OMUX combines the wavelengths onto a single SMF. The fiber is broken into spans, each followed by an **EDFA** for loss compensation. At the receiver end, an **Optical Demultiplexer (ODEMUX)** separates the wavelengths back into `N` individual outputs, each detected by a dedicated photodetector.

**Multiplexer Function (1.5 marks):**
The OMUX is a wavelength-selective combiner realized using **Arrayed Waveguide Gratings (AWG)** or **Thin-Film Filters (TFF)**. It accepts multiple wavelength inputs and routes them onto a single output fiber with insertion loss typically **3–5 dB**.

**Demultiplexer Function (1 mark):**
The ODEMUX performs the reverse operation — splitting the composite signal into its constituent wavelengths. AWG-based devices provide high channel isolation (>30 dB) and low insertion loss.

**EDFA Function (1.5 marks):**
The Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier is a **broadband optical amplifier** operating in the **C-band (1525–1565 nm)**. Pumped by a 980 nm or 1480 nm laser, it provides gain of **20–30 dB** simultaneously to all DWDM channels, eliminating the need for electrical regeneration every few kilometers.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q:** A DWDM system uses **48 channels** spaced at **100 GHz** on the ITU-T grid, each carrying **200 Gbps** DP-16QAM data. Calculate:
1. The **total optical bandwidth** occupied.
2. The **aggregate bit rate**.
3. The **spectral efficiency** in bps/Hz.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Optical Bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f_{\text{total}} &= N \times \Delta f_{\text{channel}} \\[6pt]
                       &= 48 \times 100~\text{GHz} \\[6pt]
                       &= 4800~\text{GHz} = 4.8~\text{THz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate Bit Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{agg}} &= N \times B_{\text{ch}} \\[6pt]
              &= 48 \times 200~\text{Gbps} \\[6pt]
              &= 9600~\text{Gbps} = 9.6~\text{Tbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Spectral Efficiency (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{B_{\text{agg}}}{\Delta f_{\text{total}}} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{9600~\text{Gbps}}{4800~\text{GHz}} \\[6pt]
    &= 2~\text{bps/Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note (0 marks, comment for examiner):** DP-16QAM theoretically supports **8 bps/Hz** (4 bits/symbol × 2 polarizations), but the **roll-off factor** of the Nyquist pulse shaping (typically 0.1–0.2) and the **guard band** reduce this to ~2 bps/Hz per channel allocation. This is why the computed η = 2 bps/Hz is realistic.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q:** Explain the principle of **Optical CDMA (OCDMA)** with a block diagram of an **incoherent time-spreading OCDMA system**. Define **Optical Orthogonal Codes (OOC)** and state the cardinality bound.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** OCDMA is a spread-spectrum multiple-access technique in which each user's low-rate data bit is multiplied (in the time domain) by a unique high-rate binary code sequence (the user's signature). All users transmit simultaneously on the same wavelength. The receiver correlates the incoming composite signal with the desired user's code; only the matched user produces a sharp autocorrelation peak that exceeds a threshold, enabling detection.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.3**. The system consists of `K` users, each with an **encoder** (typically an optical tapped-delay line or AWG-based encoder) that imprints the OOC pattern onto each data bit. The encoded streams are combined in a **passive star coupler** and broadcast over a shared fiber. Each receiver contains a **decoder** matched to one user's code, followed by a photodetector and threshold detector.

**OOC Definition (1.5 marks):** An Optical Orthogonal Code is a family of binary sequences characterized by `(F, w, λₐ, λ_c)`. The conditions are:

- $\Phi_{aa}(\tau) \le \lambda_a$ for $\tau \ne 0$
- $\Phi_{ab}(\tau) \le \lambda_c$ for all $\tau$ and $a \ne b$

**Cardinality Bound (1.5 marks):** For λₐ = λ_c = 1:

$$|C| \le \left\lfloor \frac{F - 1}{w(w - 1)} \right\rfloor$$

This places a hard limit on the number of simultaneous users; for example, F = 31 and w = 3 yields a maximum of **5 users**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q:** A **coherent OCDMA system** uses spectral phase encoding with **M = 8 spectral bins** and **4 users**, each assigned a distinct Hadamard code of length 8. The input pulse has peak power **P₀ = 1 mW** and pulse width **τ = 1 ps**.
1. What is the **peak power of the autocorrelation peak** at the matched decoder?
2. Compute the **cross-correlation interference level** for an unmatched user (in terms of P₀).
3. Why is **coherent OCDMA** preferred over incoherent OCDMA for high-user-count systems?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Autocorrelation Peak (2 marks):**
When the receiver's phase mask exactly matches the transmitter's, all M bins add **in phase**, producing constructive interference. The peak power scales as the **square of the field** summed coherently:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{peak}} &= M^2 \times P_0 / M = M \cdot P_0 \\[6pt]
                &= 8 \times 1~\text{mW} = 8~\text{mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Equivalently, the **field amplitude** of each bin adds coherently as √(P₀/M)·M = √(M P₀); squaring gives M·P₀.)

**Step 2 — Cross-Correlation Level (2 marks):**
For an **unmatched user**, the phase mask is uncorrelated with the transmitted one, so the spectral components add **incoherently on average** (random phases). The expected power is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{cross}} &\approx \frac{1}{M} \cdot M \cdot \frac{P_0}{M} = \frac{P_0}{M} \\[6pt]
                  &= \frac{1~\text{mW}}{8} = 0.125~\text{mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **processing gain** (ratio of peak to cross-correlation):

$$
G_p = \frac{P_{\text{peak}}}{P_{\text{cross}}} = M = 8 \quad (\approx 9~\text{dB})
$$

**Step 3 — Why Coherent OCDMA Is Preferred (3 marks):**
1. **Higher Cardinality:** Coherent encoding using Hadamard or bipolar codes supports up to **M users** for M spectral bins, vs. the Johnson-bound-limited incoherent case.
2. **Better MAI Suppression:** Coherent processing provides true orthogonal codes, eliminating the **Multiple Access Interference (MAI)** that limits incoherent OCDMA.
3. **Higher Security:** The phase pattern is harder to detect and decode without the matched mask, making coherent OCDMA attractive for **secure military and avionics** networks.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE MARKS in WDM / OCDMA questions:**
>
> 1. **Off-by-one channel count:** Forgetting the **+1** in `N = (Δλ span / Δλ) + 1`. Always count both endpoints.
> 2. **Mixing F and w in OOC bound:** Some students write `|C| ≤ (F-1)/w²`. The correct denominator is `w(w-1)`, not `w²`.
> 3. **Confusing autocorrelations:** Off-phase autocorrelation `Φ_aa(τ ≠ 0) ≤ λ_a` ≠ in-phase `Φ_aa(0) = w`. Markers award **only 0.5 marks** for stating "autocorrelation = w" without specifying phase.
> 4. **DWDM vs CWDM grid:** Stating CWDM spacing as **0.8 nm** (a DWDM value). CWDM is **20 nm** per the ITU-T G.694.2 grid.
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Always specify **GHz vs THz**, **nm vs μm**, **dB vs linear** — failing to convert loses 0.5 marks easily.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram:** KTU awards **2–3 marks** just for a clean block diagram in Part B (a). Even a hand-drawn sketch with labeled blocks (Tx → MUX → Fiber → EDFA → DEMUX → Rx) gets full marks if neat.
> 7. **Forgetting to take the floor:** Cardinality must be an **integer**; always apply ⌊·⌋.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ **WDM** = multiple λ's on one fiber; **CWDM** = 20 nm spacing, **DWDM** = 0.8 nm or finer (ITU-T G.694.1).
- ✅ **WDM System Blocks:** `Tx_i → OMUX → SMF (+ EDFAs) → ODEMUX → Rx_i`.
- ✅ **EDFAs** amplify the entire **C-band (1525–1565 nm)** in one shot — this is WDM's main economic advantage.
- ✅ **OCDMA** = spread spectrum over optical domain; each user has a unique **signature code**.
- ✅ **Coherent OCDMA** uses **spectral phase encoding** (Hadamard / bipolar codes); **Incoherent OCDMA** uses **time-spreading** (OOC, prime codes).
- ✅ **OOC parameters:** `(F, w, λₐ, λ_c)`. Bounds: λₐ ≤ 1, λ_c ≤ 1.
- ✅ **Cardinality bound:** `|C| ≤ ⌊(F − 1) / (w(w − 1))⌋` (for λₐ = λ_c = 1).
- ✅ **Autocorrelation peak** = `w` (in-phase); off-phase ≤ `λₐ`.
- ✅ **Aggregate rate** `= N × B_ch`; **spectral efficiency** `= B_agg / Δf_total`.
- ✅ **ITU-T G.694.1** = DWDM grid (frequency-based, typically 12.5 GHz / 25 GHz / 50 GHz / 100 GHz spacing).
- ✅ **ITU-T G.694.2** = CWDM grid (wavelength-based, 20 nm spacing from 1271 to 1611 nm).
- ✅ **AWG** = Arrayed Waveguide Grating — the workhorse MUX/DEMUX for high-channel-count DWDM.
- ✅ **EDFA gain window** = 1525–1565 nm; for L-band use a different fiber amplifier.
- ✅ **Dispersion limit** decreases as **B²** — higher bit rates need dispersion-compensated links.
- ✅ **Spectral phase encoding peak power** = `M · P₀`; cross-talk ≈ `P₀/M`; **processing gain = M**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components - Couplers, Splitters, Add/Drop Multiplexers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components - Couplers, Splitters, Add/Drop Multiplexers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Optical Couplers

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
An **optical coupler** is a passive multi-port photonic device that splits an incoming optical signal into two or more outputs, or conversely, combines two or more input signals into a single output, while maintaining specified power ratios and phase relationships between the ports. A directional coupler has four ports (two inputs and two outputs) and exhibits wavelength- and direction-dependent power transfer governed by evanescent field coupling between adjacent waveguides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coupler vs. Splitter vs. Combiner (KTU Board Favourite Distinction)**
> - A **Coupler** is bidirectional — it can split **and** combine signals, often with a defined *coupling ratio* (not necessarily 50:50).
> - A **Splitter** is unidirectional in usage — it divides one input into *N* equal parts (1×N passive power splitter).
> - A **Combiner** is the reverse operation of a splitter (N×1 passive power combiner). A single 2×2 coupler can act as both.
> - **Add/Drop Multiplexer (ADM/OADM)** is a *wavelength-selective* coupler that routes specific wavelengths in/out of a WDM line.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a **railway junction**. A single train (optical signal) enters the junction; based on how the switches are set, it either continues on the main line ("through" port) or is routed onto a side track ("coupled/drop" port). The percentage of trains sent to the side track is the **coupling ratio**. An **Add/Drop Multiplexer** is a *smart* railway junction that switches trains based on their painted colour (wavelength) — only red-painted trains are diverted, blue trains pass through unchanged.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants and Metrics used in Coupler Design**
> - Operating wavelength windows: **1310 nm** (O-band) and **1550 nm** (C-band)
> - Typical fiber core diameter: **8–10 µm** (single-mode), **50/62.5 µm** (multimode)
> - Refractive index contrast: **Δ ≈ 0.003 to 0.01** (standard SMF-28 fiber)
> - Standard coupling ratios fabricated: **50:50, 90:10, 80:20, 95:5, 99:1**

---

### 1.2 Optical Splitters

**Formal Definition:**
An **optical power splitter** is a passive device with one input port and *N* output ports that distributes the input optical power approximately equally (or per a prescribed ratio) among all outputs. The most common configurations are **1×2, 1×4, 1×8, 1×16, 1×32, and 1×64**. Splitters are characterized by *splitting ratio*, *insertion loss*, and *uniformity*.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Picture a **water tap fitted with a manifold** — one incoming pipe (the input fiber) feeds *N* outlet taps. Whether you open one tap or all taps, the *total* water flow you receive is less than what entered, because energy is divided. The further you split (more taps), the weaker the flow at each tap — this is the **splitting loss** of *−10 log₁₀(N) dB**.

---

### 1.3 Add/Drop Multiplexers (ADM / OADM / ROADM)

**Formal Definition:**
An **Optical Add-Drop Multiplexer (OADM)** is a wavelength-selective passive (or hybrid) device that *removes* (drops) one or more specified wavelength channels from a WDM composite signal and simultaneously *inserts* (adds) new channels at the same wavelengths, allowing other wavelengths to pass through transparently. When the add/drop wavelengths can be selected dynamically via software control, the device is termed a **Reconfigurable OADM (ROADM)**.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a **post office sorting facility**. A truck arrives carrying mixed mail of all colours (wavelengths). The sorting machine separates all *red* envelopes (drop λ₁) and inserts freshly printed red envelopes (add λ₁), while letting blue, green, and yellow envelopes (other λ) ride straight through to the next town.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Two Principal OADM Realizations**
> 1. **Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) based OADM** — uses a circulator + Bragg grating to back-reflect the dropped wavelength.
> 2. **Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI) based / Thin-Film Filter (TFF) based OADM** — uses interference or dielectric thin-film stacks for wavelength selection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of a 2×2 Directional Coupler

A 2×2 fused-fiber or integrated-optic directional coupler consists of two single-mode waveguides brought close enough that the **evanescent field** of one waveguide couples into the other over an interaction length *L*. The coupled-mode equations yield the following behaviour:

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Light injected at Port 1** propagates in waveguide A as a guided mode with power *P₁*.
2. Over the interaction region, **evanescent field overlap** causes a periodic power exchange between waveguides A and B.
3. The **coupling coefficient** *κ* depends on waveguide separation *d*, refractive index difference *Δn*, wavelength *λ*, and interaction length *L*.
4. After length *L*, power at the **through port (Port 3)** is $P_3 = P_1 \cos^2(\kappa L)$ and power at the **coupled port (Port 4)** is $P_4 = P_1 \sin^2(\kappa L)$.
5. The condition $\kappa L = \pi/4$ yields a **3 dB (50:50) coupler**.
6. The sum $P_3 + P_4 = P_1$ (lossless ideal coupler), confirming conservation of energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why wavelength dependence?** The coupling coefficient *κ* is inversely proportional to wavelength in fused-fiber couplers. This property is exploited to build **wavelength-selective couplers (WDM couplers)** that route 1310 nm and 1550 nm to different output ports — the basis of coarse WDM (CWDM) access networks.

---

### 2.2 Types of Couplers (KTU Classification)

| S.No | Coupler Type | Fabrication Principle | Typical Application |
|------|-------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Fused Fiber Coupler (FBT)** | Two fibers twisted, heated, and stretched to fuse cores | Power tapping, WDM |
| 2 | **Integrated-Optic / Planar Waveguide Coupler** | Photolithography on LiNbO₃, silica-on-silicon, InP | High-density WDM, ROADM |
| 3 | **Micro-Optic Coupler** | Lensed GRIN optics or bulk beam-splitter cubes | Laboratory, test equipment |
| 4 | **Star Coupler** | Tree or planar topology of 2×2 couplers | LAN distribution, broadcast |
| 5 | **Pump Coupler / WDM Coupler** | Wavelength-selective fused coupler | EDFA pump combining (980/1550 nm) |

---

### 2.3 Types of Splitters

| S.No | Splitter Type | Manufacturing Method | Typical Loss Range |
|------|--------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Fused Biconical Taper (FBT) Splitter** | Heat-and-pull on bundled fibers | 3.5–17 dB for 1×2 to 1×64 |
| 2 | **Planar Lightwave Circuit (PLC) Splitter** | Silica waveguides on Si substrate | 3.5–18.5 dB for 1×2 to 1×64 |
| 3 | **Fiber-Optic Tap** | Single fused coupler | 0.5–20 dB tap ratio |

---

### 2.4 Operating Principle of an FBG-Based OADM

**Operational Logic:**

1. The **WDM composite signal** (λ₁ + λ₂ + ... + λₙ) enters **Port 1 of a 3-port optical circulator**.
2. Light exits Port 2 and strikes the **Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG)** through an optical circulator.
3. The FBG reflects only the wavelength that satisfies the Bragg condition $\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$, where $\Lambda$ is the grating period and $n_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective index.
4. The **reflected (dropped) wavelength** re-enters the circulator at Port 2 and is routed out via **Port 3** (the Drop port).
5. **Other wavelengths** pass through the FBG unaffected and exit via the **Through port**.
6. A new channel at the same wavelength λ_B is injected at the **Add port**, travels in reverse through the circulator, and joins the through-going wavelengths at the output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The circulator is non-reciprocal** — it uses Faraday rotation in YIG or similar magneto-optic materials. A **reciprocal** alternative is an **isolator + 3 dB coupler** arrangement, but this has 3 dB intrinsic penalty.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of the literal pipe `|` character in all formulas below to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|----------------------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | Coupled port power | $P_4 = P_1 \sin^2(\kappa L)$ | Watts; ratio is dimensionless |
| 2 | Through port power | $P_3 = P_1 \cos^2(\kappa L)$ | Watts |
| 3 | Coupling ratio | $CR = \dfrac{P_{\text{coupled}}}{P_{\text{total}}} = \sin^2(\kappa L)$ | 0 to 1 (or expressed in %) |
| 4 | Splitting ratio in dB | $CR_{dB} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{coupled}}}{P_{\text{total}}}\right)$ | dB |
| 5 | Splitting loss (ideal *N*-way) | $L_{\text{split}} = 10 \log_{10}(N)$ | dB; $N$ = number of outputs |
| 6 | Insertion Loss (IL) | $IL = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | dB; per output port |
| 7 | Excess Loss (EL) | $EL = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sum P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | dB; total device loss |
| 8 | Return Loss (RL) | $RL = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{reflected}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | dB; should be high (negative dB → large positive value) |
| 9 | Directivity (D) | $D = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{unwanted coupled}}}{P_{\text{input coupled}}}\right)$ | dB; isolation at unused port |
| 10 | Cross-talk (CT) | $CT = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{wrong port}}}{P_{\text{right port}}}\right)$ | dB; in WDM/MUX devices |
| 11 | Bragg wavelength (FBG) | $\lambda_B = 2\, n_{\text{eff}}\, \Lambda$ | nm; $\Lambda$ = grating period |
| 12 | FBG wavelength selectivity (approx.) | $\Delta\lambda \approx \lambda_B \left(\dfrac{\Lambda}{L_g} + \dfrac{\delta n}{n_{\text{eff}}}\right)$ | nm; depends on grating length $L_g$ and index modulation depth $\delta n$ |
| 13 | Power at each port of 1×N splitter (ideal) | $P_{\text{port}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{N}$ | Watts |
| 14 | Uniformity of splitter | $U = IL_{\max} - IL_{\min}$ | dB; should be low |
| 15 | Splitting loss table | 1×2 → **3.0 dB**, 1×4 → **6.0 dB**, 1×8 → **9.0 dB**, 1×16 → **12.0 dB**, 1×32 → **15.0 dB**, 1×64 → **18.0 dB** | dB; ideal minimum |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Component Used | Why It Matters |
|------------------|----------------|----------------|
| **FTTH / PON networks** (GPON, XGS-PON) | 1×32 or 1×64 PLC splitter | Single OLT feeds 32–64 ONUs passively — eliminates active electronics in the field. |
| **Long-haul DWDM trunk lines** | ROADM + WDM couplers | Reconfigurable add/drop without O-E-O conversion → transparency, lower latency, lower cost. |
| **EDFA pump modules** | 980/1550 nm WDM coupler | Combines pump laser output with signal band with < 0.5 dB insertion loss. |
| **CATV / Broadcast networks** | 1×N star coupler | Distributes the same video feed to thousands of subscribers. |
| **Optical coherence tomography / Sensors** | 90:10 or 99:1 fused coupler | Taps a small reference beam for interferometric sensing. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Coupled-Mode Equations for a Directional Coupler

The 2×2 directional coupler is governed by the **coupled-mode theory** for two parallel, synchronously-coupled single-mode waveguides. We derive the field amplitudes in each waveguide as a function of propagation distance *z*.

**Starting Coupled-Mode Equations:**

$$
\frac{dA_1(z)}{dz} = i\,\beta_1 A_1(z) + i\,\kappa_{12}\, A_2(z)
$$

$$
\frac{dA_2(z)}{dz} = i\,\beta_2 A_2(z) + i\,\kappa_{21}\, A_1(z)
$$

where:

- $A_1(z), A_2(z)$ = complex mode amplitudes in waveguide 1 and 2 at position *z*.
- $\beta_1, \beta_2$ = propagation constants of the uncoupled modes.
- $\kappa_{12}, \kappa_{21}$ = coupling coefficients; for a lossless reciprocal coupler $\kappa_{12} = \kappa_{21} = \kappa$ (a real number).

**Step 1 — Apply the symmetric assumption $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta$ (synchronous coupling):**

$$
\frac{dA_1}{dz} = i\,\beta\, A_1 + i\,\kappa\, A_2
$$

$$
\frac{dA_2}{dz} = i\,\beta\, A_2 + i\,\kappa\, A_1
$$

**Step 2 — Transform to remove the common phase factor. Substitute $A_k(z) = a_k(z) e^{i\beta z}$:**

$$
\frac{da_1}{dz} = i\,\kappa\, a_2
$$

$$
\frac{da_2}{dz} = i\,\kappa\, a_1
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the first equation again and substitute the second:**

$$
\frac{d^2 a_1}{dz^2} = i\,\kappa\, \frac{da_2}{dz} = i\,\kappa\,(i\,\kappa\, a_1) = -\kappa^2 a_1
$$

$$
\therefore\;\frac{d^2 a_1}{dz^2} + \kappa^2 a_1 = 0
$$

This is a second-order linear ODE with solution:

$$
a_1(z) = C_1 \cos(\kappa z) + C_2 \sin(\kappa z)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions for a 2×2 coupler (only Port 1 excited):**

- $A_1(0) = A_0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_1(0) = A_0$
- $A_2(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_2(0) = 0$

From the first BC: $C_1 = A_0$. From the first-order ODE at $z=0$:

$$
\left.\frac{da_1}{dz}\right|_{z=0} = i\,\kappa\, a_2(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 \kappa = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Therefore the amplitude in waveguide 1 is:**

$$
a_1(z) = A_0 \cos(\kappa z)
$$

**Step 6 — Obtain $a_2(z)$ from the first-order equation:**

$$
a_2(z) = \frac{1}{i\kappa}\frac{da_1}{dz} = \frac{1}{i\kappa}\bigl(-A_0\kappa \sin(\kappa z)\bigr) = i A_0 \sin(\kappa z)
$$

**Step 7 — Recover the full fields by multiplying back the phase factor:**

$$
A_1(z) = A_0 \cos(\kappa z)\, e^{i\beta z}
$$

$$
A_2(z) = i A_0 \sin(\kappa z)\, e^{i\beta z}
$$

The factor *i* in $A_2$ indicates a **π/2 phase shift** between through and coupled ports — a critical signature for diagnostic identification of a fused coupler.

**Step 8 — Compute output powers at *z = L* (the end of coupling region):**

$$
P_3 = \vert A_1(L)\vert^2 = P_{\text{in}} \cos^2(\kappa L)
$$

$$
P_4 = \vert A_2(L)\vert^2 = P_{\text{in}} \sin^2(\kappa L)
$$

**Step 9 — Sum to confirm lossless operation:**

$$
P_3 + P_4 = P_{\text{in}}\bigl[\cos^2(\kappa L) + \sin^2(\kappa L)\bigr] = P_{\text{in}}
$$

**Step 10 — Coupling ratio derivation for a 3 dB coupler (50:50):**

Set $\kappa L = \pi/4$, giving $\cos^2(\pi/4) = \sin^2(\pi/4) = 1/2$.

$$
CR = \frac{P_4}{P_3 + P_4} = \frac{1/2}{1/2 + 1/2} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%
$$

In decibels: $CR_{dB} = -10 \log_{10}(0.5) = 3.0\;\text{dB}$ per port.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Loss Budget for a 1×8 PLC Splitter in FTTH

**Given:**
- Input optical power $P_{\text{in}} = +5\;\text{dBm}$ (laser launch power).
- A 1×8 PLC splitter with **per-port insertion loss of 10.5 dB** (1.5 dB excess loss + 9 dB splitting loss).
- Connector loss at input: **0.3 dB**, at each output: **0.2 dB**.
- Fiber span to ONU: **5 km** at **0.25 dB/km @ 1490 nm**.

**Step 1 — Compute total loss budget from OLT port to a single ONU:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &= L_{\text{connector,in}} + L_{\text{splitter}} + L_{\text{connector,out}} + L_{\text{fiber}} \\
&= 0.3 + 10.5 + 0.2 + (5 \times 0.25) \\
&= 0.3 + 10.5 + 0.2 + 1.25 \\
&= 12.25\;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute received power at ONU:**

$$
P_{\text{ONU}} = P_{\text{in}} - L_{\text{total}} = 5 - 12.25 = -7.25\;\text{dBm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with GPON ONU sensitivity class B+ (−27 dBm):**

Margin $= -7.25 - (-27) = 19.75\;\text{dB}$. **Adequate headroom.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: FBG Design for Dropping λ = 1550.0 nm

**Given:** $n_{\text{eff}} = 1.468$ for SMF-28 at 1550 nm. We want to drop 1550 nm.

**Step 1 — Compute grating period from the Bragg condition:**

$$
\Lambda = \frac{\lambda_B}{2\,n_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1550.0\;\text{nm}}{2 \times 1.468} = \frac{1550.0}{2.936} = 528.0\;\text{nm}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify that 1551 nm is NOT reflected (channel spacing 100 GHz ≈ 0.8 nm):**

$$
\Lambda = 528.0\;\text{nm} \Rightarrow \lambda_B = 2 \times 1.468 \times 528.0 = 1550.0\;\text{nm} \;\;✓
$$

For a grating with index modulation $\delta n = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ and length $L_g = 10\;\text{mm}$:

$$
\Delta\lambda \approx \lambda_B \left(\frac{\Lambda}{L_g} + \frac{\delta n}{n_{\text{eff}}}\right) \approx 1550 \times \left(\frac{5.28 \times 10^{-4}}{10} + \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.468}\right) \approx 1550 \times (5.28 \times 10^{-5} + 3.4 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 0.61\;\text{nm}
$$

This selectivity of **0.61 nm** fits inside a 100 GHz DWDM channel grid (≈0.8 nm) — **adequate for add/drop**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Coupler & Splitter Performance

| Metric | 2×2 Fused Coupler | 2×2 Planar Waveguide Coupler | 1×8 PLC Splitter | 1×8 FBT Splitter |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Insertion loss (typical) | 0.05–0.2 dB | 0.5–1.0 dB | 10.0–10.7 dB | 10.5–11.5 dB |
| Excess loss | < 0.1 dB | 0.5–1.0 dB | 1.0–1.5 dB | 1.5–2.5 dB |
| Return loss | > 65 dB | > 50 dB | > 55 dB | > 45 dB |
| Directivity | > 65 dB | > 50 dB | N/A | N/A |
| Wavelength dependence | Moderate | Low | Low | High |
| Cost (relative) | Low | High | Low | Very Low |
| Best application | Pump combiner, tapping | Dense WDM, ROADM | FTTH/PON | Low-budget FTTH |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: 2×2 Fused Fiber Directional Coupler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Port 1 Input] -->|P1 = Pin| REGION((Coupling Region Length L))
    REGION -->|P3 = Pin cos^2 kL| P3[Port 3 Through]
    REGION -->|P4 = Pin sin^2 kL| P4[Port 4 Coupled]
    P2[Port 2 Isolated]:::iso
    REGION -.->|Negligible| P2

    classDef iso fill:#ffd5d5,stroke:#aa0000,stroke-dasharray: 4 4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Light launched into Port 1 emerges at Ports 3 (through) and 4 (coupled). Port 2 is ideally isolated; power reaching it is quantified by the *directivity* of the device.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Tree-of-Couplers Forming a 1×8 Splitter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Input Fiber Pin] --> S1((2x2 Coupler A))
    S1 -->|50 percent| B1((2x2 Coupler B))
    S1 -->|50 percent| B2((2x2 Coupler C))
    B1 -->|50 percent| O1((2x2 Coupler D))
    B1 -->|50 percent| O2((2x2 Coupler E))
    B2 -->|50 percent| O3((2x2 Coupler F))
    B2 -->|50 percent| O4((2x2 Coupler G))
    O1 --> OUT1[Port 1 Pout = Pin / 8]
    O2 --> OUT2[Port 2 Pout = Pin / 8]
    O3 --> OUT3[Port 3 Pout = Pin / 8]
    O4 --> OUT4[Port 4 Pout = Pin / 8]

    classDef splitter fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75
    class S1,B1,B2,O1,O2,O3,O4 splitter
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Three cascaded stages of 2×2 couplers ($\log_2 8 = 3$ stages) split the input into 8 equal outputs. The ideal splitting loss is $3 \times 3.01 = 9.03\;\text{dB}$.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: FBG-Based OADM with Optical Circulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WDM_IN[Composite WDM Input\nlambda1 lambda2 lambda3] -->|Port 1| CIRC[3-Port Optical Circulator]
    CIRC -->|Port 2 to FBG| FBG[Fiber Bragg Grating\nlambdaB = 2 neff Lambda]
    FBG -->|Reflected lambdaB| CIRC
    FBG -->|Pass-through lambda2 lambda3| THRU[Through Port]
    CIRC -->|Port 3 Output| DROP[Drop Port: lambdaB]
    ADD_IN[Add Port: lambdaB new] --> CIRC
    CIRC -->|Port 2 to line| ADD_OUT[Combined Output: lambda2 lambda3 new lambdaB]

    classDef act fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1a7a1a
    class CIRC,FBG act
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The circulator routes incoming WDM traffic to the FBG; the FBG back-reflects λ_B to the circulator which diverts it to the Drop port. A new λ_B injected at the Add port is routed to the output, completing the add/drop cycle.

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Reconfigurable OADM (ROADM) Block Diagram

```macket
flowchart TB
    IN[Trunk WDM In] --> DEMUX[Demultiplexer\nWavelength Selective Switch]
    DEMUX -->|lambda1| SW1[2x2 WSS lambda1]
    DEMUX -->|lambda2| SW2[2x2 WSS lambda2]
    DEMUX -->|lambda3| SW3[2x2 WSS lambda3]
    SW1 -->|Through OR Drop| MUX[Mux / Combiner]
    SW2 -->|Through OR Drop| MUX
    SW3 -->|Through OR Drop| MUX
    MUX --> OUT[Trunk WDM Out]
    SW1 -.->|Drop| TX1[Local Transponder lambda1]
    SW1 -.->|Add| RX1[Local Receiver lambda1]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A **Wavelength Selective Switch (WSS)** per channel allows any wavelength to be either passed through, locally dropped, or replaced with an added channel. Software control enables remote reconfiguration — hence *Reconfigurable* OADM.

---
