## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define an optical coupler. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a 2×2 fused fiber directional coupler. State the expressions for through and coupled port powers.*

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

An **optical coupler** is a passive device that distributes or combines optical power among multiple fibers/waveguides while preserving wavelength and phase information.

A 2×2 fused fiber coupler is fabricated by twisting two single-mode fibers, heating them with a flame/electric arc, and stretching the molten region so the cores come into close proximity. The **evanescent field** of the mode in fiber A couples into fiber B over the coupling length *L*.

If $P_1$ is the input power at Port 1 and $\kappa$ is the coupling coefficient, the powers at the output ports are:

$$P_3 = P_1 \cos^2(\kappa L) \quad \text{(Through)}$$
$$P_4 = P_1 \sin^2(\kappa L) \quad \text{(Coupled)}$$

When $\kappa L = \pi/4$, the device acts as a **3 dB (50:50) coupler**. The output at Port 4 carries a π/2 phase lead relative to Port 3, a property used in coherent receivers and Mach-Zehnder interferometers.

**(Self-assessment: 3/3 marks if expressions + diagram + 3 dB condition are all stated.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is an optical add-drop multiplexer (OADM)? Briefly explain the working of an FBG-based OADM with a block diagram.*

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

An **OADM** is a wavelength-selective device that drops one or more specified wavelengths from a WDM line while letting others pass through, and simultaneously inserts (adds) new channels at the same wavelengths.

In an **FBG-based OADM**, the WDM composite signal enters Port 1 of a three-port **optical circulator** and exits from Port 2 into a **Fiber Bragg Grating** whose Bragg wavelength is $\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$.

The FBG **back-reflects** only the wavelength $\lambda_B$ (the channel to be dropped); this reflected signal re-enters the circulator and is routed to Port 3 (Drop port). Other wavelengths pass through the FBG to the *Through* port. A new channel at $\lambda_B$ is injected at the *Add* port, traverses the circulator in the reverse direction, and emerges on the line combined with the surviving wavelengths.

**(Self-assessment: 3/3 marks if circulator + Bragg condition + add/drop operation are explained.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### ✦ Question A — 14 Marks [Internal Choice Option 1]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of coupled-mode theory, derive expressions for the through-port and coupled-port powers of a 2×2 directional coupler. State the condition for a 3 dB coupler. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define and explain the following performance parameters of an optical coupler, with their typical dB values: (i) Excess loss, (ii) Insertion loss, (iii) Return loss, (iv) Directivity, (v) Cross-talk. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the **coupled-mode equations** for two synchronous single-mode waveguides with coupling coefficient $\kappa$ and propagation constant $\beta$:

$$
\frac{dA_1}{dz} = i\beta A_1 + i\kappa A_2 \qquad\qquad \frac{dA_2}{dz} = i\beta A_2 + i\kappa A_1
$$

Applying the substitution $A_k(z) = a_k(z) e^{i\beta z}$ decouples the phase evolution:

$$
\frac{da_1}{dz} = i\kappa\, a_2 \qquad\qquad \frac{da_2}{dz} = i\kappa\, a_1
$$

Differentiating once more:

$$
\frac{d^2 a_1}{dz^2} = i\kappa\frac{da_2}{dz} = i\kappa(i\kappa a_1) = -\kappa^2 a_1
$$

General solution:

$$
a_1(z) = C_1\cos(\kappa z) + C_2 \sin(\kappa z)
$$

With boundary conditions $a_1(0) = A_0$ (only Port 1 excited) and $a_2(0) = 0$ (Port 2 isolated at input):

- $C_1 = A_0$
- $\dfrac{da_1}{dz}\bigg|_{z=0} = i\kappa a_2(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$

Therefore:

$$
a_1(z) = A_0\cos(\kappa z), \qquad a_2(z) = iA_0\sin(\kappa z)
$$

Restoring the common phase factor:

$$
A_1(z) = A_0\cos(\kappa z)e^{i\beta z}, \qquad A_2(z) = iA_0\sin(\kappa z)e^{i\beta z}
$$

At the device output $z = L$:

$$
P_3 = \vert A_1(L)\vert^2 = P_{\text{in}}\cos^2(\kappa L) \quad \text{(Through)}
$$

$$
P_4 = \vert A_2(L)\vert^2 = P_{\text{in}}\sin^2(\kappa L) \quad \text{(Coupled)}
$$

**Condition for 3 dB coupler:** Set $\kappa L = \pi/4$, giving $P_3 = P_4 = P_{\text{in}}/2$.

$$\boxed{\;P_3 = P_{\text{in}}\cos^2(\kappa L), \quad P_4 = P_{\text{in}}\sin^2(\kappa L), \quad \text{3 dB when }\kappa L = \pi/4\;}$$

**Marks distribution suggestion (7):** Coupled-mode equations setup (2), integration and BCs (2), final power expressions (2), 3 dB condition (1).

---

**(b) Definitions and typical dB values (7 marks):**

| # | Parameter | Definition | Typical dB Value |
|---|-----------|------------|------------------|
| (i) | **Excess Loss (EL)** | Internal loss beyond ideal splitting; ratio of total output power to input. $EL = -10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sum P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | < 0.1 dB (premium FBT); 0.5–1.0 dB (PLC) |
| (ii) | **Insertion Loss (IL)** | Loss introduced into a specific output path. $IL = -10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | For 1×8 splitter: 9–11 dB per port |
| (iii) | **Return Loss (RL)** | Measure of power reflected back to source. $RL = -10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | > 50 dB (high quality) |
| (iv) | **Directivity (D)** | Ability to suppress coupling into the *unintended* port. $D = -10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{unwanted}}}{P_{\text{intended}}}\right)$ | > 50 dB |
| (v) | **Cross-talk (CT)** | Unwanted signal leaking from one channel to another (esp. in WDM). $CT = -10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{wrong}}}{P_{\text{right}}}\right)$ | < −30 dB for OADM; < −40 dB for WDM mux |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question A**
> - **Do not confuse Excess Loss with Insertion Loss.** Excess loss is over the *whole* device (sum of all outputs vs. input); insertion loss is per *individual* output port. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this conflation.
> - In part (a), students frequently forget to apply the **synchronous-coupling assumption** $\beta_1 = \beta_2$. State this explicitly to earn full marks.
> - Do not omit the **boundary conditions** $a_1(0)=A_0$, $a_2(0)=0$. Without them the derivation cannot be completed.
> - For (b), **do not write only the formula** — write the formula *and* a one-line definition *and* the typical dB value. Each component typically carries 1.4 marks.

---

### ✦ Question B — 14 Marks [Internal Choice Option 2]

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) based Optical Add-Drop Multiplexer (OADM) with the help of a block diagram. Derive the expression for the Bragg wavelength and comment on the role of the optical circulator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An optical transmitter launches +5 dBm into a 1×16 PLC splitter with an excess loss of 1.5 dB. The splitter is connected to a fiber of length 8 km with attenuation 0.3 dB/km. Each output port has a connector loss of 0.2 dB. Compute: (i) the total insertion loss per output port, (ii) the optical power available at the receiver if the receiver sensitivity is −28 dBm, (iii) the system margin. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) FBG-based OADM working (7 marks):**

The Fiber Bragg Grating is a periodic perturbation of the refractive index in the core of an optical fiber, formed by exposing the fiber to an **intense UV interference pattern** (phase-mask writing technique). The grating period $\Lambda$ is typically ~500 nm for 1550 nm operation.

**Bragg wavelength derivation:**

The Bragg condition arises from constructive interference of back-reflected light from successive grating planes. The path difference between two successive reflections is $2\Lambda \sin\theta$ where $\theta$ is the angle of incidence inside the fiber. For forward-propagating guided modes that back-reflect ($\theta = 90°$), the condition for constructive interference is:

$$2\Lambda\, n_{\text{eff}} = m\lambda_B \quad (m = 1, 2, 3, \ldots)$$

For first-order Bragg reflection ($m = 1$):

$$\boxed{\;\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}}\, \Lambda\;}$$

**Block diagram and signal flow:**

A 3-port optical circulator is a **non-reciprocal** device using Faraday rotation. Light entering Port 1 exits Port 2 only; light entering Port 2 exits Port 3 only; light entering Port 3 exits Port 1 only.

1. WDM composite ($\lambda_1, \lambda_2, ..., \lambda_n$) enters **Circulator Port 1**.
2. Light exits at **Port 2** and strikes the FBG.
3. FBG reflects only $\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$ back into Port 2 of the circulator; this signal exits at **Port 3 = Drop port**.
4. Other wavelengths pass through the FBG to the **Through port**.
5. A new $\lambda_B$ is injected at the **Add port (Port 3)**, re-enters the circulator, and is routed to Port 1, exiting to the trunk line.

**Role of the circulator:** Provides **directional routing** with low loss (~0.5 dB) and high isolation (>40 dB) between ports without requiring active electronics — essential for transparent optical add/drop.

**Marks distribution suggestion (7):** Bragg derivation (2), block diagram (1), step-by-step operation (3), circulator role (1).

---

**(b) Numerical power-budget problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $P_{\text{in}} = +5\;\text{dBm}$
- 1×16 splitter, $L_{\text{excess}} = 1.5\;\text{dB}$
- Fiber length $L = 8\;\text{km}$, attenuation $\alpha = 0.3\;\text{dB/km}$
- Connector loss per port $L_c = 0.2\;\text{dB}$

**Step 1 — Compute ideal splitting loss for 1×16:**

$$
L_{\text{split}} = 10\log_{10}(16) = 12.04\;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total insertion loss per output port:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IL &= L_{\text{split}} + L_{\text{excess}} + L_c \\
&= 12.04 + 1.5 + 0.2 \\
&= 13.74\;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

(If the question asks for IL *including the fiber*, add fiber loss next.)

**Step 3 — Compute fiber loss:**

$$
L_{\text{fiber}} = \alpha \times L = 0.3 \times 8 = 2.4\;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total link loss and received power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &= L_{\text{split}} + L_{\text{excess}} + L_c + L_{\text{fiber}} \\
&= 12.04 + 1.5 + 0.2 + 2.4 \\
&= 16.14\;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
P_{\text{rx}} = P_{\text{in}} - L_{\text{total}} = 5 - 16.14 = -11.14\;\text{dBm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute system margin:**

$$
\text{Margin} = P_{\text{rx}} - P_{\text{sensitivity}} = -11.14 - (-28) = 16.86\;\text{dB}
$$

**Interpretation:** A positive margin of 16.86 dB indicates the link will operate reliably with substantial headroom against component aging, temperature variations, and connector degradation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question B**
> - **Forgetting to add excess loss** to the splitting loss is the most common 1-mark error. Excess loss is *in addition to* $10\log_{10}(N)$.
> - In the Bragg derivation, students often skip showing the constructive-interference logic. Write $\Delta = 2n_{\text{eff}}\Lambda$ explicitly and state $m\lambda_B$ for the *m*-th order Bragg condition.
> - Do **not** state the circulator is "reciprocal" — it is **non-reciprocal** (Faraday rotation). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this misconception.
> - For the numerical, always include the **unit dBm** consistently and end with a one-line interpretation of the margin.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Couplers, Splitters, OADM**

- [ ] A **2×2 fused fiber coupler** obeys $P_3 = P_1 \cos^2(\kappa L)$ and $P_4 = P_1 \sin^2(\kappa L)$; **3 dB** condition is $\kappa L = \pi/4$; coupled port carries $\pi/2$ phase lead.
- [ ] **Coupling coefficient $\kappa$** depends on core separation $d$, $\Delta n$, wavelength $\lambda$, and interaction length $L$. It is *inversely* wavelength-dependent in FBT couplers.
- [ ] **Five canonical performance metrics** — Excess Loss, Insertion Loss, Return Loss, Directivity, Cross-talk. Be ready to write the formula **and** typical dB range for each.
- [ ] A **1×N splitter** has *ideal* splitting loss $= 10\log_{10}(N)$; real devices add *excess loss* (0.5–2.5 dB) and *non-uniformity* (0.5–1.5 dB) on top.
- [ ] **FBT** = cheap, fused fibers; **PLC** = planar silica-on-silicon, lower loss and better uniformity, used in modern FTTH.
- [ ] An **OADM** drops/Adds wavelengths *selectively*; **ROADM** adds software reconfigurability via Wavelength Selective Switches (WSS).
- [ ] **FBG-based OADM** requires an **optical circulator** + **FBG**; Bragg condition is $\lambda_B = 2n_{\text{eff}}\Lambda$ (first order, $m=1$).
- [ ] The **circulator is non-reciprocal** (Faraday rotation in YIG/magneto-optic crystals), as opposed to a *reciprocal* beam-splitter cube.
- [ ] **WDM coupler / pump combiner** is a fused coupler designed to combine 980 nm pump laser with 1550 nm signal in EDFAs.
- [ ] **ROADM block**: Demux → bank of 2×2 WSS per channel → Mux. Software-control of each WSS enables any combination of add/drop/pass-through.
- [ ] **PON reference**: A 1×32 PLC splitter with B+ class ONUs (−27 dBm sensitivity) is the canonical FTTH deployment; typical link budget margin is 15–20 dB.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Month Tip for KTU Exam:**
> Memorize the **3 dB condition**, **Bragg formula**, **splitting-loss formula**, and the **definitions of all five coupler performance parameters**. These four items account for ~70% of marks asked on this topic across the last five KTU university exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fiber Grating Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fiber Grating Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Fiber Grating Filter** is a passive in-fiber photonic device in which the **refractive index of the core** is permanently and periodically modulated along the longitudinal axis of an optical fiber. This periodic index perturbation acts as a **wavelength-selective mirror or filter**, reflecting specific wavelengths (Bragg wavelength) while transmitting others, based on the principle of **distributed Bragg reflection** governed by the phase-matching condition.

There are two principal classes relevant to Module 4:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Class 1 — Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG):** Short-period grating ($\Lambda \approx 0.2$–$0.5 \,\mu\text{m}$) that **couples forward-propagating core mode to backward-propagating core mode**. Acts as a **reflection filter / narrowband mirror**.
>
> **Class 2 — Long Period Grating (LPG) / Transmission Grating:** Long-period grating ($\Lambda \approx 100$–$1000 \,\mu\text{m}$) that couples the core mode to **co-propagating cladding modes**. Acts as a **band-rejection (loss) filter** in transmission.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Wavelength Hurdle Track":**
> Imagine a long athletic track where colored hurdles of a fixed spacing $\Lambda$ are placed. Runners wearing different colored jerseys represent different optical wavelengths. Only the runner whose stride **exactly matches the hurdle spacing** resonates with the obstacles and bounces back (reflected). All other runners simply stride past and continue forward (transmitted). The grating period $\Lambda$ is the "tuned key" that decides which color/wavelength gets reflected.

- The **grating period** $\Lambda$ plays the role of the resonance selector.
- The **index modulation depth** $\Delta n$ plays the role of the hurdle height (controls reflection strength).
- The **grating length** $L$ controls how sharp the wavelength selection is (selectivity).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Core refractive index of standard single-mode fiber: $n_{\text{eff}} \approx \mathbf{1.45}$
- Speed of light in vacuum: $c = \mathbf{3 \times 10^{8} \,\text{m/s}}$
- Typical Bragg wavelength range (C-band): $\lambda_B = \mathbf{1550 \,\text{nm}}$
- Typical index modulation: $\Delta n = \mathbf{10^{-5}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-3}}$
- Grating lengths: $L = \mathbf{1 \,\text{mm}}$ to $\mathbf{10 \,\text{cm}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between **Bragg gratings (reflection-type)** and **Long Period Gratings (transmission-type)**. Examiner questions often test the mode-coupling direction and the resulting spectral response shape.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The operation of a fiber grating rests on **Coupled-Mode Theory (CMT)**. Here is the structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Index Perturbation:** The fiber core is exposed to a spatially periodic UV interference pattern (typically using a phase mask), creating a permanent **refractive index modulation**:
$$\Delta n(z) = \Delta n_{\max} \cos\left(\frac{2\pi z}{\Lambda} + \phi(z)\right)$$
where $\phi(z)$ is the grating chirp function (zero for uniform gratings).

- **Step 2 — Phase-Matching Condition:** The grating provides a periodic boundary that satisfies momentum conservation. For a mode of effective index $n_{\text{eff}}$ propagating in direction $\beta$, the round-trip phase of one grating period must equal $2\pi$ for constructive interference.

- **Step 3 — Mode Coupling (Why it reflects):** When the phase-matching condition is met, **energy is coherently scattered backward** from the forward mode. The cumulative effect over $N = L/\Lambda$ periods produces a near-total reflection (for a strong grating, peak reflectivity $\to 100\%$).

- **Step 4 — Wavelength Selectivity (How it filters):** Only wavelengths satisfying the Bragg resonance condition are reflected with high efficiency. Wavelengths off-resonance pass through unaffected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition:** A uniform grating is analogous to a **Distributed Bragg Reflector (DBR)** mirror in semiconductor lasers — the difference is that it is written directly inside the fiber core, eliminating any interface losses.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols / Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | **First-Order Bragg Condition** | $\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$ | $\lambda_B$ [m], $n_{\text{eff}}$ dimensionless, $\Lambda$ [m] | Core equation for FBG |
| 2 | **LPG Phase-Matching** | $\lambda_i = (n_{\text{core}} - n_{\text{clad}}^{(i)})\Lambda$ | $n_{\text{clad}}^{(i)}$ is i-th cladding mode index | Resonant coupling wavelength |
| 3 | **Coupling Coefficient (DC)** | $\sigma = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \overline{\delta n_{\text{eff}}}$ | $\overline{\delta n_{\text{eff}}}$ average index change [m⁻¹] | DC self-coupling term |
| 4 | **AC Coupling Coefficient** | $\kappa = \frac{\pi}{\lambda} \nu \overline{\delta n_{\text{eff}}}$ | $\nu$ is fringe visibility (0 to 1) | Strength of grating |
| 5 | **Peak Reflectivity** | $R_{\max} = \tanh^2(\kappa L)$ | Dimensionless | Strong grating limit |
| 6 | **Bandwidth (Full Width)** | $\Delta \lambda_{\text{FWHM}} = \lambda_B \sqrt{\left(\frac{\Lambda}{L}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\Delta n}{2 n_{\text{eff}}}\right)^2}$ | [m] | Approximate form |
| 7 | **Group Delay (uniform)** | $\tau_g = \frac{d \theta_r}{d \omega}$ | seconds | Dispersion of reflected pulse |
| 8 | **Chirped Grating Delay** | $\tau(\lambda) = \dfrac{2 n_{\text{eff}} L(\lambda)}{c}$ | seconds | For dispersion compensation |
| 9 | **Apodization Function** | $A(z) = \exp\!\left[-4 \ln 2 \left(\frac{z - L/2}{L}\right)^2\right]$ | dimensionless | Gaussian profile, suppresses sidelobes |
| 10 | **Effective Refractive Index** | $n_{\text{eff}} = \beta / k_0$ | $k_0 = 2\pi / \lambda$ | Mode propagation constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Chirped fiber Bragg gratings are the industry standard for **chromatic dispersion compensation** in 10 Gbps and 40 Gbps long-haul links. They are also used as **WDM mux/demux filters**, **gain flatteners for EDFAs**, **sensors** (temperature/strain), and **pulse shapers** in ultrafast laser systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the First-Order Bragg Condition

We derive the fundamental resonance condition for a fiber Bragg grating by considering the **phase accumulation** of two counter-propagating waves over one grating period.

**Setup:**
- Forward-propagating wave: $E_f(z,t) = A_f e^{j(\omega t - \beta z)}$
- Backward-propagating wave: $E_b(z,t) = A_b e^{j(\omega t + \beta z)}$
- Grating vector: $K = \dfrac{2\pi}{\Lambda}$

**Phase-Matching Requirement:** For efficient coupling, the grating must supply the momentum difference between the two waves:

$$
\beta_f - (-\beta_b) = K
$$

Since both waves travel in the same core mode ($\beta_f = \beta_b = \beta$):

$$
2\beta = \frac{2\pi}{\Lambda}
$$

Substitute $\beta = \dfrac{2\pi n_{\text{eff}}}{\lambda_B}$:

$$
2 \cdot \frac{2\pi n_{\text{eff}}}{\lambda_B} = \frac{2\pi}{\Lambda}
$$

Cancel $2\pi$ from both sides:

$$
\frac{2 n_{\text{eff}}}{\lambda_B} = \frac{1}{\Lambda}
$$

Solving for $\lambda_B$:

$$
\boxed{\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda}
$$

**Logic Explanation:** Each grating period introduces a phase shift of $2\pi$. For the forward wave scattered by the grating to constructively interfere with the backward wave, the optical round-trip path of $2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$ must equal one full wavelength $\lambda_B$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Peak Reflectivity for a Uniform Grating

From **Coupled-Mode Theory**, the reflectivity of a uniform grating of length $L$ is given by solving two coupled differential equations. For a single-mode Bragg grating:

**Let** $\Delta\beta = \beta - \beta_0 = 2\pi n_{\text{eff}}\left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_B}\right)$ (detuning parameter).

The closed-form reflectivity is:

$$
r = \frac{-j \kappa \sin(q L)}{q \cos(q L) + j \Delta\beta \sin(q L)}
$$

where $q = \sqrt{\kappa^2 - \Delta\beta^2}$.

The **power reflectivity** is $R = \vert r \vert^2$:

$$
R = \frac{\kappa^2 \sin^2(q L)}{q^2 \cos^2(q L) + \Delta\beta^2 \sin^2(q L)}
$$

At the Bragg resonance, $\Delta\beta = 0 \Rightarrow q = \kappa$, and $\sin^2(\kappa L)/\cos^2(\kappa L) = \tan^2(\kappa L)$. Therefore:

$$
R_{\text{peak}} = \tan^2(\kappa L)
$$

For a **strong grating** where $\kappa L \gg 1$, the tangent saturates to 1, but the proper closed form is:

$$
\boxed{R_{\max} = \tanh^2(\kappa L)}
$$

**Logic Explanation:** As $\kappa L \to \infty$, the grating becomes a perfect mirror. As $\kappa L \to 0$, $R \to (\kappa L)^2$ (Born-approximation / weak grating regime).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** A uniform FBG is inscribed in a single-mode fiber with $n_{\text{eff}} = 1.447$. The grating period is $\Lambda = 0.535 \,\mu\text{m}$ and the length is $L = 2 \,\text{cm}$. The peak index modulation is $\Delta n = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ with visibility $\nu = 1$. Compute (a) the Bragg wavelength, (b) the AC coupling coefficient, (c) the peak reflectivity.

**Given:**
- $n_{\text{eff}} = 1.447$
- $\Lambda = 0.535 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}$
- $L = 0.02 \,\text{m}$
- $\Delta n = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$
- $\nu = 1$

**Part (a) — Bragg Wavelength:**

$$
\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda = 2 \times 1.447 \times 0.535 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:
- $2 \times 1.447 = 2.894$
- $2.894 \times 0.535 = 1.54829$
- $1.54829 \times 10^{-6} = 1.548 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}$

$$
\boxed{\lambda_B \approx 1548.3 \,\text{nm}}
$$

**Part (b) — AC Coupling Coefficient:**

$$
\kappa = \frac{\pi}{\lambda_B} \cdot \nu \cdot \Delta n = \frac{\pi}{1.5483 \times 10^{-6}} \times 1 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Compute term by term:
- $\dfrac{\pi}{1.5483 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.029 \times 10^{6} \,\text{m}^{-1}$
- $2.029 \times 10^{6} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} = 507.2 \,\text{m}^{-1}$

$$
\boxed{\kappa \approx 507 \,\text{m}^{-1}}
$$

**Part (c) — Peak Reflectivity:**

Compute $\kappa L$:

$$
\kappa L = 507 \times 0.02 = 10.14
$$

Since $\kappa L \gg 1$, use $\tanh^2$:

$$
R_{\max} = \tanh^2(10.14) \approx 1.0
$$

More precisely, $\tanh(10.14) \approx 0.99999970$, so:

$$
\boxed{R_{\max} \approx 0.9999994 \approx 100\%}
$$

**Logical Check:** A 2 cm grating with $\Delta n = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ is a strong grating. Full reflection at Bragg wavelength is expected — the result is consistent.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: FBG vs LPG

| Property | Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) | Long Period Grating (LPG) |
|----------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Typical period $\Lambda$ | $0.2$ – $0.5 \,\mu\text{m}$ | $100$ – $1000 \,\mu\text{m}$ |
| Mode coupling | Forward core $\leftrightarrow$ Backward core | Forward core $\rightarrow$ Co-prop cladding |
| Spectral response | Reflection band (sharp) | Loss band (broad) in transmission |
| Phase-matching equation | $\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$ | $\lambda_i = (n_{\text{core}} - n_{\text{clad}}^{(i)})\Lambda$ |
| Typical bandwidth | $0.05$ – $0.5 \,\text{nm}$ | $10$ – $100 \,\text{nm}$ |
| Primary applications | WDM filters, dispersion compensation, sensors | EDFA gain flattening, band-rejection filters, sensors |
| Back-reflection? | Yes (major) | No (low) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fiber Grating Filter System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph srcBlock[Optical Source]
        src[Multi Wavelength Input Signal]
    end

    srcBlock --> circ[Optical Circulator Port 1]

    circ -- Port 2 --> fbgAssembly

    subgraph fbgAssembly[Fiber Grating Assembly]
        fbg1[FBG lambda 1 reflector]
        fbg2[FBG lambda 2 reflector]
        fbg3[FBG lambda 3 reflector]
    end

    fbgAssembly -- Reflected lambda target --> circ
    circ -- Port 3 --> drop[Dropped Wavelength Output]

    fbgAssembly -- Transmitted wavelengths --> thru[Through Output to Next Channel]

    style srcBlock fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style fbgAssembly fill:#7f6000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style drop fill:#385723,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style thru fill:#385723,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```

**Figure 1 — OADM using cascaded FBGs:** A 3-port optical circulator routes incoming WDM signals to a series of FBG reflectors. The target channel is reflected back to the circulator drop port; remaining channels pass through to the next stage.

### 4.2 Refractive Index Profile of a Uniform Fiber Bragg Grating

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    zAxis[Propagation Direction z]
    nAxis[Effective Index n]

    subgraph gratingProfile[Index Modulation along z]
        peak1[Peak n]
        valley1[Valley n]
        peak2[Peak n]
        valley2[Valley n]
        peak3[Peak n]
        valley3[Valley n]
    end

    nAxis --- peak1
    nAxis --- valley1
    nAxis --- peak2
    nAxis --- valley2
    nAxis --- peak3
    nAxis --- valley3

    zAxis --- peak1
    zAxis --- peak2
    zAxis --- peak3

    Lbrace[Grating Length L]:::lbl
    Lperiod[Period Lambda]:::lbl

    classDef lbl fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
```

**Figure 2 — Uniform FBG Index Profile:** The core refractive index oscillates between $n_{\text{eff}} + \Delta n / 2$ and $n_{\text{eff}} - \Delta n / 2$ with spatial period $\Lambda$, over a total physical length $L$.

### 4.3 Spectral Response Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph fbgResp[FBG Response Reflection Spectrum]
        fbgAxis1[Wavelength nm]
        fbgPeak[Sharp Peak at lambda B]
        fbgSideL[Side Lobes]:::warn
    end

    subgraph lpgResp[LPG Response Transmission Loss Spectrum]
        lpgAxis1[Wavelength nm]
        lpgBand[Broad Loss Band at lambda i]
    end

    fbgResp --- lpgResp

    classDef warn fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#c00,color:#000
```

**Figure 3 — Spectral Signatures:** FBG shows a narrow, sharp reflection peak (ideal for WDM demux); LPG shows a broad transmission loss notch (ideal for gain flattening and rejection filtering).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Apodization (tapering $\Delta n$ at the grating ends) is used in practice to suppress the side-lobes seen in the FBG spectrum. Students are expected to mention this in design-type KTU questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a Fiber Bragg Grating. State the first-order Bragg condition and explain its significance in optical filtering.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG)** is a periodic modulation of the core refractive index along the length of an optical fiber, written by exposing the germanium-doped core to a UV interference pattern. It functions as a **wavelength-selective reflector**.

The **first-order Bragg condition** is:

$$
\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda
$$

where $n_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective refractive index of the core mode and $\Lambda$ is the grating period.

**Significance:** This equation determines the exact wavelength that is reflected back by the grating. Wavelengths satisfying this condition undergo constructive interference and are strongly reflected; all other wavelengths pass through transparently. This makes the FBG a **narrowband optical filter** central to WDM systems, dispersion compensation, and fiber-optic sensing. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for significance)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between a Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) and a Long Period Grating (LPG) in terms of mode coupling and application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | FBG | LPG |
|---|---|---|
| Grating period | $\sim 0.5 \,\mu\text{m}$ (short) | $\sim 100$–$1000 \,\mu\text{m}$ (long) |
| Mode coupling | Forward core $\leftrightarrow$ Backward core | Forward core $\rightarrow$ Co-propagating cladding |
| Spectral behavior | Narrow reflection band | Broad transmission loss band |
| Application | WDM mux/demux, dispersion compensation, lasers | EDFA gain flattening, band-rejection filters, sensors |

**Key insight:** FBG reflects a specific wavelength back into the fiber; LPG transfers energy from the core to lossy cladding modes, producing a notch in transmission. **(3 marks — comparison matrix)**

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the first-order Bragg resonance condition for a uniform Fiber Bragg Grating. Explain the physical meaning of each parameter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A uniform FBG of length $L = 1.5 \,\text{cm}$ is inscribed in a fiber with $n_{\text{eff}} = 1.448$. The grating period is $\Lambda = 0.5355 \,\mu\text{m}$ and the induced index change has a peak value $\Delta n = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ with unity visibility. Determine the Bragg wavelength, the AC coupling coefficient, and the peak power reflectivity. Comment on whether the grating behaves as a strong or weak reflector. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider two counter-propagating core modes in a single-mode fiber. The forward wave has propagation constant $+\beta$ and the backward wave has $-\beta$. A periodic index perturbation of period $\Lambda$ provides a grating vector:

$$
K = \frac{2\pi}{\Lambda}
$$

For **constructive interference** (phase matching), the momentum mismatch between the two waves must be supplied by the grating:

$$
\beta - (-\beta) = K \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2\beta = \frac{2\pi}{\Lambda}
$$

Using $\beta = \dfrac{2\pi n_{\text{eff}}}{\lambda_B}$:

$$
\frac{4\pi n_{\text{eff}}}{\lambda_B} = \frac{2\pi}{\Lambda}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda}
$$

**Physical meaning:**
- $n_{\text{eff}}$: Effective index of the propagating core mode — depends on fiber design.
- $\Lambda$: Spatial period of the index modulation — controlled during UV writing.
- $\lambda_B$: The unique wavelength that experiences coherent back-reflection.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $n_{\text{eff}} = 1.448$, $L = 0.015 \,\text{m}$, $\Lambda = 0.5355 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}$, $\Delta n = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$, $\nu = 1$.

**Step 1 — Bragg Wavelength:**

$$
\lambda_B = 2 \times 1.448 \times 0.5355 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5508 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda_B \approx 1550.8 \,\text{nm}}
$$

**Step 2 — AC Coupling Coefficient:**

$$
\kappa = \frac{\pi}{\lambda_B} \nu \Delta n = \frac{\pi}{1.5508 \times 10^{-6}} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

- $\dfrac{\pi}{1.5508 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.0259 \times 10^{6} \,\text{m}^{-1}$
- $\kappa = 2.0259 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 303.9 \,\text{m}^{-1}$

$$
\boxed{\kappa \approx 304 \,\text{m}^{-1}}
$$

**Step 3 — Peak Reflectivity:**

$$
\kappa L = 304 \times 0.015 = 4.56
$$

Since $\kappa L$ is moderate, compute $\tanh$:

$$
\tanh(4.56) \approx 0.99984
$$

$$
R_{\max} = \tanh^2(4.56) \approx 0.99968
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\max} \approx 99.97\%}
$$

**Comment:** Since $R_{\max}$ is essentially 100%, the grating acts as a **strong reflector**. Any value $R_{\max} > 90\%$ is conventionally considered strong in the CMT framework.

---

#### Question B (Internal Choice — 14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the working of a **Long Period Grating (LPG)** filter. How does it differ from a Fiber Bragg Grating in terms of phase matching? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EDFA in a WDM link has an unwanted gain tilt of 3 dB across the C-band (1525–1565 nm). Design an LPG-based gain-flattening filter that produces the inverse tilt. The fiber has $n_{\text{core}} = 1.449$ and $n_{\text{clad}} = 1.444$. Determine the grating period $\Lambda$ required to attenuate the peak gain wavelength of 1532 nm. What is the loss at 1565 nm if the LPG has a Gaussian spectral profile of 30 nm FWHM? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — LPG Working (7 marks):**

A **Long Period Grating** has a much larger period ($100$ – $1000 \,\mu\text{m}$) than an FBG. It couples the fundamental core mode (LP₀₁) to co-propagating cladding modes (LP₀ₘ, $m \ge 2$).

**Phase-matching equation for LPG:**

$$
\lambda_i = (n_{\text{core}} - n_{\text{clad}}^{(i)})\Lambda
$$

Since the cladding modes are lossy (high attenuation in the cladding–coating interface), the coupled power is **absorbed**, producing a **loss peak** at $\lambda_i$ in the transmitted spectrum.

**Difference from FBG:** The FBG couples to a *backward* core mode with phase-matching $\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$, whereas the LPG couples to a *forward* cladding mode with phase-matching $\lambda_i = \Delta n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$. Hence the LPG is intrinsically a **transmission (loss) filter**, while the FBG is a **reflection filter**.

*(Insert a labelled schematic: Core mode → LPG → Cladding mode absorption; transmission notch in spectrum.)*

---

**Part (b) — LPG Design Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\lambda_{\text{peak}} = 1532 \,\text{nm}$, $n_{\text{core}} = 1.449$, $n_{\text{clad}} = 1.444$
- C-band edges: $1525$ – $1565 \,\text{nm}$, FWHM $= 30 \,\text{nm}$

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta n$:**

$$
\Delta n = n_{\text{core}} - n_{\text{clad}} = 1.449 - 1.444 = 0.005
$$

**Step 2 — Grating period:**

$$
\Lambda = \frac{\lambda_i}{\Delta n} = \frac{1532 \times 10^{-9}}{0.005} = 306.4 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Lambda \approx 306.4 \,\mu\text{m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Loss at 1565 nm using Gaussian profile:**

The spectral distance from peak:

$$
\Delta \lambda = 1565 - 1532 = 33 \,\text{nm}
$$

Gaussian loss (in dB) at offset $\Delta \lambda$ from peak amplitude $A_0$:

$$
L(\lambda) = A_0 \exp\!\left[-4 \ln 2 \left(\frac{\Delta \lambda}{\text{FWHM}}\right)^2\right]
$$

With $A_0 = 3 \,\text{dB}$ (target inverse tilt at peak), FWHM $= 30 \,\text{nm}$:

$$
L(1565) = 3 \cdot \exp\!\left[-4 \ln 2 \left(\frac{33}{30}\right)^2\right]
$$

Compute term by term:
- $\dfrac{33}{30} = 1.1$
- $1.1^2 = 1.21$
- $4 \ln 2 = 2.7726$
- $2.7726 \times 1.21 = 3.3548$
- $\exp(-3.3548) = 0.0349$

$$
L(1565) = 3 \times 0.0349 = 0.105 \,\text{dB}
$$

$$
\boxed{L(1565) \approx 0.1 \,\text{dB}}
$$

**Design Verdict:** The 30 nm FWHM LPG at 1532 nm provides strong attenuation (3 dB) at the gain peak and minimal loss (0.1 dB) at 1565 nm — precisely the **inverse tilt** needed to flatten the EDFA gain spectrum.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up FBG and LPG phase-matching equations** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for writing $2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$ for an LPG problem.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 2 in Bragg's condition** — students often write $\lambda_B = n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$, costing 2 marks.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** — $\Lambda$ and $L$ must be in **meters** (SI), not $\mu\text{m}$, when inserted into $\kappa$ and $\kappa L$.
> 4. **Weak vs strong grating judgement** — always compute $\kappa L$ first; use $R = (\kappa L)^2$ only if $\kappa L \ll 1$ (weak), otherwise use $R = \tanh^2(\kappa L)$.
> 5. **Missing significance/applications** — KTU awards 1 mark just for stating a real-world application (WDM, sensing, dispersion compensation).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Bragg condition for FBG: $\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$ — memorize verbatim.
> - LPG phase matching: $\lambda_i = (n_{\text{core}} - n_{\text{clad}})\Lambda$ — note the **difference** in $\Delta n$, not $2 n_{\text{eff}}$.
> - **FBG = reflection filter** (counter-propagating core mode); **LPG = transmission loss filter** (co-propagating cladding mode).
> - Peak reflectivity: $R_{\max} = \tanh^2(\kappa L)$ — strong grating $\Rightarrow R \to 1$.
> - Bandwidth depends on **both** grating length $L$ and index modulation depth $\Delta n$.
> - **Chirped gratings** ($\Lambda$ varies with $z$) are used for dispersion compensation.
> - **Apodization** (Gaussian $\Delta n$ profile) suppresses side-lobes in the reflection spectrum.
> - Wavelength tuning is achieved by changing either $\Lambda$ (during writing) or $n_{\text{eff}}$ (thermo-optic / strain-optic effects).
> - Typical C-band Bragg wavelength: $1550 \,\text{nm}$; corresponding $\Lambda \approx 0.535 \,\mu\text{m}$ for $n_{\text{eff}} = 1.448$.
> - Three core equations: **Bragg condition**, **Coupled-mode reflectivity**, **LPG phase matching** — these are the three highest-weight formulas for KTU.
> - FBG applications: WDM mux/demux, OADM, laser stabilization, dispersion compensation, structural sensing.
> - LPG applications: EDFA gain flattening, band-rejection filtering, refractive-index sensors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tunable Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tunable Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Tunable Optical Filter (TOF)**
> A **tunable optical filter** is a wavelength-selective photonic device that can be dynamically adjusted (or "tuned") to transmit or reflect a specific narrow band of optical wavelengths from a multi-wavelength composite signal, while rejecting all other wavelengths. The center wavelength of the passband can be electronically, thermally, mechanically, or acousto-optically varied across a defined **tuning range** without requiring a physical replacement of the device.

In the context of **Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM)** systems, a tunable filter acts as a programmable wavelength selector — essentially performing the inverse function of a laser, but in the receiver/filtering domain.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Analogy: The "Radio Tuner" for Light**
> Imagine you are listening to a radio where **101.1 MHz, 102.5 MHz, and 104.3 MHz** are all playing simultaneously through the same antenna wire. The tuning knob on your radio is essentially a **tunable filter** — it filters out every station except the one you want to hear.
>
> Now translate this to **optical communication**: Instead of radio stations, we have optical channels at **1550.0 nm, 1550.8 nm, 1551.6 nm** etc., all traveling together inside a single optical fiber. A **tunable optical filter** is the "knob" that lets the receiver pick **only one** wavelength channel at a time, while discarding all others.
>
> Without tunable filters, we would need a **separate physical filter** for every WDM channel — a logistical nightmare for 80-channel DWDM systems!

## 1.3 Why Tunable Filters Are Critical in KTU Syllabus Context

| Application | Function of Tunable Filter |
|-------------|----------------------------|
| **WDM Network Reconfiguration** | Dynamically route/add/drop wavelengths |
| **Optical Add-Drop Multiplexers (OADM)** | Select a specific channel from a multiplexed stream |
| **Optical Spectrum Analyzers** | Scan across wavelengths to measure signal spectrum |
| **Wavelength-Selective Switching** | Direct specific wavelengths to specific output ports |
| **Receivers in DWDM** | Demultiplex channels without fixed demux hardware |

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Exam Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Six Mandatory Performance Parameters**
> 1. **Free Spectral Range (FSR)** — the spacing between adjacent transmission peaks (in nm or GHz)
> 2. **Finesse (ℱ)** — ratio of FSR to bandwidth; measures spectral sharpness
> 3. **Passband Bandwidth (Δλ)** — the **3-dB** spectral width of the transmission peak
> 4. **Tuning Range** — the total wavelength span over which the filter can be tuned
> 5. **Insertion Loss** — optical power loss at the selected wavelength (in dB)
> 6. **Tuning Speed** — time required to switch from one wavelength to another (ms or μs)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Tunable Optical Filters

Tunable filters are broadly classified based on their **filtering mechanism** and **tuning technique**.

### A. Based on Filtering Mechanism

> **1. Interference-Based Filters**
> - Fabry-Perot Interferometer (FPI) Filter
> - Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI) Filter
> - Michelson Interferometer Filter
> - Thin-Film Interference Filter

> **2. Dispersion-Based Filters**
> - Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) Filter
> - Arrayed Waveguide Grating (AWG) [primarily a demux, but tunable variants exist]

> **3. Acousto-Optic Filters**
> - Acousto-Optic Tunable Filter (AOTF)

> **4. Other Mechanisms**
> - Liquid Crystal Tunable Filter (LCTF)
> - Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) based filters

### B. Based on Tuning Technique

| Tuning Method | Physical Principle | Typical Speed |
|---------------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Mechanical** | Piezoelectric or MEMS actuation of cavity length | ms range |
| **Thermal** | Thermo-optic effect (refractive index change with temperature) | seconds |
| **Electro-optic** | Pockels / Kerr effect (refractive index change with voltage) | μs – ns |
| **Acousto-optic** | Acoustic wave-induced Bragg diffraction | μs |
| **Liquid Crystal** | Voltage-controlled molecular orientation | ms |

## 2.2 The Fabry-Perot Interferometer (FPI) Filter — Detailed Operation

The **Fabry-Perot filter** is the most fundamental and most-tested tunable filter in KTU examinations. It consists of two parallel, partially-reflecting mirrors separated by a distance **d**, forming an optical cavity of length **L**.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Multi-wavelength input** enters the cavity through the first mirror.
2. Light undergoes **multiple internal reflections** between the two mirrors.
3. At each reflection, a small portion of light **transmits** through the second mirror.
4. The transmitted beams **interfere constructively** only at specific resonance wavelengths that satisfy the **resonance condition**.
5. By varying **L** (mechanically, thermally, or electro-optically), the resonance wavelength shifts — achieving tuning.

### The Resonance Condition

For constructive interference, the round-trip phase shift must equal an integer multiple of $2\pi$:

$$2 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot n L = 2 \pi m$$

where $m$ is an integer (mode number), $n$ is the refractive index, $L$ is the cavity length, and $\lambda$ is the wavelength in vacuum.

Simplifying gives the **resonance wavelength**:

$$\lambda_m = \frac{2 n L}{m}$$

> [!NOTE] **Tuning Insight**
> Tuning is achieved by either:
> - Changing **L** (mechanical / piezoelectric / MEMS)
> - Changing **n** (thermo-optic / electro-optic)
> Both shift the resonance wavelength $\lambda_m$ to a new value.

## 2.3 The Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI) Filter

The MZI filter uses **two 3-dB couplers** connected by two waveguide arms of slightly different lengths. The optical path difference $\Delta L$ creates wavelength-dependent interference at the output.

**Transmission function** (assuming lossless 3-dB couplers):

$$T_{MZI}(\lambda) = \cos^2\left(\frac{\pi n \Delta L}{\lambda}\right)$$

Tuning is achieved by changing $\Delta L$ (often thermally, via thin-film heaters on one arm).

## 2.4 Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) Filter

A Fiber Bragg Grating is a periodic variation of refractive index inscribed in the core of an optical fiber. It reflects a specific wavelength (the **Bragg wavelength**) and transmits all others.

**Bragg Condition:**

$$\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$$

where $n_{eff}$ is the effective refractive index of the fiber core and $\Lambda$ is the grating period.

> **Tuning of FBG**: Achieved by:
> - **Strain tuning** (mechanical stretching/compression → changes $\Lambda$)
> - **Thermal tuning** (temperature change → changes $n_{eff}$ and $\Lambda$)
> - **Acousto-optic tuning** (acoustic wave creates a moving grating)

## 2.5 Acousto-Optic Tunable Filter (AOTF)

An AOTF uses **acousto-optic interaction** in a birefringent crystal (e.g., LiNbO₃). An RF acoustic wave launched via a piezoelectric transducer creates a **traveling refractive-index grating** inside the crystal.

**Acousto-optic Bragg condition:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v_a \cdot \Delta n}{f_a}$$

where $v_a$ is the acoustic velocity, $\Delta n$ is the birefringence, and $f_a$ is the acoustic frequency.

> Tuning is achieved by varying $f_a$ (typically in the 30–250 MHz range). The AOTF can be tuned in **microseconds**, making it the **fastest** among the standard KTU-listed tunable filters.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — TUNABLE FILTERS**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | **Resonance wavelength (FPI)** | $\lambda_m = \dfrac{2 n L}{m}$ | $m$ = mode number, $n$ = RI, $L$ = cavity length | nm |
| 2 | **Free Spectral Range (FSR) in wavelength** | $\text{FSR}_\lambda = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{2 n L}$ | spacing between adjacent transmission peaks | nm |
| 3 | **FSR in frequency** | $\text{FSR}_\nu = \dfrac{c}{2 n L}$ | $c$ = speed of light | Hz |
| 4 | **Finesse** | $\mathcal{F} = \dfrac{\pi \sqrt{R}}{1 - R}$ | $R$ = mirror reflectivity | dimensionless |
| 5 | **3-dB Bandwidth (FWHM)** | $\Delta \lambda = \dfrac{\text{FSR}_\lambda}{\mathcal{F}} = \dfrac{\lambda^2 (1 - R)}{2 \pi n L \sqrt{R}}$ | spectral width of passband | nm |
| 6 | **Bragg wavelength (FBG)** | $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$ | $\Lambda$ = grating period | nm |
| 7 | **MZI transmission** | $T_{MZI} = \cos^2\left(\dfrac{\pi n \Delta L}{\lambda}\right)$ | $\Delta L$ = arm length difference | dimensionless |
| 8 | **AOTF wavelength** | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_a \Delta n}{f_a}$ | $v_a$ = acoustic velocity, $f_a$ = acoustic frequency | nm |
| 9 | **Tuning sensitivity (thermal, FPI)** | $\dfrac{\Delta \lambda}{\Delta T} = \lambda \left(\dfrac{1}{n}\dfrac{dn}{dT} + \dfrac{1}{L}\dfrac{dL}{dT}\right)$ | thermo-optic + thermal expansion | nm/K |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Long-haul DWDM networks** — tunable filters enable **wavelength-routing** in reconfigurable optical add-drop multiplexers (ROADMs), eliminating the need for fixed-wavelength inventory.
- **Optical cross-connects (OXC)** — used as wavelength-selective switches in backbone networks.
- **Test & measurement** — tunable filters in **optical spectrum analyzers (OSAs)** allow sweeping across the C-band (1530–1565 nm) or L-band (1565–1625 nm).
- **Cable TV (CATV)** networks — selecting specific RF subcarriers modulated onto optical carriers.
- **Defense & LIDAR** systems — selecting specific spectral lines from broadband returns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Free Spectral Range (FSR) of a Fabry-Perot Filter

### Setup
Consider a Fabry-Perot cavity of length $L$, filled with medium of refractive index $n$. Two adjacent resonance modes occur at mode numbers $m$ and $m+1$.

### Step 1 — Write the resonance condition for mode $m$:

$$\lambda_m = \frac{2 n L}{m}$$

### Step 2 — Write the resonance condition for the adjacent mode $m+1$:

$$\lambda_{m+1} = \frac{2 n L}{m+1}$$

### Step 3 — FSR is the difference $\lambda_m - \lambda_{m+1}$:

$$\text{FSR}_\lambda = \lambda_m - \lambda_{m+1} = 2 n L \left( \frac{1}{m} - \frac{1}{m+1} \right)$$

### Step 4 — Simplify using common denominator:

$$\text{FSR}_\lambda = 2 n L \cdot \frac{(m+1) - m}{m(m+1)} = \frac{2 n L}{m(m+1)}$$

### Step 5 — For large $m$, approximate $m(m+1) \approx m^2$:

$$\text{FSR}_\lambda \approx \frac{2 n L}{m^2}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute $\lambda_m = 2nL/m \Rightarrow m = 2nL/\lambda_m$:

$$\text{FSR}_\lambda \approx \frac{2 n L}{\left(\dfrac{2 n L}{\lambda_m}\right)^2} = \frac{2 n L \cdot \lambda_m^2}{4 n^2 L^2}$$

### Step 7 — Final simplified result:

$$\boxed{\text{FSR}_\lambda = \frac{\lambda_m^2}{2 n L}}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** A **longer cavity** → **smaller FSR** → more closely spaced transmission peaks. A **shorter cavity** → **larger FSR** → wider wavelength spacing between peaks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Finesse of a Fabry-Perot Filter

### Setup
The finesse depends solely on the mirror reflectivity $R$. It is defined as the ratio of FSR to the 3-dB bandwidth $\delta\lambda$:

$$\mathcal{F} = \frac{\text{FSR}_\lambda}{\delta\lambda}$$

### Step 1 — The Airy function for FPI transmission (without proof, stated standard result):

$$T_{FP} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{4 R \sin^2(\delta/2)}{(1 - R)^2}}$$

where $\delta = \dfrac{4 \pi n L}{\lambda} \cos\theta$ is the round-trip phase.

### Step 2 — The 3-dB bandwidth occurs when the denominator equals 2 (i.e., transmission is 1/2 of peak):

$$1 + \frac{4 R \sin^2(\delta_{3dB}/2)}{(1 - R)^2} = 2$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\sin^2(\delta_{3dB}/2)$:

$$\frac{4 R \sin^2(\delta_{3dB}/2)}{(1 - R)^2} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin^2(\delta_{3dB}/2) = \frac{(1 - R)^2}{4 R}$$

### Step 4 — For small arguments, $\sin(x) \approx x$:

$$\left(\frac{\delta_{3dB}}{2}\right)^2 \approx \frac{(1 - R)^2}{4 R}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $\delta_{3dB}$:

$$\delta_{3dB} = \frac{(1 - R)}{\sqrt{R}}$$

### Step 6 — Full-width at half-maximum in terms of FSR:

Recall that the FSR corresponds to $\delta_{FSR} = 2\pi$. Therefore:

$$\delta_{3dB} = \frac{2\pi}{\mathcal{F}}$$

### Step 7 — Equate the two expressions and solve for $\mathcal{F}$:

$$\frac{2\pi}{\mathcal{F}} = \frac{1 - R}{\sqrt{R}}$$

$$\boxed{\mathcal{F} = \frac{2\pi \sqrt{R}}{1 - R} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{R}}{1 - R} \cdot 2 \equiv \frac{\pi \sqrt{R}}{1 - R} \text{ (commonly written)}}$$

Wait — let me correct the standard textbook expression:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{F} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{R}}{1 - R}}$$

> **Verification:** For $R = 0.95$, $\mathcal{F} \approx \frac{\pi \times 0.9747}{0.05} \approx 61.2$. For $R = 0.99$, $\mathcal{F} \approx 313$. High reflectivity → very high finesse → very sharp transmission peaks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: 3-dB Bandwidth of FPI

Using the result from Section 3.2:

$$\delta\lambda_{3dB} = \frac{\text{FSR}_\lambda}{\mathcal{F}} = \frac{\lambda^2}{2 n L} \cdot \frac{1 - R}{\pi \sqrt{R}}$$

$$\boxed{\delta\lambda_{3dB} = \frac{\lambda^2 (1 - R)}{2 \pi n L \sqrt{R}}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Tuning Range of a FPI Filter

Suppose the cavity length is varied from $L_{min}$ to $L_{max}$ (or refractive index from $n_{min}$ to $n_{max}$). The corresponding resonance wavelength varies from:

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{2 n_{min} L_{min}}{m}, \quad \lambda_{max} = \frac{2 n_{max} L_{max}}{m}$$

> [!NOTE] **Tuning Range Definition**
> $$\Delta\lambda_{tune} = \lambda_{max} - \lambda_{min} = \frac{2}{m}\left(n_{max} L_{max} - n_{min} L_{min}\right)$$

For thermal tuning of a silica-based FPI ($dn/dT \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-5}$ /K, $dL/dT \approx 0.55 \times 10^{-6}$ /K), the tuning sensitivity is approximately:

$$\frac{d\lambda}{dT} \approx \lambda \left(\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dT}\right) \approx 0.012 \text{ nm/K for } \lambda = 1550 \text{ nm}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A Fabry-Perot tunable filter has cavity length $L = 100\,\mu m$, refractive index $n = 1.5$, and mirror reflectivity $R = 0.9$. Operating at $\lambda = 1550$ nm. Calculate the FSR, finesse, and 3-dB bandwidth.

### Step 1 — Calculate FSR:

$$\text{FSR}_\lambda = \frac{\lambda^2}{2 n L} = \frac{(1550 \times 10^{-9})^2}{2 \times 1.5 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\text{FSR}_\lambda = \frac{2.4025 \times 10^{-12}}{3.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 8.01 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 8.01 \text{ nm}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Finesse:

$$\mathcal{F} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{R}}{1 - R} = \frac{\pi \times \sqrt{0.9}}{1 - 0.9} = \frac{3.1416 \times 0.9487}{0.1} = 29.81$$

### Step 3 — Calculate 3-dB Bandwidth:

$$\delta\lambda = \frac{\text{FSR}_\lambda}{\mathcal{F}} = \frac{8.01 \text{ nm}}{29.81} = 0.269 \text{ nm}$$

### Final Answer:
- FSR = **8.01 nm**
- Finesse = **29.81** (dimensionless)
- 3-dB Bandwidth = **0.269 nm**

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix of KTU Filter Types

| Parameter | FPI | MZI | FBG | AOTF |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|------|
| **Tuning Range** | Wide (10–100 nm) | Moderate (5–20 nm) | Wide (40+ nm with strain) | Very Wide (400+ nm) |
| **Tuning Speed** | ms | ms – s | ms | μs (fastest) |
| **Insertion Loss** | Low (1–3 dB) | Moderate (3–5 dB) | Low (1–2 dB) | Moderate (3–5 dB) |
| **Bandwidth** | Very Narrow (sub-nm) | Broad (nm-scale) | Very Narrow (sub-nm) | Moderate (1–3 nm) |
| **Finesse** | 10–300 | N/A (sinusoidal) | N/A (single peak) | N/A |
| **Typical Application** | WDM channel selection | Coarse WDM | DWDM, sensing | Fast switching, OSA |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Tunable Filter Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MULTIWAVELENGTH OPTICAL INPUT"] --> B["TUNABLE OPTICAL FILTER"]
    B --> C1["INTERFERENCE BASED"]
    B --> C2["DISPERSION BASED"]
    B --> C3["ACOUSTO OPTIC BASED"]
    B --> C4["LIQUID CRYSTAL BASED"]
    C1 --> D1["FABRY PEROT FPI"]
    C1 --> D2["MACH ZEHNDER MZI"]
    C1 --> D3["MICHELSON"]
    C1 --> D4["THIN FILM"]
    C2 --> E1["FIBER BRAGG GRATING FBG"]
    C2 --> E2["ARRAYED WAVEGUIDE GRATING AWG"]
    C3 --> F1["AOTF"]
    C4 --> G1["LCTF"]
    D1 --> H1["TUNING: PIEZO MEMS"]
    D2 --> H2["TUNING: THERMO OPTIC"]
    D3 --> H3["TUNING: MECHANICAL"]
    E1 --> H4["TUNING: STRAIN OR THERMAL"]
    F1 --> H5["TUNING: RF FREQUENCY"]
    G1 --> H6["TUNING: APPLIED VOLTAGE"]
    H1 --> OUT["SELECTED WAVELENGTH CHANNEL OUTPUT"]
    H2 --> OUT
    H3 --> OUT
    H4 --> OUT
    H5 --> OUT
    H6 --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Fabry-Perot Filter Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["MULTIWAVELENGTH INPUT FIBER"] --> M1["PARTIALLY REFLECTING MIRROR 1"]
    M1 --> CAV["OPTICAL CAVITY LENGTH L REFRACTIVE INDEX n"]
    CAV --> M2["PARTIALLY REFLECTING MIRROR 2"]
    M2 --> OUT["FILTERED OUTPUT SINGLE WAVELENGTH"]
    CAV -. TUNING SIGNAL .-> CTRL["TUNING CONTROLLER PIEZO OR THERMAL OR EO"]
    CTRL -. ADJUSTS L OR n .-> CAV
    M2 -. REFLECTED LIGHT .-> M1
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Acousto-Optic Tunable Filter (AOTF)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RF["RF SIGNAL GENERATOR FREQUENCY fa"] --> PZT["PIEZOELECTRIC TRANSDUCER"]
    PZT --> CRYST["BIREFRINGENT CRYSTAL LINBO3"]
    INPOL["INPUT POLARIZED LIGHT"] --> CRYST
    CRYST --> TE["TE POLARIZED OUTPUT"]
    CRYST --> TM["TM POLARIZED OUTPUT"]
    CRYST -. ACOUSTIC WAVE BRAGG GRATING .-> PZT
    OUT1["SELECTED WAVELENGTH CHANNEL"] --> TE
    OUT2["REJECTED WAVELENGTHS"] --> TM
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) Tunable Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["MULTIWAVELENGTH INPUT"] --> CIRC["OPTICAL CIRCULATOR PORT 1"]
    CIRC --> FBG["FIBER BRAGG GRATING PERIOD LAMBDA"]
    FBG -. REFLECTS LAMBDA BRAGG .-> CIRC
    CIRC --> REF["REFLECTED OUTPUT SELECTED CHANNEL"]
    FBG --> TRANS["TRANSMITTED OUTPUT REMAINING CHANNELS"]
    STRAIN["STRAIN OR THERMAL TUNER"] -. CHANGES LAMBDA AND NEFF .-> FBG
```

## 4.5 Spectral Response Characteristics Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FPI RESPONSE: COMB OF SHARP PEAKS"]
    B["MZI RESPONSE: SINUSOIDAL PERIODIC"]
    C["FBG RESPONSE: SINGLE NARROW PEAK"]
    D["AOTF RESPONSE: SINGLE TUNABLE PEAK"]
    A --> SUM["ALL ARE USED FOR WAVELENGTH SELECTION IN WDM DWDM SYSTEMS"]
    B --> SUM
    C --> SUM
    D --> SUM
```
