## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the principle of fiber-optic illumination used in entertainment displays. Mention any two advantages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
A high-intensity lamp (tungsten-halogen, metal-halide, or RGB LED) is housed inside an *illuminator*. An elliptical reflector focuses the lamp's output onto the polished input face of a bundle of optical fibers. Light enters each fiber within the acceptance cone defined by the numerical aperture:
$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\sqrt{n_1^{2}-n_2^{2}})$$
It then propagates by **Total Internal Reflection** at the core-cladding interface to the distal end, where it exits as a bright point (end-emitter) or along the length (side-emitter).

**Two advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Electrical Safety:** No electrical conductors are routed to the display point — only light travels. Safe in pools, near flammable décor, or in explosive atmospheres.
2. **No Heat at Output:** Only visible/IR photons below the cut-off of the fiber reach the emission point, so decorative materials (fabric flowers, wax, paper) are not scorched.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Distinguish between end-emitting and side-emitting optical fibers. Give one entertainment application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | End-Emitting Fiber | Side-Emitting Fiber |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Emission region | Only at the output face | Along entire fiber length |
| Mechanism | Polished output face | Controlled scattering (micro-bends, dopants) |
| Attenuation goal | Minimize loss | Intentional controlled loss |
| Typical use | Point lights, museum spots | Pool edges, star ceilings |
| Application example | Christmas tree star | Swimming pool perimeter glow |

*(1.5 marks for distinction table, 1 mark for one application each, 0.5 mark for conclusion.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the construction and working of a **fiber-optic illumination system** used for decorative entertainment purposes. Illustrate with a block diagram and describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**1. Construction (3 marks):**
A fiber-optic entertainment illuminator consists of the following sub-units:

- **Lamp Module:** A tungsten-halogen (50–250 W), metal-halide, or RGB LED source. Chosen for color rendering index (CRI > 90 for museums) or for color tunability (LED for theme parks).
- **Reflector:** An elliptical or parabolic dichroic-coated reflector that collects the lamp's spherical emission and focuses it onto the fiber bundle's input ferrule. The dichroic coating reflects visible light but transmits IR, keeping the fiber input cool.
- **Cold Mirror / IR-UV Filter:** Removes residual infrared (heat) and ultraviolet (degradation) components.
- **Color Wheel (optional):** A motorised disc carrying 4–8 dichroic color filters; rotation produces smooth color cross-fades.
- **Fiber Bundle Ferrule:** A precisely machined brass or aluminium ferrule that aligns the polished fiber input faces at the reflector's focal point.
- **Fiber Harness:** A bundle of 50–500 individual plastic (PMMA) or silica fibers, sheathed in a PVC or metallised jacket for mechanical protection and ambient-light blocking.
- **Output End-Fittings:** Lenses, star-pattern terminators, or side-emitter mounts.

**2. Working (3 marks):**
The lamp emits diverging light. The reflector's geometry redirects these rays into a converging cone aimed at the fiber ferrule. Each fiber accepts rays within its acceptance cone (half-angle $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}\text{NA}$). Once inside, light undergoes multiple total internal reflections along the fiber's length. At the output end, light emerges as a cone dictated by the NA, producing a bright, colored, non-coherent point source at the display location.

**3. Block Diagram (1 mark):** (Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**(b)** A museum uses an end-emitting plastic fiber (NA = 0.5, attenuation = 200 dB/km) to illuminate an artifact located **25 m** from a 75 W illuminator. Compute **(i)** the total attenuation in dB, **(ii)** the optical power reaching the artifact, and **(iii)** the coupling efficiency if the reflector cone half-angle is 20° and the fiber face has a single-layer anti-reflection coating reducing reflection loss to 1%. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**(i) Total attenuation in dB (2 marks):**
$$L = 25 \text{ m} = 0.025 \text{ km}$$
$$A_{dB} = \alpha \times L = 200 \times 0.025 = 5 \text{ dB}$$

**(ii) Optical power reaching the artifact (2 marks):**
Power ratio:
$$10^{A_{dB}/10} = 10^{0.5} \approx 3.162$$
Power at artifact (before face losses):
$$P_{artifact} = \frac{75}{3.162} \approx 23.72 \text{ W}$$
After applying 1% loss at the output face: $P_{out} = 23.72 \times 0.99 \approx 23.48$ W.

**(iii) Coupling efficiency (3 marks):**
Acceptance half-angle:
$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.5) = 30°$$
Reflector cone half-angle $\theta_r = 20°$. Since $\theta_r < \theta_a$, the geometric capture is complete ($\eta_{geo} = 1$).
Transmission through AR-coated face: $T = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$.
For a single fiber, packing fraction = 1.
$$\boxed{\eta_{coupling} = 1 \times 0.99 = 0.99 = 99\%}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **side-emitting optical fiber**. Derive an expression for the optical power radiated per unit length of such a fiber. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A side-emitting fiber is constructed by introducing controlled scattering centers along the length of a standard step-index fiber. Methods include:
- **Dopant scattering:** Adding TiO₂ or BaSO₄ micro-particles in the cladding
- **Micro-bending:** Mechanically perturbing the fiber axis periodically
- **Gas microbubbles:** Introducing tiny voids in the core
- **Surface treatment:** Chemically etching the cladding to roughen the core-cladding interface

These scattering centers deflect guided modes out of the TIR cone, radiating them sideways.

**Working (2 marks):**
Light from the illuminator enters the fiber and propagates by TIR. At each scattering center, a fraction of the optical power is deflected out of the guided-mode set and radiated into the surrounding medium (typically a transparent diffuser jacket for visible aesthetics). The fiber therefore appears uniformly bright along its length.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Let $P(x)$ be the guided power at distance $x$. In an infinitesimal length $dx$, the fraction of power scattered sideways is $\gamma \, dx$, where $\gamma$ is the scattering coefficient (m⁻¹). Thus:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dP}{dx} &= -\gamma P \\
\int_{P_0}^{P(L)} \frac{dP}{P} &= -\gamma \int_0^L dx \\
\ln\!\frac{P(L)}{P_0} &= -\gamma L \\
P(L) &= P_0 \, e^{-\gamma L}
\end{aligned}$$

The **power radiated per unit length** at position $x$ is:
$$p_{rad}(x) = -\frac{dP}{dx} = \gamma P_0 \, e^{-\gamma x} = \gamma P(x)$$

**(b)** A 4 m side-emitting fiber is fed with 8 W at $x = 0$. The scattering coefficient is $\gamma = 0.5$ m⁻¹. Find **(i)** the power remaining at the far end, **(ii)** the total power radiated sideways, and **(iii)** the brightness at $x = 2$ m relative to $x = 0$ in dB. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**(i) Power at the far end (2 marks):**
$$P(4) = 8 \times e^{-0.5 \times 4} = 8 \times e^{-2} = 8 \times 0.1353 \approx 1.083 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Total power radiated sideways (2 marks):**
$$P_{side} = P_0 - P(L) = 8 - 1.083 = 6.917 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Brightness at x = 2 m relative to x = 0 in dB (3 marks):**
$$P(2) = 8 \times e^{-0.5 \times 2} = 8 \times e^{-1} = 8 \times 0.3679 \approx 2.943 \text{ W}$$
Ratio:
$$\frac{P(2)}{P(0)} = \frac{2.943}{8} = 0.3679$$
In decibels:
$$10 \log_{10}(0.3679) = 10 \times (-0.4343) = -4.343 \text{ dB}$$

So the fiber at 2 m is **4.34 dB dimmer** than at the input end.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Entertainment Applications Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing attenuation units:** Plastic fiber attenuation is typically **200–1000 dB/km**, NOT 0.2–0.5 dB/km like telecom silica. Many students lose 2 marks by quoting telecom values.
> 2. **Forgetting Fresnel losses at BOTH ends:** When computing output power, students often apply attenuation but ignore the ~3.5% reflection loss at each face (≈7% combined).
> 3. **Mixing end-emitter and side-emitter equations:** The exponential $e^{-\gamma L}$ applies only to side-emitters. End-emitters use $10^{-\alpha L/10}$.
> 4. **Unit errors in $\gamma$:** The scattering coefficient is in **m⁻¹**, NOT km⁻¹. Always convert fiber length to meters before substituting.
> 5. **NA formula sign error:** $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^{2}-n_2^{2}}$, NOT $\sqrt{n_2^{2}-n_1^{2}}$. With $n_1 > n_2$ always, the square root is real and positive.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Entertainment applications** use optical fibers as *passive light guides*, not information channels.
- **End-emitting fibers** deliver a point of light; **side-emitting fibers** glow along their length.
- The acceptance cone is defined by **NA = $\sqrt{n_1^{2}-n_2^{2}}$**; plastic fibers (NA ≈ 0.5) capture more light than silica (NA ≈ 0.2).
- **Attenuation values for entertainment plastic fibers:** typically **150–400 dB/km**, far higher than telecom silica (**≤ 0.5 dB/km**).
- **Attenuation formula (end-emitter):** $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L/10}$ with $\alpha$ in dB/km and $L$ in km.
- **Side-emitter decay:** $P(L) = P_0 \, e^{-\gamma L}$ with $\gamma$ in m⁻¹ and $L$ in m. Power radiated per unit length is $\gamma P(x)$.
- **Fresnel loss at uncoated air-glass face ≈ 3.5%** (use $R = ((n_1-n_0)/(n_1+n_0))^2$).
- **Illuminator components:** lamp, reflector, IR/UV filter, color wheel, ferrule.
- **DMX-512** is the industry standard control protocol for color/intensity modulation.
- **Five key advantages:** electrical isolation, no heat at output, EMI immunity, flexibility, single-source multi-point delivery.
- **Typical applications:** Christmas trees, museum spotlights, pool outlines, star-field ceilings, signage, theatrical props, architectural façade lighting.
- **Safety codes applicable:** IEC 60364-7-702 (pool installations) and NFPA 70 (US) — fiber optics satisfy these by keeping mains electricity outside wet/explosive zones.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors - Types and Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT636 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Sensors - Types and Principles # MODULE 4 — SENSORS: TYPES AND PRINCIPLES > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This module deals with the classification, operating principles, and signal-modulation mechanisms of optical and electronic sensors used in modern telecommunication and instrumentation systems. A deep understanding of how light interacts with matter (refraction, interference, polarization, evanescence) is critical to scoring in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). --- ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of a Sensor A **sensor** is a physical device, module, or subsystem whose purpose is to detect events or changes in its environment and send the corresponding information to other electronics, frequently a computer processor. In the context of **Optical Communication (PEECT636)**, a sensor translates a physical, chemical, or biological measurand — such as temperature, pressure, strain, refractive index, or chemical concentration — into a measurable optical signal using the principles of light-matter interaction. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, sensors are classified into two broad families: | Family | Operating Domain | Energy Conversion | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Electronic Sensors** | Electrical domain | Resistive / Capacitive / Inductive / Piezoelectric | | **Optical (Fiber-Optic) Sensors** | Optical domain | Intensity / Phase / Wavelength / Polarization | > [!NOTE] > **Definition Highlight:** A **Fiber Optic Sensor (FOS)** is a sensor that uses an optical fiber either as the sensing element (intrinsic sensor) or as a means of relaying signals from a remote sensor to the electronics that process the signals (extrinsic sensor). --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a thermometer. The mercury inside expands when it gets hot — a **physical change** (volume of mercury) is converted into a **readable signal** (the height of the mercury column). An **optical sensor** works the same way, but uses **light** instead of mercury: - **The measurand** (e.g., temperature) perturbs some property of light — its brightness, color, or phase. - **The perturbed light** travels through the fiber to a detector (photodiode). - **The detector** converts the optical perturbation back into an electrical signal that we can read. > Think of the optical fiber as a **"light wire"**, and the sensor head as a **"translator"** that writes the environmental information onto the light wave. The detector at the other end **"reads"** the message. --- ## 1.3 General Characteristics of an Ideal Sensor A KTU Board examiner typically expects students to know the following performance characteristics: - **Sensitivity** — Output change per unit input change. - **Dynamic Range** — Ratio between the largest and smallest measurable input. - **Resolution** — Smallest detectable change in the measurand. - **Linearity** — Degree to which output is directly proportional to input. - **Selectivity / Specificity** — Ability to discriminate the target measurand from environmental noise. - **Response Time / Bandwidth** — Speed of reaction to input changes. - **Accuracy & Precision** — Closeness to true value and reproducibility, respectively. > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic — "S-D-R-L-S-R-A-P"** for sensor characteristics: **S**ensitivity, **D**ynamic range, **R**esolution, **L**inearity, **S**electivity, **R**esponse time, **A**ccuracy, **P**recision. # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Classification of Optical Sensors (KTU 2024 Module 4 Core) Optical sensors, particularly fiber-optic sensors, are classified based on the **light property that gets modulated** by the measurand. This is the highest-weightage concept in this module. ### A. Intensity-Modulated Fiber Optic Sensors - The measurand changes the **amplitude (intensity)** of light reaching the detector. - Simplest demodulation: direct photodetection. - **Working Principle:** Light from a source is launched into a fiber. The sensing region attenuates, scatters, or reflects light depending on the measurand. The detector reads the intensity variation. > [!NOTE] > **Common Mechanisms for Intensity Modulation:** > - **Microbending loss:** Mechanical deformation causes light to leak out of the fiber core. > - **Evanescent wave coupling:** The measurand changes the refractive index around the cladding, altering guided power. > - **Absorption modulation:** The measurand absorbs light at specific wavelengths. > - **Reflectance modulation:** The measurand changes the reflectivity of a target surface. **KTU Critical Formula — Microbend Loss:** $$\alpha_{mb} = \frac{K}{d^{2}}$$ Where $\alpha_{mb}$ is the microbending loss coefficient, $d$ is the fiber core diameter, and $K$ is a constant depending on the mode distribution and deformation geometry. --- ### B. Phase-Modulated Fiber Optic Sensors (Interferometric) - The measurand changes the **optical phase** of light traveling through the fiber. - Demodulation requires an **interferometer** (Mach-Zehnder, Michelson, Sagnac, or Fabry-Perot). - Most sensitive class of FOS — detects phase changes on the order of **$10^{-6}$ radians**. **KTU Critical Formula — Phase Shift Induced by Measurand:** $$\Delta \phi = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \, (n \, \Delta L + L \, \Delta n)$$ Where: - $\Delta L$ — physical elongation of the fiber. - $\Delta n$ — change in refractive index due to the strain-optic effect. - $L$ — length of the sensing fiber. - $\lambda$ — operating wavelength in vacuum. - $n$ — effective refractive index of the guided mode. --- ### C. Wavelength-Modulated Fiber Optic Sensors - The measurand shifts the **peak wavelength** of the optical signal. - Demodulation uses an **optical spectrum analyzer (OSA)** or a matched filter. - Includes **Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG)** sensors — the KTU Board favourite. **KTU Critical Formula — Bragg Wavelength:** $$\lambda_B = 2 \, n_{eff} \, \Lambda$$ Where: - $\lambda_B$ — Bragg (reflected) wavelength. - $n_{eff}$ — effective refractive index of the core mode. - $\Lambda$ — grating period (physical spacing between grating planes). **Strain Sensitivity:** $$\Delta \lambda_B = \lambda_B \, (1 - p_e) \, \varepsilon$$ Where $\varepsilon$ is the applied axial strain and $p_e$ is the **effective strain-optic coefficient** ($\approx 0.22$ for silica at 1550 nm). **Temperature Sensitivity:** $$\Delta \lambda_B = \lambda_B \, (\alpha + \xi) \, \Delta T$$ Where $\alpha$ is the thermal expansion coefficient and $\xi$ is the thermo-optic coefficient. --- ### D. Polarization-Modulated Fiber Optic Sensors - The measurand changes the **state of polarization (SOP)** of the guided light. - Demodulation uses **polarization-maintaining fiber** and **polarization analyzers**. - Common in current sensors (Faraday effect) and magnetic field sensors. **KTU Critical Formula — Faraday Rotation:** $$\theta = V \, B \, L$$ Where: - $\theta$ — rotation angle of the polarization plane (radians). - $V$ — Verdet constant of the fiber material (rad / T / m). - $B$ — magnetic flux density (Tesla). - $L$ — length of fiber exposed to the magnetic field. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Symbols | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Microbend Loss | $\alpha_{mb} = K / d^{2}$ | $K$, $d$ | dB/m, $\mu$m | | Phase Modulation | $\Delta \phi = (2\pi / \lambda)(n \Delta L + L \Delta n)$ | $n, L, \Delta L, \Delta n, \lambda$ | rad | | Bragg Wavelength | $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$ | $n_{eff}, \Lambda$ | nm, nm | | FBG Strain Sensitivity | $\Delta \lambda_B = \lambda_B (1 - p_e) \varepsilon$ | $p_e, \varepsilon$ | nm, dimensionless | | FBG Temperature Sensitivity | $\Delta \lambda_B = \lambda_B (\alpha + \xi) \Delta T$ | $\alpha, \xi, \Delta T$ | nm, K$^{-1}$, K | | Faraday Rotation | $\theta = V B L$ | $V, B, L$ | rad, T/m/T, T, m | | Sagnac Rotation | $\Delta \phi = (8 \pi A / \lambda c) \, \Omega$ | $A, \Omega$ | rad, m$^{2}$, rad/s | | Detector Responsivity | $R = I_{ph} / P_{opt}$ | $I_{ph}, P_{opt}$ | A/W | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Sensor Type | Industry Use Case | | :--- | :--- | | Microbend FOS | Civil structural health monitoring (bridges, dams) | | FBG Sensor | Oil & gas downhole temperature/pressure profiling | | Sagnac Interferometer | Fiber optic gyroscope (FOG) for aircraft navigation | | Mach-Zehnder FOS | Hydrophone arrays in submarine SONAR systems | | Evanescent Wave Sensor | Chemical / biological toxin detection | | Polarimetric Sensor | High-voltage current measurement in power grids | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** FOS systems are immune to **electromagnetic interference (EMI)**, can operate in **explosive environments** (no electrical sparks), and tolerate extreme **temperatures up to 300°C** — making them irreplaceable in oil refineries, nuclear plants, and aerospace applications. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Phase Modulation by Strain (Elasto-Optic Effect) We begin with the fundamental phase of a light wave traveling through a fiber of length $L$: $$\phi = \beta \, L$$ Where $\beta$ is the propagation constant of the guided mode. When strain $\varepsilon$ is applied: - The physical length changes: $L \rightarrow L + \Delta L$. - The refractive index changes: $n \rightarrow n + \Delta n$. The differential change in phase is therefore: $$d\phi = \beta \, dL + L \, d\beta$$ Rewriting in terms of small finite changes: $$\Delta \phi = \beta \, \Delta L + L \, \Delta \beta$$ For a single-mode fiber, $\beta = (2 \pi / \lambda) \, n_{eff}$, so: $$\Delta \beta = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \, \Delta n_{eff}$$ Substituting: $$\Delta \phi = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \, n_{eff} \, \Delta L + \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \, L \, \Delta n_{eff}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta \phi = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \left( n_{eff} \, \Delta L + L \, \Delta n_{eff} \right)}$$ This is the **generalized phase-shift equation** that KTU examiners expect students to derive from first principles. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: FBG Bragg Wavelength Shift Under Strain Starting from the Bragg condition: $$\lambda_B = 2 \, n_{eff} \, \Lambda$$ Differentiating both sides with respect to strain $\varepsilon$: $$\frac{d\lambda_B}{d\varepsilon} = 2 \left( \frac{d n_{eff}}{d\varepsilon} \Lambda + n_{eff} \frac{d\Lambda}{d\varepsilon} \right)$$ Using the photoelastic relation: $$\frac{d n_{eff}}{d\varepsilon} = -p_e \, n_{eff}$$ And the geometric relation: $$\frac{d\Lambda}{d\varepsilon} = \Lambda$$ Substituting: $$\frac{d\lambda_B}{d\varepsilon} = 2 \left( -p_e \, n_{eff} \Lambda + n_{eff} \, \Lambda \right) = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda (1 - p_e)$$ Since $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$: $$\boxed{\frac{\Delta \lambda_B}{\Delta \varepsilon} = \lambda_B \, (1 - p_e)}$$ For silica fiber at $\lambda = 1550$ nm, $p_e \approx 0.22$, giving a strain sensitivity of approximately **1.2 pm / $\mu\varepsilon$**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** A fiber Bragg grating sensor has the following parameters at room temperature (25°C): - $n_{eff} = 1.448$ - $\Lambda = 535$ nm - $p_e = 0.22$ - $\alpha = 0.55 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C (thermal expansion of silica) - $\xi = 8.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C (thermo-optic coefficient) The sensor is subjected to a strain of $1000 \, \mu\varepsilon$ and a temperature rise of 50°C. Calculate the **total shift** in Bragg wavelength. **Step 1 — Compute the unstrained Bragg wavelength at 25°C:** $$\lambda_B = 2 \times 1.448 \times 535 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}$$ $$\lambda_B = 1549.36 \, \text{nm}$$ **Step 2 — Compute wavelength shift due to strain:** $$\Delta \lambda_{B, \varepsilon} = \lambda_B \, (1 - p_e) \, \varepsilon$$ $$\Delta \lambda_{B, \varepsilon} = 1549.36 \times (1 - 0.22) \times 1000 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\Delta \lambda_{B, \varepsilon} = 1549.36 \times 0.78 \times 10^{-3} = 1.208 \, \text{nm}$$ **Step 3 — Compute wavelength shift due to temperature:** $$\Delta \lambda_{B, T} = \lambda_B \, (\alpha + \xi) \, \Delta T$$ $$\Delta \lambda_{B, T} = 1549.36 \times (0.55 + 8.6) \times 10^{-6} \times 50$$ $$\Delta \lambda_{B, T} = 1549.36 \times 9.15 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 = 0.709 \, \text{nm}$$ **Step 4 — Total shift (superposition):** $$\Delta \lambda_{B, total} = \Delta \lambda_{B, \varepsilon} + \Delta \lambda_{B, T}$$ $$\Delta \lambda_{B, total} = 1.208 + 0.709 = 1.917 \, \text{nm}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{B, final} = 1549.36 + 1.92 = 1551.28 \, \text{nm}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cross-Sensitivity Note:** In real FBG sensors, strain and temperature both shift the Bragg wavelength, creating a **cross-sensitivity problem**. KTU Board questions often ask how to decouple these two effects — the answer is using a **reference FBG** (strain-isolated) or a dual-grating configuration. # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fiber Optic Sensor System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Light Source

3 dB Splitter] B --> C[Sensing Region
Fiber Optic Head] C --> D[Reference Region
Compensation Arm] D --> E[Photodetector
PIN or APD] E --> F[Signal Conditioner
Amplifier and Filter] F --> G[Processor and Display
Microcontroller Unit] C -. modulates light .-> E style A fill:#e3f2fd style C fill:#fff9c4 style E fill:#c8e6c9 style G fill:#f8bbd0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - The **light source** (A) provides stable optical power. > - The **coupler** (B) splits light into sensing and reference arms. > - The **sensing region** (C) is where the measurand modulates the light. > - The **photodetector** (E) converts the modulated light into an electrical signal. > - The **processor** (G) interprets the signal into a meaningful reading. --- ## 4.2 Classification Flowchart of Sensors ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Sensors] --> S1[Electronic Sensors] S0 --> S2[Optical Sensors] S1 --> E1[Resistive

KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define a fiber optic sensor. Differentiate between intrinsic and extrinsic fiber optic sensors with one example each.** **Model Answer:** A fiber optic sensor (FOS) is a device that uses an optical fiber to sense a physical, chemical, or biological parameter by modulating a property of light (intensity, phase, wavelength, or polarization) traveling through it. | Feature | Intrinsic FOS | Extrinsic FOS | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Sensing location | Inside the fiber itself | Outside the fiber; fiber only relays light | | Light-fiber interaction | Direct (evanescent, microbend) | Indirect (external transducer) | | Example | Microbend strain sensor | Fiber-coupled pyrometer | --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **State and explain the principle of operation of a Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensor.** **Model Answer:** An FBG is a periodic modulation of the refractive index inscribed in the core of a single-mode fiber using UV laser interference. It acts as a wavelength-selective mirror, reflecting a specific wavelength (the Bragg wavelength $\lambda_B$) given by: $$\lambda_B = 2 \, n_{eff} \, \Lambda$$ When a measurand (strain or temperature) changes $n_{eff}$ or $\Lambda$, $\lambda_B$ shifts. By tracking this shift using an OSA or matched filter demodulator, the magnitude of the measurand is recovered. --- ## PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has an internal choice between Question A and Question B, each carrying sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Mach-Zehnder Interferometric (MZI) fiber optic sensor** for the measurement of strain. **(b) [7 Marks)** Derive the expression for the phase shift produced by an axial strain $\varepsilon$ in a single-mode fiber. #### Model Solution for (a): The Mach-Zehnder Interferometric (MZI) sensor consists of: 1. A laser source of wavelength $\lambda$. 2. A 3-dB fiber coupler to split the beam into **two arms**: sensing arm and reference arm. 3. A sensing arm of length $L$ exposed to the measurand. 4. A reference arm (shielded from the measurand). 5. A second 3-dB coupler to recombine the two beams. 6. Photodetectors at both output ports. When strain is applied to the sensing arm, its optical path length changes, producing a phase difference: $$\Delta \phi = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \, n \, L \, (1 - p_e) \, \varepsilon$$ The recombined beams interfere, and the photodetector output varies sinusoidally with $\Delta \phi$. By counting interference fringes, the strain magnitude is recovered. #### Model Solution for (b): Refer to **Section 3.1** for the full derivation. Final boxed result: $$\boxed{\Delta \phi = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \left( n \, \Delta L + L \, \Delta n \right)}$$ Where $\Delta L = \varepsilon L$ and $\Delta n = -p_e \, n \, \varepsilon$, leading to: $$\Delta \phi = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \, n \, L \, \varepsilon \, (1 - p_e)$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of an **FBG-based temperature sensor**. Derive the expression for its temperature sensitivity. **(b) [7 Marks]** A fiber optic gyroscope (Sagnac interferometer) is used in an inertial navigation system. With the relevant formula, explain how rotation rate $\Omega$ is measured. #### Model Solution for (a): **Construction:** A periodic refractive index grating is UV-inscribed in the core of a single-mode fiber. The grating period $\Lambda$ is typically 500–550 nm for telecom wavelengths. **Working:** The Bragg wavelength is $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$. When temperature changes by $\Delta T$: $$\Delta \lambda_B = \lambda_B (\alpha + \xi) \Delta T$$ This is derived as follows: $$\frac{d\lambda_B}{dT} = 2 \left( \Lambda \frac{dn_{eff}}{dT} + n_{eff} \frac{d\Lambda}{dT} \right)$$ With $dn_{eff}/dT = \xi n_{eff}$ and $d\Lambda / dT = \alpha \Lambda$: $$\frac{d\lambda_B}{dT} = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda (\alpha + \xi) = \lambda_B (\alpha + \xi)$$ For silica at 1550 nm: $\alpha + \xi \approx 9.15 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, giving a sensitivity of about **14 pm / °C**. #### Model Solution for (b): The Sagnac interferometer consists of a fiber coil of $N$ turns and radius $R$. Light is split into two counter-propagating beams that traverse the coil. When the coil rotates with angular velocity $\Omega$, the two beams experience different path lengths due to the Sagnac effect: $$\Delta L = \frac{4 \pi R^2 N \Omega}{c}$$ The corresponding phase shift is: $$\Delta \phi = \frac{8 \pi A N \Omega}{\lambda c}$$ Where $A = \pi R^2$ is the area of a single loop. The photodetector output is processed to extract $\Omega$, forming the basis of the **Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG)** used in aircraft, missiles, and autonomous vehicles. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing intrinsic vs extrinsic sensors** — lose 1 mark if examples are swapped. > 2. **Forgetting the $(1 - p_e)$ factor** in strain-phase derivations — lose 2 marks in Part B. > 3. **Mixing up units** — KTU strictly expects SI units (T for Tesla, not Gauss). > 4. **Skipping the block diagram** in MZI/FBG questions — examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for the diagram. > 5. **Not stating the operating wavelength (1550 nm)** for typical FBG problems — lose 0.5 mark for vagueness. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - A **sensor** converts a physical measurand into a readable signal; **FOS** specifically uses light modulation. - Optical sensors are classified into **intensity, phase, wavelength, and polarization** modulated types — this is the KTU Board classification spine. - **FBG sensors** are wavelength-modulated and governed by $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$. - **Microbend sensors** are intensity-modulated and rely on loss proportional to $1/d^{2}$. - **Mach-Zehnder and Sagnac** interferometers are phase-modulated sensors with extremely high sensitivity (down to nanoradian levels). - The **elasto-optic coefficient** $p_e \approx 0.22$ for silica — memorize this for derivations. - **Cross-sensitivity** (strain + temperature) in FBG sensors requires a **reference grating** or dual-parameter demodulation scheme. - Always draw a **block diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts — KTU examiners allocate at least 1 mark for it. - Sagnac effect is the operating principle of the **Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG)** used in modern inertial navigation. - Faraday effect ($\theta = VBL$) is the principle behind **optical current transformers** in power systems.
